> 2. **Forgetting the "4" derivation step** — students often jump to the formula without showing the force balance. KTU expects the derivation.
> 3. **No design comment** — losing the 1-mark "design implication" is common. Always relate the numerical answer to engineering practice.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION BULLETS — Read this 30 minutes before your exam:**

- ✅ **Composite** = Matrix + Fiber + Interface. Skip any → poor composite.
- ✅ **Rule of Mixtures (ROM)** is the **#1 most-asked concept** in KTU Module 1.
- ✅ **Longitudinal modulus** = **Voigt / Iso-strain** → Use the **direct** ROM: $E_{cl} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$.
- ✅ **Transverse modulus** = **Reuss / Iso-stress** → Use the **inverse** ROM: $1/E_{ct} = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$.
- ✅ **Always** state the volume-fraction constraint: $V_f + V_m = 1$ (or $= 1 - V_v$ if voids exist).
- ✅ **Critical fiber length** $l_c = \sigma_f d / (2\tau_i)$ — memorize and know how to derive.
- ✅ **Fiber length $l > l_c$** → fiber breaks (efficient). **$l < l_c$** → fiber pulls out (inefficient).
- ✅ **Specific strength** $\sigma/\rho$ and **specific modulus** $E/\rho$ are the key design drivers for aerospace/automotive.
- ✅ **Anisotropy** is the **defining trait** of composites — properties depend on direction.
- ✅ **Always check which phase fails first** in strength problems (compare $\sigma'_m$ with $\sigma_m$).
- ✅ **Matrix-dominated transverse properties** → Transverse modulus is much lower than longitudinal (typical ratio 1:5 to 1:20).
- ✅ **Interphase** is the "third phase" — controls load transfer; weak interface = premature failure.
- ✅ **Aspect ratio $l/d$** is critical for discontinuous fiber reinforcement efficiency.
- ✅ **CTE tailoring** is a unique advantage — composites can be made with **zero** or **negative** thermal expansion.
- ✅ **Voids** ($V_v$) reduce ALL properties — minimize through proper processing (autoclave, vacuum bagging).
- ✅ **Common KTU 14-mark pattern:** Theory (7) + Numerical (7). Practice both halves.
- ✅ **Common units to remember:** GPa for modulus, MPa for strength, kg/m³ for density, kN·m/kg for specific properties.
- ✅ **Typical exam numbers:** $V_f$ between 0.3 and 0.6, $E_f$ (carbon) = 230 GPa, $E_f$ (glass) = 72 GPa, $E_m$ (epoxy) = 3–5 GPa.
- ✅ **Karamba, Boeing 787, Airbus A350** — know one real-world example to score extra marks in "applications" sub-questions.
- ✅ **KTU frequently tests:** Definition, ROM derivation, Critical fiber length, Comparison with metals, Anisotropy explanation.

**Best of luck with your KTU 2024 Scheme examination!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET413 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Composite Materials**, the term **"Functions"** refers to the distinct engineering roles and responsibilities performed by each constituent phase of a composite — namely the **Matrix**, the **Reinforcement**, and the **Interfacial Region (Interface/Interphase)**. These functions are not arbitrary; they are deliberately engineered so that the synergistic combination of dissimilar materials produces a final composite whose properties exceed those of its individual constituents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMET413 Module 1):**
> *A composite material is a heterogeneous, multiphase engineering material consisting of two or more chemically distinct constituents combined at a macroscopic level to produce a new material with properties superior to those of the individual components. The **functions** of each phase — matrix, reinforcement, and interface — are designed to collectively bear mechanical, thermal, environmental, and chemical loads.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Concrete & Steel" Building Model

Imagine you are constructing a multi-storey building:

- **Steel Rods (Rebar)** → These are extremely strong in **tension** (being pulled apart) but weak and prone to buckling in **compression**. Their **function** is to carry tensile loads.
- **Concrete** → Excellent in **compression** (being squashed) but brittle and weak in tension. Its **function** is to hold the rebars in place, protect them from corrosion, and distribute compressive loads uniformly.
- **Bond Zone (Concrete-Steel Contact)** → The friction and chemical adhesion between concrete and steel. Its **function** is to **transfer stress** from the weak concrete to the strong steel so they act as a single unit.

This is **exactly** how a fibre-reinforced polymer (FRP) composite works. Carbon/glass fibres (rebar) carry tensile loads, the polymer resin (concrete) holds them and transfers loads, and the fibre-matrix interface (bond zone) ensures stress transfer. The composite is far stronger, lighter, and more durable than either material alone.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A composite is more than a mixture — it is a **functionally engineered system** where each component has a specific job. Removing or degrading any one function (e.g., poor interfacial bonding) can cause catastrophic failure of the entire structure.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following standard engineering metrics are used to quantify the **functions** of composite constituents:

- **Volume Fraction ($V_f$, $V_m$):** Ratio of volume of a constituent to total composite volume. Unitless. Typical fibre $V_f$ in advanced composites: **0.3 to 0.7**.
- **Weight (Mass) Fraction ($W_f$, $W_m$):** Ratio of mass of a constituent to total composite mass. Unitless.
- **Density ($\rho$):** Mass per unit volume, in **kg/m³** or **g/cm³**.
- **Modulus ($E$):** Stiffness, in **GPa**.
- **Tensile Strength ($\sigma$):** Maximum tensile stress, in **MPa** or **GPa**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module 1 specifically expects students to be able to (a) identify each constituent, (b) state its primary function(s), and (c) explain how the function contributes to the overall composite performance.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Function #1 — The Matrix (Binder Phase)

The **matrix** is the continuous phase that surrounds, supports, and protects the reinforcement. Its functions are:

1. **Load Transfer Mechanism** — Transfers applied external loads to the reinforcement through shear stresses at the interface. Without the matrix, the fibres would have no way to "feel" the external load.
2. **Reinforcement Bonding & Positioning** — Holds the reinforcement in the desired geometric orientation (unidirectional, woven, random) and at the correct spacing/volume fraction.
3. **Environmental Protection** — Shields fibres (especially glass and aramid) from moisture, chemicals, UV radiation, oxidation, and abrasion.
4. **Shape & Surface Finish** — Provides the final moulded shape and a smooth, defect-free surface finish.
5. **Crack Propagation Resistance** — Plastic deformation of the matrix absorbs energy and deflects cracks, increasing fracture toughness.
6. **Transverse Load Bearing** — Carries loads in directions where the reinforcement is weak (e.g., perpendicular to unidirectional fibres).

> **Common Matrix Materials:** Thermosetting polymers (epoxy, polyester, vinyl ester), thermoplastic polymers (PEEK, PPS), metals (aluminium, titanium in MMCs), ceramics (SiC, alumina in CMCs).

### 2.2 Function #2 — The Reinforcement (Dispersed/Load-Bearing Phase)

The **reinforcement** is the discontinuous, stronger, and stiffer phase. Its primary functions are:

1. **Primary Load Bearing (Strength & Stiffness)** — Carries the majority of the mechanical load, especially tensile and flexural loads.
2. **Stiffness Enhancement** — Increases the Young's modulus and flexural rigidity of the composite.
3. **Property Directionality Control** — By orienting fibres strategically (e.g., 0°/45°/-45°/90° layups), engineers can tailor strength and stiffness in specific directions.
4. **Thermal/Electrical Conductivity Tailoring** — Carbon fibres provide electrical conductivity; ceramic fibres provide thermal resistance.
5. **Weight Reduction** — High specific strength (strength-to-weight ratio) and specific modulus (modulus-to-weight ratio).

> **Common Reinforcement Forms:** Continuous fibres (carbon, glass, aramid/Kevlar), discontinuous (chopped) fibres, whiskers, particles, nanofillers (CNTs, graphene).

### 2.3 Function #3 — The Interface / Interphase

The **interface** is the boundary region between the matrix and reinforcement, often extending as an **interphase** with distinct chemical/physical properties. Its functions are:

1. **Stress Transfer Bridge** — The most critical function. Transfers mechanical stress from the matrix to the high-strength fibres via shear stresses.
2. **Adhesion & Wettability Control** — Ensures the matrix "wets out" the fibre surface during processing, eliminating voids.
3. **Crack Deflection Barrier** — A properly engineered interface deflects propagating cracks, increasing toughness. A too-strong interface causes brittle failure.
4. **Chemical Compatibility Buffer** — Reduces chemical reactions between dissimilar constituents at elevated temperatures.
5. **Thermal Expansion Mismatch Accommodation** — Absorbs differential thermal stresses caused by mismatched coefficients of thermal expansion (CTE).

> **Interfacial Bonding Mechanisms:** Mechanical interlocking, chemical bonding, van der Waals forces, and adsorption/wetting.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula Name** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Variables & Units** | **Engineering Utility** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Volume Fraction of Fibre | $V_f = \dfrac{v_f}{v_c} = \dfrac{v_f}{v_f + v_m}$ | $v_f$ = fibre volume, $v_m$ = matrix volume, $v_c$ = composite volume (m³) | Determines reinforcement content; critical for stiffness/strength prediction |
| Volume Fraction of Matrix | $V_m = 1 - V_f$ | Unitless | Matrix content for bonding & protection |
| Weight Fraction of Fibre | $W_f = \dfrac{w_f}{w_c} = \dfrac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m}$ | $w$ = weight (kg), $\rho$ = density (kg/m³) | Used in cost analysis & prepreg manufacturing |
| Density (Rule of Mixtures) | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | $\rho$ in kg/m³ | Predicts composite weight for aerospace design |
| Modulus — Parallel (Voigt) Loading | $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | $E$ in GPa | Iso-strain condition; fibre-aligned loading |
| Modulus — Series (Reuss) Loading | $\dfrac{1}{E_c} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | $E$ in GPa | Iso-stress condition; transverse loading |
| Strength — Parallel (Upper Bound) | $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ | $\sigma$ in MPa | Predicts tensile strength along fibre direction |
| Specific Strength | $\dfrac{\sigma_c}{\rho_c}$ | Units: m²/s² (or kNm/kg) | Lightweight design merit index |
| Specific Modulus | $\dfrac{E_c}{\rho_c}$ | Units: m²/s² (or kNm/kg) | Stiffness-to-weight merit index |
| Critical Fibre Length | $L_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}$ | $L_c$ = critical length, $d$ = fibre diameter, $\tau_i$ = interfacial shear strength (m, MPa) | Minimum fibre length for effective load transfer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule:** In all "function" calculations, ensure $V_f + V_m = 1$ exactly. KTU examiners frequently test this unit-consistency check.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The "functions" concept is the foundational design philosophy behind:
- **Boeing 787 Dreamliner** — 50% composite materials by weight (carbon fibre/epoxy), reducing fuel burn by 20%.
- **Formula 1 Monocoque** — Carbon-fibre composites provide crashworthiness and rigidity at minimum weight.
- **Wind Turbine Blades** — Glass-fibre composites (function: fatigue resistance + low weight) for 80+ metre blades.
- **Orthopaedic Implants** — Hydroxyapatite-reinforced polymer composites (function: biocompatibility + load bearing).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volume Fraction Relationship

**Starting Principle:** The total volume of the composite is the sum of the volumes of its constituents (assuming no voids for the ideal case).

$$v_c = v_f + v_m$$

**Step 1 — Divide both sides by $v_c$:**

$$\frac{v_c}{v_c} = \frac{v_f}{v_c} + \frac{v_m}{v_c}$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the definition of volume fraction.** The left side equals 1, and the right side defines $V_f$ and $V_m$:

$$1 = V_f + V_m$$

**Step 3 — Solve for matrix volume fraction:**

$$V_m = 1 - V_f$$

> **Interpretation:** This is a fundamental identity used in **every** composite property calculation. It tells us that increasing fibre content automatically decreases matrix content.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Rule of Mixtures for Density (ROM-Density)

**Starting Principle:** Density is mass per unit volume. The total mass of the composite equals the sum of the masses of its constituents.

$$m_c = m_f + m_m$$

**Step 1 — Express mass in terms of density and volume** ($m = \rho \cdot v$):

$$\rho_c \cdot v_c = \rho_f \cdot v_f + \rho_m \cdot v_m$$

**Step 2 — Divide both sides by the total composite volume $v_c$:**

$$\rho_c = \rho_f \cdot \frac{v_f}{v_c} + \rho_m \cdot \frac{v_m}{v_c}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute volume fractions:**

$$\boxed{\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m}$$

> **Interpretation:** The composite density is a **linear weighted average** of the constituent densities, weighted by volume fraction. This is the **parallel (Voigt) model** and represents the upper bound of composite density.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Rule of Mixtures for Longitudinal Modulus (ROM-Parallel)

**Assumption — Iso-strain condition (parallel loading):** When a load is applied parallel to continuous, aligned fibres, both the fibre and matrix experience the **same strain** ($\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$).

**Step 1 — Express stress in each phase** using Hooke's law ($\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$):

$$\sigma_f = E_f \cdot \varepsilon_c \quad ; \quad \sigma_m = E_m \cdot \varepsilon_c$$

**Step 2 — Express the total force on the composite as the sum of forces on each phase:**

$$F_c = F_f + F_m$$

**Step 3 — Convert forces to stresses and areas** ($F = \sigma \cdot A$):

$$\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$$

**Step 4 — Divide by total area and substitute the iso-strain condition:**

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f \cdot \frac{A_f}{A_c} + \sigma_m \cdot \frac{A_m}{A_c}$$

$$\sigma_c = E_f \varepsilon_c \cdot \frac{A_f}{A_c} + E_m \varepsilon_c \cdot \frac{A_m}{A_c}$$

**Step 5 — Factor out $\varepsilon_c$ and substitute area fractions = volume fractions (for aligned continuous fibres):**

$$\sigma_c = \varepsilon_c \left( E_f V_f + E_m V_m \right)$$

**Step 6 — Apply Hooke's law to the composite ($\sigma_c = E_c \varepsilon_c$) and solve:**

$$\boxed{E_c^{\parallel} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m}$$

> **Interpretation:** This is the **Voigt upper bound** for composite stiffness. The fibre stiffness $E_f$ is weighted more heavily as $V_f$ increases.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Inverse Rule of Mixtures for Transverse Modulus (ROM-Series)

**Assumption — Iso-stress condition (series loading, transverse to fibres):** When a load is applied **perpendicular** to fibres, both phases experience the **same stress** ($\sigma_c = \sigma_f = \sigma_m$) but **different strains**.

**Step 1 — Apply Hooke's law to each phase:**

$$\varepsilon_f = \frac{\sigma_c}{E_f} \quad ; \quad \varepsilon_m = \frac{\sigma_c}{E_m}$$

**Step 2 — Express total composite strain as the volume-weighted average of phase strains:**

$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f V_f + \varepsilon_m V_m$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the expressions from Step 1:**

$$\varepsilon_c = \frac{\sigma_c}{E_f} V_f + \frac{\sigma_c}{E_m} V_m$$

**Step 4 — Apply Hooke's law to the composite and divide both sides by $\sigma_c$:**

$$\frac{1}{E_c} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{E_c^{\perp}} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}}$$

> **Interpretation:** This is the **Reuss lower bound**. It explains why the transverse stiffness of a unidirectional composite is **much lower** than its longitudinal stiffness — the soft matrix dominates transverse loading.

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Critical Fibre Length

**Engineering Question:** What is the minimum fibre length required for the matrix to fully load the fibre up to its ultimate tensile strength?

**Step 1 — Force balance on a single fibre of length $L$ and diameter $d$:** The tensile force in the fibre ($F_f$) must equal the shear force transferred through the fibre-matrix interface:

$$F_f = \sigma_f \cdot A_f = \sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

$$F_{shear} = \tau_i \cdot A_{interface} = \tau_i \cdot (\pi d \cdot L)$$

**Step 2 — Set them equal for the critical condition (fibre just reaches its ultimate stress):**

$$\sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot L_c$$

**Step 3 — Cancel $\pi d$ from both sides:**

$$\sigma_f \cdot \frac{d}{4} = \tau_i \cdot L_c$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the critical length $L_c$:**

$$\boxed{L_c = \frac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{4 \tau_i}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Factor of 2:** Some textbooks (notably Kelly & Zweben) present $L_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f d}{2 \tau_i}$ when assuming shear stress acts on **both ends** of the fibre (semi-circular load build-up). Always check which convention your KTU examiner follows. The factor of 2 vs. 4 is a **favourite trick question** in KTU valuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Composite Material

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[External Load Applied] --> B[Matrix Phase]
    B -- "Stress Transfer via Shear" --> C[Fibre-Matrix Interface]
    C -- "Load Transfer to Fibres" --> D[Reinforcement Phase]
    D -- "Tensile Load Carried" --> E[Strong & Stiff Composite Response]
    B -- "Protects & Binds" --> D
    B -- "Carries Transverse Load" --> E
    C -- "Deflects Cracks" --> E
    A2[Environmental Attack] --> B2[Matrix Phase]
    B2 -- "Shields Fibres" --> D2[Reinforcement Phase]
    B2 -- "Shields" --> C2[Fibre-Matrix Interface]
    F[Thermal Expansion Mismatch] --> C2
    C2 -- "Absorbs Differential Strain" --> E2[Stable Composite Performance]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Constituent Functions Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MatrixFunctions
        M1[Load Transfer via Shear]
        M2[Bind and Hold Reinforcement]
        M3[Environmental Protection]
        M4[Shape and Surface Finish]
        M5[Transverse Load Bearing]
        M6[Crack Energy Absorption]
    end
    subgraph ReinforcementFunctions
        R1[Primary Tensile Load Bearing]
        R2[Stiffness Enhancement]
        R3[Property Directionality Control]
        R4[Specific Strength and Modulus]
    end
    subgraph InterfaceFunctions
        I1[Stress Transfer Bridge]
        I2[Adhesion and Wettability]
        I3[Crack Deflection Barrier]
        I4[Thermal Expansion Buffer]
        I5[Chemical Compatibility Zone]
    end
    MatrixFunctions --> CompositeOutput[Engineered Composite Performance]
    ReinforcementFunctions --> CompositeOutput
    InterfaceFunctions --> CompositeOutput
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Function-to-Application Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F1[Fibre Load-Bearing Function] --> APP1[Aerospace Primary Structures]
    F2[Matrix Environmental Shield] --> APP2[Marine Boat Hulls]
    F3[Interface Stress Transfer] --> APP3[Pressure Vessels]
    F4[Lightweight High Stiffness] --> APP4[Wind Turbine Blades]
    F5[Tailorable Anisotropy] --> APP5[Sports Equipment - Tennis Rackets, F1 Cars]
    F6[Biocompatibility] --> APP6[Orthopaedic Bone Implants]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper PEMET413]**
*State the primary functions of the matrix and reinforcement phases in a fibre-reinforced composite material.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Functions of the Matrix (1½ marks):**
1. To bind the reinforcement together and hold it in the desired geometric position.
2. To transfer the applied external load to the reinforcement through shear stresses at the interface.
3. To protect the reinforcement from environmental degradation (moisture, chemicals, abrasion).
4. To provide the final shape and a smooth surface finish.
5. To carry transverse (off-axis) loads and to absorb energy through plastic deformation, thereby increasing fracture toughness.

**Functions of the Reinforcement (1½ marks):**
1. To bear the primary mechanical load, especially tensile and flexural loads, because reinforcement is the stronger and stiffer phase.
2. To enhance the overall stiffness (Young's modulus) of the composite.
3. To provide directional control of mechanical, thermal, and electrical properties through fibre orientation.
4. To deliver high specific strength and specific modulus, enabling lightweight structural design.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
*Explain the function of the fibre-matrix interface in a composite. Why is it called the "stress transfer bridge"?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Function of the Interface (2 marks):**
The fibre-matrix interface is the boundary region where the matrix and reinforcement meet. Its functions are:
1. **Stress transfer:** It transmits mechanical load from the weaker matrix to the stronger reinforcement via interfacial shear stresses.
2. **Adhesion & wettability:** It ensures the matrix properly wets out the fibre surface during processing, eliminating voids and weak spots.
3. **Crack deflection:** A well-engineered interface deflects propagating cracks, increasing the composite's fracture toughness.
4. **Thermal buffering:** It accommodates the differential thermal expansion between fibre and matrix at elevated temperatures.

**Why "Stress Transfer Bridge" (1 mark):**
The interface acts as a **mechanical bridge** between two dissimilar materials. Without it, the strong fibres would simply slide out of the matrix under load, and the composite would fail. The interface is the "bridge" that allows the stress to "cross over" from the matrix to the fibre, enabling the composite to behave as a unified, load-bearing system.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **functions of the matrix, reinforcement, and interface** in a composite material. Discuss how the failure of each function affects the composite's overall performance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A unidirectional glass-fibre reinforced epoxy composite contains **60% by volume** of glass fibres. Given: $\rho_f = 2.55 \text{ g/cm}^3$, $\rho_m = 1.20 \text{ g/cm}^3$, $E_f = 72 \text{ GPa}$, $E_m = 3.5 \text{ GPa}$, $\sigma_{f,u} = 2.0 \text{ GPa}$, $\sigma_{m,u} = 80 \text{ MPa}$. Calculate:
(i) The density of the composite.
(ii) The longitudinal Young's modulus using the rule of mixtures.
(iii) The longitudinal tensile strength.
(iv) The specific modulus. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Matrix Functions (2 Marks):**
- Transfers load to fibres via shear (1 mark)
- Binds, protects, provides shape, carries transverse load (1 mark)

**Reinforcement Functions (2 Marks):**
- Bears primary tensile/flexural load (1 mark)
- Enhances stiffness, enables directional properties, provides high specific strength (1 mark)

**Interface Functions (2 Marks):**
- Acts as stress transfer bridge via shear (1 mark)
- Provides adhesion, deflects cracks, buffers thermal mismatch (1 mark)

**Failure Effects (1 Mark):**
- Matrix failure → loss of fibre protection and load transfer; environmental degradation.
- Reinforcement failure → catastrophic loss of strength and stiffness.
- Interface failure → fibre pull-out, debonding, catastrophic composite failure.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $V_f = 0.60$, $V_m = 0.40$, $\rho_f = 2.55 \text{ g/cm}^3$, $\rho_m = 1.20 \text{ g/cm}^3$, $E_f = 72 \text{ GPa}$, $E_m = 3.5 \text{ GPa}$, $\sigma_{f,u} = 2.0 \text{ GPa} = 2000 \text{ MPa}$, $\sigma_{m,u} = 80 \text{ MPa}$.

**(i) Composite Density (1 Mark):**

$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$$

$$\rho_c = (2.55)(0.60) + (1.20)(0.40)$$

$$\rho_c = 1.530 + 0.480$$

$$\boxed{\rho_c = 2.010 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

**(ii) Longitudinal Modulus (2 Marks):**

$$E_c^{\parallel} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$$

$$E_c^{\parallel} = (72)(0.60) + (3.5)(0.40)$$

$$E_c^{\parallel} = 43.20 + 1.40$$

$$\boxed{E_c^{\parallel} = 44.60 \text{ GPa}}$$

**(iii) Longitudinal Tensile Strength (2 Marks):**

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_{f,u} V_f + \sigma_{m,u} V_m$$

$$\sigma_c = (2000)(0.60) + (80)(0.40)$$

$$\sigma_c = 1200 + 32$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_c = 1232 \text{ MPa}}$$

**(iv) Specific Modulus (2 Marks):**

$$\text{Specific Modulus} = \frac{E_c^{\parallel}}{\rho_c} = \frac{44.60 \text{ GPa}}{2.010 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

Convert units for consistency: $44.60 \text{ GPa} = 44.60 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa} = 44.60 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$, and $2.010 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 2010 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

$$\text{Specific Modulus} = \frac{44.60 \times 10^9}{2010} = 22.19 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$\boxed{\text{Specific Modulus} \approx 22.19 \text{ MN·m/kg} = 22.19 \text{ GPa·cm}^3/\text{g}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - **Unit mismatch trap:** Students frequently forget to convert $\sigma_{f,u}$ from GPa to MPa before adding to $\sigma_{m,u}$ in MPa. This is a **2-mark** deduction in KTU scripts.
> - **Volume fraction identity:** If you use $V_m = 0.50$ instead of $0.40$ (because $V_f + V_m$ must equal 1), every subsequent answer is wrong. **Always verify** $V_f + V_m = 1$ first.
> - **Specific modulus units:** The examiner may accept any consistent unit system (GPa/(g/cm³) or MN·m/kg), but **must include units in the final answer** to avoid a ½-mark deduction.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **critical fibre length** $L_c$ in a fibre-reinforced composite. Derive the expression for $L_c$ in terms of fibre strength, fibre diameter, and interfacial shear strength. Discuss what happens if the actual fibre length $L$ is **(i) much less than $L_c$** and **(ii) much greater than $L_c$**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Carbon fibres of **7 μm diameter** and tensile strength **4.0 GPa** are embedded in an epoxy matrix with interfacial shear strength **30 MPa**. Calculate:
(i) The critical fibre length.
(ii) The critical aspect ratio.
(iii) If the fibres are only **2 mm** long, can they be loaded to their ultimate strength? Justify with a calculation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Concept of Critical Fibre Length (2 Marks):**
The critical fibre length $L_c$ is the **minimum fibre length** required for the matrix to transfer sufficient load through the interface to stress the fibre up to its ultimate tensile strength. If $L < L_c$, the fibre will pull out of the matrix before reaching its full strength, resulting in inefficient load transfer. If $L \gg L_c$, the fibre can be fully loaded and the composite achieves its maximum strength.

**Derivation (3 Marks):**
For force balance on a single fibre of length $L$ and diameter $d$, the tensile force in the fibre at its centre equals the shear force transferred through the cylindrical interface:

$$\sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot L_c$$

At the critical condition, $\sigma_f$ = $\sigma_{f,u}$ (ultimate fibre stress):

$$\sigma_{f,u} \cdot \frac{d}{4} = \tau_i \cdot L_c$$

$$\boxed{L_c = \frac{\sigma_{f,u} \cdot d}{4 \tau_i}}$$

**Effects of L relative to L_c (2 Marks):**

- **$L \ll L_c$ (Very short fibres):** The matrix cannot transfer enough load to fracture the fibre. The fibre pulls out of the matrix with little resistance. Composite strength is dominated by matrix strength and pull-out friction, not fibre strength. This is the regime of **chopped-fibre composites** with low efficiency.
- **$L \gg L_c$ (Very long/continuous fibres):** The fibre is fully loaded to its ultimate strength before pull-out can occur. The composite achieves its maximum possible strength, and the fibre-matrix interface governs failure. This is the regime of **continuous-fibre composites** used in aerospace primary structures.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $d = 7 \text{ μm} = 7 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$, $\sigma_{f,u} = 4.0 \text{ GPa} = 4.0 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$, $\tau_i = 30 \text{ MPa} = 30 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$.

**(i) Critical Fibre Length (2 Marks):**

$$L_c = \frac{\sigma_{f,u} \cdot d}{4 \tau_i} = \frac{(4.0 \times 10^9)(7 \times 10^{-6})}{4 \times (30 \times 10^6)}$$

$$L_c = \frac{28 \times 10^3}{120 \times 10^6} = \frac{28000}{120 \times 10^6}$$

$$L_c = 2.333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{L_c \approx 0.233 \text{ mm} = 233 \text{ μm}}$$

**(ii) Critical Aspect Ratio (2 Marks):**

$$\text{Critical Aspect Ratio} = \frac{L_c}{d} = \frac{233 \times 10^{-6}}{7 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 33.3$$

$$\boxed{\frac{L_c}{d} \approx 33.3}$$

**(iii) Analysis for L = 2 mm (3 Marks):**

Actual fibre length: $L = 2 \text{ mm} = 2000 \text{ μm} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$.

Compare $L$ with $L_c$:

$$\frac{L}{L_c} = \frac{2000 \text{ μm}}{233 \text{ μm}} \approx 8.58$$

Since $L = 2 \text{ mm} \gg L_c = 0.233 \text{ mm}$, the fibre length is **much greater** than the critical length. Therefore, **the fibres CAN be loaded to their ultimate strength** before any pull-out occurs. The composite will fail by fibre fracture rather than fibre pull-out, achieving high efficiency.

$$\boxed{\text{Yes, } L = 2 \text{ mm} \gg L_c = 0.233 \text{ mm}, \text{ so fibres will reach ultimate strength.}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):**
> - **Unit conversion errors:** Mixing μm with m in the same equation. Always convert **all** dimensions to SI units (metres, Pascals) before substituting. Wrong units = **2 marks** lost.
> - **Factor of 2 vs. 4 confusion:** Some KTU modules use $L_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f d}{2 \tau_i}$ (Kelly's convention). The result will differ by a factor of 2. **Read the textbook/module reference carefully** and stick to one convention throughout the exam.
> - **Comparison logic:** In part (iii), state explicitly whether $L > L_c$ or $L < L_c$ **and** the consequence. Vague answers lose the 1 conceptual mark.
> - **Formula for Part (a) derivation:** Examiners award **1 mark for setting up the force balance**, **1 mark for substituting the critical condition**, and **1 mark for the final algebraic rearrangement**. Skipping any step loses a mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Functions" in Composite Materials:**

- **Three Primary Constituents & Their Functions:**
  - **Matrix** → Binds, transfers load, protects, provides shape, carries transverse load, absorbs energy.
  - **Reinforcement** → Bears primary load, enhances stiffness, controls directionality, provides high specific strength.
  - **Interface/Interphase** → Stress transfer bridge, adhesion, crack deflection, thermal buffering.

- **Volume Fraction Identity (MUST Memorise):**
  $V_f + V_m = 1$ (no voids assumed).

- **Rule of Mixtures (ROM) — Two Versions:**
  - **Parallel (Voigt) — Iso-strain:** $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ → Longitudinal direction.
  - **Series (Reuss) — Iso-stress:** $\dfrac{1}{E_c} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ → Transverse direction.

- **Density ROM (Always Parallel):** $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$.

- **Critical Fibre Length:** $L_c = \dfrac{\sigma_{f,u} \cdot d}{4 \tau_i}$ (or with factor 2 — check convention).

- **Aspect Ratio Rule:** $L \gg L_c$ → fibre fracture (efficient); $L \ll L_c$ → fibre pull-out (inefficient).

- **Typical Fibre $V_f$ in Advanced Composites:** 0.3 to 0.7 (e.g., 0.6 for aerospace).

- **Why Interface is Critical:** Without proper stress transfer at the interface, even the strongest fibres act like loose fibres in a "bag" — the composite fails prematurely by debonding and pull-out.

- **Specific Strength / Specific Modulus:** These are the **merit indices** used in aerospace and automotive design. Always compute them when asked for "lightweight design" applications.

- **Common KTU Trap:** Mixing GPa and MPa without conversion; always convert before substituting.

- **Always state the function before the formula:** In 7-mark questions, examiners reward students who **explain the engineering meaning** of the calculation, not just plug in numbers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Composites Based on Structure and Matrix
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET413 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Composites Based on Structure and Matrix

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Composite Material** is a heterogeneous, multiphase engineered material formed by the rational combination of two or more distinct constituents (a **matrix** and a **reinforcement**) at a macroscopic level, which are insoluble in each other and are separated by a distinct **interface**. The combined system exhibits properties superior to those of its individual constituents acting alone.

For KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a composite is officially defined as:

> *"A material system comprising two or more distinct phases — a continuous phase (matrix) and a dispersed phase (reinforcement) — combined at a macroscopic level, where the constituents retain their identities and the interface plays a critical role in load transfer."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The classification of composites is foundational to the entire course. Every subsequent module on mechanics, manufacturing, and applications builds upon this taxonomy. Mastering both **matrix-based** and **structure-based** classifications is mandatory for Part A and Part B questions in ESE.

The two primary classification axes (according to KTU PEMET413) are:

| Axis | Basis of Classification | Key Categories |
|------|-------------------------|----------------|
| **Axis 1** | Type of Matrix Material | PMC, MMC, CMC, C/C |
| **Axis 2** | Geometry/Structure of Reinforcement | Fiber, Particulate, Laminate, Sandwich |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a **mud wall** (traditional Kerala *vaazhcha veedu* construction). On its own, wet mud is weak, cracks easily, and cannot bear tensile loads. But when you embed **straw (reinforcement)** into the mud (matrix), the resulting wall is far stronger, more crack-resistant, and durable. The mud binds everything together (matrix function) while the straw carries the tensile load (reinforcement function).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — Reinforced Concrete**
> - **Concrete** = Matrix (strong in compression, weak in tension)
> - **Steel Rebar** = Reinforcement (strong in tension)
> - **Bond/Adhesion at interface** = The grip that allows stress transfer
> - The resulting composite is strong in BOTH tension and compression — a property neither phase possesses alone.

Another classic example: **Plywood** is a laminate composite where thin wood veneers (plies) are stacked with alternating grain directions and bonded by adhesive. Each ply alone warps and splits easily, but the laminate is dimensionally stable and strong.

---

### 1.3 The Three Essential Phases of Every Composite

1. **Matrix (Continuous Phase):** Binds the reinforcement, transfers load to it, protects it from environment, and maintains fiber position. Provides shape and surface finish.
2. **Reinforcement (Dispersed Phase):** The load-bearing skeleton — usually stiffer and stronger than the matrix. Carries most of the applied load.
3. **Interface (Interphase):** The critical region of contact between matrix and reinforcement. Governs the efficiency of load transfer via shear stress.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Design Rule (KTU Expected Answer):**
> *Rule of Mixtures Concept* → The composite property lies between the properties of the constituents, weighted by their **volume fraction**. For a two-phase system, $V_f + V_m = 1$, where $V$ represents the volume fraction.

---

### 1.4 Real-World Importance

Composite classification matters because it **dictates the manufacturing route, application domain, and cost**. For example:
- **PMCs** → Boeing 787 fuselage, tennis rackets (cheap, lightweight, low-temp use)
- **MMCs** → Automotive brake rotors, aerospace structural parts (high-temp, high-strength)
- **CMCs** → Jet engine turbine blades, rocket nozzles (extreme temperature)
- **C/C** → Spacecraft nose cones, Formula-1 brake discs (ultra-high-temp)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Based on Matrix Material

The matrix dictates the **operating temperature range, environmental resistance, manufacturing process, and dominant application**. KTU PEMET413 mandates comprehensive coverage of the following four categories:

#### 2.1.1 Polymer Matrix Composites (PMC)
- **Matrix:** Organic polymer resin (thermoplastic or thermosetting).
- **Reinforcement:** Glass, carbon, aramid (Kevlar) fibers most common.
- **Operating Temperature:** Typically up to **200 °C – 250 °C** (thermoset limit).
- **Density Range:** **1.2 – 2.0 g/cm³** (very lightweight).
- **Advantages:** Low cost, easy fabrication, high strength-to-weight ratio, corrosion resistance.
- **Disadvantages:** Low maximum service temperature, moisture absorption, poor transverse properties.
- **Examples:** Fiberglass (GFRP), Carbon-fiber reinforced polymer (CFRP), Kevlar-epoxy.
- **Applications:** Aircraft interiors, automotive body panels, marine hulls, sporting goods, wind turbine blades.
- **Sub-classification:**
  - *Thermoplastic Matrix Composites* — Nylon, PEEK, PPS reinforced; can be remelted; better impact resistance.
  - *Thermosetting Matrix Composites* — Epoxy, polyester, vinylester, phenolic; irreversible cure; superior creep resistance.

#### 2.1.2 Metal Matrix Composites (MMC)
- **Matrix:** Light metals — Aluminum, Magnesium, Titanium, Copper.
- **Reinforcement:** SiC, Al₂O₃, B₄C, C (graphite), TiB₂.
- **Operating Temperature:** Up to **400 °C – 600 °C**.
- **Density Range:** **2.5 – 4.5 g/cm³** (moderate).
- **Advantages:** High specific stiffness, high temperature capability, good thermal/electrical conductivity, no moisture absorption.
- **Disadvantages:** Higher cost than PMC, complex manufacturing, limited ductility.
- **Examples:** Al-SiC (aluminum reinforced with silicon carbide particles), Al-Al₂O₃, Ti-SiC.
- **Applications:** Automotive drive shafts, brake rotors, piston rings, aerospace structural components, electronic heat sinks.

#### 2.1.3 Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMC)
- **Matrix:** Engineering ceramics — Al₂O₃, SiC, Si₃N₄, ZrO₂.
- **Reinforcement:** SiC fibers, C fibers, Al₂O₃ fibers (often in whisker or continuous form).
- **Operating Temperature:** Up to **1200 °C – 1600 °C** (extreme).
- **Density Range:** **2.5 – 4.0 g/cm³**.
- **Advantages:** Outstanding high-temperature strength, oxidation resistance, hardness, low thermal expansion.
- **Disadvantages:** Brittle, expensive, difficult to fabricate, low fracture toughness (though reinforcement improves this).
- **Examples:** SiC/SiC, C/SiC, Al₂O₃/Al₂O₃.
- **Applications:** Jet engine turbine blades, rocket nozzles, heat shield tiles, cutting tools.

#### 2.1.4 Carbon-Carbon Composites (C/C)
- **Matrix:** Carbon (graphite).
- **Reinforcement:** Carbon fibers (continuous or woven).
- **Operating Temperature:** Up to **2000 °C – 2800 °C** in inert atmosphere.
- **Density Range:** **1.6 – 2.0 g/cm³**.
- **Advantages:** Highest known strength-to-weight at very high temperature, excellent thermal shock resistance, low thermal expansion.
- **Disadvantages:** Extremely expensive, oxidizes above 400 °C in air (requires protective coating).
- **Examples:** Carbon fiber reinforced carbon matrix composites.
- **Applications:** Spacecraft nose cones, Formula-1 brake discs, missile re-entry vehicle leading edges, rocket motor nozzles.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "PMMC" Sequence (low to high temperature capability):**
> **P**MC < **M**MC < **C**MC < **C**/C
> (Polymer → Metal → Ceramic → Carbon/Carbon)

---

### 2.2 Classification Based on Structure / Reinforcement Geometry

The **shape, size, orientation, and distribution** of the reinforcement fundamentally control the **anisotropy, mechanical response, and manufacturing technique**.

#### 2.2.1 Fiber-Reinforced Composites (FRC)
The most important class for structural engineering. Fibers carry the load; matrix transfers it and protects fibers.

- **Continuous Fiber Composites:** Long, unbroken fibers (e.g., carbon tows) provide maximum strength and stiffness along the fiber direction. Highly anisotropic. Examples: Unidirectional CFRP tape, woven fabric laminates.
- **Discontinuous (Short) Fiber Composites:** Chopped fibers (typically 3 mm – 50 mm long) randomly or preferentially oriented. More isotropic, cheaper, easier to mold. Examples: SMC (Sheet Molding Compound), injection-molded glass-nylon.
- **Whisker-Reinforced:** Single-crystal short fibers with near-theoretical strength. Used in MMC and CMC.

**Critical Concept — Critical Fiber Length ($L_c$):**
The minimum fiber length required for the fiber to develop its ultimate strength before the matrix fails. Below $L_c$, the fiber pulls out instead of breaking.

$$L_c = \frac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}$$

where $\sigma_f$ = fiber ultimate strength, $d$ = fiber diameter, $\tau_i$ = interfacial shear strength.

- If $L < L_c$ → fiber is too short; failure by **pull-out**.
- If $L > 15 L_c$ → fiber behaves as continuous; maximum efficiency.

#### 2.2.2 Particulate Composites
- **Reinforcement:** Equiaxed particles (spherical, cubic, platelet) of ceramics, metals, or glass.
- **Particle Size:** Typically 1 µm to 100 µm.
- **Volume Fraction:** Can be very high (up to 0.6 in some MMCs).
- **Effect:** Primarily enhances **stiffness, hardness, wear resistance, and dimensional stability** rather than strength.
- **Examples:** Concrete (gravel in cement), Al-SiC pistons, WC-Co cutting tools (cermets), filled polymers (talc-filled polypropylene).

#### 2.2.3 Laminate Composites
- **Structure:** Two or more distinct layers (plies) of composite materials bonded together.
- **Ply Orientation:** Layers can be stacked at 0°, ±45°, 90° etc. to tailor the **stiffness and strength** in multiple directions.
- **Cross-Ply Laminate:** Plies at 0° and 90° alternately.
- **Angle-Ply Laminate:** Plies at +θ and -θ alternately.
- **Hybrid Laminate:** Different fiber types in different plies (e.g., carbon + glass).
- **Examples:** Plywood, GFRP boat hulls, aircraft wing skins (CFRP laminates).
- **Why Laminate?** Tailorable anisotropic properties, damage tolerance, fail-safe design.

#### 2.2.4 Sandwich Composites
- **Structure:** A low-density **core** (honeycomb, foam, balsa) bonded between two thin, stiff **face sheets** (skins).
- **Face Sheets:** Usually aluminum, CFRP, or GFRP.
- **Core:** Honeycomb (aluminum, Nomex, paper), PVC foam, PU foam, end-grain balsa.
- **Principle:** Carries transverse loads by **skin tension/compression** and **core shear** — gives extremely high bending stiffness at very low weight.
- **Bending stiffness scales as:** $D \propto \dfrac{E_f \cdot t_f \cdot d^2}{2}$, where $d$ is the distance between face sheets — hence the dramatic effect of thick, lightweight cores.
- **Examples:** Aircraft floors, wind turbine blades, racing car monocoque chassis, ski, surfboard.

#### 2.2.5 Filled Composites
- **Structure:** Matrix loaded with fine filler particles (< 10 µm) for cost reduction or property modification.
- **Function:** Reduce cost, modify viscosity, improve surface finish, fire retardancy, or electrical conductivity.
- **Examples:** CaCO₃-filled PVC, carbon-black-filled rubber (tyres), silica-filled epoxy.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following equations are **board-exam mandatory** for composite mechanics problems.

| # | Property | Rule of Mixtures Formula | Symbol Legend | Validity |
|---|----------|--------------------------|---------------|----------|
| 1 | Volume Fraction Constraint | $V_f + V_m = 1$ | $V_f$ = fiber, $V_m$ = matrix | Always |
| 2 | Composite Density | $\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m$ | $\rho$ = density | Exact |
| 3 | Young's Modulus — Voigt (Isostrain) | $E_c = V_f E_f + V_m E_m$ | $E$ = Young's modulus | Upper bound |
| 4 | Young's Modulus — Reuss (Isostress) | $\dfrac{1}{E_c} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | — | Lower bound |
| 5 | Composite Strength (longitudinal) | $\sigma_c = V_f \sigma_f + V_m \sigma_m$ | $\sigma$ = strength | Simplification |
| 6 | Critical Fiber Length | $L_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \, d}{2 \tau_i}$ | $d$ = dia, $\tau_i$ = IFSS | For short fibers |
| 7 | Specific Strength | $\dfrac{\sigma_c}{\rho_c}$ | — | Design metric |
| 8 | Specific Modulus | $\dfrac{E_c}{\rho_c}$ | — | Stiffness/weight |
| 9 | Volume-to-Weight Fraction | $W_f = \dfrac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m}$ | $W$ = weight fraction | Conversion |
| 10 | Sandwich Bending Stiffness | $D = \dfrac{E_f t_f d^2}{2}$ | $t_f$ = face thickness, $d$ = core depth | Sandwich design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** The two bounds (Voigt and Reuss) give the **upper** and **lower** limits of the composite modulus. The actual modulus lies between them. For long fibers aligned with load, the result is close to Voigt; for transverse loading or short fibers, closer to Reuss.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** CFRP (carbon-epoxy) is used in Boeing 787 (50% composite by weight) because the high specific strength of the PMC reduces fuel burn by 20%.
- **Automotive:** Al-SiC MMC brake discs (Porsche, Mercedes) have 3× the wear life of cast iron at half the weight.
- **Energy:** Wind turbine blades (up to 80 m long) are GFRP sandwich structures — the only material system that can deliver the required stiffness-to-weight ratio economically.
- **Space:** C/C nose cones survive 1600 °C re-entry temperatures that would melt any metal — proving the practical value of matrix-based classification.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **exhaustive analytical derivations** of the rule-of-mixtures formulas that form the mathematical backbone of composite classification. KTU board exams frequently expect these derivations, especially the Voigt and Reuss models.

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Composite Density ($\rho_c$)

The **density of a composite is a linear, volume-weighted average** of the densities of its constituents. This is an *exact* formula because density is an *intensive, non-directional* property (no interface or load-transfer effects to complicate it).

**Starting Assumptions:**

Let a composite of total volume $V_c$ be composed of:
- A reinforcement phase of volume $V_f$ and density $\rho_f$
- A matrix phase of volume $V_m$ and density $\rho_m$

By conservation of volume: $V_c = V_f + V_m$

By conservation of mass: $M_c = M_f + M_m$

**Step 1 — Express the Volume Fractions:**

$$V_f = \frac{V_f}{V_c} \quad \text{and} \quad V_m = \frac{V_m}{V_c}$$

such that $V_f + V_m = 1$.

**Step 2 — Write the Mass Equation:**

$$M_c = \rho_c V_c, \quad M_f = \rho_f V_f, \quad M_m = \rho_m V_m$$

Conservation: $\rho_c V_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$.

**Step 3 — Divide by Total Volume $V_c$:**

$$\rho_c = \frac{\rho_f V_f}{V_c} + \frac{\rho_m V_m}{V_c}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Volume Fractions:**

$$\boxed{\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m}$$

This is the **Rule of Mixtures for Density** — an *exact* result, not a bound.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Voigt Model — Longitudinal Modulus (Isostrain, Upper Bound)

**Physical Setup:**
A unidirectional continuous fiber composite loaded **parallel** to the fiber direction. Both fiber and matrix experience the **same strain** $\varepsilon$ (this is why it is called the isostrain model). The fibers and matrix are assumed to act as **parallel "springs"** carrying the load together.

**Visualization:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Load } F \;\rightarrow\;& [\;\text{Fiber} \;\vert\;\vert\; \text{Matrix}\;] \;\rightarrow\; \text{Fixed Wall} \\
&\text{both phases see the same } \varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Strain Compatibility (Isostrain):**

$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon$$

**Step 2 — Force Equilibrium:**
The total force is the sum of forces in each phase (parallel load path):

$$F_c = F_f + F_m$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $F = \sigma A$:**

$$\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$$

**Step 4 — Divide by Total Area $A_c$:**

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f \frac{A_f}{A_c} + \sigma_m \frac{A_m}{A_c}$$

If we assume uniform cross-section, the area fractions equal volume fractions: $A_f/A_c = V_f$ and $A_m/A_c = V_m$.

$$\sigma_c = V_f \sigma_f + V_m \sigma_m$$

**Step 5 — Apply Hooke's Law ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$):**
Since $\varepsilon$ is the same in both phases:

$$E_c \varepsilon = V_f E_f \varepsilon + V_m E_m \varepsilon$$

**Step 6 — Cancel $\varepsilon$ on both sides:**

$$\boxed{E_{c,\parallel} = V_f E_f + V_m E_m} \quad \text{— Voigt / Isostrain / Upper Bound}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Reuss Model — Transverse Modulus (Isostress, Lower Bound)

**Physical Setup:**
The same unidirectional composite is now loaded **perpendicular** to the fibers. Both phases now experience the **same stress** $\sigma$ (isostress). The phases are stacked in **series** along the loading direction.

**Visualization:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Load } F \;\rightarrow\;& \begin{bmatrix} \text{Fiber} \\ \text{Matrix} \end{bmatrix}_{\text{series}} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Fixed Wall} \\
&\text{both phases see the same } \sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Stress Equilibrium (Isostress):**

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f = \sigma_m = \sigma$$

**Step 2 — Strain Compatibility (Series):**
The total displacement of the composite equals the sum of displacements in each phase:

$$u_c = u_f + u_m$$

Since $u = \varepsilon \cdot L$ and lengths are proportional to volume fractions:

$$\varepsilon_c L_c = \varepsilon_f L_f + \varepsilon_m L_m$$

**Step 3 — Divide by $L_c$:**

$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f \frac{L_f}{L_c} + \varepsilon_m \frac{L_m}{L_c} = \varepsilon_f V_f + \varepsilon_m V_m$$

**Step 4 — Apply Hooke's Law ($\varepsilon = \sigma / E$):**
Since $\sigma$ is the same in both phases:

$$\frac{\sigma}{E_c} = V_f \frac{\sigma}{E_f} + V_m \frac{\sigma}{E_m}$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $\sigma$ on both sides:**

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{E_{c,\perp}} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}} \quad \text{— Reuss / Isostress / Lower Bound}$$

**Useful Inverse Form:**

$$E_{c,\perp} = \frac{E_f E_m}{V_f E_m + V_m E_f}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Critical Fiber Length ($L_c$)

This is essential for classifying whether a fiber is "short" or "continuous" and for understanding the load transfer mechanism in discontinuous fiber composites.

**Physical Setup:**
A short fiber of length $L$ and diameter $d$ embedded in a matrix. Load is transferred from the matrix to the fiber via **interfacial shear stress** $\tau_i$ acting on the cylindrical fiber surface.

**Step 1 — Tensile Force Carried by the Fiber:**
The maximum tensile force in the fiber is:

$$F_{\text{fiber}} = \sigma_f \cdot A_f = \sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Load Transferred from the Matrix:**
This force is transferred from the matrix to the fiber through interfacial shear over the fiber's lateral surface $A_{\text{shear}} = \pi d \cdot (L/2)$ — note the factor of $L/2$ because shear builds up from both ends, so the **effective shear transfer length is $L/2$**:

$$F_{\text{shear}} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot \frac{L}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Equate the Two (Force Balance at Fiber Failure):**
At the **critical length**, the fiber develops its full strength just as the interface reaches its shear limit:

$$\sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot \frac{L_c}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the Equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_f \cdot \frac{d^2}{4} &= \tau_i \cdot d \cdot \frac{L_c}{2} \\
\sigma_f \cdot d &= 2 \tau_i L_c \\
L_c &= \frac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{L_c = \frac{\sigma_f \, d}{2 \tau_i}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation (KTU Frequently Asked):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L < L_c &\Rightarrow \text{Fiber pulls out; reinforcement inefficient.} \\
L > 15\,L_c &\Rightarrow \text{Fiber behaves as continuous; maximum reinforcement efficiency.}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.5 Worked Example: Volume-to-Weight Fraction Conversion

**Problem:** A glass/epoxy composite has $V_f = 0.4$, $\rho_f = 2.5\ \text{g/cm}^3$, $\rho_m = 1.2\ \text{g/cm}^3$. Find the weight fraction of the fiber.

**Step 1 — Mass of Each Phase (per unit volume of composite):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_f &= \rho_f \cdot V_f = 2.5 \times 0.4 = 1.0 \ \text{g} \\
m_m &= \rho_m \cdot V_m = 1.2 \times 0.6 = 0.72 \ \text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Mass of Composite:**

$$m_c = m_f + m_m = 1.0 + 0.72 = 1.72 \ \text{g}$$

**Step 3 — Weight Fraction of Fiber:**

$$W_f = \frac{m_f}{m_c} = \frac{1.0}{1.72} = 0.5814 \approx 58.14\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that although the fiber occupies only 40% of the volume, it accounts for ~58% of the weight — because glass is more than twice as dense as epoxy. This distinction is critical in cost estimation and aerospace weight budgeting.

---

### 3.6 Worked Example: Voigt and Reuss Modulus Bounds

**Problem:** A unidirectional glass/epoxy composite has $V_f = 0.5$, $E_f = 72\ \text{GPa}$, $E_m = 3.5\ \text{GPa}$. Compute the upper and lower bounds of the longitudinal and transverse modulus.

**Step 1 — Voigt Bound (Longitudinal):**

$$E_{c,\parallel} = V_f E_f + V_m E_m = (0.5)(72) + (0.5)(3.5) = 36 + 1.75 = 37.75\ \text{GPa}$$

**Step 2 — Reuss Bound (Transverse):**

$$\frac{1}{E_{c,\perp}} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} = \frac{0.5}{72} + \frac{0.5}{3.5} = 0.00694 + 0.14286 = 0.1498\ \text{GPa}^{-1}$$

$$E_{c,\perp} = \frac{1}{0.1498} \approx 6.68\ \text{GPa}$$

**Step 3 — Result Comparison:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{c,\parallel} &= 37.75\ \text{GPa} \quad \text{(stiff — fibers carry load)} \\
E_{c,\perp} &= 6.68\ \text{GPa} \quad \text{(compliant — only matrix resists)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ratio $E_{c,\parallel} / E_{c,\perp} \approx 5.65$ — a clear demonstration of the **anisotropic nature** of unidirectional fiber composites. This is the physical reason why laminates (multi-angle stacking) are used in real structures.

### 4.4 Schematic — Sandwich Composite Cross-Section

A schematic representation of a sandwich composite's three-layer architecture — a popular diagram topic for **7-mark Part B questions**.

```mermaid
graph TB
    U1[TOP FACE SHEET] --> U2[COMPLIANT ADHESIVE LAYER]
    U2 --> U3[CORE HONEYCOMB OR FOAM]
    U3 --> U4[COMPLIANT ADHESIVE LAYER]
    U4 --> U5[BOTTOM FACE SHEET]
    U1 --> U11[Material: CFRP or GFRP or Al]
    U1 --> U12[Role: Carries Tensile Compressive Bending Stress]
    U3 --> U31[Material: Nomex or Aluminum Honeycomb or PVC Foam or Balsa]
    U3 --> U32[Role: Resists Transverse Shear]
    U3 --> U33[Role: Maximizes Distance d Between Skins]
    U5 --> U51[Material: Same as Top Skin]
    U5 --> U52[Role: Counterbalances Bending Moment]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Board Answers:** The bending stiffness of a sandwich panel is governed by the **separation distance $d$** between the two skins (face sheets). Doubling $d$ increases the bending stiffness by a factor of **four** (linear in skin stiffness × $d^2$). This is why sandwich construction is preferred for large, lightweight, stiff structures such as wind turbine blades and aircraft floors.
