## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"Differentiate between Polymer Matrix, Metal Matrix, and Ceramic Matrix Composites with suitable examples."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | PMC | MMC | CMC |
|---------|-----|-----|-----|
| **Matrix Material** | Polymer resin (Epoxy, PEEK) | Light metal (Al, Mg, Ti) | Engineering ceramic (Al₂O₃, SiC) |
| **Reinforcement Examples** | Glass, Carbon, Aramid fibers | SiC, Al₂O₃ particles/whiskers | SiC fibers, C fibers |
| **Operating Temperature** | Up to ~250 °C | Up to ~600 °C | Up to ~1600 °C |
| **Density** | 1.2 – 2.0 g/cm³ (very low) | 2.5 – 4.5 g/cm³ | 2.5 – 4.0 g/cm³ |
| **Key Advantage** | Lightweight, low cost | High specific stiffness | High temperature strength |
| **Key Disadvantage** | Low T, moisture absorption | Complex fabrication, cost | Brittle, expensive |
| **Typical Application** | Aircraft interiors, sports goods | Brake rotors, drive shafts | Turbine blades, nozzles |

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"What is a sandwich composite? Why are sandwich structures used in aircraft flooring?"** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

A **sandwich composite** is a special class of laminate consisting of two thin, stiff, strong **face sheets (skins)** bonded to a thick, lightweight, low-density **core**. The faces are typically CFRP, GFRP, or aluminum sheets; the core is usually a honeycomb (Nomex or aluminum), PVC foam, or end-grain balsa.

**Why used in aircraft flooring:**
1. **Extremely high bending stiffness-to-weight ratio** — the bending stiffness $D \propto E_f t_f d^2$ scales with the square of the distance $d$ between the two face sheets. A thick but light core therefore makes the panel enormously stiff at minimal weight.
2. **Reduced structural mass** — critical for fuel economy; flooring must not add significant weight to a fuselage.
3. **Fire and smoke compliance** — phenolic/Nomex honeycomb cores meet FAR 25.853 fire regulations.
4. **Easy integration of services** — wiring, ducts, and insulation can be routed through the open honeycomb cells.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students must answer **one full question of 14 marks** from a choice of two. The model answers below give the exact depth expected at the board level.

---

#### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Classify composites based on the type of matrix material. Discuss the characteristics, advantages, and applications of each class.** (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Composites are classified into four major categories based on the matrix used:

**(1) Polymer Matrix Composites (PMC)**
- **Matrix:** Thermosets (epoxy, polyester, phenolic) and thermoplastics (PEEK, PPS, nylon).
- **Reinforcement:** Glass, carbon, aramid fibers.
- **Characteristics:** Low density, room-temperature fabrication, good chemical resistance, anisotropic behavior.
- **Advantages:** Low cost, high specific strength, easy processing, corrosion resistance.
- **Disadvantages:** Low maximum service temperature (~250 °C), moisture absorption, poor transverse properties.
- **Applications:** Aircraft secondary structures, wind turbine blades, automotive panels, marine hulls, sports equipment, printed circuit boards.

**(2) Metal Matrix Composites (MMC)**
- **Matrix:** Aluminum, magnesium, titanium, copper.
- **Reinforcement:** SiC, Al₂O₃, B₄C, graphite, TiB₂ (as particles, whiskers, or short fibers).
- **Characteristics:** Operate at higher temperatures than PMCs; ductile metal matrix toughens the brittle ceramic reinforcement.
- **Advantages:** High specific stiffness, high temperature capability, good thermal/electrical conductivity, no moisture absorption, wear resistance.
- **Disadvantages:** Higher cost than PMCs, complex liquid-state or solid-state fabrication, limited ductility.
- **Applications:** Automotive brake rotors and pistons, drive shafts, cylinder liners, aerospace structural parts, electronic heat sinks.

**(3) Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMC)**
- **Matrix:** Al₂O₃, SiC, Si₃N₄, ZrO₂.
- **Reinforcement:** SiC fibers, carbon fibers, Al₂O₃ fibers.
- **Characteristics:** Excellent retention of strength at very high temperatures; the ceramic reinforcement arrests cracks that would otherwise propagate catastrophically.
- **Advantages:** Outstanding high-temperature strength (up to 1600 °C), oxidation resistance, hardness, low thermal expansion, low density.
- **Disadvantages:** Brittle, expensive, difficult to fabricate, requires weak fiber-matrix interface for toughening.
- **Applications:** Jet engine turbine blades and combustor liners, rocket nozzles, heat shield tiles, cutting tool inserts.

**(4) Carbon-Carbon Composites (C/C)**
- **Matrix:** Carbon (graphite).
- **Reinforcement:** Carbon fibers.
- **Characteristics:** Both phases are carbon — the ultimate high-temperature material in inert or vacuum environments.
- **Advantages:** Highest known specific strength at extreme temperatures (up to 2800 °C in inert atmosphere), excellent thermal shock resistance, low coefficient of thermal expansion, lightweight.
- **Disadvantages:** Extremely expensive, oxidizes above ~400 °C in air (requires SiC protective coatings), long fabrication cycle.
- **Applications:** Spacecraft nose cones and leading edges, Formula-1 brake discs, missile re-entry vehicles, rocket motor throats and nozzles.

**Summary Table:**

| Class | Max Temp | Density (g/cm³) | Relative Cost | Signature Application |
|-------|----------|-----------------|---------------|----------------------|
| PMC | 250 °C | 1.2 – 2.0 | Low | Aircraft interiors |
| MMC | 600 °C | 2.5 – 4.5 | Medium | Brake rotors |
| CMC | 1600 °C | 2.5 – 4.0 | High | Turbine blades |
| C/C | 2800 °C (inert) | 1.6 – 2.0 | Very High | Spacecraft |

---

**(b) Explain in detail the classification of composites based on the geometry/structure of the reinforcement.** (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Based on the **shape, size, and orientation** of the reinforcement, composites are classified into five categories:

**(1) Fiber-Reinforced Composites (FRC)**
The fibers carry the principal load while the matrix transfers it via shear at the interface. FRCs are the most important class for structural engineering.
- **Continuous Fiber Composites:** Long, unbroken fibers aligned in one or more directions. Provide maximum strength and stiffness along the fiber axis. Highly anisotropic.
  - *Unidirectional:* All fibers parallel — maximum longitudinal strength.
  - *Woven fabric:* Fibers interlaced in 0° and 90° — balanced properties in-plane.
  - *Braided:* Fibers intertwined diagonally — good for tubular structures.
- **Discontinuous (Short) Fiber Composites:** Chopped fibers (3–50 mm) randomly or preferentially distributed. More isotropic, cheaper, suitable for injection molding and compression molding.
  - **Critical fiber length $L_c$** governs whether the fiber is effective: $L_c = \sigma_f d / (2 \tau_i)$.
  - If $L < L_c$ → fiber pulls out, low efficiency. If $L > 15 L_c$ → fiber behaves as continuous.
- **Whisker-Reinforced Composites:** Single-crystal short fibers with near-theoretical tensile strength. Used in MMC and CMC.

**(2) Particulate Composites**
- Reinforcement consists of **equiaxed particles** (spheres, cubes, platelets) with sizes typically 1–100 µm.
- Enhance **stiffness, hardness, wear resistance, and dimensional stability** rather than strength.
- High volume fractions are possible (up to 60% in MMCs).
- Examples: Concrete (gravel + cement), Al-SiC pistons, WC-Co cemented carbides, rubber filled with carbon black.

**(3) Laminate Composites**
- Stacks of two or more **plies (laminae)** of unidirectional or fabric composites bonded together.
- Plies are oriented at various angles (0°, ±45°, 90°) to tailor stiffness and strength in multiple directions.
- **Cross-ply:** 0°/90° stacking.
- **Angle-ply:** ±θ stacking.
- **Hybrid:** Different fiber types in different plies.
- Why laminate? To obtain **quasi-isotropic or tailored anisotropic** response, improved damage tolerance, and fail-safe behavior.
- Examples: Plywood, aircraft wing skins (CFRP), boat hulls.

**(4) Sandwich Composites**
- Two thin, stiff **face sheets** (skins) bonded to a thick, lightweight **core**.
- Cores: Honeycomb (aluminum, Nomex), PVC foam, PU foam, balsa wood.
- The core separates the skins, maximizing the second moment of area. Bending stiffness scales as $D \propto E_f t_f d^2$.
- Examples: Aircraft floors, wind turbine blades, racing car chassis, skis, satellite structures.

**(5) Filled Composites**
- Matrix loaded with **fine filler particles** (< 10 µm) for cost reduction or property modification.
- Modify viscosity, improve surface finish, add flame retardancy, or impart electrical conductivity.
- Examples: CaCO₃-filled PVC pipes, carbon-black-filled rubber tyres, silica-filled epoxy sealants.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes in Classification Questions**
> 1. **Confusing matrix-based vs. structure-based classification.** Many students list "fiber-reinforced" as a matrix class — it is a *structure* class, and the matrix can be polymer, metal, or ceramic.
> 2. **Forgetting examples.** A classification without a representative example loses at least 1 mark. Always cite at least one example per class.
> 3. **Missing the "interphase"** as the third phase. Examiners appreciate students who mention that the interface/interphase is as important as matrix and reinforcement.
> 4. **Not drawing a labeled diagram.** A neat classification tree or block diagram earns 1–2 easy marks. Always include one.
> 5. **Omitting operating temperature ranges** in matrix-based classification — examiners love to see numerical data.

---

#### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) With neat diagrams, classify fiber-reinforced composites. Explain the role of the interface and derive the critical fiber length equation.** (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Classification of Fiber-Reinforced Composites:**

**Diagram (Schematic):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{FIBER-REINFORCED COMPOSITES} \\
&\quad\Big\downarrow \\
&\;\;\begin{cases}
\textbf{A. Continuous Fiber} \\
\quad\;\;\begin{cases}
\text{i. Unidirectional} \\
\text{ii. Woven fabric} \\
\text{iii. Braided}
\end{cases} \\
\textbf{B. Discontinuous (Short) Fiber} \\
\quad\;\;\begin{cases}
\text{i. Aligned short fibers} \\
\text{ii. Randomly oriented short fibers}
\end{cases} \\
\textbf{C. Whisker-Reinforced}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Continuous Fiber Composites:**
- Fibers run uninterrupted through the matrix, often several centimeters or meters long.
- Load is transferred via shear at the interface, building up from the fiber ends.
- **Unidirectional:** All fibers parallel — maximum longitudinal stiffness and strength.
- **Woven fabric:** 0°/90° weave — balanced in-plane properties, easier handling.
- **Braided:** Helical fiber interlacing — excellent for tubular and contoured parts.

**Discontinuous (Short) Fiber Composites:**
- Fiber length typically 3 mm – 50 mm.
- Easier to process by injection molding, compression molding, extrusion.
- Properties depend strongly on:
  - **Fiber length** (relative to $L_c$)
  - **Orientation distribution** (random vs. aligned)
  - **Fiber-matrix adhesion**

**Whisker-Reinforced Composites:**
- Single-crystal short fibers with diameters 1–10 µm and aspect ratios 50–10 000.
- Near-theoretical tensile strength (no internal flaws).
- Used in MMCs and CMCs (e.g., SiC-whisker-reinforced alumina cutting tools).

**Role of the Interface:**
The interface is the **region of contact** between matrix and reinforcement. Its primary functions are:
1. **Load transfer:** Shear stresses at the interface transfer applied load from the matrix (which is weaker) to the reinforcement (which is stronger). Without good adhesion, the reinforcement is useless.
2. **Crack deflection:** A weak interface deflects propagating cracks, increasing fracture toughness.
3. **Environmental barrier:** Protects fibers from moisture, oxidation, and chemical attack.
4. **Thermal mismatch accommodation:** Relieves thermal stresses arising from differential expansion.

**Derivation of Critical Fiber Length ($L_c$):**

[Same as Section 3.4 above — repeated here for completeness]

**Step 1:** Maximum tensile force in a fiber at fracture:
$$F_{\text{fiber}} = \sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

**Step 2:** Maximum load transferable through interfacial shear (assuming shear builds from both ends, effective length $L/2$):
$$F_{\text{shear}} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot \frac{L}{2}$$

**Step 3:** At critical length, equate the two forces:
$$\sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot \frac{L_c}{2}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify:
$$\boxed{L_c = \frac{\sigma_f \, d}{2 \tau_i}}$$

**Engineering rule:** For $L > 15 L_c$, the fiber behaves as continuous and develops its full strength.

---

**(b) Define a laminate composite. Explain the difference between cross-ply and angle-ply laminates with diagrams. Give two examples of laminate composites in real engineering.** (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
A **laminate composite** is a multi-layered material formed by bonding two or more **plies (laminae)** of unidirectional or fabric-reinforced composite material, often at different fiber orientations, to produce a tailored structural response. Each ply retains its individual identity and orientation.

**Notation:** A laminate with $n$ plies is denoted by its stacking sequence, e.g., $[0°/90°/0°/90°]$ or $[+45°/-45°/0°/-45°/+45°]$.

**Cross-Ply Laminates:**

In a cross-ply laminate, plies are oriented only at **0° and 90°** (orthogonal to each other).

```
PLY 1 : = = = = = = = = = =  (0°)
PLY 2 : | | | | | | | | | |  (90°)
PLY 3 : = = = = = = = = = =  (0°)
PLY 4 : | | | | | | | | | |  (90°)
```

- **Properties:** Balanced and symmetric (in symmetric cross-ply) — no coupling between bending and twisting.
- **Strengths:** Good in-plane stiffness along 0° and 90° directions.
- **Weakness:** Low shear stiffness; not optimal for off-axis loading.
- **Examples:** Most general-purpose GFRP panels, printed circuit board substrates.

**Angle-Ply Laminates:**

In an angle-ply laminate, plies are oriented at equal and opposite angles **+θ and -θ** (typically ±45°).

```
PLY 1 : ////  (+45°)
PLY 2 : \\\\  (-45°)
PLY 3 : ////  (+45°)
PLY 4 : \\\\  (-45°)
```

- **Properties:** Excellent **shear stiffness and strength** — the ±45° plies directly resist in-plane shear.
- **Weakness:** Lower axial (0°) stiffness than unidirectional or cross-ply.
- **Examples:** Helicopter rotor blades, aircraft wing skins (with additional 0° plies).

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Cross-Ply (0°/90°) | Angle-Ply (±θ) |
|---------|---------------------|-----------------|
| Fiber orientations | 0° and 90° | +θ and -θ (typically ±45°) |
| Axial stiffness (0°) | High | Lower |
| Transverse stiffness (90°) | High | Lower |
| Shear stiffness | Moderate | **High** |
| Coupling effects | Minimal | Possible bend-twist coupling |
| Typical use | Boat hulls, general panels | Rotor blades, wing skins |

**Real-Engineering Examples of Laminate Composites:**

1. **Aircraft Wing Skin (Boeing 787):** A laminate composite of CFRP plies stacked as $[+45°/-45°/0°/-45°/+45°/90°/...]_s$ (quasi-isotropic) — gives nearly equal stiffness in all in-plane directions while keeping weight minimal. This is the largest single composite application in civil aviation.

2. **Marine Boat Hulls (Yachts, Naval Crafts):** GFRP cross-ply or quasi-isotropic laminates with a vinylester skin coat and a PVC foam or end-grain balsa core sandwich — provide excellent corrosion resistance in seawater, high impact tolerance, and low maintenance.

3. **Helicopter Rotor Blades (Eurocopter, Boeing AH-64):** Multi-directional CFRP angle-ply laminates with a honeycomb core, optimized for combined flapping, feathering, and centrifugal loads.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Laminate Questions**
> 1. **Do not confuse ply and layer.** A "ply" is a single lamina; a "laminate" is the entire stack.
> 2. **Forgetting the "s" (symmetric) subscript** — the answer loses marks if the student does not mention that balanced/symmetric lay-ups are used to avoid bend-twist coupling.
> 3. **Mixing up cross-ply and angle-ply.** A common error: stating that a $[±45°]$ laminate is "cross-ply." Cross-ply is **strictly 0°/90°**.
> 4. **Omitting the symmetric rule** — to avoid out-of-plane warping under thermal/mechanical loads, laminates are typically symmetric about the mid-plane.
> 5. **Not citing engineering examples** — examiners reward real-world awareness (e.g., Boeing 787, helicopter blades).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

Use this checklist as your **final-day revision summary** before the KTU exam.

#### ✅ A. Classification by Matrix — Must-Know Points
- [ ] **Four matrix classes** in increasing temperature capability: **PMC < MMC < CMC < C/C**.
- [ ] PMC uses **polymer resin**; reinforcement is usually glass, carbon, or aramid fiber. Max T ≈ **250 °C**.
- [ ] MMC uses **light metals** (Al, Mg, Ti); reinforcement is SiC, Al₂O₃, B₄C. Max T ≈ **600 °C**.
- [ ] CMC uses **ceramics** (Al₂O₃, SiC); reinforcement is ceramic fibers. Max T ≈ **1600 °C**.
- [ ] C/C uses **carbon matrix + carbon fiber**; max T ≈ **2800 °C in inert atmosphere**; oxidizes in air above 400 °C.
- [ ] **Mnemonic for temperature:** *P → M → C → C* (Polymer → Metal → Ceramic → Carbon).

#### ✅ B. Classification by Structure — Must-Know Points
- [ ] **Fiber-reinforced** is the most important structural class — sub-divided into **continuous, discontinuous (short), and whisker**.
- [ ] **Critical fiber length:** $L_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f d}{2 \tau_i}$; rule of thumb $L > 15 L_c$ → continuous behavior.
- [ ] **Particulate composites** enhance stiffness/hardness, not strength; examples: concrete, Al-SiC, cermets.
- [ ] **Laminates** stack plies at varied angles for tailored in-plane response; **cross-ply = 0°/90°**, **angle-ply = ±θ**.
- [ ] **Sandwich composites** use thin stiff skins + thick lightweight core; bending stiffness $\propto d^2$.
- [ ] **Filled composites** are matrix + fine filler for cost reduction or property modification.

#### ✅ C. Rule of Mixtures — Board-Exam Critical
- [ ] Density: $\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m$ — **exact**.
- [ ] Voigt (longitudinal/isostrain): $E_{c,\parallel} = V_f E_f + V_m E_m$ — **upper bound**.
- [ ] Reuss (transverse/isostress): $\dfrac{1}{E_{c,\perp}} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ — **lower bound**.
- [ ] Volume fraction constraint: $V_f + V_m = 1$.
- [ ] Weight fraction conversion: $W_f = \dfrac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m}$.

#### ✅ D. Engineering Application Examples (High-Value for 1-mark Add-ons)
- [ ] Boeing 787 → **CFRP** (PMC) — 50% of airframe weight.
- [ ] Wind turbine blades → **GFRP sandwich**.
- [ ] Aircraft brake discs → **C/C composite**.
- [ ] Helicopter rotor blades → **CFRP angle-ply laminate**.
- [ ] Cutting tools → **WC-Co cermet** (particulate composite).
- [ ] Aircraft flooring → **Honeycomb sandwich** (CFRP skins + Nomex core).
- [ ] Concrete = classic **particulate composite** (gravel + cement).
- [ ] Plywood = classic **laminate composite**.

#### ✅ E. Top 3 Common Mistakes to Avoid
1. Confusing **matrix-based** and **structure-based** classifications.
2. Forgetting to state the **interface role** — examiners love this point.
3. Not drawing a **labeled diagram** — it is worth 1–2 free marks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam-Day Tip:** Whenever asked to "classify composites," **always start with a clean block diagram or classification tree** showing both matrix and structure axes, then describe each class with at least one example and one application. This structure alone guarantees 80% of the marks for a classification question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smart Composites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET413 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Smart Composites

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

A **Smart Composite** (also called an *Intelligent Composite* or *Adaptive Composite*) is a hybrid, multi-phase engineered material formed by the deliberate integration of **smart materials** (sensor/actuator phases) and **structural constituents** (reinforcement and matrix phases) such that the resulting composite possesses the inherent ability to **sense** its external stimuli, **diagnose** its internal state, and **actuate** a controlled response, mimicking biological nervous–muscular feedback systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Strict):** A *smart composite* is NOT merely a composite that contains a smart material. It must exhibit a **closed-loop sensory–actuation response** within a single structural entity, achieving multifunctionality (load-bearing + non-structural intelligence) simultaneously.

### Classification of Smart Composites

Smart composites are classified by the **degree of intelligence** they exhibit:

| Class | Behavior | Example System |
|---|---|---|
| **Passive Smart Composite** | Responds *externally only* to a stimulus (no internal feedback) | Optical-fiber strain sensor embedded in a CFRP laminate |
| **Active Smart Composite** | Has an inherent *sensing + actuation + control* loop | SMA-actuated morphing wing skin |
| **Adaptive (Cognitive) Smart Composite** | Self-modulates response using embedded processors / neural networks | Self-healing polymer composite with embedded microcapsules |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Living Skin"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine the human skin. It is *not* a single material — it is a layered **composite** of epidermis, dermis, and hypodermis. Embedded within it are **sensors** (nerve endings for touch, heat, pain) and **actuators** (muscle fibres) that contract in response. A *Smart Composite* works on the exact same architectural principle:
> - **Matrix (dermis)** → structural backbone.
> - **Reinforcement (collagen fibres)** → mechanical strength.
> - **Sensors (nerve endings)** → piezoelectric / optical fibres that "feel" strain/damage.
> - **Actuators (muscle fibres)** → SMA / piezoceramic patches that "react" by changing shape.
> - **Control (spinal cord / brain)** → embedded microcontroller that closes the feedback loop.

## 1.3 Key Functional Phases in a Smart Composite

1. **Matrix phase** — Polymer, metal, or ceramic base.
2. **Reinforcement phase** — Fibres / particles providing mechanical strength.
3. **Sensory phase** — Converts mechanical/thermal/chemical signals into electrical/optical outputs.
4. **Actuation phase** — Converts electrical/thermal/magnetic inputs into mechanical displacement.
5. **Control / Processor phase** — Microelectronic chip / FPGA / neural controller.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** A *piezoelectric composite* (e.g., PZT fibres in epoxy) is an **active** smart composite if the SAME PZT is used for both sensing AND actuation via the *converse* and *direct* piezoelectric effects, respectively. If the PZT only senses, it is *passive*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles — The Three Functional Loops

A smart composite operates via one or more of these coupled physical effects:

- **(A) Direct Sensing Loop:** External stimulus → *Sensory phase* (e.g., piezoelectric direct effect) → Electrical signal → *Processor* → Decision.
- **(B) Actuation Loop:** *Processor command* → *Actuation phase* (e.g., SMA heating, converse piezoelectric) → Mechanical strain → Structural shape change.
- **(C) Self-Healing Loop:** Damage event → *Embedded healing agent* (microcapsule / vascular network) → Polymerisation → Crack closure → Property recovery.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **must-know** for the KTU 2024 ESE. Note: $\vert$ is used for absolute value to prevent table syntax breakage.

### 2.2.1 Piezoelectric Constitutive Equations

| Equation No. | Constitutive Relation | Physical Meaning | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Eq. 1** | $D_i = d_{ik}\,\sigma_k + \varepsilon^{T}_{ij}\,E_j$ | Direct effect: stress $\sigma$ produces electric displacement $D$ | $D$: C/m² , $\sigma$: Pa , $E$: V/m |
| **Eq. 2** | $\varepsilon_{ij} = s^{E}_{ijkl}\,\sigma_{kl} + d_{kij}\,E_k$ | Converse effect: electric field $E$ produces strain $\varepsilon$ | $\varepsilon$: dimensionless |
| **Eq. 3** | $k = d_{33}\,\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon^{T}_{33}\,s^{E}_{33}}}$ | Electromechanical coupling coefficient $k$ | dimensionless, $0 \le k \le 1$ |

Where:
- $d_{ik}$ = **piezoelectric strain coefficient** (m/V or C/N).
- $\varepsilon^{T}_{ij}$ = **permittivity at constant stress** (F/m).
- $s^{E}_{ijkl}$ = **elastic compliance at constant electric field** (1/Pa).
- $E$ = applied electric field (V/m).

### 2.2.2 Shape Memory Alloy (SMA) Thermomechanics

| Equation | Relation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Eq. 4** | $\xi = \dfrac{T - M_s}{M_f - M_s}$ | Martensite fraction during *cooling* ($M_s \rightarrow M_f$) |
| **Eq. 5** | $\varepsilon_{recoverable} = \xi \cdot \varepsilon^{TW}_{\max}$ | Recoverable strain via transformation |
| **Eq. 6** | $H = \dfrac{\sigma_{crit}}{\xi}$ | Heater power to actuate phase change |

where $\varepsilon^{TW}_{\max}$ for NiTi $\approx$ **8 %** and for CuZnAl $\approx$ **4 %**.

### 2.2.3 Rule of Mixtures for Smart Hybrid Composites

For a smart composite with structural fibre (volume fraction $V_f$) and smart phase (volume fraction $V_s$), with $V_f + V_s + V_m = 1$:

$$E_c = E_f\,V_f + E_s\,V_s + E_m\,(1 - V_f - V_s)$$

$$\rho_c = \rho_f\,V_f + \rho_s\,V_s + \rho_m\,(1 - V_f - V_s)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used in **aerospace morphing-wing design** (NASA / DRDO programs), **civil structural health monitoring (SHM)** of bridges, **automotive crash sensors**, and **biomedical prosthetics** with embedded sensory feedback.

## 2.3 Hierarchical Working Logic — Why & How

- **Why embed a sensor?** To detect *in-situ* delamination, fibre breakage, or impact damage that is invisible to the naked eye.
- **How?** Piezoelectric fibres (e.g., PZT-5H) generate a measurable voltage when strained; optical fibres (FBG gratings) shift their Bragg wavelength $\lambda_B = 2\,n_{eff}\,\Lambda$ under strain.
- **Why an actuator?** To enable *morphing* — wings that change camber, satellite booms that unfurl, helicopter blades that suppress vibration.
- **How?** SMAs undergo martensite-to-austenite phase transition upon heating past the austenite start temperature $A_s$, recovering pre-deformed shape.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Piezoelectric Constitutive Equations from First Principles

### 3.1.1 Direct Piezoelectric Effect Derivation

The **direct piezoelectric effect** couples mechanical stress $\sigma$ to electric displacement $D$. We start from a Taylor expansion of the electric displacement $D_i$ about its equilibrium value:

$$D_i = D_i^{0} + \left(\dfrac{\partial D_i}{\partial \sigma_k}\right)_{E} \sigma_k + \left(\dfrac{\partial D_i}{\partial E_j}\right)_{\sigma} E_j + \ldots$$

Assuming the material is initially unpolarised ($D_i^{0} = 0$) and truncating at first order:

$$D_i = d_{ik}\,\sigma_k + \varepsilon^{T}_{ij}\,E_j$$

where $d_{ik} = \left(\dfrac{\partial D_i}{\partial \sigma_k}\right)_E$ is the **third-rank piezoelectric strain coefficient tensor** (units: C/N) and $\varepsilon^{T}_{ij} = \left(\dfrac{\partial D_i}{\partial E_j}\right)_{\sigma}$ is the **dielectric permittivity tensor at constant stress** (units: F/m).

> **Step-by-step conversion logic:**
> - Line 1: Thermodynamic Taylor expansion in two intensive variables ($\sigma$ and $E$).
> - Line 2: Linearisation is valid because piezoelectric coefficients are nearly constant for small signal operation — this is the **small-signal regime** assumption used in KTU board problems.

### 3.1.2 Converse Piezoelectric Effect Derivation

By the principle of **thermodynamic reciprocity** (Maxwell relation applied to the Gibbs free energy $G$), the cross-coupling coefficients must be equal:

$$\left(\dfrac{\partial D_i}{\partial \sigma_k}\right)_E = \left(\dfrac{\partial \varepsilon_k}{\partial E_i}\right)_{\sigma} = d_{ik}$$

This is the rigorous origin of the symmetry used in Eq. 2. Applying the same Taylor expansion to mechanical strain $\varepsilon_{ij}$:

$$\varepsilon_{ij} = s^{E}_{ijkl}\,\sigma_{kl} + d_{kij}\,E_k$$

### 3.1.3 Derivation of Electromechanical Coupling Coefficient $k$

The energy conversion efficiency between electrical and mechanical forms is captured by $k$. Consider a piezoelectric rod under uniaxial stress $\sigma_3$ and field $E_3$:

$$D_3 = d_{33}\,\sigma_3 + \varepsilon^{T}_{33}\,E_3$$
$$\varepsilon_3 = s^{E}_{33}\,\sigma_3 + d_{33}\,E_3$$

The stored mechanical energy per unit volume is:

$$U_{mech} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\sigma_3 \cdot \varepsilon_3 = \dfrac{1}{2}\,s^{E}_{33}\,\sigma_3^{2} + \dfrac{1}{2}\,d_{33}\,\sigma_3\,E_3$$

The stored electrical energy per unit volume is:

$$U_{elec} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,E_3 \cdot D_3 = \dfrac{1}{2}\,d_{33}\,\sigma_3\,E_3 + \dfrac{1}{2}\,\varepsilon^{T}_{33}\,E_3^{2}$$

The **mutual (coupled) energy** term in both expressions is $\dfrac{1}{2}\,d_{33}\,\sigma_3\,E_3$. The dimensionless coupling factor is defined as the ratio of the geometric mean of stored forms to the product of self-energies:

$$k^2 = \dfrac{U_{mutual}^{2}}{U_{mech,\,self} \cdot U_{elec,\,self}} = \dfrac{d_{33}^{2}}{\varepsilon^{T}_{33}\,s^{E}_{33}}$$

Taking the square root gives Eq. 3 stated earlier.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shape Memory Strain Recovery

Consider a NiTi wire of original length $L_0$ deformed to length $L_1$ in the martensitic phase (low temperature). The total plastic strain is:

$$\varepsilon_{plastic} = \dfrac{L_1 - L_0}{L_0}$$

The martensite fraction $\xi$ evolves linearly with temperature $T$ during heating from austenite-start $A_s$ to austenite-finish $A_f$:

$$\xi(T) = \begin{cases} 0, & T \le A_s \\ \dfrac{T - A_s}{A_f - A_s}, & A_s \le T \le A_f \\ 1, & T \ge A_f \end{cases}$$

The recoverable strain at any intermediate temperature is therefore:

$$\varepsilon_{recovered}(T) = \xi(T)\cdot \varepsilon_{plastic}$$

And at full austenitisation ($T \ge A_f$):

$$\varepsilon_{recovered}^{max} = \varepsilon_{plastic} = \varepsilon^{TW}_{max}$$

For **NiTi** (Nitinol): $\varepsilon^{TW}_{max} \approx 0.08$ (i.e., **8 %**). For **Cu-Al-Zn**: $\varepsilon^{TW}_{max} \approx 0.04$ (i.e., **4 %**). This enormous recoverable strain is what makes SMAs uniquely suited as *embedded actuators* in smart composites.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Smart Phases

| Smart Phase | Stimulus | Response | Magnitude | Response Time |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| PZT (piezoelectric) | Stress / $E$-field | Voltage / strain | $\varepsilon \sim 0.1\,\%$ | $\mu$s (microsecond) |
| PVDF (polymer piezo) | Stress / $E$-field | Voltage / strain | $\varepsilon \sim 0.1\,\%$ | $\mu$s |
| NiTi (SMA) | Temperature | Strain recovery up to **8 %** | large displacement | s (second) |
| ER / MR fluid | $E$ / $B$ field | Viscosity change (kPa) | tunable yield stress | ms (millisecond) |
| Optical fibre (FBG) | Strain / temperature | Wavelength shift $\Delta\lambda_B$ | pm resolution | $\mu$s |
| Magnetostrictive (Terfenol-D) | Magnetic field | Strain $\sim 0.2\,\%$ | large force | $\mu$s |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Smart Composite System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ExtStim["External Stimulus Layer"]
        S1["Mechanical Load sigma"]
        S2["Temperature T"]
        S3["Electric Field E"]
        S4["Magnetic Field B"]
    end

    subgraph SensPhase["Sensory Phase"]
        PZT["Piezoelectric Fibre PZT 5H"]
        FBG["Fibre Bragg Grating FBG"]
        SMA1["SMA Wire Resistance"]
    end

    subgraph CtrlPhase["Control Processor Layer"]
        ADC["Analog to Digital Converter"]
        MCU["Embedded Microcontroller MCU"]
        DAC["Digital to Analog Converter"]
    end

    subgraph ActPhase["Actuation Phase"]
        PZTa["Piezoelectric Patch Converse"]
        SMAa["SMA Heater Joule Circuit"]
        MRa["Magnetorheological Damper"]
    end

    subgraph StructPhase["Structural Composite Phase"]
        CFRP["CFRP Laminate Matrix"]
        GFRP["GFRP Sandwich Core"]
    end

    S1 --> PZT
    S1 --> FBG
    S2 --> SMA1
    S3 --> PZT
    S4 --> MRa

    PZT --> ADC
    FBG --> ADC
    SMA1 --> ADC

    ADC --> MCU
    MCU --> DAC
    DAC --> PZTa
    DAC --> SMAa
    DAC --> MRa

    PZTa --> CFRP
    SMAa --> CFRP
    MRa --> GFRP
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Classification Tree — Types of Smart Composites

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SC["Smart Composites"]

    SC --> PSC["Passive Smart Composites"]
    SC --> ASC["Active Smart Composites"]
    SC --> COG["Cognitive Adaptive Composites"]

    PSC --> PSC1["FBG Strain Sensor in CFRP"]
    PSC --> PSC2["Piezoelectric Sensor Layer Only"]
    PSC --> PSC3["Thermal Chromic Coating Composite"]

    ASC --> ASC1["SMA Morphing Wing Composite"]
    ASC --> ASC2["Piezoelectric Sensing plus Actuation Composite"]
    ASC --> ASC3["Magnetorheological Fluid Damper Composite"]

    COG --> COG1["Self Healing Microcapsule Composite"]
    COG --> COG2["Neural Network Controlled Morphing Skin"]
    COG --> COG3["Damage Tolerant Self Diagnosing Composite"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Process Flow — One-Way Shape Memory Effect Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Step 1 Austenite High T Parent Phase"]
    B["Step 2 Cool to Martensite Mf Low T Twinned"]
    C["Step 3 Apply Load Detwinning Induced Strain"]
    D["Step 4 Release Load Residual Strain Remains"]
    E["Step 5 Heat Above As Austenite Recovers Shape"]
    F["Step 6 Original Shape Restored"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F -.->|"Cool again"| B
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q:** Define *smart composite*. Differentiate between **active** and **passive** smart composites with one example each.

**Model Answer:**

A **smart composite** is a multi-phase material that integrates structural constituents (fibre, matrix) with at least one smart phase (sensor or actuator) such that the composite can sense external stimuli and/or actuate a response, while simultaneously carrying mechanical load.

| Feature | Passive Smart Composite | Active Smart Composite |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Responds *passively* (no control loop) | Has *closed-loop* sensing + actuation |
| Components | Only sensors embedded | Both sensors AND actuators present |
| External control required | Yes (signal processed externally) | No (autonomous, in-built control) |
| Example | Optical FBG sensor embedded in CFRP | SMA-actuated morphing aircraft wing |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write *"smart composite = composite with piezoelectric material"*. This is **wrong** unless the piezoelectric phase performs BOTH sensing AND actuation (active) — otherwise it is merely a *piezo-composite*, not a *smart* composite in the strict KTU sense.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q:** State the **direct** and **converse** piezoelectric effects. Write the corresponding constitutive equations.

**Model Answer:**

- **Direct piezoelectric effect:** Production of an electric charge (or voltage) on the surfaces of certain non-centrosymmetric crystals when subjected to mechanical stress.
  $$D_i = d_{ik}\,\sigma_k + \varepsilon^{T}_{ij}\,E_j$$
- **Converse (inverse) piezoelectric effect:** Production of mechanical strain in such crystals when subjected to an external electric field.
  $$\varepsilon_{ij} = s^{E}_{ijkl}\,\sigma_{kl} + d_{kij}\,E_k$$

The tensors $d_{ik}$ are thermodynamically identical in both effects (Maxwell reciprocity).

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module 1, 14-Mark Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working of the **one-way shape memory effect** in a NiTi alloy. Discuss why this property is exploited in smart composite actuators. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A unidirectional smart composite contains **60 %** volume fraction of carbon fibre ($E_f = 230$ GPa) and **10 %** volume fraction of embedded PZT-5H piezo-ceramic fibres ($E_s = 60$ GPa) in an epoxy matrix ($E_m = 3.5$ GPa). Calculate the **longitudinal Young's modulus** of the composite using the rule of mixtures. Comment on the dominant load-bearing phase. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

The **one-way shape memory effect (OWSME)** in NiTi is a diffusionless, solid-state, thermoelastic martensitic transformation. The cycle is as follows:

1. **High-temperature parent phase (Austenite, B2 cubic):** Above the austenite finish temperature $A_f$, the alloy exists as austenite, with the original parent shape $L_0$.
2. **Cooling to martensite (M_f):** On cooling below the martensite finish temperature $M_f$, the crystal structure transforms to monoclinic martensite (B19′). In this state, the material is soft and can be easily deformed by twinning without dislocation movement.
3. **Loading in martensitic state:** An external load reorients the martensite variants (detwinning), producing a large *apparent plastic strain* of up to **8 %** in NiTi, leaving the wire at length $L_1$.
4. **Unloading:** The load is removed; the detwinned martensite is stable, so the strain *appears* to be permanent (length remains $L_1$).
5. **Heating above A_s:** On heating past the austenite start temperature $A_s$, the reverse transformation begins. The crystal reverts to the parent austenite phase, and the material *recovers its original shape*, returning to length $L_0$.
6. **Cooling again:** On cooling, the austenite transforms back to twinned martensite but **without** reverting to the deformed state. Hence the effect is "one-way" — only heating produces shape recovery; cooling does not spontaneously redeform the part.

> **Why exploited in smart composites:**
> - Massive recoverable strain (8 % NiTi) is **40–80 times** larger than piezoelectric strain (0.1 %).
> - Enables *morphing* structures (aircraft wings, satellite antennas, stent deployment).
> - Embedding SMA wires in a CFRP matrix provides a distributed actuator with no moving parts.
> - Activation by **Joule heating** (resistive, ~1–10 A current) makes it electrically addressable.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Given:** $V_f = 0.60$ (carbon fibre), $V_s = 0.10$ (PZT-5H), $V_m = 1 - 0.60 - 0.10 = 0.30$ (epoxy).

**Young's moduli:** $E_f = 230$ GPa, $E_s = 60$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa.

Applying the **rule of mixtures** for longitudinal (isostrain) loading:

$$E_c = E_f\,V_f + E_s\,V_s + E_m\,V_m$$

Substituting values:

$$E_c = (230)(0.60) + (60)(0.10) + (3.5)(0.30)$$

$$E_c = 138 + 6 + 1.05$$

$$\boxed{E_c = 145.05 \text{ GPa}}$$

> **Comment on dominant phase:** The carbon fibre ($V_f = 0.60$ and $E_f = 230$ GPa) contributes **95.1 %** of the total stiffness, while PZT contributes only 4.1 % and the matrix 0.7 %. Hence carbon fibre is the **dominant load-bearing phase**, and the PZT-5H phase acts primarily as the *sensing/actuation* element with negligible structural penalty. This demonstrates the design philosophy of smart composites: **structural integrity is preserved while intelligence is added** as a low-volume-fraction functional phase.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** Students frequently forget to *recompute the matrix volume fraction* as $V_m = 1 - V_f - V_s$ when THREE phases are present. Using $V_m = 1 - V_f$ is a common error that costs **2 marks** instantly. Also, units of GPa must be consistent throughout — mixing GPa and MPa is another **1-mark deduction**.

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **(a)** Explain the phenomenon of **piezoelectricity** in smart composites. Derive the constitutive equations for the direct and converse effects. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the classification of **smart composites** with suitable examples. Compare the response magnitudes and response times of piezoelectric, SMA, and magnetorheological smart phases. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

*(Piezoelectricity is a linear electromechanical coupling exhibited by non-centrosymmetric crystals such as quartz, PZT, PVDF, and BaTiO₃. The "piezo" effect arises because the centres of positive and negative charge in the unit cell do not coincide under stress, producing a net dipole moment per unit volume = electric displacement $D$. Conversely, applying an electric field distorts the unit cell, producing mechanical strain $\varepsilon$.)*

The **constitutive equations** are derived from a first-order Taylor expansion of the thermodynamic potential $D$ and $\varepsilon$ in intensive variables $\sigma$ and $E$ (shown in full in Section 3.1 of these notes):

$$D_i = d_{ik}\,\sigma_k + \varepsilon^{T}_{ij}\,E_j \quad \text{(Direct effect)}$$

$$\varepsilon_{ij} = s^{E}_{ijkl}\,\sigma_{kl} + d_{kij}\,E_k \quad \text{(Converse effect)}$$

**Tensor symmetry** (Maxwell relation on Gibbs free energy $G$):

$$\left(\dfrac{\partial D_i}{\partial \sigma_k}\right)_E = \left(\dfrac{\partial \varepsilon_k}{\partial E_i}\right)_{\sigma} = d_{ik}$$

This proves the same $d_{ik}$ tensor governs both effects, justifying the dual use of PZT in sensing AND actuation in a *single* active smart composite.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

*(Full classification tree and the analytical matrix are presented in Section 4.2 and Section 3.3 of these notes.)*

The comparison table below is the *key expected answer*:

| Smart Phase | Operating Principle | Strain/Response Magnitude | Response Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Piezoelectric (PZT-5H)** | Direct + converse piezoelectric effect | $\varepsilon \sim 0.1\,\%$, $d_{33} = 593$ pC/N | $\mu$s (very fast) |
| **Shape Memory Alloy (NiTi)** | Thermoelastic martensitic transformation | $\varepsilon \sim 8\,\%$ | seconds (slow, due to heating) |
| **Magnetorheological (MR) fluid** | Field-induced particle chain formation | Tunable yield stress up to 100 kPa | ms (moderate) |

> **Key insight for the examiner:** SMAs provide the **largest displacement** but the **slowest response**; piezoelectrics provide the **fastest** but **smallest** displacement; MR fluids provide **variable damping** for vibration control. An *optimal* smart composite often **combines all three** to cover complementary operating regimes.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Smart composite = structural composite + sensory + actuation + control** in one entity.
- Three classes: **Passive** (sensor only), **Active** (sensor + actuator), **Cognitive/Adaptive** (self-healing, neural control).
- **Piezoelectricity** is governed by two coupled linear constitutive equations with a *symmetric* coupling tensor $d_{ik}$ (Maxwell reciprocity).
- **PZT** is the most common ceramic piezoelectric; **PVDF** is the most common polymeric piezoelectric (flexible, suited for curved composite skins).
- **SMA (NiTi)** offers the **highest recoverable strain** among all smart phases (~8 %), but is **slow** due to thermal actuation.
- The **one-way SME** requires *external* cooling + reloading to redeform; the **two-way SME** (trained alloys) spontaneously cycles but with reduced strain (~2–4 %).
- For **rule of mixtures** in a THREE-phase smart composite: $V_f + V_s + V_m = 1$; always recompute $V_m$ last.
- **FBG (Fibre Bragg Grating)** sensors shift Bragg wavelength $\lambda_B = 2\,n_{eff}\,\Lambda$ under strain — the standard for distributed SHM.
- **Self-healing** composites use **microcapsules** (White et al., 2001) or **vascular networks**; healing efficiency can exceed 75 % in some systems.
- **Curie temperature** $T_c$ is the upper limit of piezoelectricity — above it the material loses its spontaneous polarisation.
- KTU board **favourite topics**: piezo constitutive equations, OWSME cycle diagram, rule-of-mixtures three-phase numerical.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industrial Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET413 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Industrial Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Applications of Composites** refer to the engineering deployment of fiber-reinforced polymers (FRPs), metal matrix composites (MMCs), ceramic matrix composites (CMCs), and hybrid composite systems across diverse industrial sectors — **aerospace, automotive, marine, civil infrastructure, sports, biomedical, defense, energy, and electrical** — where conventional monolithic materials (metals, alloys, neat polymers, or ceramics) fail to deliver the required combination of **specific strength, specific stiffness, fatigue resistance, corrosion resistance, tailorable anisotropy, and weight efficiency**.

The scope of this topic, as prescribed under **Module 1** of **PEMET413**, demands the student recognize the *material–manufacturing–application* trinity. The industrial applicability of a composite is governed by three key performance metrics standardized in KTU board examinations:

| Metric | Symbol | Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Specific Strength** | $\sigma_s$ | Strength-to-density ratio $\frac{\sigma_f}{\rho_c}$ | $\text{kN}\cdot\text{m/kg}$ or $\text{MPa}\cdot\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$ |
| **Specific Modulus** | $E_s$ | Modulus-to-density ratio $\frac{E_f}{\rho_c}$ | $\text{GPa}\cdot\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$ or $\text{kN}\cdot\text{m/kg}$ |
| **Strength-to-Weight Ratio** | $SWR$ | Practical figure-of-merit under operational loading | Dimensionless / $\text{kNm/kg}$ |

where $\sigma_f$ is the **ultimate tensile strength**, $E_f$ is the **Young's modulus**, and $\rho_c$ is the **composite density**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Bamboo-Reinforced Mud Wall"**
> Imagine building a wall using pure mud. It cracks under its own weight and crumbles in rain. But if you embed thin strips of bamboo (strong, lightweight fibers) inside the mud (matrix), the wall becomes remarkably strong, weather-resistant, and lightweight. **Mud = Matrix, Bamboo = Reinforcement, Wall = Composite.**
> Modern industrial composites follow the exact same logic — but with **carbon fiber, glass fiber, or Kevlar** as the "bamboo" and **epoxy, polyester, or PEEK** as the "mud". The art of industrial application lies in choosing the *right bamboo in the right mud, oriented in the right direction, for the right job*.

### 1.2.1 Why Composites Dominate Industrial Selection

A typical KTU board answer must establish the **four pillars of industrial relevance**:

1. **Weight Reduction (Mass Efficiency):** Aircraft and EV industries target **15–30% weight reduction** by replacing aluminum ($\rho \approx 2.7\ \text{g/cm}^3$) with CFRP ($\rho \approx 1.55\ \text{g/cm}^3$).
2. **Tailorable Properties:** Anisotropic layup ($[0°/45°/90°/-45°]_s$) allows engineers to "dial in" stiffness/strength along critical load paths — a luxury impossible with isotropic metals.
3. **Corrosion & Fatigue Resistance:** GFRP rebars in coastal bridges outlast steel by **2–3 times** in chloride-rich environments.
4. **Part Consolidation:** A single CFRP monocoque replaces **40–100 metallic components** in a car chassis, reducing assembly cost and failure points.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Composite Design Constants (must memorize for KTU exam):**
> - **Standard density of E-Glass fiber:** $\rho_f = 2.54\ \text{g/cm}^3$
> - **Standard density of Carbon fiber (PAN-based):** $\rho_f = 1.75\text{–}1.95\ \text{g/cm}^3$
> - **Standard density of Aramid (Kevlar 49) fiber:** $\rho_f = 1.44\ \text{g/cm}^3$
> - **Standard density of Epoxy (matrix):** $\rho_m = 1.1\text{–}1.25\ \text{g/cm}^3$
> - **Density of Aerospace-grade Al 7075:** $\rho = 2.81\ \text{g/cm}^3$
> - **Density of Mild Steel:** $\rho = 7.85\ \text{g/cm}^3$
> - **Universal 1 kg weight saving in aircraft = ~ $100–$300 annual fuel cost saved** (operational metric).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Industrial Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Composite

Before diving into applications, the KTU examiner expects students to articulate **WHY** a particular composite family is selected. The decision tree is governed by four operational demands:

| Operational Demand | Dominant Composite Class | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Maximum strength at minimum weight** (Aerospace) | **CFRP** (Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer) | Highest $E_s$ and $\sigma_s$ among FRPs |
| **Low cost + moderate strength** (Automotive) | **GFRP** (Glass Fiber Reinforced Polymer) | Lowest $/kg among structural composites |
| **Impact & ballistic resistance** (Defense) | **Aramid/Kevlar composites** | Highest specific energy absorption (SEA) |
| **High-temperature strength** (Turbines, brakes) | **CMCs** (e.g., SiC/SiC) | Retain strength above 1000 °C |
| **Dimensional stability at elevated T** | **MMCs** (e.g., Al-SiC) | Low CTE, high thermal conductivity |
| **Electrical insulation + mechanical load** | **GFRP / Epoxy composites** | High resistivity + good $E$ |

## 2.2 Application-Wise Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Aerospace Industry (The Flagship Application)

The aerospace sector consumes **~ 35–40% of global advanced composites production** by value.

**Components manufactured from composites:**
- **Primary structure:** Wings (CFRP skins + honeycomb core), fuselage barrels (CFRP), empennage, helicopter rotor blades.
- **Secondary structure:** Floor beams, control surfaces, doors, interior panels.
- **Engine components (CMCs):** Turbine shrouds, combustor liners (SiC/SiC), exit flaps (in LEAP & GE9X engines).

**Quantitative Justification (KTU-favourite calculation):**
- Boeing 787 Dreamliner: **50% composites by weight** (mostly CFRP).
- Airbus A350 XWB: **53% composites** by weight.
- Weight reduction over aluminum airframe: **~ 20%**.
- Fuel efficiency improvement: **~ 15–20%**.
- Corrosion-related maintenance reduction: **~ 30%**.

**Why CFRP over aluminum?**
Aluminum has a fatigue limit that limits airframe life to ~ 20–30 years. CFRP does not suffer from the classical fatigue crack-propagation failure mode (though it has its own unique failure modes like delamination and impact damage).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** The specific modulus $E_s$ of CFRP is ~ **3–5 times** that of Al 7075. This is the single most important metric justifying CFRP in wing-skin applications where **bending stiffness $D \propto E \cdot t^3$** — a small drop in $E$ must be compensated by thicker skin, defeating the weight-saving objective.

---

### 2.2.2 Automotive Industry

The automotive industry is the **largest-volume consumer** of composites (by mass).

**Application zones:**
| Zone | Material | Component |
|---|---|---|
| **Body-in-White (BIW)** | CFRP (BMW i3, i8) | Passenger cell (LifeDrive architecture) |
| **Body Panels** | SMC (Sheet Molding Compound), GMT | Bonnet, fender, tailgate |
| **Drive Shaft** | CFRP / Epoxy | Propeller shaft (NVH + mass reduction) |
| **Leaf Springs** | GFRP / Epoxy | Parabolic leaf spring |
| **Brake Discs** | C/C composites | High-performance disc brakes |
| **Pistons** | Al/SiC MMC | Reduced reciprocating mass |
| **Battery Enclosure** | CFRP / SMC | EV battery housing |

**Engineering Justification:**
- A **CFRP drive shaft** can run at much higher critical speed than a steel shaft, **eliminating the intermediate bearing** and reducing NVH (Noise-Vibration-Harshness).
- The **CFRP leaf spring** in Volvo and Mercedes trucks weighs **~ 14 kg vs. ~ 38 kg for steel** — a **63% weight saving**.
- **C/SiC brake discs** (Porsche, Ferrari) weigh **~ 50% less than cast iron** and offer **5× longer service life**.

---

### 2.2.3 Marine Industry

The marine sector demands materials that resist:
1. **Saltwater corrosion** (eliminates steel and aluminum in many zones).
2. **Marine organism fouling**.
3. **High-cycle wave loading** (fatigue).
4. **Fire, smoke, and toxicity (FST) compliance** (especially in naval vessels).

**Composite Applications in Marine:**
- **Hull, deck, superstructure** of yachts, patrol boats, minesweepers, and corvettes (mostly GFRP with sandwich core).
- **Masts of stealth frigates** (e.g., Visby-class corvette, Swedish Navy) — **CFRP sandwich construction** to reduce radar cross-section.
- **Propeller blades** in water-taxi / tug applications.
- **Piping systems** for ballast and bilge (GRP pipes).
- **Radomes** (GFRP / syntactic foam) on naval vessels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important KTU Point:** The **Visby-class corvette** is the world's first all-composite stealth warship — hull, decks, bulkheads, mast, and superstructure are **100% carbon-fiber/vinyl-ester sandwich composite**, giving it a **radar cross-section (RCS) of a small fishing boat**.

---

### 2.2.4 Civil Engineering & Construction

| Application | Composite | Advantage |
|---|---|---|
| **Bridge decks** | GFRP / Carbon hybrid | Corrosion resistance, ~ 75% weight reduction over concrete |
| **External strengthening** | CFRP laminates / wraps | Retrofitting of beams, columns for seismic upgrade |
| **Reinforcement bars (rebar)** | GFRP / BFRP (Basalt) | Non-corroding, non-magnetic, used in MRI rooms & coastal piers |
| **Cladding panels** | GFRP/FRP sandwich | Lightweight facade, complex shapes |
| **Pipes** | GRP/GRE | Chemical plants, desalination, sewerage |

**KTU-Favourite Number:** **Every 1% increase in bridge rebar corrosion** adds **~ $1–1.5 billion/year** in US bridge maintenance cost. GFRP rebar eliminates this recurring cost.

---

### 2.2.5 Sports & Recreation

This is the **most visible** end-use sector where composites are marketed to consumers.

| Sport | Composite Component | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Cycling** | CFRP frame, fork, handlebar, wheels | Stiffness + ultra-light |
| **Tennis** | CFRP/GFRP racquet | High stiffness + damping |
| **Golf** | CFRP shaft | Light-swing + low torque |
| **Skiing** | CFRP/GFRP/Aramid hybrid | Torsional rigidity + impact absorption |
| **Formula 1** | CFRP monocoque chassis (survival cell) | Crash energy absorption at < 20 g driver deceleration |
| **Cricket** | CFRP / Wood composite bat | Light swing + power |
| **Sailing/Yachting** | CFRP mast | Reduced weight aloft = righting moment gain |

---

### 2.2.6 Wind Energy

- **Rotor blades** of modern wind turbines (3 MW+) are **GFRP + CFRP hybrid** sandwich structures, up to **80–100 m long**.
- Blade root → CFRP (stiffness).
- Blade body → GFRP (cost + fatigue).
- Leading edge → GFRP + polyurethane coating (erosion).

---

### 2.2.7 Defense & Ballistic Protection

- **Body armor:** Aramid (Kevlar), UHMWPE (Dyneema), or hybrid composite panels.
- **Helmets:** Aramid composite (e.g., PASGT, ACH).
- **Vehicle armor:** Ceramic-faced composite (Al₂O₃ or SiC tiles bonded to a CFRP/Kevlar backing).
- **Rocket motor casings:** CFRP-wound (filament-wound) to replace steel.
- **Radomes:** GFRP with syntactic foam core for missile guidance.

**Critical Metric — Specific Energy Absorption (SEA):**

$$
SEA = \frac{\text{Energy absorbed}}{\rho_c \cdot A \cdot t} \quad [\text{J/kg·m}^2]
$$

Aramid composites typically show **SEA > 100 J/kg·m²**, far exceeding steel (~ 25 J/kg·m²).

---

### 2.2.8 Electrical & Electronics

- **PCB substrates:** FR-4 (glass-fiber / epoxy) — the workhorse of the electronics industry.
- **Insulator rods, transformer housings:** GFRP / DMC (Dough Molding Compound).
- **Cable trays, bus-bar supports:** GFRP — non-conductive + non-magnetic.
- **Antenna reflectors:** CFRP-skinned honeycomb (high stiffness, near-zero thermal distortion).

---

### 2.2.9 Biomedical Applications

- **Prosthetic sockets:** CFRP / GFRP layups.
- **External fixators and orthotic braces:** Thermoplastic composites.
- **X-ray / CT scanner tables:** GFRP / Carbon — **radiolucent** (transparent to X-rays).
- **Bone plates and screws:** CFR-PEEK (polyetheretherketone) — bioinert, modulus close to bone (avoids stress shielding).
- **Dental applications:** Fiber-reinforced composite (FRC) bridges and posts.

---

### 2.2.10 Chemical & Industrial Process Equipment

- **Storage tanks, pressure vessels, reactors:** GRP / dual-laminate (thermoplastic liner + GRP shell).
- **Pumps, valves, fan blades:** Vinyl-ester / GFRP.
- **Stacks and chimneys:** GRP (in FGD scrubbers of power plants).
- **Rollers, drive shafts, conveyor parts:** CFRP / GFRP.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are universally tested in KTU PEMET413 Module-1 questions related to industrial application selection.**

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Application Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_s = \dfrac{\sigma_f}{\rho_c}$ | Strength per unit mass | Aerospace structural weight optimization |
| 2 | $E_s = \dfrac{E_f}{\rho_c}$ | Stiffness per unit mass | Wing-skin, drive-shaft design |
| 3 | $SWR = \dfrac{\sigma_f}{\rho_c \cdot g}$ | Strength per unit weight | Civil/automotive component selection |
| 4 | $SEA = \dfrac{E_{abs}}{\rho_c \cdot A \cdot t}$ | Crash energy absorbed per unit mass | Crashworthiness, ballistic protection |
| 5 | $\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m$ | Rule of mixtures (density) | Material selection for minimum mass |
| 6 | $V_f + V_m = 1$ | Volume conservation | Volumetric analysis |
| 7 | $\% W_s = \dfrac{\rho_{metal} - \rho_{comp}}{\rho_{metal}} \times 100$ | Percentage weight saving | Comparative studies (aluminum vs CFRP) |
| 8 | $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Longitudinal modulus (Voigt model) | Unidirectional composite stiffness |
| 9 | $D = \dfrac{E \cdot t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ | Flexural rigidity of a skin | Sandwich panel design |
| 10 | $N_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi}{2 L^2} \sqrt{\dfrac{E \cdot I \cdot G \cdot J}{\rho_c \cdot A}}$ | Critical speed of a shaft | CFRP drive-shaft application |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The selection of composite for a given industrial application is **not a materials decision in isolation** — it is a **systems-engineering trade-off** involving:

1. **Performance requirement** (load, temperature, environment).
2. **Manufacturing route** (hand layup, RTM, filament winding, autoclave, pultrusion).
3. **Cost per kg saved** (most-cited KTU trade-off: **$5–50 per kg saved in aerospace vs. $0.50–2 per kg saved in automotive**).
4. **Regulatory certification** (FAA, EASA, IMO, BIS for respective sectors).
5. **End-of-life / recyclability** (becoming critical in EU post-2025).

The board examiner expects the student to demonstrate the ability to **map an operational demand → a specific composite class → a specific manufacturing process → a quantified benefit**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Quantitative Worked Example 1 — Material Selection for an Aircraft Wing Skin

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Style 14-Mark-Type Analysis: Choose between CFRP and Al 7075 for an aircraft wing skin, given operational and material data.**

### 3.1.1 Given Data

| Property | CFRP (UD, $V_f = 0.6$) | Al 7075-T6 |
|---|---|---|
| Longitudinal strength, $\sigma_f$ | $1500\ \text{MPa}$ | $570\ \text{MPa}$ |
| Longitudinal modulus, $E_f$ | $135\ \text{GPa}$ | $72\ \text{GPa}$ |
| Density, $\rho_c$ | $1.55\ \text{g/cm}^3$ | $2.81\ \text{g/cm}^3$ |

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Calculate Specific Strength ($\sigma_s$)

$$
\sigma_s^{CFRP} = \frac{\sigma_f^{CFRP}}{\rho_c^{CFRP}} = \frac{1500\ \text{MPa}}{1.55\ \text{g/cm}^3} = 967.7\ \text{MPa·cm}^3/\text{g}
$$

Convert to SI-consistent units ($\text{kN·m/kg}$):
Since $1\ \text{MPa} = 1\ \text{MN/m}^2 = 10^6\ \text{N/m}^2$ and $1\ \text{g/cm}^3 = 10^3\ \text{kg/m}^3$:

$$
\sigma_s^{CFRP} = \frac{1500 \times 10^6\ \text{N/m}^2}{1.55 \times 10^3\ \text{kg/m}^3} = 9.677 \times 10^5\ \text{N·m/kg} = 967.7\ \text{kN·m/kg}
$$

$$
\sigma_s^{Al} = \frac{570 \times 10^6}{2.81 \times 10^3} = 2.028 \times 10^5\ \text{N·m/kg} = 202.8\ \text{kN·m/kg}
$$

$$
\text{Ratio} = \frac{\sigma_s^{CFRP}}{\sigma_s^{Al}} = \frac{967.7}{202.8} = 4.77
$$

**Interpretation:** CFRP delivers **4.77× higher specific strength** than Al 7075.

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Calculate Specific Modulus ($E_s$)

$$
E_s^{CFRP} = \frac{135 \times 10^9}{1.55 \times 10^3} = 8.71 \times 10^7\ \text{N·m/kg} = 87.1\ \text{MN·m/kg}
$$

$$
E_s^{Al} = \frac{72 \times 10^9}{2.81 \times 10^3} = 2.56 \times 10^7\ \text{N·m/kg} = 25.6\ \text{MN·m/kg}
$$

$$
\text{Ratio} = \frac{E_s^{CFRP}}{E_s^{Al}} = \frac{87.1}{25.6} = 3.40
$$

**Interpretation:** CFRP delivers **3.40× higher specific stiffness** than Al 7075.

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Weight Saving for Equal Bending Stiffness

For a wing skin carrying bending loads, the flexural rigidity per unit width is:

$$
D = \frac{E \cdot t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}
$$

For equal $D$ between two materials:

$$
t_{CFRP} = t_{Al} \cdot \left(\frac{E_{Al}}{E_{CFRP}}\right)^{1/3} = t_{Al} \cdot \left(\frac{72}{135}\right)^{1/3} = t_{Al} \cdot (0.533)^{1/3} = 0.811\ t_{Al}
$$

Mass per unit area comparison:

$$
m_{CFRP} = \rho_{CFRP} \cdot t_{CFRP} = 1.55 \cdot 0.811 = 1.257\ \text{(relative units)}
$$

$$
m_{Al} = \rho_{Al} \cdot t_{Al} = 2.81 \cdot 1.0 = 2.81\ \text{(relative units)}
$$

$$
\% W_s = \frac{2.81 - 1.257}{2.81} \times 100 = 55.3\%
$$

**Conclusion:** For **equal bending stiffness**, switching from Al 7075 to CFRP yields a **~ 55% mass saving** for the wing skin — confirming the dominance of CFRP in modern wing design.

---

## 3.2 Quantitative Worked Example 2 — Composite Drive-Shaft Critical Speed

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Style Application Problem: Justify replacement of a steel drive shaft with a CFRP shaft by comparing critical speeds.**

### 3.2.1 Given

- Steel shaft: $L = 1.5\ \text{m}$, $D = 50\ \text{mm}$, $\rho_{st} = 7850\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $E_{st} = 210\ \text{GPa}$, $G_{st} = 80\ \text{GPa}$.
- CFRP shaft: $V_f = 0.55$, $E_{L} = 140\ \text{GPa}$, $G_{LT} = 6\ \text{GPa}$, $\rho_c = 1580\ \text{kg/m}^3$.

### 3.2.2 Step 1 — Compute Cross-Sectional Area and Moments of Inertia

Assuming the same outer diameter $D = 50\ \text{mm}$ for both:

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2
$$

$$
I = \frac{\pi D^4}{64} = \frac{\pi (0.05)^4}{64} = 3.068 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}^4
$$

$$
J = \frac{\pi D^4}{32} = 6.136 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}^4
$$

### 3.2.3 Step 2 — Apply Critical Speed Formula

$$
N_{cr} = \frac{\pi}{2 L^2}\sqrt{\frac{E \cdot I \cdot G \cdot J}{\rho \cdot A}}
$$

**For Steel:**

$$
N_{cr}^{st} = \frac{\pi}{2 (1.5)^2}\sqrt{\frac{(210 \times 10^9)(3.068 \times 10^{-7})(80 \times 10^9)(6.136 \times 10^{-7})}{(7850)(1.963 \times 10^{-3})}}
$$

Numerator inside the square root:
$$
(210 \times 10^9)(3.068 \times 10^{-7}) = 6.443 \times 10^4\ \text{N·m}^2
$$
$$
(80 \times 10^9)(6.136 \times 10^{-7}) = 4.909 \times 10^4\ \text{N·m}^2
$$
$$
\text{Product} = (6.443 \times 10^4)(4.909 \times 10^4) = 3.163 \times 10^9
$$

Denominator:
$$
(7850)(1.963 \times 10^{-3}) = 15.41
$$

$$
\frac{E \cdot I \cdot G \cdot J}{\rho \cdot A} = \frac{3.163 \times 10^9}{15.41} = 2.053 \times 10^8
$$

$$
N_{cr}^{st} = \frac{\pi}{4.5}\sqrt{2.053 \times 10^8} = 0.698 \times 14.328 \times 10^3 = 10.00 \times 10^3\ \text{rad/s}
$$

Converting to RPM:
$$
N_{cr}^{st} = \frac{10{,}000 \times 60}{2\pi} = 95{,}493\ \text{RPM} \approx 95{,}500\ \text{RPM}
$$

**For CFRP:**

$$
N_{cr}^{CFRP} = \frac{\pi}{2 (1.5)^2}\sqrt{\frac{(140 \times 10^9)(3.068 \times 10^{-7})(6 \times 10^9)(6.136 \times 10^{-7})}{(1580)(1.963 \times 10^{-3})}}
$$

Numerator inside the square root:
$$
(140 \times 10^9)(3.068 \times 10^{-7}) = 4.295 \times 10^4
$$
$$
(6 \times 10^9)(6.136 \times 10^{-7}) = 3.682 \times 10^3
$$
$$
\text{Product} = (4.295 \times 10^4)(3.682 \times 10^3) = 1.582 \times 10^8
$$

Denominator:
$$
(1580)(1.963 \times 10^{-3}) = 3.102
$$

$$
\frac{E \cdot I \cdot G \cdot J}{\rho \cdot A} = \frac{1.582 \times 10^8}{3.102} = 5.10 \times 10^7
$$

$$
N_{cr}^{CFRP} = 0.698 \times \sqrt{5.10 \times 10^7} = 0.698 \times 7141 = 4984\ \text{rad/s}
$$

Converting to RPM:
$$
N_{cr}^{CFRP} = \frac{4984 \times 60}{2\pi} = 47{,}580\ \text{RPM} \approx 47{,}600\ \text{RPM}
$$

### 3.2.4 Step 3 — Engineering Conclusion

| Metric | Steel | CFRP | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|
| Critical speed | 95,500 RPM | 47,600 RPM | Steel is stiffer, but **CFRP is light enough to exceed operating speed** |
| Mass of 1.5 m shaft | 23.1 kg | 4.65 kg | **80% mass reduction** |
| Operating speed | < 5000 RPM | < 5000 RPM | Both shafts are **safe** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** Although CFRP has a *lower* critical speed than steel, **it is still far above the operating speed** in automotive driveshaft applications. The real industrial win is the **80% mass reduction** which reduces NVH, eliminates the center bearing (since CFRP can be a single piece in one piece over the entire length), and improves fuel economy.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Composite Class vs. Industrial Sector

| Industrial Sector | Dominant Composite | Manufacturing Route | Performance Driver |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aerospace primary | CFRP | Autoclave prepreg | $E_s$, $\sigma_s$, fatigue |
| Aerospace engine | CMCs (SiC/SiC) | CVI, melt infiltration | High-T strength |
| Automotive BIW | CFRP / SMC | RTM, compression molding | Crashworthiness, mass |
| Drive shaft | CFRP | Filament winding | $N_{cr}$, mass |
| Brake disc | C/C, C/SiC | CVD, liquid infiltration | $T$ resistance, $SEA$ |
| Marine hull | GFRP sandwich | Hand layup, infusion | Corrosion, mass |
| Stealth warship | CFRP + VARTM | VARTM, infusion | RCS, corrosion |
| Bridge deck | GFRP / hybrid | Pultrusion | Corrosion, durability |
| Seismic retrofit | CFRP wrap | Wet layup | $\sigma_s$, bond strength |
| Wind blade | GFRP + CFRP | VARTM, prepreg | $E_s$, fatigue, $L$ |
| Body armor | Aramid / UHMWPE | Layup + pressing | $SEA$, $V_{50}$ |
| Helmet | Aramid composite | Compression molding | Ballistic limit |
| Rocket motor casing | CFRP | Filament winding | $E_s$, burst pressure |
| PCB substrate | FR-4 (GF/epoxy) | Compression | Dielectric, dimensional |
| Transformer insulation | GFRP / DMC | Compression | Dielectric strength |
| Prosthetic limb | CFRP / CF-PEEK | Layup, additive | Mass, biocompatibility |
| X-ray table | CFRP sandwich | Autoclave | Radiolucency, stiffness |
| Storage tank | GRP / dual-laminate | Filament winding | Chemical resistance |
| Ski / racquet | CFRP / Aramid | Prepreg / bladder | Damping, $E_s$ |
| F1 chassis | CFRP honeycomb | Autoclave | Crash, $E_s$ |
| EV battery enclosure | CFRP / SMC | Compression, RTM | Fire, mass, sealing |

This matrix is the **single most-likely artifact** the KTU board examiner expects a student to reproduce.
