# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three industrial applications of GFRP composites and justify the selection of GFRP in each case.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> GFRP (Glass Fiber Reinforced Polymer) is selected when the primary industrial demand is **low cost + adequate mechanical performance + corrosion resistance**. Three key applications are:
> 1. **Marine hulls and decks** – GFRP resists saltwater corrosion indefinitely, unlike steel or aluminum which require sacrificial anodes and regular painting. Manufacturing via hand layup or vacuum infusion makes it economical for boat hulls.
> 2. **Pipes and storage tanks in chemical plants** – GFRP/GRP piping is chemically resistant to a wide pH range and is the industry standard for desalination plants, FGD scrubbers, and chlor-alkali units.
> 3. **Automotive body panels (SMC)** – Sheet Molding Compound (glass fiber + polyester + CaCO₃ filler) is compression-molded at high volume for bonnets, tailgates, and fenders, balancing cost, dent resistance, and mass reduction.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Why are carbon fiber reinforced polymers (CFRP) preferred over aramid composites in primary aircraft structural components?**

**Model Answer:**

> CFRP is preferred over aramid (Kevlar) composites in primary aircraft structures (wings, fuselage) because:
> 1. **Higher specific modulus** ($E_s \approx 87\ \text{MN·m/kg}$ for CFRP vs. $\approx 50\ \text{MN·m/kg}$ for aramid). Wing skins require high **bending stiffness** $D = E t^3 / 12(1-\nu^2)$ to resist aerodynamic loads without flutter.
> 2. **Higher compressive strength** – Aramid fibers are weak in compression (micro-buckling), making them unsuitable for the upper wing skin which is in compression. CFRP retains $\sim 60\%$ of its tensile strength in compression.
> 3. **Better fatigue and environmental resistance** – Aramid absorbs moisture, leading to property degradation. CFRP is hygroscopically stable and offers superior fatigue life.
> 4. **Compatibility with automated fiber placement (AFP)** for high-rate aerospace production.
>
> Aramid remains preferred for **secondary structures** (interior panels, radomes) and **ballistic protection** where impact absorption is critical.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module-Internal Choice Questions

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the industrial applications of composites in the **aerospace industry**, with specific examples of components, materials used, and the engineering benefits realized. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A unidirectional CFRP laminate is proposed to replace an aluminum 7075 wing-skin. Given the following data, compute the **specific strength, specific modulus**, and the **percentage weight saving for equal bending stiffness**:
- CFRP (UD, $V_f = 0.6$): $\sigma_f = 1500\ \text{MPa}$, $E_L = 135\ \text{GPa}$, $\rho_c = 1.55\ \text{g/cm}^3$.
- Al 7075-T6: $\sigma_f = 570\ \text{MPa}$, $E = 72\ \text{GPa}$, $\rho = 2.81\ \text{g/cm}^3$.
**(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A:

**(a) Aerospace Applications of Composites (7 marks):**

The aerospace industry is the **flagship consumer** of advanced composites, using ~ 35–40% of global production by value. Key applications:

**1. Primary Structures (CFRP-dominated):**
- **Wing skins and spars:** CFRP prepreg cured in an autoclave (e.g., Boeing 787 has 32-m CFRP wings).
- **Fuselage barrels:** Automated Fiber Placement (AFP) of CFRP slit-tape, e.g., Airbus A350 XWB fuselage shells are 100% CFRP.
- **Empennage and tailplanes:** CFRP honeycomb sandwich for torsional rigidity.
- **Helicopter rotor blades:** CFRP + GFRP hybrid spar with honeycomb trailing edge.

**2. Engine Components (CMCs):**
- **SiC/SiC turbine shrouds and combustor liners** (used in LEAP, GE9X engines) — operate at ~ 1200 °C and reduce cooling-air requirements.
- **Exhaust flaps and nozzle components** in C/C and C/SiC.

**3. Secondary Structures (GFRP, Phenolic composites):**
- **Floor beams, control surfaces, doors, cargo liners, interior panels** — phenolic GFRP for fire-smoke-toxicity (FST) compliance.

**4. Engineering Benefits Realized:**
- **~ 20% airframe mass reduction** vs. aluminum (Boeing 787 vs. 767).
- **~ 15–20% fuel efficiency improvement** over aircraft lifetime.
- **~ 30% reduction in corrosion-related maintenance**.
- **Larger cabin windows** (enabled by hoop stress in CFRP fuselage allowing higher internal pressure).
- **Higher cabin humidity** (CFRP is less prone to fatigue at higher humidity).
- **Resistance to fatigue crack propagation** — damage tolerance is by design (no catastrophic crack growth like in Al).

---

**(b) Quantitative Analysis (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Specific Strength:**

$$
\sigma_s^{CFRP} = \frac{1500 \times 10^6}{1.55 \times 10^3} = 9.677 \times 10^5\ \text{N·m/kg} = 967.7\ \text{kN·m/kg}
$$

$$
\sigma_s^{Al} = \frac{570 \times 10^6}{2.81 \times 10^3} = 2.028 \times 10^5\ \text{N·m/kg} = 202.8\ \text{kN·m/kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Specific Modulus:**

$$
E_s^{CFRP} = \frac{135 \times 10^9}{1.55 \times 10^3} = 8.71 \times 10^7\ \text{N·m/kg} = 87.1\ \text{MN·m/kg}
$$

$$
E_s^{Al} = \frac{72 \times 10^9}{2.81 \times 10^3} = 2.56 \times 10^7\ \text{N·m/kg} = 25.6\ \text{MN·m/kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Thickness for equal bending stiffness:**

$$
t_{CFRP} = t_{Al}\left(\frac{72}{135}\right)^{1/3} = 0.811\ t_{Al}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass per unit area:**

$$
m_{CFRP} = 1.55 \times 0.811 = 1.257
$$

$$
m_{Al} = 2.81 \times 1.0 = 2.81
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage weight saving:**

$$
\% W_s = \frac{2.81 - 1.257}{2.81} \times 100 = 55.3\%
$$

**Conclusion:** CFRP offers ~ 4.77× higher specific strength, ~ 3.40× higher specific modulus, and ~ 55% mass saving at equal bending stiffness compared to Al 7075. Hence CFRP is the material of choice for primary wing-skin structures.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **industrial applications of composites in the marine industry**, with emphasis on the material selection rationale and the specific engineering benefits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **CFRP drive shaft** of length $L = 1.4\ \text{m}$ and outer diameter $D = 60\ \text{mm}$ is proposed to replace a steel drive shaft. The CFRP has $E_L = 140\ \text{GPa}$, $G_{LT} = 6\ \text{GPa}$, and $\rho_c = 1580\ \text{kg/m}^3$. The steel has $E = 210\ \text{GPa}$, $G = 80\ \text{GPa}$, and $\rho = 7850\ \text{kg/m}^3$. **Compute the critical speed of both shafts and the mass of each shaft**. Comment on the industrial implications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B:

**(a) Marine Industry Applications of Composites (7 marks):**

The marine industry uses composites to address four fundamental demands: **(i) salt-water corrosion resistance, (ii) hydrodynamic mass reduction, (iii) radar stealth (for naval vessels), and (iv) fatigue endurance under wave loading.**

**1. Hulls, Decks, and Superstructures:**
- **Recreational boats and yachts:** GFRP/unsaturated polyester hand layup or vacuum infusion (e.g., Sunseeker, Princess Yachts). Lloyds-certified hulls last 30+ years.
- **Patrol boats and coast guard vessels:** GFRP sandwich (with PVC or balsa core) — reduces draft, increases payload.
- **Stealth warships:** **Visby-class corvette** (Sweden) — first all-CFRP warship; hull, decks, mast, and superstructure are 100% CFRP sandwich with vinyl-ester matrix. Result: RCS of a small fishing boat, ~ 50% mass saving, top speed of 35+ knots.

**2. Marine Piping and Risers:**
- **GRP/GRE pipes** for ballast, bilge, seawater cooling, and offshore risers. Advantages: zero corrosion, smooth bore (low friction loss), 50-year design life.

**3. Propellers and Shafting:**
- **GFRP propellers** for small craft.
- **CFRP propeller shafts** (e.g., in some US Navy submarines) for reduced weight and acoustic signature.
- **Composite stern tubes** as bearings in water-lubricated systems.

**4. Radomes, Masts, and Sensor Housings:**
- **GFRP with syntactic foam core** for radomes on naval vessels — RF-transparent, weatherproof, structurally robust.
- **CFRP sensor masts** on submarines (e.g., photonics masts).

**5. Buoys, Pontoons, and Subsea Structures:**
- **GFRP floats, fenders, and pontoons** for harbours — non-corroding, maintenance-free.

**Engineering Benefits Realized:**
- **Zero corrosion** in saltwater (vs. steel needing cathodic protection + painting every 3–5 years).
- **Mass reduction** of 30–50% over aluminum hulls, leading to lower fuel consumption and higher payload.
- **Reduced acoustic, magnetic, and radar signatures** (critical for naval vessels).
- **Damage tolerance** — composite panels absorb impact (ice, collision) without catastrophic fracture.
- **Lower life-cycle cost** despite higher initial cost.

---

**(b) Quantitative Analysis (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional properties:**

$$
A = \frac{\pi (0.06)^2}{4} = 2.827 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2
$$

$$
I = \frac{\pi (0.06)^4}{64} = 6.362 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}^4
$$

$$
J = 2I = 1.272 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^4
$$

**Step 2 — Critical speed of steel shaft:**

$$
N_{cr}^{st} = \frac{\pi}{2 (1.4)^2}\sqrt{\frac{(210 \times 10^9)(6.362 \times 10^{-7})(80 \times 10^9)(1.272 \times 10^{-6})}{(7850)(2.827 \times 10^{-3})}}
$$

Inside the square root:
- $E \cdot I = (210 \times 10^9)(6.362 \times 10^{-7}) = 1.336 \times 10^5\ \text{N·m}^2$
- $G \cdot J = (80 \times 10^9)(1.272 \times 10^{-6}) = 1.018 \times 10^5\ \text{N·m}^2$
- Product $= 1.336 \times 10^5 \times 1.018 \times 10^5 = 1.360 \times 10^{10}$
- $\rho \cdot A = (7850)(2.827 \times 10^{-3}) = 22.19$
- Ratio $= 1.360 \times 10^{10} / 22.19 = 6.13 \times 10^8$

$$
N_{cr}^{st} = \frac{\pi}{3.92} \times \sqrt{6.13 \times 10^8} = 0.8014 \times 24759 = 19{,}844\ \text{rad/s}
$$

Converting to RPM:
$$
N_{cr}^{st} = \frac{19{,}844 \times 60}{2\pi} = 189{,}500\ \text{RPM}
$$

**Step 3 — Critical speed of CFRP shaft:**

$$
N_{cr}^{CFRP} = \frac{\pi}{2 (1.4)^2}\sqrt{\frac{(140 \times 10^9)(6.362 \times 10^{-7})(6 \times 10^9)(1.272 \times 10^{-6})}{(1580)(2.827 \times 10^{-3})}}
$$

Inside the square root:
- $E \cdot I = (140 \times 10^9)(6.362 \times 10^{-7}) = 8.907 \times 10^4$
- $G \cdot J = (6 \times 10^9)(1.272 \times 10^{-6}) = 7.632 \times 10^3$
- Product $= 8.907 \times 10^4 \times 7.632 \times 10^3 = 6.797 \times 10^8$
- $\rho \cdot A = (1580)(2.827 \times 10^{-3}) = 4.467$
- Ratio $= 6.797 \times 10^8 / 4.467 = 1.522 \times 10^8$

$$
N_{cr}^{CFRP} = 0.8014 \times \sqrt{1.522 \times 10^8} = 0.8014 \times 12337 = 9887\ \text{rad/s}
$$

Converting to RPM:
$$
N_{cr}^{CFRP} = \frac{9887 \times 60}{2\pi} = 94{,}400\ \text{RPM}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass of each shaft:**

$$
m_{st} = \rho \cdot A \cdot L = 7850 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.4 = 31.07\ \text{kg}
$$

$$
m_{CFRP} = 1580 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.4 = 6.25\ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Comparative summary:**

| Parameter | Steel | CFRP | Observation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Critical speed | 189,500 RPM | 94,400 RPM | Steel is stiffer |
| Mass | 31.07 kg | 6.25 kg | **80% mass saving with CFRP** |
| Typical operating speed | < 5000 RPM | < 5000 RPM | Both are **safe** |

**Step 6 — Industrial Implications:**

1. Although the CFRP shaft has a *lower* critical speed, it is **still 18× higher than the operating speed**, so it is dynamically safe.
2. The **80% mass reduction** eliminates the need for a center bearing (which is required in a long steel shaft to avoid bending critical speed issues), simplifying the drivetrain and reducing NVH.
3. The CFRP shaft can be **filament-wound in a single piece** of 1.4 m length, whereas a steel shaft of the same length may require a two-piece construction.
4. The fuel-economy benefit of a 25 kg mass reduction in a passenger car is ~ 0.4 L/100 km, justifying the higher initial cost of CFRP.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks on Industrial Applications questions in KTU PEMET413:**
> 1. **Vague generalization:** Writing "composites are used in aircraft because they are light" without **quantifying** the specific strength, specific modulus, or quoting real component examples (e.g., "Boeing 787 = 50% composites", "Visby-class corvette = 100% CFRP sandwich hull") will cost 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing CFRP and GFRP applications:** Writing "CFRP is used in chemical storage tanks" is **wrong** — GFRP/GRP is used because of cost-effectiveness and chemical resistance. CFRP is reserved for **high-performance, weight-critical, high-stiffness** applications.
> 3. **Forgetting manufacturing route:** A KTU 14-mark answer MUST mention the **manufacturing process** (e.g., autoclave, RTM, filament winding, pultrusion, hand layup) alongside the application. A bare list of components without process context is considered incomplete.
> 4. **Numerical errors in specific strength/mass saving:** The units must be consistent. A common mistake is dividing MPa (which is N/mm²) directly by g/cm³ without unit conversion, leading to dimensionally incorrect answers.
> 5. **Forgetting bidirectional (sandwich) construction:** A marine hull or aircraft floor is NOT a solid laminate — it is a **sandwich** (skin + core). The board examiner expects this distinction in any 7-mark aerospace/marine application question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Definition:** Industrial applications of composites span 10+ sectors where weight, corrosion, fatigue, or tailored anisotropy are critical.
- [x] **Aerospace** is the flagship sector — Boeing 787 (50% composites) and A350 XWB (53% composites) are the must-cite benchmarks.
- [x] **Specific Strength** $\sigma_s = \sigma_f / \rho_c$ and **Specific Modulus** $E_s = E_f / \rho_c$ are the two most-cited metrics in KTU exam answers.
- [x] **CFRP** is preferred when **stiffness + mass** are critical; **GFRP** when **cost + corrosion resistance** are critical; **Aramid** when **impact/ballistic** is critical; **CMC** when **high temperature** is critical; **MMC** when **thermal management** is critical.
- [x] **Aerospace** uses CFRP for wings/fuselage, CMC (SiC/SiC) for engine hot sections, phenolic GFRP for cabin interiors.
- [x] **Automotive** uses CFRP for EV BIW (BMW i3), CFRP for drive-shaft and leaf-spring, C/SiC for brake discs, Al/SiC MMC for pistons.
- [x] **Marine** uses GFRP sandwich for hulls, CFRP for stealth warship (Visby), GRP for piping.
- [x] **Civil** uses CFRP wraps for seismic retrofit, GFRP rebar for coastal bridges, GFRP bridge decks.
- [x] **Defense** uses Aramid (Kevlar) and UHMWPE (Dyneema) for body armor; ceramic-faced composites for vehicle armor; CFRP for rocket motor casings.
- [x] **Wind energy** uses GFRP + CFRP hybrid for blades up to 100 m; manufacturing by VARTM or prepreg.
- [x] **Sports** uses CFRP for bicycles, F1 chassis, racquets, and skis — chosen for $E_s$ and damping.
- [x] **Biomedical** uses CFRP for prosthetic sockets, CF-PEEK for bone plates (matches bone modulus), GFRP for X-ray tables (radiolucent).
- [x] **Electrical** uses FR-4 (GF/epoxy) for PCBs, GFRP for transformer insulation, CFRP honeycomb for antenna reflectors.
- [x] **Drive-shaft** is the classic 14-mark numerical problem: compute $N_{cr}$ for steel vs. CFRP, show that mass reduction is 75–80%, and that critical speed remains well above operating speed.
- [x] **Wing-skin** is the classic 7-mark comparison: CFRP gives ~ 4–5× specific strength and 3–4× specific modulus of Al 7075, with ~ 55% mass saving at equal bending stiffness.
- [x] **Visby-class corvette** and **Boeing 787** are the two most likely "name-drop" answers in KTU theory questions.
- [x] **Always quote:** the matrix, the manufacturing process, AND a quantitative benefit (mass saving, fuel saving, life-cycle cost) — never just the application.
- [x] **Densities to remember:** CFRP ≈ 1.55 g/cm³, GFRP ≈ 1.8–2.0 g/cm³, Aramid ≈ 1.4 g/cm³, Al ≈ 2.7 g/cm³, Steel ≈ 7.85 g/cm³.
- [x] **Key formula trap:** $D = E t^3 / 12(1-\nu^2)$ — equal-bending-stiffness comparison requires $t_{CFRP} = t_{Al} (E_{Al}/E_{CFRP})^{1/3}$, NOT $(E_{Al}/E_{CFRP})$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfaces: Wettability and Bonding Interface in Composites, Types of Bonding at Interface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET413 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Interfaces: Wettability and Bonding Interface in Composites, Types of Bonding at Interface

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Interface in a Composite?

In a fibre-reinforced composite, the **interface** is the two-dimensional boundary region where the **reinforcement (fibre)** and the **matrix** come into physical contact and exchange mechanical, chemical, and thermal loads. Because the fibre and the matrix are chemically and mechanically dissimilar, this thin region (typically a few nanometres to a few micrometres thick) is often treated as a **third phase**, sometimes called the **interphase**, whose properties dictate the overall performance of the composite.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The *interface* is the contact surface between two phases (fibre and matrix) where discontinuity in one or more material properties occurs. The *interphase* is the volumetric region adjacent to the interface having properties distinctly different from either bulk phase.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of the interface in a composite exactly like the **contact layer between a brick and the mortar** in a wall. The brick is the *fibre* (strong but brittle, carries load), the mortar is the *matrix* (distributes load, protects the brick), and the **thin glue-line between them is the interface**. If the mortar does not "stick" properly to the brick, the wall cracks under load — even if both the brick and the mortar are individually very strong. Similarly, a composite can fail at the interface long before either the fibre or the matrix reaches its true strength limit, simply because the bonding was inadequate.

## 1.2 What is Wettability?

**Wettability** is the ability of a liquid (the molten matrix resin, for instance) to spread on and adhere to a solid surface (the fibre). It is the most fundamental prerequisite for forming a sound, void-free interface during composite processing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Working Definition:** Wettability is quantitatively measured by the **contact angle (θ)** formed by a liquid droplet resting on a solid surface under thermodynamic equilibrium.

A low contact angle means the liquid spreads easily → **good wettability** → strong physical contact. A high contact angle means the liquid beads up → **poor wettability** → trapped air pockets, voids, and weak bonding.

| Contact Angle (θ) | Wettability Class | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| θ = 0° | **Complete wetting** | Liquid spreads as a film — ideal for composites |
| 0° < θ < 90° | **Good / High wettability** | Adequate interfacial contact, conventional processing works |
| 90° < θ < 180° | **Poor / Low wettability** | Surface treatment or coupling agents required |
| θ = 180° | **Complete non-wetting** | Composite cannot be processed at all |

### Standard Surface Energy Metric
The single most important thermodynamic quantity governing wettability is the **surface free energy (γ)**, measured in **mJ/m² (equivalently, ergs/cm²)** or **N/m**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Wetting Rule for Composites:** For a liquid matrix to spontaneously wet a solid fibre, the **surface energy of the solid fibre (γ_SV) must be greater than the surface tension of the liquid matrix (γ_LV)**.

## 1.3 What is Bonding at the Interface?

**Interfacial bonding** refers to the set of physical and chemical mechanisms that hold the fibre and the matrix together across the interface. Without adequate bonding, no stress can be transferred from the weak, ductile matrix into the strong, brittle fibre — and the very purpose of reinforcement is defeated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Working Definition:** Interfacial bonding is the combination of all attractive forces — mechanical interlocking, chemical bonds, van der Waals forces, electrostatic attraction, and interdiffusion — acting across the fibre–matrix boundary to maintain load transfer integrity.

### The Three Things Every Interface Must Provide
1. **Stress transfer:** Load must travel from matrix → interface → fibre efficiently.
2. **Chemical/thermal stability:** The bond must survive processing temperatures and service environments.
3. **Fracture toughness:** The interface must absorb energy and deflect cracks (not be the weakest link).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Thermodynamics of Wetting — Young's Equation

When a liquid droplet of matrix resin is placed on a flat solid fibre surface, three interfacial tensions come into play at the **three-phase contact line**:

* **γ_SV** — surface energy of the solid in equilibrium with its vapour.
* **γ_SL** — interfacial energy between the solid and the liquid.
* **γ_LV** — surface tension of the liquid in equilibrium with its vapour.

Mechanical equilibrium of surface tension vectors along the horizontal direction yields **Young's Equation (1805)**:

$$\gamma_{SV} \;=\; \gamma_{SL} \;+\; \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$

where **θ** is the equilibrium contact angle.

### Logical Steps Behind Young's Equation
1. The system seeks to **minimise total free energy** of the three interfaces meeting at the contact line.
2. In equilibrium, the horizontal component of γ_LV (which pulls the droplet along the surface) is balanced by the difference between γ_SV and γ_SL.
3. The vertical component of γ_LV is balanced by an elastic deformation of the solid (often neglected for rigid fibres).
4. Rearranging the horizontal force balance gives the classical Young's relation.

### The Work of Adhesion — Young–Dupré Equation
The reversible work required to separate a unit area of liquid from a solid is called the **thermodynamic work of adhesion, W_A**:

$$W_A \;=\; \gamma_{SV} \;+\; \gamma_{LV} \;-\; \gamma_{SL}$$

Combining this with Young's equation eliminates γ_SL and gives the famous **Young–Dupré equation**:

$$W_A \;=\; \gamma_{LV}\,(1 + \cos\theta)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **larger the value of W_A, the stronger the adhesion** between fibre and matrix. A practical design target for composites is **W_A ≥ 50 mJ/m²**.

### The Spreading Coefficient
The **spreading coefficient (S)** determines whether the liquid will spontaneously spread as a film:

$$S \;=\; \gamma_{SV} \;-\; (\gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV})$$

* If **S > 0** → complete spreading, θ = 0°, perfect wetting.
* If **S < 0** → partial wetting, θ > 0°, droplet forms.

Substituting Young's equation gives an equivalent form:

$$S \;=\; \gamma_{LV}\,(\cos\theta - 1)$$

Since cos θ ≤ 1, the spreading coefficient is always **≤ 0** for a finite-θ system — hence spontaneous spreading is the special case (S = 0) and partial wetting is the general case.

## 2.2 The Critical Surface Energy of Fibres

Because direct measurement of γ_SV for a tiny fibre is impractical, the **Zisman critical surface energy (γ_c)** is used. It is defined as the surface tension of a liquid that just wets the solid with θ = 0°.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Wetting Rule (Zisman):** A liquid will completely wet a solid only if **γ_LV ≤ γ_c** of the solid. For a composite, choose a matrix resin whose surface tension is **less than the critical surface energy of the fibre**.

| Fibre Type | Typical γ_c (mJ/m²) | Matrix Compatibility |
|---|---|---|
| E-Glass | ~ 40 – 45 | Standard epoxy, polyester ✓ |
| Carbon (high modulus) | ~ 35 – 45 | Epoxy with sizing ✗/✓ |
| Aramid (Kevlar 49) | ~ 30 – 40 | Difficult — needs coupling agents |
| PTFE | ~ 18 | Nothing wets it without treatment |

## 2.3 Types of Bonding at the Interface

KTU 2024 explicitly lists **five fundamental bonding mechanisms** that operate (often simultaneously) at the fibre–matrix interface. Their relative magnitudes differ vastly, and the design of commercial fibre sizings aims to maximise the appropriate combination.

### 2.3.1 Mechanical Bonding
* Arises from **physical interlocking** of the matrix with surface roughness, pores, or re-entrant features of the fibre.
* Governed by surface topography: a rough fibre provides more mechanical keying than a smooth one.
* Typical contribution: **moderate**, independent of chemistry.
* *Example:* Untreated carbon fibre vs. plasma-etched carbon fibre.

### 2.3.2 Chemical Bonding
* Strongest mechanism: formation of **covalent, ionic, or coordinate bonds** across the interface.
* Requires reactive functional groups (e.g., –OH, –COOH) on the fibre surface and complementary groups on the matrix.
* Typical bond energy: **200 – 1000 kJ/mol** (an order of magnitude higher than physical bonds).
* *Example:* Silane coupling agents on glass fibre form Si–O–Si bridges between fibre and epoxy.

### 2.3.3 Physical (Van der Waals) Bonding
* Arises from **London dispersion forces, Keesom (dipole–dipole) forces, and Debye (induced dipole) forces**.
* Always present at every interface, but **weak individually** (≈ 0.5 – 40 kJ/mol).
* Becomes significant only when the contact area is large, i.e., when wettability is excellent.
* *Example:* Polymer matrix on clean, smooth carbon fibre without chemical functionalisation.

### 2.3.4 Electrostatic Bonding
* Originates from **transfer of electrons** between the fibre and matrix when their work functions (Fermi levels) differ, creating a charged double layer.
* The resulting **Coulombic attraction** across the interface contributes to the shear strength.
* Typical contribution: **small to moderate**, often only a few MPa of interfacial shear strength.
* *Example:* Carbon fibre in a polymer matrix.

### 2.3.5 Interdiffusion / Interphasial Bonding
* Polymer chains (or atomic species) from the matrix **penetrate and entangle** with the fibre surface region, forming a graded **interphase** of finite thickness (5 – 50 nm).
* Governed by **reptation and mutual diffusion** of macromolecular chains.
* Typical contribution: **moderate**, depends on processing time and temperature.
* *Example:* Thermoplastic matrices on aramid fibres; metal–metal diffusion bonds in MMCs.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Condition | Units | Practical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Young's Equation | $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ | mJ/m² | Predict contact angle from surface energies |
| 2 | Young–Dupré Work of Adhesion | $W_A = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$ | mJ/m² | Quantify thermodynamic adhesion |
| 3 | Work of Adhesion (Dupré form) | $W_A = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL}$ | mJ/m² | Alternative form from interfacial energies |
| 4 | Spreading Coefficient | $S = \gamma_{SV} - (\gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}) = \gamma_{LV}(\cos\theta - 1)$ | mJ/m² | Test if complete wetting occurs |
| 5 | Critical Wetting Condition (Zisman) | $\gamma_{LV} \leq \gamma_c$ | mJ/m² | Matrix selection rule |
| 6 | Adhesion Tension | $A = \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}$ | mJ/m² | Capillary pressure driver |
| 7 | Interfacial Shear Strength (Kelly–Tyson) | $\tau_i = \dfrac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \cdot l_c}$ | MPa | Critical fibre length calculation |
| 8 | Critical Fibre Length | $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \cdot \tau_i}$ | m | Minimum fibre length for full load transfer |
| 9 | Capillary Pressure | $P_c = \dfrac{2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{r}$ | Pa | Drives resin into fibre tows |
| 10 | Bond Energy Ratio (Chemical/Physical) | ≈ 10 to 50 × | dimensionless | Justifies use of coupling agents |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU answers, always quote the full equation, define every symbol, and state the units.** Examiners deduct marks if θ is called "contact angle" without specifying it is the **thermodynamic equilibrium contact angle** measured inside the liquid.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Aerospace (carbon/epoxy):** Wettability testing of prepreg tow is a mandatory QC test; poor wetting → microvoids → delamination under fatigue.
* **Automotive (glass/polyester SMC):** Silane coupling agents (chemical bonding) turn a wettable interface into a chemically bonded one, doubling the transverse flexural strength.
* **Biomedical (hydroxyapatite/PEEK):** Plasma treatment improves wettability of PEEK to enable bone-cell adhesion on implants.
* **Civil (steel-reinforced concrete):** Mechanical + chemical bonding between rebar ribs and cement paste is a direct analogue of fibre–matrix interfacial bonding.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Young's Equation from Force Balance

Consider a sessile liquid droplet resting on an ideal, smooth, rigid, horizontal solid substrate. Three interfaces meet at the **three-phase contact line**: solid–vapour (S–V), solid–liquid (S–L), and liquid–vapour (L–V). Each interface exerts a **tangential surface tension force per unit length** of the contact line.

**Step 1 — Define the forces per unit length of contact line:**
* $\gamma_{LV}$ acts along the liquid–vapour interface, tangent to the droplet surface.
* $\gamma_{SV}$ acts along the solid surface, pulling the contact line outward (away from the droplet).
* $\gamma_{SL}$ acts along the solid–liquid interface, pulling the contact line inward (toward the liquid).

**Step 2 — Resolve γ_LV into components along the solid surface (horizontal):**
The contact angle θ is conventionally measured **inside the liquid** between the tangent to the L–V interface and the solid surface.

* Horizontal component of $\gamma_{LV}$ = $\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ (pulls contact line outward, away from droplet centre).
* Vertical component of $\gamma_{LV}$ = $\gamma_{LV}\sin\theta$ (balanced by elastic reaction of the solid; ignored for rigid fibres).

**Step 3 — Apply horizontal equilibrium (ΣF_x = 0) at the contact line:**

The outward pull from the dry solid surface must balance the inward pull of the wet solid surface plus the horizontal pull of the liquid surface:

$$\gamma_{SV} \;=\; \gamma_{SL} \;+\; \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$

This is **Young's equation**, with no approximations other than a smooth, rigid, homogeneous, non-deformable solid.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Young–Dupré Work of Adhesion

**Step 1 — Definition of W_A:**
The **thermodynamic work of adhesion**, $W_A$, is the reversible work per unit area required to separate two condensed phases originally in contact, creating two new surfaces (each of unit area) in their own vapour.

* Creating the new solid–vapour surface costs energy = $\gamma_{SV}$ per unit area.
* Creating the new liquid–vapour surface costs energy = $\gamma_{LV}$ per unit area.
* Destroying the old solid–liquid interface recovers energy = $\gamma_{SL}$ per unit area.

**Step 2 — Energy balance for the separation process:**

$$W_A \;=\; \gamma_{SV} \;+\; \gamma_{LV} \;-\; \gamma_{SL}$$

This is the **Dupré equation** (1869).

**Step 3 — Eliminate γ_SL using Young's equation:**

From Young's equation, $\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$.

Substitute into Dupré's equation:

$$W_A \;=\; \gamma_{SV} \;+\; \gamma_{LV} \;-\; (\gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta)$$

The γ_SV terms cancel, leaving the celebrated **Young–Dupré equation**:

$$W_A \;=\; \gamma_{LV}\,(1 + \cos\theta)$$

**Step 4 — Limiting-case checks:**

* When $\theta = 0°$ (complete wetting): $W_A = 2\gamma_{LV}$. The maximum work of adhesion equals **twice the surface tension of the liquid**.
* When $\theta = 90°$: $W_A = \gamma_{LV}$.
* When $\theta = 180°$ (complete non-wetting): $W_A = 0$. No thermodynamic adhesion at all.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Critical Fibre Length

The **critical fibre length ($l_c$)** is the minimum length a fibre must have so that the maximum fibre stress ($\sigma_f$) at its centre can reach the fibre ultimate tensile strength before the interface shears off.

**Step 1 — Force balance on a fibre fragment of length $l$ embedded in matrix:**
The tensile load carried by the fibre fragment, $\pi (d/2)^2 \sigma_f$, is transferred across the cylindrical interface of area $\pi d l$ via interfacial shear stress $\tau_i$.

$$\pi \frac{d^2}{4} \sigma_f \;=\; \pi d\, l\, \tau_i$$

**Step 2 — Solve for fibre length $l$:**

$$l \;=\; \frac{\sigma_f \, d}{4 \tau_i}$$

**Step 3 — The fragment reaches the fibre's ultimate strength $\sigma_{fu}$ when $l = l_c$:**

$$l_c \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{fu} \, d}{4 \tau_i}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Many KTU textbooks and answer keys (including the canonical Kelly–Tyson model with a parabolic shear distribution) write the **factor 4 in the denominator as 2**, i.e., $l_c = \sigma_{fu} d / (2\tau_i)$. Both forms appear; the **factor-4 form assumes uniform shear** (conservative estimate) and the **factor-2 form assumes a perfectly plastic shear distribution**. State your assumption explicitly in the KTU exam.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

A glass fibre of diameter **d = 10 μm** and ultimate tensile strength **σ_fu = 3.5 GPa** is embedded in an epoxy matrix. A single-fibre pull-out test gives an interfacial shear strength **τ_i = 35 MPa**. Calculate the critical fibre length.

**Step 1 — Identify given data:**

$$d = 10 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}, \quad \sigma_{fu} = 3.5 \times 10^{9}\,\text{Pa}, \quad \tau_i = 35 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the uniform-shear formula:**

$$l_c \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{4 \tau_i}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numbers:**

$$l_c \;=\; \frac{(3.5 \times 10^{9}) \cdot (10 \times 10^{-6})}{4 \cdot (35 \times 10^{6})}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the numerator and denominator separately:**

$$\text{Numerator} = 3.5 \times 10^{9} \times 10 \times 10^{-6} = 3.5 \times 10^{4}\,\text{N/m}$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 4 \times 35 \times 10^{6} = 1.4 \times 10^{8}\,\text{N/m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Final division:**

$$l_c \;=\; \frac{3.5 \times 10^{4}}{1.4 \times 10^{8}} \;=\; 2.5 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m} \;=\; 0.25\,\text{mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** The critical length is only **0.25 mm**, while real glass fibres are typically several mm to several cm long. Hence the **aspect ratio (l/d) is far greater than the critical value**, and the fibres are efficiently loaded to near their ultimate strength in the composite.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Bonding Types

| Bonding Type | Origin | Typical Bond Energy (kJ/mol) | Range of Action | Role in Composites |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mechanical | Surface roughness & keying | 0 (geometric, not energetic) | Up to surface features (~μm) | Contributes to sliding friction and pull-out toughening |
| Chemical (covalent/ionic) | Electron sharing / transfer | 200 – 1000 | 0.1 – 0.3 nm | Dominant strength contributor when present |
| Physical (van der Waals) | Induced & permanent dipoles | 0.5 – 40 | 0.2 – 1 nm | Always present, dominant in unreactive systems |
| Electrostatic | Charge transfer / double layer | 5 – 20 | 0.5 – 5 nm | Small but measurable in carbon–polymer systems |
| Interdiffusion | Chain entanglement / atomic mixing | 10 – 50 | 5 – 50 nm | Creates the interphase, enables thermoplastic bonding |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Composite Interface

The diagram below shows the **functional interaction architecture** of the fibre–matrix interface, illustrating the interrelationships between wettability, contact angle, bonding types, and the resulting mechanical performance.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[FIBRE PHASE\nReinforcement] --> nodeB[Interface Region\nFew nm to few um]
    nodeC[MATRIX PHASE\nContinuous Binder] --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeD[STRESS TRANSFER\nMECHANISM]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Load Carried by Fibre]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Matrix Cracking Control]
    nodeB --> nodeG[BONDING MECHANISMS]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Mechanical\nInterlocking]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Chemical\nCovalent or Ionic]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[Physical\nvan der Waals]
    nodeG --> nodeK[Electrostatic\nCharge Transfer]
    nodeG --> nodeL[Interdiffusion\nChain Entanglement]
    nodeB --> nodeM[WETTABILITY CHECK]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Contact Angle Theta]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Work of Adhesion WA]
    nodeO --> nodeP[INTERPHASE QUALITY]
    nodeP --> nodeQ[Composite Strength]
    nodeP --> nodeR[Composite Toughness]
    nodeH --> nodeQ
    nodeI --> nodeQ
    nodeJ --> nodeQ
    nodeK --> nodeQ
    nodeL --> nodeQ
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow for Wettability and Bonding Selection

This flowchart maps the engineering decision logic a designer uses to assess whether a given fibre–matrix combination will form a usable composite interface.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[START: Select Fibre and Matrix] --> nodeB[Measure Gamma LV of Matrix\nand Gamma SV of Fibre]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Is Gamma SV greater than Gamma LV?}
    nodeC -- NO --> nodeD[Use Surface Treatment\nPlasma or Chemical Etching]
    nodeC -- YES --> nodeE[Measure Contact Angle Theta]
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF{Is Theta less than 90 degrees?}
    nodeF -- NO --> nodeG[Apply Coupling Agent\nSilane or Titanate]
    nodeF -- YES --> nodeH[Calculate Work of Adhesion WA]
    nodeG --> nodeE
    nodeH --> nodeI{Is WA greater than 50 mJ per m2?}
    nodeI -- NO --> nodeJ[Reject Pair OR Redesign Sizing]
    nodeI -- YES --> nodeK[Compute Critical Fibre Length lc]
    nodeK --> nodeL{Is Fibre Aspect Ratio above lc per d?}
    nodeL -- NO --> nodeM[Use Longer Fibres or Higher Tau]
    nodeL -- YES --> nodeN[APPROVED INTERFACE\nProceed to Composite Fabrication]
```

## 4.3 Five Bonding Types — Architecture Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[FIBRE SURFACE] --> nodeM[INTERPHASE]
    nodeM --> nodeB[MATRIX]
    nodeA -. Mechanical Keying .-> nodeM
    nodeA -. Covalent or Ionic Bridge .-> nodeM
    nodeA -. van der Waals Layer .-> nodeM
    nodeA -. Electrostatic Double Layer .-> nodeM
    nodeA -. Chain Interdiffusion Zone .-> nodeM
    nodeM -. Strong Interface Brittle .-> nodeB
    nodeM -. Weak Interface Tough .-> nodeB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering design tension:** A *strong* interface (dominated by chemical bonding) maximises stiffness and strength but produces **brittle** composites. A *weak* interface (dominated by mechanical and physical bonding with some debonding) reduces stiffness slightly but dramatically increases **fracture toughness** through fibre pull-out. Real composites are designed with an **optimal intermediate bond strength** that balances both extremes — a key reason that **coupling agents are formulated as dilute, controllable monolayers** rather than thick glue layers.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]

**Define the term *contact angle* in the context of fibre–matrix wettability. What is its significance in composite fabrication?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 per logical unit):**

1. **Definition (1 mark):** The contact angle **θ** is the angle measured *through the liquid* between the tangent to the liquid–vapour interface and the solid surface at the three-phase contact line, under thermodynamic equilibrium.
2. **Wettability indicator (1 mark):** A small θ (< 90°) indicates good wettability, meaning the matrix resin spreads and makes intimate contact with the fibre; a large θ (> 90°) indicates poor wettability and a tendency to form voids.
3. **Significance in fabrication (1 mark):** Adequate wettability is a prerequisite for void-free processing, efficient stress transfer, and high mechanical performance of the composite.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**List any *three* types of bonding mechanisms that operate at the fibre–matrix interface in a composite. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 per mechanism):**

1. **Chemical bonding (1 mark):** Covalent or ionic bonds across the interface; *e.g.*, silane coupling agent on E-glass reacting with epoxy's epoxide group.
2. **Mechanical bonding (1 mark):** Physical interlocking due to fibre surface roughness; *e.g.*, plasma-roughened carbon fibre in epoxy.
3. **Physical (van der Waals) bonding (1 mark):** London dispersion and dipole forces; *e.g.*, untreated polyethylene fibre in a polyurethane matrix.

*(Acceptable alternatives: electrostatic bonding, interdiffusion bonding — student may substitute any of the five types with a relevant example.)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> * Students most commonly lose marks by (a) writing Young's equation without defining the three surface energies, (b) forgetting that contact angle is measured **inside the liquid**, (c) confusing **work of adhesion** $W_A$ with **work of cohesion**, and (d) stating the critical fibre length formula without specifying the **shear-distribution assumption** (uniform vs perfectly plastic). Always state assumptions and define all symbols.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Derive **Young's equation** for the equilibrium contact angle of a liquid on a solid surface. Clearly define every term. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A droplet of epoxy resin (γ_LV = 42 mJ/m²) is placed on a glass fibre surface. If the contact angle is **30°**, calculate the **(i)** work of adhesion, **(ii)** the solid–liquid interfacial energy γ_SL (given γ_SV = 60 mJ/m²), and **(iii)** the spreading coefficient. Comment on whether complete wetting is possible. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to A(a) — Derivation of Young's Equation (7 Marks)

1. **Setup and free-body description (2 marks):**
   * Draw a sessile liquid droplet on a flat solid surface. Identify the three interfaces: S–V, S–L, and L–V. Label contact angle θ inside the liquid.
   * State that each interface exerts a tangential force per unit length of contact line equal to its surface tension: γ_SV, γ_SL, γ_LV.

2. **Force resolution (2 marks):**
   * Resolve γ_LV into horizontal component γ_LV cos θ and vertical component γ_LV sin θ.
   * State that the vertical component is balanced by the solid's elastic reaction (neglected for rigid fibre).

3. **Horizontal equilibrium (2 marks):**
   * Apply ΣF_x = 0: outward γ_SV equals inward γ_SL plus outward γ_LV cos θ.

   $$\gamma_{SV} \;=\; \gamma_{SL} \;+\; \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$

4. **Conclusion (1 mark):**
   * State the conditions of validity: smooth, rigid, homogeneous solid; absence of line tension; system at thermodynamic equilibrium.

---

#### Model Solution to A(b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:** $\gamma_{LV} = 42\,\text{mJ/m}^2$, $\theta = 30°$, $\gamma_{SV} = 60\,\text{mJ/m}^2$.

**(i) Work of Adhesion (2 marks):**

$$W_A \;=\; \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta) \;=\; 42\,(1 + \cos 30°)$$

$$\cos 30° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866$$

$$W_A = 42 \times (1 + 0.866) = 42 \times 1.866 \approx 78.4\,\text{mJ/m}^2$$

**(ii) Interfacial Energy γ_SL (3 marks):**

From Young's equation: $\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$

$$\gamma_{SL} = 60 - (42 \times 0.866) = 60 - 36.4 = 23.6\,\text{mJ/m}^2$$

**(iii) Spreading Coefficient and Comment (2 marks):**

$$S \;=\; \gamma_{SV} - (\gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}) = 60 - (23.6 + 42) = -5.6\,\text{mJ/m}^2$$

Since **S < 0**, the resin will **not completely wet** the fibre; it will form a finite contact angle (consistent with the given θ = 30°, which is small but not zero). However, the work of adhesion 78.4 mJ/m² is well above the 50 mJ/m² design threshold, so the resulting bond is still strong and acceptable for structural composite applications.

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **five major types of bonding mechanisms** that can operate at the fibre–matrix interface in fibre-reinforced composites, with one example of each. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A unidirectional glass-fibre/epoxy composite contains fibres of diameter **15 μm** and ultimate tensile strength **2.8 GPa**. The interfacial shear strength measured from a single-fibre fragmentation test is **30 MPa**.
   (i) Calculate the **critical fibre length** $l_c$ using the uniform-shear assumption.
   (ii) If the actual fibre length is **3 mm**, what is the **aspect ratio (l/d)**, and is it sufficient for full fibre loading?
   (iii) Explain, with a sketch in words, what happens when a fibre shorter than $l_c$ is loaded. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to B(a) — Five Bonding Types (7 Marks, 1.4 per type)

1. **Mechanical bonding (1.4 marks):** Physical interlocking of the matrix with surface asperities, pores, or re-entrant features of the fibre. Example: plasma-etched carbon fibre in epoxy.

2. **Chemical bonding (1.4 marks):** Covalent, ionic, or coordinate bonds formed across the interface between reactive functional groups on the fibre and matrix. Example: silane coupling agent forming Si–O–Si bridges on E-glass with epoxy.

3. **Physical / van der Waals bonding (1.4 marks):** Universal weak attractive forces (London dispersion, Keesom dipole, Debye induced dipole). Example: untreated UHMWPE fibre in polypropylene matrix.

4. **Electrostatic bonding (1.4 marks):** Charge transfer between fibre and matrix when Fermi levels differ, creating a Coulombic double layer. Example: carbon fibre in epoxy.

5. **Interdiffusion / interphasial bonding (1.4 marks):** Penetration and entanglement of polymer chains or diffusion of atoms across the boundary, creating a graded interphase. Example: thermoplastic PEEK matrix on aramid fibre.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given:** $d = 15 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$, $\sigma_{fu} = 2.8 \times 10^9\,\text{Pa}$, $\tau_i = 30 \times 10^6\,\text{Pa}$, $l = 3 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$.

**(i) Critical Fibre Length (3 marks):**

Using the uniform-shear model:

$$l_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \cdot d}{4 \tau_i} = \frac{2.8 \times 10^9 \times 15 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \times 30 \times 10^6}$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 2.8 \times 10^9 \times 15 \times 10^{-6} = 4.2 \times 10^4\,\text{N/m}$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 1.2 \times 10^8\,\text{N/m}^2$$

$$l_c = \frac{4.2 \times 10^4}{1.2 \times 10^8} = 3.5 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m} = 0.35\,\text{mm}$$

**(ii) Aspect Ratio and Loading Check (2 marks):**

$$\frac{l}{d} = \frac{3 \times 10^{-3}}{15 \times 10^{-6}} = 200$$

$$\frac{l_c}{d} = \frac{3.5 \times 10^{-4}}{15 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 23.3$$

Since $l/d = 200 \gg l_c/d = 23.3$, the fibre is **far longer than the critical length**, so the fibre will be **fully loaded to near its ultimate strength** at its centre. Load transfer is efficient.

**(iii) Behaviour of a Sub-Critical Fibre (2 marks):**

If $l < l_c$, the interfacial shear surface is not large enough to develop the full fibre stress σ_fu at the centre. The maximum stress reached in the fibre is:

$$\sigma_{f,\max} = \frac{4 \tau_i l}{d} \;<\; \sigma_{fu}$$

The fibre pulls out of the matrix rather than fracturing. The composite fails by **fibre pull-out** at a much lower stress than the fibre's intrinsic strength, and the reinforcement efficiency drops sharply.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — keep this mental map for the KTU exam hall.**

* **Interface vs. Interphase:** Interface = 2-D boundary; Interphase = 3-D finite-thickness region with graded properties.
* **Wettability prerequisite:** Without adequate wetting, no bonding — even strong chemical bonds cannot form across a void.
* **Contact angle rule:** Small θ ⇒ good wetting ⇒ high work of adhesion. Threshold for structural composites: θ < 30° ideally.
* **Young's equation:** $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ — must be quoted with **all three terms defined**.
* **Young–Dupré equation:** $W_A = \gamma_{LV}(1+\cos\theta)$ — direct measure of thermodynamic adhesion.
* **Spreading coefficient:** $S = \gamma_{LV}(\cos\theta - 1) \leq 0$ always for finite θ.
* **Critical surface energy rule (Zisman):** $\gamma_{LV} \leq \gamma_c$ (of fibre) for complete wetting.
* **Five bonding types (memorise order):** Mechanical, Chemical, Physical (vdW), Electrostatic, Interdiffusion.
* **Chemical bonds are ~10–50× stronger than physical bonds** — justifies the use of coupling agents.
* **Critical fibre length (uniform shear):** $l_c = \sigma_{fu} d / (4\tau_i)$; (Kelly–Tyson plastic shear): $l_c = \sigma_{fu} d / (2\tau_i)$. **Always state the assumption.**
* **Aspect ratio rule:** Real composite design demands $l/d \gg l_c/d$ (typically by a factor of ≥ 5) for full fibre utilisation.
* **Strong vs. weak interface trade-off:** Strong interface → high strength, low toughness; weak interface → high toughness, lower stiffness. **Real design = optimum intermediate.**
* **Coupling agents** (e.g., silanes) convert a physical-bonded interface into a chemically bonded one, raising both strength and moisture resistance.
* **KTU 2024 marking weightage tip:** A complete answer for a derivation question must include (1) free-body diagram description, (2) force balance, (3) algebraic rearrangement, (4) final equation, and (5) statement of validity conditions. Omitting any one costs a full mark.
* **Units to quote on the answer sheet:** Surface energies in mJ/m², contact angles in degrees, stresses in MPa, lengths in mm or μm.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fibers: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET413 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Fibers: Introduction # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fibers in Composites:** A **fiber** is a discrete, elongated reinforcement material characterized by a high **aspect ratio** (length-to-diameter ratio, typically $l/d \geq 100$) and a length much greater than its cross-sectional dimension. Fibers serve as the primary **load-bearing constituent** in a fiber-reinforced composite (FRC), transferring stress from the weaker matrix phase through the **fiber-matrix interface**. In the context of **PEMET413 – Composite Materials** (KTU 2024 Scheme), fibers are classified as the **reinforcement phase** of a composite system. They are the principal contributor to the **stiffness** and **strength** of the composite, while the matrix holds them in position, transfers load, and protects them from environmental damage. > [!NOTE] > **Why fibers, not particles?** A particle has roughly equal dimensions in all directions (aspect ratio $\approx 1$). When loaded, a particle cannot efficiently bridge cracks. A fiber, because of its extreme length, can carry tensile loads along its longitudinal axis at stress levels often **5× to 50× higher** than the bulk material of the same composition. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners) Imagine a **bunch of uncooked spaghetti** dropped into a bowl of wet cement. The cement (matrix) is weak in tension — it cracks easily when pulled. But once it hardens around the spaghetti (fibers), any attempt to pull the block apart lengthwise forces the load to travel *through* the long strands. Each strand acts like a tiny bridge, refusing to let the crack open. - The **spaghetti** = fiber (high tensile strength along its length) - The **cement** = matrix (brittle, holds fibers together, protects them) - The **block of cement-spaghetti** = composite (stronger than cement alone, tougher than spaghetti alone) This is the fundamental idea behind every fiber-reinforced composite — from a fiberglass boat hull to a carbon-fiber aircraft wing. --- ## 1.3 Why Fibers? The Engineering Motivation | Driver | Explanation | |---|---| | **High specific strength** | Strength divided by density (e.g., carbon fiber: ~**2000 MPa·cm³/g**) | | **High specific modulus** | Stiffness per unit weight, critical for aerospace | | **Anisotropic tailoring** | Properties can be directed along fiber orientation | | **Crack bridging** | Fibers bridge cracks, preventing catastrophic failure | | **Tailorable performance** | Changing fiber type/volume fraction tunes properties | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric (KTU 2024 High-Yield):** The **specific strength** of carbon fiber (~**2450 kN·m/kg**) is roughly **10× that of structural steel** (~**250 kN·m/kg**). This single fact is a frequent **2-mark question** in KTU exams. --- ## 1.4 The Role of Fibers in the Composite Triad A composite material is fundamentally a **three-phase system**: $$ \text{Composite} = \underbrace{\text{Fiber}}_{\text{Reinforcement}} + \underbrace{\text{Matrix}}_{\text{Binder}} + \underbrace{\text{Interface}}_{\text{Stress Transfer Zone}} $$ - **Fiber** → provides strength and stiffness - **Matrix** → distributes load, provides shape, protects fiber - **Interface (Interphase)** → the engineered chemical/physical bond between fiber and matrix (often via a **sizing** or **coupling agent**) Without effective stress transfer at the interface, even the strongest fiber is useless — like a rope with no knot tying it to the load. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 General Characteristics of a Reinforcement Fiber A "good" engineering fiber must satisfy several physical and mechanical criteria simultaneously. The most important are listed below in order of KTU exam relevance. ### 2.1.1 Geometric Characteristics - **Diameter ($d_f$):** Typically **5 µm to 20 µm** for most polymer/glass/carbon fibers. Smaller diameters mean fewer flaws per unit length (following **Griffith's flaw theory**). - **Length ($l_f$):** Can be continuous (rovings, tows) or discontinuous (chopped strands). - **Aspect Ratio ($l/d$):** The single most important geometric parameter. - $l/d < 100$ → **short fiber** (used in injection-molded thermoplastics) - $l/d \geq 100$ → **long / continuous fiber** (used in aerospace laminates) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Fiber Length ($l_c$):** The minimum fiber length required to build up sufficient interfacial shear stress to fracture the fiber under tensile load. Below $l_c$, the fiber pulls out instead of breaking. $$ l_c = \frac{\sigma_f \cdot d_f}{2 \tau_i} $$ where: - $\sigma_f$ = ultimate tensile strength of the fiber - $d_f$ = fiber diameter - $\tau_i$ = interfacial (matrix–fiber) shear strength ### 2.1.2 Mechanical Characteristics - **Tensile Strength ($\sigma_f$):** Maximum longitudinal tensile stress the fiber can sustain (units: **MPa** or **GPa**). - **Young's Modulus ($E_f$):** Stiffness along the fiber axis (units: **GPa**). - **Elongation at Break ($\epsilon_f$):** Strain at fracture, usually **1% – 5%** for brittle fibers, **>20%** for aramid. - **Density ($\rho_f$):** Determines **specific** properties (important in aerospace). ### 2.1.3 Thermal & Chemical Characteristics - **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE, $\alpha_f$):** Mismatch with matrix $\alpha_m$ causes residual thermal stresses. - **Thermal stability:** Maximum service temperature before degradation. - **Chemical compatibility:** Must not react adversely with the matrix. --- ## 2.2 Classification of Fibers (KTU High-Yield Taxonomy) Fibers are classified along **three orthogonal axes** — examiners love asking this as a 3-mark or 7-mark question. ### Axis 1: By Origin | Category | Examples | |---|---| | **Natural fibers** | Jute, flax, hemp, sisal, coir, cotton, silk | | **Synthetic / Man-made fibers** | Glass, carbon, aramid, boron, ceramic (Al₂O₃, SiC) | ### Axis 2: By Material Family | Category | Examples | |---|---| | **Organic fibers** | Aramid (Kevlar, Nomex), UHMWPE (Dyneema), natural fibers | | **Inorganic fibers** | Glass (E, S), carbon, boron, ceramic (SiC, Al₂O₃) | ### Axis 3: By Length / Form | Category | Form | Typical Application | |---|---|---| | **Continuous fibers** | Rovings, tows, unidirectional tapes | Aerospace primary structures | | **Woven fibers** | Plain, twill, satin fabrics | Marine, automotive panels | | **Chopped / Short fibers** | 3 mm – 50 mm strands | Injection-molded components | | **Mats** | Randomly oriented chopped strands | Low-load panels, insulation | --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Equations for the KTU 2024 ESE — Module 1** | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \, d_f}{2 \tau_i}$ | Critical fiber length for efficient reinforcement | | 2 | $\text{Aspect Ratio} = \dfrac{l}{d}$ | Determines short vs. long fiber regime | | 3 | $\sigma_{c,\text{long}} = \sigma_f \, V_f + \sigma_m \, (1-V_f)$ | **Rule of Mixtures** (longitudinal strength) | | 4 | $E_{c,\text{long}} = E_f \, V_f + E_m \, (1-V_f)$ | **Rule of Mixtures** (longitudinal modulus) | | 5 | $\text{Specific Strength} = \dfrac{\sigma_f}{\rho_f}$ | Strength per unit weight (aerospace metric) | | 6 | $\text{Specific Modulus} = \dfrac{E_f}{\rho_f}$ | Stiffness per unit weight | | 7 | $V_f + V_m = 1$ | Volume fraction conservation | | 8 | $V_{f,\text{crit}} = \dfrac{\sigma_{mu} - \sigma_m}{\sigma_f - \sigma_m}$ | Minimum fiber volume fraction for reinforcement | > [!NOTE] > All **Rule of Mixtures** equations assume: (i) fibers are continuous, (ii) perfectly aligned with the load, (iii) perfect bonding, (iv) both phases experience the **same strain** (isostrain / Voigt model). --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Fiber of Choice | Why | |---|---|---| | **Aerospace (primary structure)** | Carbon fiber (IM/HS) | Ultra-high specific strength and stiffness | | **Aircraft interior** | Aramid (Kevlar) | Flame retardant, low smoke | | **Wind turbine blades** | E-glass | Cost-effective, good fatigue resistance | | **Marine / boat hulls** | E-glass | Corrosion resistance, low cost | | **Racing car monocoques** | Carbon fiber (HM) | Extreme stiffness-to-weight ratio | | **Automotive door panels** | Natural fiber (flax) | Sustainability, acoustic damping | | **Bullet-proof vests** | Aramid (Kevlar) / UHMWPE | High energy absorption | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section provides the **complete derivations** that KTU examiners expect in Part B answers. The two highest-weight derivations for Module 1 are: 1. **Rule of Mixtures for longitudinal modulus** ($E_{c,\text{long}}$) 2. **Critical fiber length** ($l_c$) --- ## 3.1 Derivation: Rule of Mixtures for Longitudinal Modulus ### Assumptions (state explicitly in exam answers) 1. Fibers are **continuous** and **uniformly distributed** in the matrix. 2. Fibers are **perfectly aligned** parallel to the loading direction. 3. **Perfect bonding** exists at the fiber–matrix interface (no slip). 4. Both fiber and matrix experience the **same longitudinal strain** ($\epsilon_c = \epsilon_f = \epsilon_m$) — this is the **isostrain (Voigt) condition**. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Load equilibrium:** The total load carried by the composite is the sum of loads carried by the fiber and the matrix: $$ P_c = P_f + P_m $$ **Step 2 — Express load in terms of stress and area:** Using $P = \sigma \cdot A$ for each phase: $$ \sigma_c \, A_c = \sigma_f \, A_f + \sigma_m \, A_m $$ **Step 3 — Introduce volume fractions:** Dividing both sides by the total area $A_c$: $$ \sigma_c = \sigma_f \left(\frac{A_f}{A_c}\right) + \sigma_m \left(\frac{A_m}{A_c}\right) = \sigma_f \, V_f + \sigma_m \, V_m $$ where $V_f = A_f/A_c$ and $V_m = A_m/A_c$ are the **volume fractions** of fiber and matrix. **Step 4 — Apply Hooke's Law under isostrain:** Since $\epsilon_c = \epsilon_f = \epsilon_m = \epsilon$, we have $\sigma_i = E_i \, \epsilon$ for each phase: $$ \sigma_c = E_f \, \epsilon \, V_f + E_m \, \epsilon \, V_m = \epsilon \, (E_f V_f + E_m V_m) $$ But also $\sigma_c = E_c \, \epsilon$. Equating: $$ E_c \, \epsilon = \epsilon \, (E_f V_f + E_m V_m) $$ **Step 5 — Final Result (Rule of Mixtures):** $$ \boxed{\,E_{c,\text{long}} = E_f \, V_f + E_m \, (1 - V_f)\,} $$ with $V_m = 1 - V_f$. > [!NOTE] > The strength version, $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m (1-V_f)$, is valid only at the **strain at fiber fracture**. Beyond that point, the rule needs modification. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Critical Fiber Length ($l_c$) ### Physical Setup Consider a **short, discontinuous fiber** embedded in a matrix, loaded in tension along the fiber axis. Because the load is transferred into the fiber through **shear** at the cylindrical fiber–matrix interface, the tensile stress in the fiber builds up from **zero at the fiber ends** to a **maximum at the fiber mid-point**. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Force balance on a half-fiber:** Take half the fiber (from end to mid-point). The tensile load at the mid-point must equal the shear force transferred through the interface of that half: $$ \sigma_f \left(\frac{\pi d_f^2}{4}\right) = \tau_i \left(\pi d_f \cdot \frac{l}{2}\right) $$ - Left side = tensile force in the fiber cross-section at the mid-point - Right side = shear force over the cylindrical interface area of half the fiber **Step 2 — Simplify the equation:** Both sides contain $\pi \, d_f$, which cancels: $$ \sigma_f \, \frac{d_f}{4} = \tau_i \, \frac{l}{2} $$ **Step 3 — Solve for $l$:** $$ l = \frac{\sigma_f \, d_f}{2 \, \tau_i} $$ This is the **minimum length** for the fiber tensile stress to build up to $\sigma_f$ (its ultimate strength). For shorter fibers, the maximum fiber stress never reaches $\sigma_f$, and the fiber **pulls out** instead of fracturing. **Step 4 — Final Result (Critical Fiber Length):** $$ \boxed{\,l_c = \frac{\sigma_f \, d_f}{2 \, \tau_i}\,} $$ ### Analytical Condition $$ \begin{aligned} \text{If } l \geq l_c &\Longrightarrow \text{Fiber fractures (efficient reinforcement)} \\ \text{If } l < l_c &\Longrightarrow \text{Fiber pulls out (inefficient reinforcement)} \end{aligned} $$ ### Typical Numerical Example (KTU-style) > **Given:** E-glass fiber with $\sigma_f = 2400 \text{ MPa}$, $d_f = 10 \, \mu\text{m}$, and epoxy matrix with $\tau_i = 50 \text{ MPa}$. **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} l_c &= \frac{\sigma_f \, d_f}{2 \tau_i} \\ &= \frac{(2400 \times 10^6) \times (10 \times 10^{-6})}{2 \times (50 \times 10^6)} \\ &= \frac{24}{100} \\ &= 0.24 \text{ mm} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** Fibers shorter than **0.24 mm** will pull out; longer fibers will fracture. This is why chopped E-glass for injection molding is typically supplied in lengths of **3–12 mm** — well above $l_c$. --- ## 3.3 Comparison Matrix of Major Engineering Fibers | Property | E-Glass | S-Glass | Carbon (HS) | Carbon (HM) | Aramid (Kevlar 49) | Boron | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Density, $\rho$ (g/cm³)** | 2.55 | 2.49 | 1.75 | 1.85 | 1.44 | 2.60 | | **Tensile Strength, $\sigma_f$ (MPa)** | 2400 | 3450 | 3500–5000 | 2400–3000 | 3000 | 3500 | | **Young's Modulus, $E_f$ (GPa)** | 72 | 86 | 230–250 | 350–450 | 125 | 400 | | **Elongation, $\epsilon_f$ (%)** | 4.5 | 5.0 | 1.5–2.0 | 0.5–0.8 | 2.5–3.5 | 1.0 | | **Specific Strength (kN·m/kg)** | 940 | 1380 | 2200–2850 | 1300–1620 | 2080 | 1340 | | **Specific Modulus (MN·m/kg)** | 28 | 35 | 130–143 | 190–245 | 87 | 154 | | **Cost (relative)** | Low | Medium | High | Very High | High | Very High | | **Max Service Temp (°C)** | 550 | 650 | 600 | 600 | 250 | 600 | | **Typical Use** | Marine, wind | Aerospace | Aircraft | Space | Ballistic, ropes | Military | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics The diagrams below are rendered using **Mermaid**. They follow the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE safeguards: all node IDs are alphanumeric (prefixed with letters) and labels contain no markdown formatting. --- ## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Fibers (Module 1 Map) ```mermaid graph TD nodeA["FIBERS USED IN COMPOSITES"] --> nodeB["NATURAL FIBERS"] nodeA --> nodeC["SYNTHETIC MAN MADE FIBERS"] nodeA --> nodeD["BY LENGTH FORM"] nodeA --> nodeE["BY MATERIAL FAMILY"] nodeB --> nodeB1["PLANT BASED
JUTE FLAX HEMP SISAL COIR COTTON"] nodeB --> nodeB2["ANIMAL BASED
SILK WOOL HAIR"] nodeC --> nodeC1["GLASS FIBERS

ON COMPOSITE"] --> nodeTrf["STRESS TRANSFER
THROUGH MATRIX
SHEAR AT INTERFACE"] nodeTrf --> nodeFiber["TENSILE STRESS
BUILDS UP IN FIBER
