# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Define Composite Material and Explain its Structure. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A composite material is a heterogeneous material system composed of **two or more distinct phases** — a **matrix** (continuous phase) and a **reinforcement** (dispersed phase) — combined at the macroscopic level to achieve properties superior to those of the individual constituents.

The **structure** of a composite refers to the geometric arrangement, distribution, volume fraction, and orientation of these phases, including the **interfacial region** between them. The structure is described by the three-phase model:

1. **Matrix phase** — binds reinforcement, transfers load, protects fibers, and provides surface finish.
2. **Reinforcement phase** — the principal load-bearing constituent, available in fibrous, particulate, or whisker form.
3. **Interface phase** — the boundary zone enabling stress transfer between matrix and reinforcement.

The structure of a composite may be classified as **fibrous, particulate, or laminated**, depending on the geometry of the reinforcement.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for three-phase model, 1 mark for classification — typical KTU marking scheme.)*

---

### Question A2: Differentiate Between Fibrous and Particulate Composite Structures. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Fibrous Composite | Particulate Composite |
|---|---|---|
| Reinforcement shape | Fibers with high aspect ratio ($l/d > 10$) | Equiaxed or near-equiaxed particles ($l/d \approx 1$) |
| Load transfer | Mainly along fiber axis | Multi-directional (isotropic in plane) |
| Anisotropy | Highly anisotropic | Quasi-isotropic |
| Strength efficiency | Very high (rule of mixtures applies in fiber direction) | Moderate (load shared by many particles) |
| Examples | Carbon fiber/epoxy, Glass fiber/polyester | SiC/Al (MMC), WC/Co (cermets) |

*(1 mark per major distinguishing feature; typical 3×1 = 3 marking.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation:** Part B questions of 14 marks carry internal choice — students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each worth 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Structure, Phases, and Density of a Composite**

`[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **three-phase structural model** of a composite material. Describe the role and function of each phase in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A unidirectional carbon-fiber reinforced epoxy composite has a **fiber volume fraction of 0.55**. Given the density of carbon fiber is **1.80 g/cc** and epoxy is **1.25 g/cc**, calculate: (i) density of the composite, (ii) weight fraction of fiber, (iii) weight fraction of matrix, and (iv) the theoretical void content if the experimentally measured density is **1.45 g/cc**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Three-Phase Structural Model (7 Marks):**

A composite material is structurally composed of **three phases**:

1. **Matrix Phase (Continuous Phase) — 2 marks:**
   The matrix is the **continuous** phase that surrounds and binds the reinforcement. Its functions are:
   - Holds the reinforcement in the desired position and geometry.
   - Transfers applied external load to the reinforcement through interfacial shear stresses.
   - Protects the reinforcement from environmental attack (moisture, chemicals, abrasion).
   - Provides the final shape, surface finish, and determines the upper service temperature limit.
   - Common matrices: **Epoxy, Polyester, PEEK** (polymer); **Aluminum, Titanium** (metal); **Al₂O₃, SiC** (ceramic).

2. **Reinforcement Phase (Dispersed Phase) — 2 marks:**
   The reinforcement is the **discontinuous, stiffer, and stronger** phase. Its functions are:
   - Carries the **major portion of the mechanical load** (especially in fibrous composites).
   - Imparts its high stiffness and strength properties to the composite.
   - Available in three structural forms:
     - **Fibrous:** Continuous fibers (e.g., carbon, glass, aramid) or short fibers.
     - **Particulate:** Equiaxed particles (e.g., SiC, Al₂O₃).
     - **Whiskers:** Single-crystal short fibers (e.g., SiC whiskers).

3. **Interface / Interphase Phase — 2 marks:**
   The interface is the **thin boundary region** (1–100 nm) between matrix and reinforcement where load is transferred. Bonding mechanisms include:
   - **Chemical bonding** (covalent/ionic).
   - **Mechanical interlocking** due to fiber surface roughness.
   - **Van der Waals forces** (weak physical bonding).
   - **Sizing/coupling agents** (e.g., silane on glass fibers) improve wetting and bonding.
   The interface is **critical** — too strong makes the composite brittle, too weak causes fiber pull-out and loss of stiffness.

4. **Neat Schematic — 1 mark:**

```
[Draw a block diagram showing three phases:]
+-----------------------+
|   Matrix (continuous) |
|  ~~  ~~~~~~~~~~  ~~   |
|  ||  fiber  ||  ~~    |
|  ||  ||||  ||  ~~     |
|  ||  ||||  ||         |
|  ~~  ~~~~~~~~~~       |
|   [== Interface ==]   |
+-----------------------+
```

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $V_f = 0.55$, $V_m = 1 - 0.55 = 0.45$
- $\rho_f = 1.80$ g/cc, $\rho_m = 1.25$ g/cc
- $\rho_{ac} = 1.45$ g/cc (actual experimental)

**(i) Composite Density (theoretical) — 2 marks:**

$$ \rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m $$

$$ \rho_c = (1.80)(0.55) + (1.25)(0.45) $$

$$ \rho_c = 0.990 + 0.5625 $$

$$ \boxed{\rho_c = 1.5525 \text{ g/cc}} $$

**(ii) Weight Fraction of Fiber — 2 marks:**

$$ W_f = \frac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_c} = \frac{(1.80)(0.55)}{1.5525} $$

$$ W_f = \frac{0.990}{1.5525} $$

$$ \boxed{W_f = 0.6377 \text{ or } 63.77\%} $$

**(iii) Weight Fraction of Matrix — 1 mark:**

$$ W_m = 1 - W_f = 1 - 0.6377 $$

$$ \boxed{W_m = 0.3623 \text{ or } 36.23\%} $$

**(iv) Void Content — 2 marks:**

$$ V_v = \frac{\rho_{th} - \rho_{ac}}{\rho_{th}} $$

$$ V_v = \frac{1.5525 - 1.45}{1.5525} = \frac{0.1025}{1.5525} $$

$$ \boxed{V_v = 0.0660 \text{ or } 6.60\%} $$

This is **unacceptably high** — the industry standard for void content in structural composites is $V_v < 1\%$. This indicates a **manufacturing defect** (e.g., poor vacuum bagging, trapped air).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: Classification, Volume-Weight Relations, and Packing**

`[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Classify composite materials based on the **type of reinforcement structure**. Explain each class with suitable examples and a representative diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A glass-fiber reinforced polyester composite has a fiber weight fraction of **0.45**. The density of glass fiber is **2.50 g/cc** and polyester is **1.30 g/cc**. Determine: (i) the densities ratio required to compute $V_f$, (ii) the **volume fraction of fibers**, (iii) the **volume fraction of matrix**, and (iv) the **mass of fibers** in a composite sample of volume **250 cm³**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Classification by Reinforcement Structure (7 Marks):**

| Class | Description | Sub-types | Examples | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Fibrous Composites** | Reinforcement is in fiber form with high aspect ratio | Continuous, short, whisker | CFRP, GFRP, Aramid/epoxy | 2 |
| **Particulate Composites** | Reinforcement is in equiaxed particle form | Random, aligned, platelet | SiC/Al (MMC), WC/Co cermets, mica/PP | 2 |
| **Laminated Composites** | Two or more layers (laminae) of different composites stacked | Bilayer, multilayer, sandwich | Cross-ply CFRP, plywood, GFRP boat hull | 2 |
| **Neat diagram** | Block diagram of each class | — | — | 1 |

(Detailed explanations of each class with examples are essential for full marks.)

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $W_f = 0.45$, $W_m = 0.55$
- $\rho_f = 2.50$ g/cc, $\rho_m = 1.30$ g/cc
- $V_c = 250$ cm³

**(i) Density Ratio (preparation step) — 1 mark:**

We first need composite density from reciprocal ROM:

$$ \frac{1}{\rho_c} = \frac{W_f}{\rho_f} + \frac{W_m}{\rho_m} = \frac{0.45}{2.50} + \frac{0.55}{1.30} $$

$$ \frac{1}{\rho_c} = 0.180 + 0.4231 = 0.6031 \text{ cc/g} $$

$$ \rho_c = \frac{1}{0.6031} = 1.6580 \text{ g/cc} $$

**(ii) Volume Fraction of Fibers — 2 marks:**

Using $W_f = \dfrac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_c}$:

$$ V_f = \frac{W_f \cdot \rho_c}{\rho_f} = \frac{(0.45)(1.6580)}{2.50} $$

$$ V_f = \frac{0.7461}{2.50} $$

$$ \boxed{V_f = 0.2984 \text{ or } 29.84\%} $$

**(iii) Volume Fraction of Matrix — 1 mark:**

$$ V_m = 1 - V_f = 1 - 0.2984 $$

$$ \boxed{V_m = 0.7016 \text{ or } 70.16\%} $$

**(iv) Mass of Fibers in 250 cm³ Sample — 3 marks:**

**Step 1:** Total mass of composite:

$$ m_c = \rho_c \cdot V_c = (1.6580 \text{ g/cc})(250 \text{ cm}^3) $$

$$ m_c = 414.50 \text{ g} $$

**Step 2:** Mass of fibers:

$$ m_f = W_f \cdot m_c = (0.45)(414.50) $$

$$ \boxed{m_f = 186.53 \text{ g}} $$

**Verification (alternative route):**

$$ m_f = \rho_f \cdot V_f \cdot V_c = (2.50)(0.2984)(250) = 186.50 \text{ g} \quad \checkmark $$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up $V_f$ (absolute volume) with $V_f$ (volume fraction):** In KTU notation, $V_f$ in the formula $V_f + V_m = 1$ refers to the *dimensionless volume fraction*, NOT the absolute volume. Always check context.
> 2. **Forgetting unit consistency:** Use g/cc and cm³ (or kg/m³ and m³). Never mix SI and CGS in the same problem.
> 3. **Wrong void content formula:** $V_v = \dfrac{\rho_{th} - \rho_{ac}}{\rho_{th}}$, NOT $\dfrac{\rho_{ac}}{\rho_{th}}$. Many students invert the numerator.
> 4. **Skipping the verification step:** Always check that $V_f + V_m = 1$ AND $W_f + W_m = 1$ at the end. KTU examiners award bonus partial credit for verified answers.
> 5. **In Part A answers:** Writing only the definition without the three-phase model loses 2 of 3 marks. Always include the **matrix + reinforcement + interface** breakdown.
> 6. **In Part B (a) answers:** Omitting the diagram costs 1 full mark out of 7. Always draw a **neat labeled schematic**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Structure of Composites**

### 🔑 Core Concepts (Must Memorize)

- [x] A composite consists of **THREE** phases: **Matrix + Reinforcement + Interface** (MFI mnemonic).
- [x] The **matrix** is continuous; the **reinforcement** is dispersed.
- [x] Composite structure is classified as **Fibrous / Particulate / Laminated**.
- [x] Fibrous composites may have **continuous, short, or whisker** fibers.
- [x] **Volume fractions sum to 1:** $V_f + V_m + V_v = 1$.
- [x] **Weight fractions sum to 1:** $W_f + W_m = 1$.
- [x] **Rule of Mixtures (ROM) for density** is **exact** for ALL composite types: $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$.
- [x] **Reciprocal ROM for density** uses weight fractions: $\dfrac{1}{\rho_c} = \dfrac{W_f}{\rho_f} + \dfrac{W_m}{\rho_m}$.
- [x] **Experimental $V_f$ formula:** $V_f = \dfrac{\rho_c - \rho_m}{\rho_f - \rho_m}$.
- [x] **Void content formula:** $V_v = \dfrac{\rho_{th} - \rho_{ac}}{\rho_{th}}$.
- [x] **Packing fractions:** Square = 0.785, Hexagonal = 0.907.

### 🎯 High-Yield Exam Topics (KTU 2024)

- [x] Derivation and application of the **Rule of Mixtures** for density.
- [x] **Conversion between volume and weight fractions** (numerical problems).
- [x] **Void content calculation** from experimental data.
- [x] **Three-phase structural model** with functions of each phase.
- [x] **Classification of composites** by reinforcement type with examples.
- [x] **Interface/Interphase** role and bonding mechanisms.
- [x] Difference between **fibrous vs. particulate** composite structures.

### 🧠 Quick-Reference Conversion Table

| From → To | Formula |
|---|---|
| $V_f \to W_f$ | $W_f = \dfrac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m}$ |
| $W_f \to V_f$ | $V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_m / \rho_m}$ |
| $\rho_c$ from $V_f$ | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)$ |
| $\rho_c$ from $W_f$ | $\dfrac{1}{\rho_c} = \dfrac{W_f}{\rho_f} + \dfrac{W_m}{\rho_m}$ |
| $V_f$ from $\rho_c$ | $V_f = \dfrac{\rho_c - \rho_m}{\rho_f - \rho_m}$ |

### 📚 Typical KTU Question Patterns

- **3-mark question:** "Define composite and explain its structure" → Cover MFI model + one classification.
- **7-mark sub-question:** "Calculate $V_f$, $W_f$, $\rho_c$" → Use ROM and conversion formulas step-by-step.
- **7-mark sub-question:** "Explain three-phase model with diagram" → Use MFI breakdown + labeled block diagram.
- **7-mark sub-question:** "Determine void content" → Use $V_v = \dfrac{\rho_{th} - \rho_{ac}}{\rho_{th}}$ with interpretation.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When solving numerical problems, **always**:
> 1. Write the **Given** data.
> 2. State the **Formula** to be used.
> 3. Show **Substitution** of values.
> 4. Present the **Final boxed answer** with correct units.
> 5. Add a **Verification** step (e.g., check $V_f + V_m = 1$).
>
> This 5-step format aligns with the KTU 2024 Scheme valuation key and typically earns full or near-full marks.

---

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET413 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Properties and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PEMET413 – Composite Materials (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1)**, the term **"Properties and Applications"** refers to the systematic study of how the *combined* characteristics of two or more distinct constituent phases — the **matrix** (continuous phase) and the **reinforcement** (dispersed phase) — govern the macroscopic mechanical, physical, thermal, chemical, and electrical response of a heterogeneous engineered material, and how this tailored response is exploited in real engineering structures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1 anchor):**
> A **composite material** is a heterogeneous, multiphase engineering material formed by the *synergistic combination* of two or more chemically distinct constituents, with a recognizable interface separating them, designed to exhibit a *weighted* combination of the *best properties* of the components. The properties of the composite are **not** the arithmetic average of its constituents; they follow **rule-of-mixtures** relationships (when aligned/continuous) or are dictated by **micromechanical load-transfer** mechanisms (when discontinuous).

### 1.1.1 Why "Properties" Demand a Dedicated Study Block

Unlike monolithic metals, ceramics, or polymers, a composite offers the engineer a **designable property space**. By changing any one of the following, the macroscopic property changes:

- Reinforcement type (fiber, particulate, laminar)
- Reinforcement material (carbon, glass, aramid, boron, ceramic)
- Reinforcement geometry (aspect ratio, volume fraction, orientation, stacking sequence)
- Matrix material (polymer, metal, ceramic — i.e., PMC, MMC, CMC)
- Interface chemistry (sizing, coupling agents)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU CO Mapping:** This topic directly satisfies **CO1** — *Understand the fundamental classifications, constituents, properties, and applications of composite materials.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a **concrete slab** to span the roof of a building. Plain cement is strong in **compression** (you can stack weight on it) but terribly weak in **tension** (it cracks the moment you try to bend it). Now, embed thin steel **rebars** (reinforcement) inside the cement paste (matrix). The result — **reinforced concrete** — is a composite that is strong in *both* compression (cement) and tension (steel).

**The intuition:**

| Concept | Real-World Analogy | Engineering Translation |
|---|---|---|
| Reinforcement | Steel rebar in concrete | High-strength, high-stiffness phase (load bearer) |
| Matrix | Cement paste | Continuous binder (transfers load, protects fibers) |
| Interface | Bond between rebar and cement | Shear transfer zone (critical region) |
| Composite property | Slab carries bending + weight | Tailored mechanical response |

> [!TIP]
> **Student Takeaway:** Whenever you hear "composite," mentally picture two materials *cooperating* through an interface. The properties you observe (stiffness, strength, density, thermal expansion) are **engineered**, not **inherited**. The applications follow directly from the engineered property envelope.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used to Quantify Composite Properties

The following **bold** constants/metrics will recur throughout this note:

- **Specific Strength (σ/ρ):** Strength-to-weight ratio, measured in **kN·m/kg** or **MPa/(g/cm³)**.
- **Specific Modulus (E/ρ):** Stiffness-to-weight ratio, measured in **MN·m/kg** or **GPa/(g/cm³)**.
- **Fiber Volume Fraction (V_f):** Dimensionless ratio = Volume of fibers / Total volume (0 ≤ V_f ≤ 1).
- **Matrix Volume Fraction (V_m):** V_m = 1 − V_f.
- **Rule of Mixtures (ROM):** Linear weighting law for aligned/continuous-reinforcement properties.
- **Halpin–Tsai Equation:** Modified ROM for *short/discontinuous* and *particulate* reinforcements.
- **Density of Composite (ρ_c):** Typically ranges from **1.5 g/cm³** (glass/epoxy) to **2.0 g/cm³** (carbon/epoxy).
- **Tensile Strength of CFRP:** Typically **1500–4000 MPa**.
- **Young's Modulus of CFRP:** Typically **70–700 GPa** (depending on fiber type/orientation).

---

## 1.4 Learning Roadmap for This Note

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The remainder of this document is sequenced as:
> **(a)** Deep theoretical analysis of *why* composites exhibit their characteristic properties (Section 2).
> **(b)** Full derivation of the **Rule of Mixtures**, **Halpin–Tsai**, and **specific property** relations (Section 3).
> **(c)** Block-level functional architecture of property–structure–application relationships (Section 4).
> **(d)** KTU 2024 Scheme exam-style Part A and Part B questions with model solutions (Section 5).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Composite Properties (KTU 2024 Module-1 Taxonomy)

Composite properties can be classified along **five engineering axes**. Each axis connects directly to an application domain.

### 2.1.1 Mechanical Properties

These are the **most heavily tested** properties in KTU examinations.

**(a) Strength**
- **Tensile strength** (σ_t): Resistance to uniaxial pulling load.
- **Compressive strength** (σ_c): Resistance to crushing load.
- **Flexural strength** (σ_f): Resistance to bending.
- **Interlaminar shear strength (ILSS):** Resistance to delamination between laminae — *critical* for laminated composites.
- **Impact strength:** Energy absorbed before fracture.

**(b) Stiffness**
- **Young's modulus (E):** Resistance to elastic deformation along a given axis.
- **Shear modulus (G):** Resistance to angular distortion.
- **Poisson's ratio (ν):** Lateral strain / axial strain.

**(c) Toughness and Fatigue**
- **Fracture toughness (K_IC):** Resistance to crack propagation — *anisotropic* in composites.
- **Fatigue life (S-N curve):** Composites typically show **superior fatigue resistance** over monolithic metals because cracks are deflected/deflected at fiber-matrix interfaces, dissipating energy.

**(d) Anisotropy**
- Unlike metals, composites are **anisotropic** (properties depend on direction).
- A unidirectional CFRP lamina has **E_L (longitudinal) ≈ 10–20× E_T (transverse)**.
- **Why?** Continuous fibers aligned along the load path carry almost all the stress in the longitudinal direction; transverse loads must be carried almost entirely by the *much weaker* matrix.

### 2.1.2 Physical Properties

| Property | Definition | Typical Composite Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Density (ρ_c) | Mass per unit volume | Lower than most metals (1.5–2.0 g/cm³ for polymer composites) |
| Specific Strength | σ_u / ρ | **Highest for CFRP** ≈ 2.5 × 10⁶ mm |
| Specific Modulus | E / ρ | **Highest for boron/epoxy** and **CFRP** |
| Porosity | Volume fraction of voids | Should be < 2% for aerospace grade |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The single biggest reason composites replaced aluminum in aircraft structures (Boeing 787, Airbus A350) is the **specific strength and specific modulus** advantage — a 20% weight saving at equal stiffness translates directly to fuel savings.

### 2.1.3 Thermal Properties

- **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE, α):** Can be *engineered to zero* (or even negative) by carefully selecting fiber-matrix volume fractions. Critical for **spacecraft and precision optics**.
- **Thermal Conductivity (k):** Polymer composites are generally insulators; adding carbon or metal fibers can tailor k.
- **Glass Transition Temperature (T_g):** Upper service limit for polymer-matrix composites (PMC).
- **Heat Deflection Temperature (HDT):** Practical short-term thermal limit.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** Invar (a metal alloy) has α ≈ 1.2 × 10⁻⁶ /°C. A well-designed carbon/epoxy laminate can have α ≈ **−0.5 × 10⁻⁶ /°C** (i.e., expands *less* than zero when heated — used in telescope mirrors and space structures).

### 2.1.4 Chemical and Environmental Properties

- **Corrosion resistance:** PMC composites are immune to galvanic corrosion and to most acids/alkalis (unlike aluminum or steel). *Critical for marine and chemical-plant applications.*
- **Moisture absorption:** Hygroscopic matrices (e.g., epoxy) absorb 1–3% water → causes **swelling and T_g depression**.
- **UV resistance:** Poor unless protective coatings applied; UV degrades epoxy and polyester matrices.
- **Fire/smoke toxicity:** Aramid (Kevlar) composites are *self-extinguishing* — used in aircraft interiors.

### 2.1.5 Electrical and Tribological Properties

- **Electrical conductivity:** Insulating (glass/epoxy) → conductive (carbon-fiber reinforced). CFRP is used as a structural ground path in Airbus A350.
- **Dielectric constant:** Glass/epoxy is a low-loss dielectric → printed circuit boards.
- **Wear resistance:** Particulate-reinforced MMCs (SiC/Al) outperform cast iron for brake rotors and cylinder liners.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Property — Micromechanical Explanation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU examiners love asking "Why does X happen?" (Bloom's Level 3 — Apply/Analyze).** Below is the conceptual "why" behind each property cluster.

### Step-by-step logic chain:

1. **Load is applied externally** → matrix transfers load to fibers through **shear at the interface**.
2. **Fiber (high modulus) carries most of the load** → contributes high stiffness/strength in the fiber direction.
3. **Matrix (low modulus, ductile) protects fibers**, redistributes stress around broken fibers, and carries transverse/shear loads.
4. **Interface (sizing/coupling agent)** ensures efficient stress transfer; weak interface → premature debonding; too-strong interface → brittle failure.
5. **Resulting property** is a *weighted combination* of fiber-dominated (anisotropic) and matrix-dominated (isotropic-ish) behavior.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Module 1)

> [!WARNING]
> **Strict Markdown Rule:** All vertical bars are rendered as `\vert` to avoid breaking the table layout. **Do NOT use `|` inside any formula cell.**

| # | Formula | Description | Symbol Meanings | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m$ | **Density rule of mixtures** | $\rho_c,\rho_f,\rho_m$ densities; $V_f,V_m$ vol. fractions | kg/m³ |
| 2 | $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | **Longitudinal Young's modulus** (continuous, aligned fibers) | $E_L$ lamina modulus along fiber | Pa (GPa typical) |
| 3 | $\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | **Transverse modulus** (inverse ROM) | $E_T$ transverse modulus | Pa |
| 4 | $\sigma_L = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ | **Longitudinal strength** (iso-strain) | $\sigma_L$ composite strength | Pa |
| 5 | $\sigma_T \approx \sigma_m \left(1 - V_f\right)$ | **Transverse strength** (approx.) | $\sigma_T$ transverse strength | Pa |
| 6 | $\nu_{LT} = \nu_f V_f + \nu_m V_m$ | **Major Poisson's ratio** | $\nu_{LT}$ Poisson's ratio longitudinal-transverse | dimensionless |
| 7 | $G_{LT} = \dfrac{G_m}{\,1 - V_f \left(1 - \dfrac{G_m}{G_f}\right)\,}$ | **In-plane shear modulus** (approx.) | $G_{LT}$ shear modulus in L-T plane | Pa |
| 8 | $\dfrac{E_L}{E_T} \approx \dfrac{E_f}{E_m}$ | **Anisotropy ratio** (for $E_f \gg E_m$) | — | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{Specific Strength} = \dfrac{\sigma_u}{\rho_c}$ | **Strength-to-weight** | $\sigma_u$ ultimate strength | kN·m/kg |
| 10 | $\text{Specific Modulus} = \dfrac{E}{\rho_c}$ | **Stiffness-to-weight** | $E$ Young's modulus | MN·m/kg |
| 11 | $\dfrac{P}{P_c} = V_f \dfrac{P_f}{P_c,f} + V_m \dfrac{P_m}{P_c,m}$ | **General ROM (property P)** | $P$ any linear-additive property | depends on $P$ |
| 12 | $\text{Halpin–Tsai: } \dfrac{E_c}{E_m} = \dfrac{1 + 2\eta V_f}{1 - \eta V_f}$ where $\eta = \dfrac{(E_f/E_m) - 1}{(E_f/E_m) + 2}$ | **Short-fiber/particulate modulus** | $\eta$ geometry factor | dimensionless |
| 13 | $\alpha_L = \dfrac{\alpha_f E_f V_f + \alpha_m E_m V_m}{E_f V_f + E_m V_m}$ | **Longitudinal CTE** (Turner model) | $\alpha_L$ axial CTE | 1/K |
| 14 | $\alpha_T = V_f \alpha_f + V_m \alpha_m$ | **Transverse CTE** (Schapery bound) | $\alpha_T$ transverse CTE | 1/K |
| 15 | $V_f^{crit} = \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_f + \sigma_m - \sigma_f'}$ | **Critical fiber vol. fraction** for strengthening | $\sigma_f'$ fiber stress at composite failure | dimensionless |
| 16 | $\eta_l = \dfrac{\sigma_f - \sigma_{f'}}{\sigma_c}$ | **Fiber efficiency factor** | $\eta_l$ load carried by fibers | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Eye:** The formulas in **rows 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 9, 10, 12, 15** are the *highest-yield* — at least 80% of the numerical problems on this topic test these.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of These Properties

> [!TIP]
> **Bridge to Applications (Section 5 will detail them):**

- **Aerospace:** Specific strength + specific modulus + fatigue life → lighter wings & fuselages (Boeing 787 is 50% composite by mass).
- **Automotive:** Crash energy absorption + stiffness → CFRP monocoque chassis (BMW i3, i8).
- **Marine:** Corrosion resistance + fatigue → hulls, propeller shafts, masts.
- **Civil:** Corrosion resistance + tensile strength → FRP rebars replacing steel in coastal bridges.
- **Sports:** Vibration damping + specific modulus → tennis rackets, F1 chassis, bicycle frames.
- **Energy:** Fatigue + corrosion → wind-turbine blades (GFRP), pressure vessels (CFRP overwrapped).
- **Electronics:** Low CTE + dielectric strength → PCB substrates (glass/epoxy).
- **Medical:** Biocompatibility + specific strength → prosthetics, orthopedic implants.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rule of Mixtures for Longitudinal Modulus (E_L)

**Objective:** Prove that for a unidirectional continuous-fiber composite loaded parallel to the fibers, $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$.

### Assumptions (KTU-standard "ideal composite")

1. Fibers are **continuous, parallel, and uniformly distributed**.
2. Perfect **bonding** at the fiber-matrix interface (no slip).
3. **Iso-strain condition:** Both fiber and matrix experience the *same* axial strain $\varepsilon_L$ (valid because they are bonded and parallel to load).
4. Fibers and matrix are **linearly elastic**.
5. Fibers are **uniformly cylindrical** with circular cross-section.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply iso-strain assumption.**

$$\varepsilon_L = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$$

**Step 2 — Express stresses using Hooke's law.**

$$\sigma_f = E_f \varepsilon_f = E_f \varepsilon_L$$

$$\sigma_m = E_m \varepsilon_m = E_m \varepsilon_L$$

**Step 3 — Apply force equilibrium (load-sharing).**

The total axial force on the composite cross-section equals the sum of forces carried by fiber and matrix:

$$F_c = F_f + F_m$$

**Step 4 — Convert forces to stresses × areas.**

$$\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ and divide by $A_c$.**

$$E_c \varepsilon_L \frac{A_c}{A_c} = E_f \varepsilon_L \frac{A_f}{A_c} + E_m \varepsilon_L \frac{A_m}{A_c}$$

$$E_c = E_f \frac{A_f}{A_c} + E_m \frac{A_m}{A_c}$$

**Step 6 — Recognize area fractions = volume fractions (for uniform prismatic geometry).**

$$\frac{A_f}{A_c} = V_f, \quad \frac{A_m}{A_c} = V_m, \quad V_f + V_m = 1$$

**Step 7 — Final result.**

$$\boxed{E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m}$$

This is the **Rule of Mixtures for longitudinal Young's modulus**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Rule of Mixtures for Longitudinal Strength ($\sigma_L$)

**Objective:** Show that $\sigma_L = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ under iso-strain, and identify when it applies.

### Assumptions

Same as Derivation 1, plus: **fibers and matrix reach ultimate strain simultaneously** (the rare but useful "iso-strain-at-failure" case).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply iso-strain until the *first* constituent reaches its ultimate strain.**

Failure strain of fiber $\varepsilon_{fu}$ and matrix $\varepsilon_{mu}$. Composite fails when *either* reaches its limit.

**Step 2 — Assume fibers fail first** (typical for $V_f > V_{f}^{crit}$). At that moment:

$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_{fu}$$

**Step 3 — Stress in fiber at this instant.**

$$\sigma_f = \sigma_{fu} = E_f \varepsilon_{fu}$$

**Step 4 — Stress in matrix at the same strain** (matrix is still elastic if $\varepsilon_{fu} < \varepsilon_{mu}$).

$$\sigma_m = E_m \varepsilon_{fu}$$

**Step 5 — Composite stress at failure.**

$$\sigma_L^{ult} = \sigma_{fu} V_f + (E_m \varepsilon_{fu}) V_m$$

**Step 6 — General iso-strain composite strength (when both fail at same strain).**

$$\boxed{\sigma_L = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering caveat (KTU often tests this):** If $\varepsilon_{fu} < \varepsilon_{mu}$, fibers fail first, leaving the matrix to carry the entire load briefly. The above formula underestimates the true strength because it ignores matrix load redistribution around broken fibers. A more realistic model includes the **Kelley–Tyson** correction term.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Inverse Rule of Mixtures for Transverse Modulus (E_T)

**Objective:** Derive the iso-stress relation $\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$.

### Assumptions

1. Fibers and matrix experience the **same transverse stress** $\sigma_T$ (iso-stress — valid when fibers are stacked in series in the loading direction).
2. Perfect bonding; linear elasticity.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply iso-stress.**

$$\sigma_T = \sigma_f = \sigma_m$$

**Step 2 — Express strains via Hooke's law.**

$$\varepsilon_f = \frac{\sigma_f}{E_f} = \frac{\sigma_T}{E_f}$$

$$\varepsilon_m = \frac{\sigma_m}{E_m} = \frac{\sigma_T}{E_m}$$

**Step 3 — Total transverse strain by compatibility (strains add in series).**

$$\varepsilon_T = \varepsilon_f \frac{t_f}{t_c} + \varepsilon_m \frac{t_m}{t_c}$$

where $t_f, t_m$ are thickness contributions along the load line.

**Step 4 — Recognize thickness ratios = volume fractions.**

$$\frac{t_f}{t_c} = V_f, \quad \frac{t_m}{t_c} = V_m$$

**Step 5 — Substitute.**

$$\varepsilon_T = \frac{\sigma_T}{E_f} V_f + \frac{\sigma_T}{E_m} V_m = \sigma_T \left( \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to isolate composite modulus $E_T = \sigma_T / \varepsilon_T$.**

$$\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$$

**Step 7 — Final result.**

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}}$$

This is the **Inverse Rule of Mixtures for transverse modulus**.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because $E_f \gg E_m$, the term $V_f/E_f$ becomes very small, and $E_T \approx E_m / V_m$. **This explains why composites are MUCH weaker transversely** — they essentially behave like the matrix in the transverse direction.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Halpin–Tsai Equation (Short-Fiber / Particulate Modulus)

**Objective:** Derive the empirical–semi-analytical form used for *discontinuous* reinforcements.

### Background

The Halpin–Tsai equation is **not** a first-principles derivation; it is a *generalized self-consistent micromechanics model* fitted to the exact elasticity solution of Eshelby and to experimental data. The KTU-accepted form is:

$$\frac{E_c}{E_m} = \frac{1 + 2\eta V_f}{1 - \eta V_f}$$

where the **geometry factor** $\eta$ is defined as:

$$\eta = \frac{\left(\dfrac{E_f}{E_m}\right) - 1}{\left(\dfrac{E_f}{E_m}\right) + 2}$$

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

**Step 1 — Start from the inverse ROM form (iso-stress).**

$$\frac{1}{E_c} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$$

**Step 2 — Introduce the *reinforcement efficiency factor* $\eta$** that accounts for (i) finite aspect ratio, (ii) fiber-matrix stiffness mismatch, (iii) interface conditions.

$$\frac{E_c}{E_m} = \frac{1 + \zeta \eta V_f}{1 - \eta V_f}$$

where $\zeta = 2$ (aspect-ratio scaling) for spherical/particulate reinforcements.

**Step 3 — Solve for $\eta$ by enforcing the limit $V_f \to 1$.**

At $V_f = 1$, the composite should reduce to pure fiber: $E_c = E_f$.

$$\frac{E_f}{E_m} = \frac{1 + \zeta \eta}{1 - \eta}$$

$$\frac{E_f}{E_m}(1 - \eta) = 1 + \zeta \eta$$

$$\frac{E_f}{E_m} - \frac{E_f}{E_m}\eta = 1 + \zeta \eta$$

$$\frac{E_f}{E_m} - 1 = \eta \left(\frac{E_f}{E_m} + \zeta \right)$$

$$\eta = \frac{\dfrac{E_f}{E_m} - 1}{\dfrac{E_f}{E_m} + \zeta}$$

**Step 4 — For $\zeta = 2$ (spherical or low aspect ratio), substitute.**

$$\eta = \frac{\dfrac{E_f}{E_m} - 1}{\dfrac{E_f}{E_m} + 2}$$

**Step 5 — Final Halpin–Tsai equation.**

$$\boxed{\frac{E_c}{E_m} = \frac{1 + 2\eta V_f}{1 - \eta V_f}, \quad \eta = \frac{(E_f/E_m) - 1}{(E_f/E_m) + 2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For long fibers (high aspect ratio $l/d > 100$), $\zeta \to 2(l/d)$**, and the Halpin–Tsai equation reduces to the standard Rule of Mixtures $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Specific Strength and Specific Modulus

**Objective:** Show how specific properties are computed from ROM.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Recall composite density (ROM, additive in volume).**

$$\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m$$

**Step 2 — Specific strength.**

$$SS = \frac{\sigma_L}{\rho_c} = \frac{\sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m}{V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m}$$

**Step 3 — Specific modulus.**

$$SM = \frac{E_L}{\rho_c} = \frac{E_f V_f + E_m V_m}{V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m}$$

**Step 4 — Typical worked evaluation.**

For **CFRP epoxy** with $V_f = 0.6$:

$$\rho_c = (0.6)(1.8) + (0.4)(1.2) = 1.08 + 0.48 = 1.56 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

$$E_L = (0.6)(230) + (0.4)(3.5) = 138 + 1.4 = 139.4 \text{ GPa}$$

$$SM = \frac{139.4}{1.56} \approx 89.4 \text{ GPa/(g/cm}^3\text{)}$$

Compare to **aluminum**: $E = 70$ GPa, $\rho = 2.7$ g/cm³ → $SM \approx 25.9$. **Composite is 3.4× stiffer per unit weight.**

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Property vs. Reinforcement Type

The following table summarizes how each *property* scales with the *type of reinforcement*. This is the **single most important comparative matrix** for KTU Module 1 viva and written exams.

| Property | Continuous Fiber (aligned) | Short Fiber | Particulate | Laminate |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $E_L$ | Highest (ROM) | Moderate (Halpin–Tsai) | Low (≈ matrix) | Depends on stacking |
| $\sigma_L$ | Highest | Moderate | Low | Depends on $0°/90°$ ratio |
| Anisotropy | Extreme | Moderate | Low | Engineered |
| CTE tailoring | Best (can be zero) | Limited | Limited | Best (quasi-iso possible) |
| Impact resistance | Moderate | Better (energy absorption) | Brittle | Best (delamination absorbs) |
| Cost | Highest | Moderate | Lowest | High |
| Formability | Difficult | Easy (extrusion, injection) | Easy (casting) | Layup required |
| Typical application | Aerospace primary structure | Automotive panels | Brake rotors, cutting tools | Boat hulls, wind blades |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** "**C**ontinuous = **C**ompetitive, **S**hort = **S**haped, **P**articulate = **P**ocket-friendly, **L**aminate = **L**ayered logic."

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Property–Structure–Application Mapping

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** (per Section IV safeguard). It avoids complex physical drawings that would fail in Mermaid, and instead maps the *interaction topology* between property drivers, the property itself, and downstream applications.

**How to read this diagram:**

1. **Inputs (blue blocks):** Five independent design variables chosen by the engineer.
2. **Core Engine (orange blocks):** The micromechanical models (ROM, Halpin–Tsai, Eshelby-based) that translate inputs → property.
3. **Property Set (green blocks):** Five engineering axes (mechanical, physical, thermal, chemical, electrical).
4. **Applications (red blocks):** Eight major application domains, each linked to specific property axes.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP, Module 1]**
> Define **Specific Strength** and **Specific Modulus**. Why are these properties preferred over raw strength/modulus when selecting materials for **aerospace** applications? Give one numerical comparison.

**Model Answer:**

- **Specific Strength** is defined as the ultimate tensile strength of a material divided by its density: $SS = \sigma_u / \rho_c$. It quantifies how much load a material can carry *per unit weight*.
- **Specific Modulus** is defined as the Young's modulus divided by the density: $SM = E / \rho_c$. It quantifies the stiffness per unit weight.
- In aerospace, the design driver is *weight minimization* (fuel efficiency, payload capacity). A material with high *raw* strength but also high density may still be undesirable. Specific strength/modulus *normalizes* out weight and gives a fair comparison.
- **Numerical example:** CFRP has $E \approx 140$ GPa, $\rho \approx 1.56$ g/cm³ → $SM \approx 89.4$ GPa·cm³/g. Aluminum 7075 has $E = 71.7$ GPa, $\rho = 2.81$ g/cm³ → $SM \approx 25.5$ GPa·cm³/g. CFRP is **3.5× stiffer per unit weight** — directly explains its dominance in Boeing 787/Airbus A350 wing and fuselage skins.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write "specific strength = strength/weight" without specifying *density* as the denominator. Examiner deducts 1 mark.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP, Module 1]**
> Differentiate between **isostrain** and **isostress** conditions in composite micromechanics. Which condition leads to the **longitudinal rule of mixtures** for modulus?

**Model Answer:**

| Condition | Physical Meaning | Resulting Formula |
|---|---|---|
| **Isostrain** | Fiber and matrix experience the *same* axial strain (valid when fibers are parallel and perfectly bonded to the matrix along the load direction) | $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ — **Longitudinal Modulus (ROM)** |
| **Isostress** | Fiber and matrix experience the *same* stress (valid when fibers are stacked in series along the load direction, e.g., transverse loading) | $\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ — **Transverse Modulus (Inverse ROM)** |

The **isostrain condition** gives the longitudinal rule of mixtures because in a unidirectional composite, the fiber and matrix are *parallel* to the load, so by compatibility they share the same strain.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Do NOT write "longitudinal = inverse ROM." Examiners will award 0 marks for that swap.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU 2024 Scheme — Module Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following.]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**

> **(a)** Derive the **Rule of Mixtures** for the **longitudinal Young's modulus** of a unidirectional continuous-fiber composite. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A unidirectional glass-fiber reinforced epoxy composite contains **$V_f = 0.45$** of E-glass fibers. Given: $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³, $\sigma_{fu} = 2200$ MPa, $\sigma_{mu} = 80$ MPa. Calculate:
>     (i) Longitudinal Young's modulus $E_L$
>     (ii) Composite density $\rho_c$
>     (iii) Specific modulus $E_L / \rho_c$
>     (iv) Longitudinal tensile strength $\sigma_L$ using the iso-strain rule of mixtures. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

1. State **assumptions** (1 mark): continuous, parallel, aligned, perfectly bonded, iso-strain, linearly elastic, uniform fibers.
2. Apply iso-strain: $\varepsilon_L = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$ (1 mark).
3. Apply Hooke's law: $\sigma_f = E_f \varepsilon_L$, $\sigma_m = E_m \varepsilon_L$ (1 mark).
4. Force equilibrium: $F_c = F_f + F_m$ (1 mark).
5. Express in stresses × areas: $\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$ (1 mark).
6. Recognize $A_i/A_c = V_i$ (1 mark).
7. Final boxed equation: $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ (1 mark).

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Longitudinal Young's modulus $E_L$:**

$$E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m = (72)(0.45) + (3.5)(0.55)$$

$$E_L = 32.4 + 1.925 = 34.325 \text{ GPa}$$

**(ii) Composite density $\rho_c$:**

$$\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m = (0.45)(2.55) + (0.55)(1.20)$$

$$\rho_c = 1.1475 + 0.660 = 1.8075 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**(iii) Specific modulus:**

$$SM = \frac{E_L}{\rho_c} = \frac{34.325}{1.8075} \approx 18.99 \text{ GPa·cm}^3/\text{g}$$

**(iv) Longitudinal tensile strength $\sigma_L$:**

$$\sigma_L = \sigma_{fu} V_f + \sigma_{mu} V_m = (2200)(0.45) + (80)(0.55)$$

$$\sigma_L = 990 + 44 = 1034 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Common mistake — using the *inverse* ROM formula for $E_L$ in part (i). This yields $E_L = 5.65$ GPa (a 6× under-estimate). Full 2 marks lost. Always confirm: longitudinal → *parallel* loading → *iso-strain* → direct ROM.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**

> **(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **three failure modes** observed in a unidirectional composite loaded along the fiber direction. How does each affect the longitudinal strength? **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A short-fiber glass/epoxy composite has $V_f = 0.30$, $l/d = 25$ (aspect ratio), $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa. Using the **Halpin–Tsai equation**, estimate the composite modulus $E_c$. Compare with the simple rule-of-mixtures (ROM) prediction. Which is more realistic for *short* fibers and why? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Failure modes (7 marks):**

Three failure modes in a unidirectional composite under longitudinal loading:

1. **Fiber Brittle Fracture (most common, $V_f > V_f^{crit}$):** Fibers break first when their tensile strength is reached. Composite fails abruptly. This is the *primary* design failure mode for CFRP and GFRP with adequate $V_f$. (2 marks)

2. **Matrix Yielding / Shear Failure ($V_f < V_f^{crit}$):** If $V_f$ is too low, the matrix reaches its yield/shear strength *before* fibers can be fully loaded. Composite fails in a ductile manner with low overall strength. (2 marks)

3. **Fiber–Matrix Interface Debonding (interfacial failure):** When the shear stress at the interface exceeds the interfacial shear strength (IFSS), debonding occurs. Fibers lose load transfer and "pull out." This is usually *designed* to some extent because pure interfacial failure absorbs energy (improves toughness). (2 marks)

A neat labelled sketch of each mode (showing crack path and fiber/matrix behavior) earns the remaining 1 mark.

**Part (b) — Halpin–Tsai vs ROM (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute stiffness ratio.**

$$\frac{E_f}{E_m} = \frac{72}{3.5} \approx 20.571$$

**Step 2 — Compute the geometry factor $\zeta$ for short fibers.**

For short fibers, $\zeta = 2(l/d) = 2(25) = 50$.

**Step 3 — Compute $\eta$.**

$$\eta = \frac{(E_f/E_m) - 1}{(E_f/E_m) + \zeta} = \frac{20.571 - 1}{20.571 + 50} = \frac{19.571}{70.571} \approx 0.2774$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Halpin–Tsai equation.**

$$\frac{E_c}{E_m} = \frac{1 + \zeta \eta V_f}{1 - \eta V_f} = \frac{1 + (50)(0.2774)(0.30)}{1 - (0.2774)(0.30)}$$

$$\frac{E_c}{E_m} = \frac{1 + 4.161}{1 - 0.0832} = \frac{5.161}{0.9168} \approx 5.629$$

$$E_c = 5.629 \times 3.5 \approx 19.70 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 5 — ROM (continuous fiber, iso-strain) prediction.**

$$E_{ROM} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m = (72)(0.30) + (3.5)(0.70) = 21.6 + 2.45 = 24.05 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 6 — Comparison.**

- ROM predicts $E_c \approx 24.05$ GPa (assumes continuous, fully aligned, perfect bonding).
- Halpin–Tsai predicts $E_c \approx 19.70$ GPa (accounts for finite aspect ratio $l/d = 25$).
- **Halpin–Tsai is more realistic for short fibers** because it incorporates the reduction in load-transfer efficiency due to the limited fiber length. Short fibers cannot develop the full stress that continuous fibers can, so the effective modulus is *lower* than the ROM prediction.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often skip showing the value of $\zeta = 2(l/d)$ for short fibers, jumping directly into the spherical-particulate formula $\zeta = 2$. This loses 2 marks. **Always check if the question specifies $l/d$ ratio.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Memory Bank):**

### Definitions to Memorize
- **Composite:** Heterogeneous, multiphase engineered material with a recognizable interface.
- **Matrix:** Continuous phase; protects, binds, redistributes load.
- **Reinforcement:** Dispersed phase; primary load bearer.
- **Interface:** Stress-transfer zone (critical region).
- **Specific Strength:** $\sigma_u / \rho_c$.
- **Specific Modulus:** $E / \rho_c$.
- **Volume Fraction $V_f$:** Phase proportion by volume.
- **Critical $V_f$:** Minimum fiber volume for composite to be stronger than matrix alone.
- **Anisotropy:** Direction-dependent properties (very high in unidirectional composites).

### Top 5 Formulae to Memorize (verbatim)
1. $\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m$
2. $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ (iso-strain, continuous aligned fiber)
3. $\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ (iso-stress, transverse loading)
4. $SS = \dfrac{\sigma_L}{\rho_c}$, $\quad SM = \dfrac{E_L}{\rho_c}$
5. Halpin–Tsai: $\dfrac{E_c}{E_m} = \dfrac{1 + 2\eta V_f}{1 - \eta V_f}$, $\eta = \dfrac{(E_f/E_m)-1}{(E_f/E_m)+2}$

### Anisotropy Rule of Thumb
- For $E_f \gg E_m$: $E_L / E_T \approx E_f / E_m$ (often 10–50× for CFRP).

### Five Critical Application-Axioms
1. **Aerospace** → Specific strength + specific modulus (CFRP wings, fuselage).
2. **Marine** → Corrosion resistance + fatigue (GFRP hulls).
3. **Automotive** → Crash energy absorption + weight (CFRP monocoque).
4. **Civil** → Corrosion resistance + tensile strength (FRP rebars).
5. **Space** → Near-zero CTE (carbon/epoxy laminates).

### Property Hierarchy (for exam answers)
- Mechanical: strength > stiffness > toughness > fatigue life (typical KTU priority).
- For aerospace: specific strength > specific modulus > CTE > corrosion resistance.
- For automotive: impact > specific modulus > formability > cost.

### Common Mistakes to Avoid
- ❌ Confusing $E_L$ (longitudinal) with $E_T$ (transverse) formulae.
- ❌ Using simple ROM for short fibers (use Halpin–Tsai).
- ❌ Forgetting units consistency (MPa vs GPa; g/cm³ vs kg/m³).
- ❌ Writing "specific strength = strength/weight" — must say *density*.

### Numerical Quick-Check (Sanity Tests)
- $E_L$ should be **between** $E_m$ and $E_f$.
- $\rho_c$ should be **between** $\rho_m$ and $\rho_f$.
- For CFRP, $E_L / \rho_c$ should be **3–5× that of aluminum**.
- Halpin–Tsai prediction ≤ ROM prediction (for finite aspect ratio).

---

> **End of KTU PEMET413 Module 1 — Properties and Applications.**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET413 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Thermoset

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Thermosets

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **thermoset** (thermosetting plastic or thermosetting polymer) is a high-molecular-weight prepolymer in a soft, viscous, liquid, or solid state that changes irreversibly into an infusible, insoluble, cross-linked polymer network upon the application of heat and/or a chemical catalyst (hardener), often combined with pressure. Once cured, thermosets cannot be remelted or reprocessed by heating without undergoing thermal degradation.

In the context of **composite materials (PEMET413)**, thermosets serve as the **matrix phase** that binds reinforcing fibers (glass, carbon, aramid) together, transfers load to the fibers, protects them from environmental attack, and maintains the geometric stability of the structural component.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Thermosets constitute approximately **60–70%** of all commercial polymer matrices used in advanced fiber-reinforced composites (FRP), particularly in aerospace, marine, automotive, and civil infrastructure applications.

## 1.2 Common Thermoset Resins in Composites

| Resin Family | Typical Application | Key Advantage |
|--------------|--------------------|--------------| 
| **Unsaturated Polyester (UPE)** | Marine, automotive, construction | Low cost, ease of processing |
| **Vinyl Ester (VE)** | Chemical tanks, pipes | Superior corrosion resistance |
| **Epoxy (EP)** | Aerospace, sporting goods | High strength, low shrinkage, excellent adhesion |
| **Phenolic (PF)** | Fire-critical interiors, ablatives | Outstanding fire/smoke performance |
| **Polyimide (PI)** | High-temperature aerospace | Service temperatures up to **300–370°C** |
| **Bismaleimide (BMI)** | Military/aerospace hot zones | High Tg (~**260–290°C**), tough |
| **Cyanate Ester (CE)** | Electronics, space structures | Low dielectric loss, low moisture uptake |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Boiled Egg" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a raw egg. The white and yolk are made of long protein chains (analogous to a **thermoplastic** — chains can be melted and reshaped). When you boil the egg, irreversible chemical bonds (cross-links) form between the proteins, turning the liquid into a solid that **cannot be un-boiled**. This boiled egg is a **thermoset** — once cross-linked, it is permanently set, and applying more heat only causes charring (degradation), not softening. In composites, the egg-white "matrix" locks the reinforcing fibers (think of them as tiny embedded straws) into a rigid, load-bearing network.

## 1.4 Why Thermosets Dominate Composite Matrices

- **Low viscosity** in the uncured state → excellent fiber wet-out
- **No volatile by-products** (with epoxy) → low void content
- **Strong covalent bonding** → high mechanical and thermal performance
- **Tailorable cure cycles** → can be processed at room temperature or elevated temperature
- **Excellent chemical resistance** → ideal for harsh service environments
- **Tailorable Tg** (Glass Transition Temperature) from ~80°C to >400°C

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Cross-linking:** A process in which polymer chains are joined together by covalent bonds to form a three-dimensional network, dramatically increasing rigidity, strength, chemical resistance, and thermal stability.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chemistry of Cure (Cross-Linking Reaction)

Thermoset cure is an **exothermic, step-growth or chain-growth addition reaction** between:
- A **resin molecule** containing reactive functional groups (e.g., epoxy rings, C=C double bonds, -OH groups)
- A **hardener/cross-linker** (e.g., amines, anhydrides, peroxides, catalysts)

### 2.1.1 Stages of Cure (Phenomenological Description)

1. **Stage A — Liquid State:** Resin and hardener are mixed; viscosity is low.
2. **Stage B — Gelation:** First infinite molecular network forms (gel point). Beyond this point, the resin loses flow and becomes a rubbery gel.
3. **Stage C — Vitrification:** Glass transition temperature (Tg) rises to equal the cure temperature; molecular motion freezes.
4. **Stage D — Final Cure:** Post-cure at higher temperature drives reaction to completion, maximizing Tg and properties.

## 2.2 Degree of Cure (α) — The Master Variable

The **degree of cure (α)** is the fraction of total reactive functional groups that have reacted:

$$\alpha = \frac{\text{Heat released up to time } t}{\text{Total heat of reaction}}$$

$$\alpha = \frac{H(t)}{H_{\text{total}}}$$

Where:
- $H(t)$ = cumulative exothermic heat released up to time *t* (J/g)
- $H_{\text{total}}$ = total heat of reaction (J/g), typically **300–600 J/g** for epoxies
- $\alpha$ is dimensionless, ranging from **0** (uncured) to **1** (fully cured)

## 2.3 Curing Kinetics (Kamal–Sourour Model)

The widely used phenomenological rate equation for thermoset cure:

$$\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = (k_1 + k_2 \alpha^m)(1-\alpha)^n$$

Where:
- $k_1, k_2$ = rate constants following **Arrhenius behavior** $k = A \exp(-E_a / RT)$
- $m, n$ = reaction order exponents (typically 0.5–2)
- $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

## 2.4 Glass Transition Temperature (Tg) vs Degree of Cure

A key engineering relationship (DiBenedetto equation):

$$\frac{T_g - T_{g0}}{T_{g\infty} - T_{g0}} = \frac{\lambda \alpha}{1 - (1-\lambda)\alpha}$$

Where:
- $T_{g0}$ = Tg of the uncured resin (typically **−20°C to +30°C**)
- $T_{g\infty}$ = Tg of the fully cured network (typically **80–250°C** for structural epoxies)
- $\lambda$ = fitting parameter (~**0.4–0.6** for most epoxies)

## 2.5 Viscosity Behavior During Cure

Viscosity first **decreases** with temperature (Arrhenius-type), reaches a minimum, then **rises sharply** near gelation:

$$\eta(T) = \eta_\infty \exp\left(\frac{E_\eta}{RT}\right)$$

Where:
- $\eta_\infty$ = pre-exponential viscosity (Pa·s)
- $E_\eta$ = flow activation energy (J/mol)
- At the **gel point**, $\eta \to \infty$ in theory; in practice, viscosity reaches **10³–10⁴ Pa·s**.

## 2.6 Storage and Shelf Life

For one-part systems (e.g., prepreg), **Arrhenius shelf-life rule** applies:

$$\log(t_{s1}) - \log(t_{s2}) = \frac{E_a}{2.303\,R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

> **Rule of Thumb:** For every **10°C reduction** in storage temperature, shelf life roughly **doubles**.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation / Parameter | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Degree of cure | $\alpha = H(t) / H_{\text{total}}$ | dimensionless | Cure monitoring (DSC) |
| Kamal–Sourour kinetics | $\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = (k_1 + k_2 \alpha^m)(1-\alpha)^n$ | s⁻¹ | Cure simulation |
| Arrhenius rate constant | $k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$ | s⁻¹ | Temperature dependence |
| DiBenedetto Tg-cure relation | $\frac{T_g - T_{g0}}{T_{g\infty} - T_{g0}} = \frac{\lambda \alpha}{1 - (1-\lambda)\alpha}$ | K | Predict final Tg |
| Viscosity (Arrhenius) | $\eta = \eta_\infty \exp(E_\eta / RT)$ | Pa·s | Resin flow modeling |
| Shelf life (Arrhenius) | $\log(t_{s1}/t_{s2}) = \frac{E_a}{2.303 R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1}-\frac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | hours | Storage life |
| Heat of reaction (epoxy) | $H_{\text{total}} \approx$ **300–600** | J/g | DSC characterization |
| Gel point (Flory) | $p_c = 2 / \bar{f}$ | dimensionless | Network formation theory |
| Ultimate Tg (epoxy) | $T_{g\infty} \approx$ **80–250** | °C | Service temperature limit |
| Cure shrinkage (volumetric) | $\Delta V/V \approx$ **1–7** | % | Residual stress in composite |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** Engineers use the **DiBenedetto equation** to optimize cure cycles in autoclave processing of aerospace composites. By predicting the Tg at any degree of cure, the operator can determine minimum cure temperature to achieve a specified service performance (e.g., $T_g \geq 180°C$ for an aircraft wing skin).

## 2.8 Thermoset vs Thermoplastic — Critical Comparison for KTU

| Property | Thermoset | Thermoplastic |
|---|---|---|
| Cross-linking | Permanent, covalent | None (linear/branched chains) |
| Remeltable | **No** | Yes |
| Creep resistance | Excellent | Poor above Tg |
| Solvent resistance | Excellent | Poor to moderate |
| Initial viscosity | Very low (processable) | High (requires melting) |
| Recyclable | Difficult | Easy |
| Storage stability | Limited (reactive) | Unlimited |
| Typical Tg | **80–400°C** | 60–350°C |
| Fiber impregnation | Easy (RT processing) | Difficult (high melt viscosity) |
| Examples | Epoxy, Polyester, BMI | PEEK, PPS, PA (Nylon) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Degree of Cure from DSC (Differential Scanning Calorimetry) Data

**Problem Setup:** A DSC scan of an epoxy resin undergoing isothermal cure at $T = 160°C$ records the following data. The total heat of reaction is $H_{\text{total}} = 450$ J/g. Calculate the degree of cure at $t = 30$ min, given $H(30) = 280$ J/g.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Write the definition of degree of cure.**

$$\alpha(t) = \frac{H(t)}{H_{\text{total}}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given numerical values.**

$$\alpha(30) = \frac{280 \text{ J/g}}{450 \text{ J/g}}$$

**Step 3 — Perform the division.**

$$\alpha(30) = 0.6222$$

**Step 4 — Express as a percentage.**

$$\alpha(30) = 62.22\%$$

**Interpretation:** At $t = 30$ min, **62.2%** of the reactive groups have been consumed. The resin is past gelation but not yet vitrified.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Arrhenius Shelf-Life Estimation

**Problem Setup:** An epoxy prepreg has a shelf life of **180 days at 25°C** (298 K). The activation energy for the degradation/cure reaction is $E_a = 80$ kJ/mol. Determine the shelf life at **−18°C** (refrigerated storage, 255 K).

**Step 1 — Write the Arrhenius shelf-life equation.**

$$\log(t_{s1}) - \log(t_{s2}) = \frac{E_a}{2.303\,R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Define the known variables.**

- $T_1 = 298$ K, $t_{s1} = 180$ days
- $T_2 = 255$ K, $t_{s2} = ?$
- $E_a = 80{,}000$ J/mol
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

**Step 3 — Compute the temperature term.**

$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{298} - \frac{1}{255}$$

$$= 0.003356 - 0.003922 = -5.66 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the prefactor on the right side.**

$$\frac{E_a}{2.303\,R} = \frac{80{,}000}{2.303 \times 8.314} = \frac{80{,}000}{19.147} = 4178.4 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply.**

$$4178.4 \times (-5.66 \times 10^{-4}) = -2.365$$

**Step 6 — Compute $\log(t_{s1})$.**

$$\log(180) = 2.2553$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $\log(t_{s2})$.**

$$\log(t_{s2}) = 2.2553 - (-2.365) = 2.2553 + 2.365 = 4.6203$$

**Step 8 — Take the antilog.**

$$t_{s2} = 10^{4.6203} = 41{,}686 \text{ days}$$

**Step 9 — Convert to years.**

$$t_{s2} = \frac{41{,}686}{365} \approx 114.2 \text{ years}$$

**Conclusion:** Refrigeration at −18°C extends shelf life from 6 months to over **114 years** — a dramatic extension. This is why aerospace prepregs are stored in freezers.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Predicting Tg from Degree of Cure (DiBenedetto)

**Problem Setup:** An epoxy has $T_{g0} = 10°C$, $T_{g\infty} = 180°C$, and $\lambda = 0.45$. If the degree of cure achieved is $\alpha = 0.85$, calculate the resulting Tg.

**Step 1 — Write the DiBenedetto equation.**

$$\frac{T_g - T_{g0}}{T_{g\infty} - T_{g0}} = \frac{\lambda \alpha}{1 - (1-\lambda)\alpha}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the right-hand side numerator.**

$$\lambda \alpha = 0.45 \times 0.85 = 0.3825$$

**Step 3 — Compute the right-hand side denominator.**

$$1 - (1-\lambda)\alpha = 1 - (0.55)(0.85) = 1 - 0.4675 = 0.5325$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ratio.**

$$\frac{0.3825}{0.5325} = 0.7183$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Tg.**

$$T_g - T_{g0} = 0.7183 \times (T_{g\infty} - T_{g0})$$

$$T_g - 10 = 0.7183 \times (180 - 10)$$

$$T_g - 10 = 0.7183 \times 170 = 122.1$$

$$T_g = 10 + 122.1 = 132.1°C$$

**Conclusion:** At 85% cure, the epoxy Tg is only **132.1°C**, well below the 180°C target. A post-cure is mandatory for high-temperature service.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range (Epoxy) | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heat of reaction | $H_{\text{total}}$ | **300–600 J/g** | Process thermal management |
| Activation energy | $E_a$ | **50–90 kJ/mol** | Cure cycle design |
| Uncured Tg | $T_{g0}$ | **−20 to +30°C** | Storage/handling |
| Fully cured Tg | $T_{g\infty}$ | **80–250°C** | Service ceiling |
| Gelation point (α) | $\alpha_{\text{gel}}$ | **0.2–0.6** | Process window end |
| Cure shrinkage | $\Delta V/V$ | **1–7%** | Residual stress, warpage |
| Exotherm peak | $T_{\text{peak}}$ | **150–250°C** | Thick-part safety |
| Viscosity minimum | $\eta_{\min}$ | **0.1–1 Pa·s** | Resin infusion viability |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Curing Process Flow — From Liquid to Cross-Linked Network

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Resin Monomers Linear Chains] --> B[Mixing with Hardener Liquid Stage]
    B --> C[Stage A: Low Viscosity Molecular Mobility High]
    C --> D[Stage B: Gelation Infinite Network Forms]
    D --> E[Stage C: Vitrification Tg Reaches Cure Temp]
    E --> F[Stage D: Post-Cure Full Network Completion]
    F --> G[Final Thermoset Network Insoluble Infusible]
    H[Application of Heat Catalyst Pressure] --> C
    I[Stage E: Degradation Overheating Exceeds Td] --> J[Charring Decomposition Irreversible]
    G -.Excessive Heat.-> I
    K[Engineering Properties] --> L[High Strength]
    K --> M[Chemical Resistance]
    K --> N[Thermal Stability]
    K --> O[Dimensional Rigidity]
    G --> K

```
