# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a thermoset polymer. Why is it preferred as a matrix material in advanced composites?**

**Model Answer:**
A thermoset is a polymer that undergoes irreversible cross-linking when subjected to heat and/or a chemical catalyst, forming a three-dimensional covalent network that cannot be remelted or reprocessed. It is preferred as a composite matrix because of: (i) low initial viscosity enabling excellent fiber wet-out, (ii) high strength and stiffness after cure, (iii) superior chemical and thermal resistance, (iv) tailorable cure cycles, and (v) ability to be processed at room temperature (RTM) or in autoclaves for aerospace parts.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between thermoset and thermoplastic matrix systems in composites.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Thermoset | Thermoplastic |
|---|---|---|
| Structure | Cross-linked (3D network) | Linear/branched chains |
| Remeltability | Not possible | Possible |
| Initial viscosity | Very low | High (melt required) |
| Creep resistance | Excellent | Poor above Tg |
| Storage/shelf life | Limited | Unlimited |
| Recyclability | Difficult | Easy |
| Typical use | Aerospace, marine | Automotive, consumer |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the **stages of cure** of a thermoset resin from liquid to fully cross-linked state. Discuss how **viscosity** and **Tg (glass transition temperature)** evolve during the process. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An epoxy prepreg has a shelf life of **120 days at 30°C (303 K)**. If the activation energy of the curing reaction is $E_a = 75$ kJ/mol, calculate the shelf life when stored at **−20°C (253 K)**. Comment on the engineering significance. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution:**

#### **Part (a) — Stages of Cure (7 Marks)**

The cure of a thermoset progresses through four distinct phenomenological stages:

1. **Stage A — Liquid State (Uncured):** Resin and hardener are mixed. The system flows easily, with viscosity in the range **0.1–10 Pa·s**. Molecular chains are still linear/branched. No cross-links formed.

2. **Stage B — Gelation:** The **gel point** is reached at a critical degree of cure $\alpha_{\text{gel}}$. An infinite molecular network first appears. The resin transitions from liquid to a **rubbery gel** that can no longer flow. Viscosity rises sharply toward **10³–10⁴ Pa·s**. Beyond this point, the part cannot be reprocessed.

3. **Stage C — Vitrification:** As cross-linking continues, the glass transition temperature Tg rises. When Tg **equals the cure temperature Tc**, molecular mobility is frozen. The resin transforms from a rubber to a **glassy solid**. Reaction kinetics becomes diffusion-controlled and slows dramatically.

4. **Stage D — Final Cure / Post-Cure:** To achieve maximum Tg and full conversion, a **post-cure** at $T > T_g$ is applied, allowing remaining reactive groups to fully cross-link. Final Tg approaches $T_{g\infty}$.

| Stage | Physical State | Viscosity Trend | Tg Trend |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | Liquid | Decreasing with T | Below Tc |
| B | Gelation onset | Sharp rise | Crossing Tc |
| C | Rubbery → Glassy | Effectively infinite | ≈ Tc |
| D | Fully cured glass | Solid | $T_{g\infty}$ |

#### **Part (b) — Shelf Life Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Apply the Arrhenius shelf-life equation:**

$$\log(t_{s1}) - \log(t_{s2}) = \frac{E_a}{2.303\,R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Given values:**
- $T_1 = 303$ K, $t_{s1} = 120$ days
- $T_2 = 253$ K, $t_{s2} = ?$
- $E_a = 75{,}000$ J/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

**Step 3 — Compute the temperature term:**

$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{303} - \frac{1}{253} = 0.003300 - 0.003953 = -6.53 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the energy prefactor:**

$$\frac{E_a}{2.303\,R} = \frac{75{,}000}{19.147} = 3917.0 \text{ K}$$

**Step 5 — Multiply:**

$$3917.0 \times (-6.53 \times 10^{-4}) = -2.558$$

**Step 6 — Compute $\log(t_{s1})$:**

$$\log(120) = 2.0792$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $\log(t_{s2})$:**

$$\log(t_{s2}) = 2.0792 - (-2.558) = 2.0792 + 2.558 = 4.6372$$

**Step 8 — Take the antilog:**

$$t_{s2} = 10^{4.6372} = 43{,}380 \text{ days} \approx 118.8 \text{ years}$$

**Engineering Significance:** Lowering storage temperature from 30°C to −20°C extends shelf life from **120 days to nearly 119 years** — an almost thousand-fold increase. This justifies the industrial practice of **freezer storage (−18°C)** of prepreg materials to prevent premature advancement of cure.

---

### **Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **DiBenedetto equation** relating the glass transition temperature (Tg) of a thermoset to its degree of cure. An epoxy resin has $T_{g0} = 5°C$, $T_{g\infty} = 175°C$, and $\lambda = 0.5$. If the resin is cured to $\alpha = 0.90$, determine the resulting Tg. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the properties, processing characteristics, and typical applications of **epoxy, polyester, and vinyl ester** resins used in fiber-reinforced composites. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution:**

#### **Part (a) — DiBenedetto Equation (7 Marks)**

The **DiBenedetto equation** describes how Tg evolves with the degree of cure in a thermosetting system:

$$\frac{T_g - T_{g0}}{T_{g\infty} - T_{g0}} = \frac{\lambda \alpha}{1 - (1-\lambda)\alpha}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the given values:**
- $T_{g0} = 5°C$, $T_{g\infty} = 175°C$, $\lambda = 0.5$, $\alpha = 0.90$

**Step 2 — Compute the numerator:**

$$\lambda \alpha = 0.5 \times 0.90 = 0.45$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator:**

$$1 - (1-\lambda)\alpha = 1 - (0.5)(0.90) = 1 - 0.45 = 0.55$$

**Step 4 — Compute the ratio:**

$$\frac{0.45}{0.55} = 0.8182$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Tg:**

$$T_g - 5 = 0.8182 \times (175 - 5) = 0.8182 \times 170 = 139.1$$

$$T_g = 5 + 139.1 = 144.1°C$$

**Conclusion:** At 90% cure, the Tg is only **144.1°C** — still significantly below the maximum 175°C. To achieve full Tg, the resin must be post-cured at a temperature above 144°C.

#### **Part (b) — Comparison of Epoxy, Polyester, Vinyl Ester (7 Marks)**

| Property | Epoxy | Polyester (UPE) | Vinyl Ester |
|---|---|---|---|
| Chemistry | Epoxy ring + amine/anhydride | Unsaturated polyester + styrene | Epoxy backbone + methacrylic acid |
| Tensile strength | **High (60–90 MPa)** | Moderate (40–70 MPa) | Moderate-High (50–80 MPa) |
| Cure shrinkage | **Low (1–3%)** | High (5–8%) | Moderate (3–6%) |
| Chemical resistance | Excellent | Good | **Excellent** |
| Viscosity (initial) | **Very low (0.5–5 Pa·s)** | Low (1–3 Pa·s) | Low (1–5 Pa·s) |
| Cost | **High** | Very low | Moderate |
| Service Tg | **80–250°C** | 70–110°C | 100–150°C |
| Fiber adhesion | **Excellent** | Moderate | Good |
| Processing | RTM, autoclave, hand layup | Hand layup, spray-up, RTM | RTM, filament winding |
| Applications | Aerospace, sporting goods, electronics | Marine, automotive, construction | Chemical tanks, pipes, marine |

**Summary:** Epoxy offers the best overall mechanical and thermal performance for high-end composites; polyester is the most economical choice for large-volume non-structural parts; vinyl ester bridges the gap with superior corrosion resistance at moderate cost.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing gelation with vitrification** — Gelation is the formation of an infinite network (no flow), vitrification is when Tg reaches Tc. Students often use these terms interchangeably — **lose 1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting units in Arrhenius calculations** — T must be in **Kelvin**, not Celsius. Activation energy in **J/mol**, not kJ/mol. **Lose 1 mark** for unit errors.
> 3. **In the DiBenedetto equation**, students often use α in % form (e.g., 90) instead of decimal (0.90). The answer comes out **100× wrong**. **Lose 2 marks**.
> 4. **Confusing Tg with melting point** — Thermosets do not have a melting point. They degrade above Td. **Lose 1 mark** if incorrectly stated.
> 5. **Skipping the post-cure step in Tg calculations** — DiBenedetto shows that Tg continues to rise after the initial cure. Always state whether post-cure is required to achieve $T_{g\infty}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **thermoset** is an irreversibly cross-linked polymer — once cured, it **cannot be remelted**.
- ✅ The **degree of cure (α)** is the fraction of reactive groups consumed: $\alpha = H(t)/H_{\text{total}}$.
- ✅ **Gelation** = flow stops; **Vitrification** = Tg reaches cure temperature Tc.
- ✅ The **Kamal–Sourour equation** $\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = (k_1 + k_2\alpha^m)(1-\alpha)^n$ governs cure kinetics.
- ✅ Rate constants follow **Arrhenius** behavior: $k = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$.
- ✅ The **DiBenedetto equation** links Tg with α: Tg grows non-linearly as cure progresses.
- ✅ **Shelf life** is exponentially extended by lowering storage temperature — **Rule: 10°C drop doubles life**.
- ✅ Common thermoset resins in composites: **Epoxy, Polyester, Vinyl Ester, Phenolic, BMI, Polyimide, Cyanate Ester**.
- ✅ Epoxy is the **default aerospace choice**; Polyester is the **default marine/automotive choice**.
- ✅ Cure shrinkage (1–7%) causes **residual stresses** — design compensation required.
- ✅ **Cure cycle design** must avoid the **degradation zone** (above Td) on the TTT diagram.
- ✅ Resin viscosity must be low enough for **fiber wet-out** but high enough to avoid **resin drainage**.
- ✅ **Tg margin rule:** Service temperature should be **at least 20–30°C below Tg**.
- ✅ **Gel point** (Flory): $p_c = 2/\bar{f}$ where $\bar{f}$ is functionality of the monomer.
- ✅ **H_total for epoxy ≈ 300–600 J/g**; **Tg∞ ≈ 80–250°C** for structural epoxies.
- ✅ Thermosets offer **superior creep, chemical, and thermal performance** versus thermoplastics — at the cost of recyclability.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermoplastic and Elastomeric Polymers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET413 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Thermoplastic and Elastomeric Polymers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Polymeric Matrices in Composites

In the realm of **Composite Materials (PEMET413)**, the **matrix** is the continuous phase that binds the reinforcement (fibers/particulates), transfers load to them, protects them from environmental damage, and maintains their relative positioning. Among the three major families of matrix materials—**metals, ceramics, and polymers**—polymer matrices dominate the modern composites industry due to their low density, ease of processing, corrosion resistance, and tunable mechanical properties.

Polymers used as matrices are broadly classified into:
1. **Thermoplastic Polymers**
2. **Thermosetting Polymers (Thermosets)**
3. **Elastomeric Polymers (Elastomers)**

This module focuses on the first and third categories, examining their structure, mechanical behavior, and applicability in advanced composite systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 2, students must clearly differentiate between thermoplastic and elastomeric matrices and justify their selection for specific composite applications such as aerospace, automotive, and biomedical engineering.

---

## 1.2 Thermoplastic Polymers — Formal Definition

A **thermoplastic polymer** is a linear or branched chain macromolecule composed of long, individual molecular chains held together by **weak secondary bonds** (van der Waals forces, dipole-dipole interactions, and hydrogen bonding). These polymers **soften upon heating and harden upon cooling**, with the process being **fully reversible** without any significant chemical change.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** *"Thermoplastics are high molecular weight polymers in which the long-chain molecules are held together by relatively weak van der Waals type secondary forces, allowing the material to be repeatedly softened by heat and hardened by cooling."*

### Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **bowl of cooked spaghetti** 🍝. Each spaghetti strand is an independent polymer chain. The strands are entangled but not chemically bonded to each other. When you heat the bowl, the strands slide past one another more easily (the spaghetti becomes soft and slippery). When cooled, they firm up again. You can reheat and cool the same bowl **multiple times** without the spaghetti "setting" into a rigid cake. This is exactly how a thermoplastic behaves!

---

## 1.3 Elastomeric Polymers — Formal Definition

An **elastomer** is a polymer that exhibits **large, reversible elastic deformation** (typically up to 200–1000% elongation) under applied stress at room temperature. Elastomers are characterized by:
- A **lightly cross-linked** (vulcanized) network of long, coiled polymer chains.
- The ability to return to original shape upon removal of stress.
- Behavior governed by **entropy** (rather than enthalpy) during deformation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** *"Elastomers are polymers that can be stretched to several times their original length and, upon release of the applied stress, return rapidly to their original dimensions. They possess a cross-linked molecular structure but with very low cross-link density, allowing chain segmental mobility."*

### Conceptual Analogy

Think of a **balloon** 🎈. You stretch it, and it elongates enormously. The moment you let go, it snaps back to its original shape. The rubber molecules inside act like coiled springs—lightly tied to each other at a few points (the "ties" are cross-links), but free to uncoil when pulled. The ties prevent permanent flow, so the balloon always recovers.

---

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range |
|--------|--------|---------------|
| Glass Transition Temperature | $T_g$ | $-100°C$ to $+150°C$ |
| Melting Temperature | $T_m$ | $100°C$ to $+400°C$ |
| Degree of Polymerization | $DP$ | $10^2$ to $10^5$ |
| Molecular Weight (Number Avg.) | $M_n$ | $10^4$ to $10^7$ g/mol |
| Cross-link Density | $\nu$ | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-3}$ mol/cm³ |
| Elastic Recovery | — | 90–99.5% |
| Service Temperature | $T_{ser}$ | Up to $T_g$ (elastomers) / $T_m$ (thermoplastics) |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exams:** *Thermoplastics = "T" for "Temporary bonds" → reversible. Elastomers = "E" for "Elastic recovery".*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermoplastic Polymers — Structure and Behavior

### 2.1.1 Molecular Architecture

The **backbone** of a thermoplastic is a covalently bonded sequence of repeating monomer units. The molecular weight $M$ of a thermoplastic is a critical parameter, related to the **degree of polymerization (DP)** by:

$$M = (DP) \times M_0$$

where $M_0$ is the molecular weight of the repeat unit. The longer the chain, the higher the **entanglement density**, which directly controls mechanical strength and melt viscosity.

### 2.1.2 Classification of Thermoplastics

**A) Amorphous Thermoplastics** — Chains are randomly arranged (no long-range order). Examples: Polystyrene (PS), Polycarbonate (PC), PMMA. Properties:
- Transparent
- Gradual softening (no sharp melting point)
- $T_g$ governs upper service temperature

**B) Semi-Crystalline Thermoplastics** — Contain both ordered (crystalline) and disordered (amorphous) regions. Examples: Polyethylene (PE), Polypropylene (PP), Nylon (PA), PEEK. Properties:
- Translucent/Opaque
- Sharp melting point ($T_m$)
- Higher chemical resistance
- Better creep resistance than amorphous

### 2.1.3 Thermal Transitions

The thermal behavior of a thermoplastic is governed by two key transitions:

$$T_g < T < T_m \Rightarrow \text{Rubbery / leathery state}$$

$$T < T_g \Rightarrow \text{Glassy (brittle) state}$$

$$T > T_m \Rightarrow \text{Viscous flow state (processable)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Numerical Questions:** Know that the **melting point $T_m$** is related to the **heat of fusion $\Delta H_f$** and **entropy of fusion $\Delta S_f$** by the thermodynamic relation: $T_m = \Delta H_f / \Delta S_f$.

### 2.1.4 Commonly Used Thermoplastic Matrices in Composites

| Polymer | Abbreviation | $T_g$ (°C) | $T_m$ (°C) | Density (g/cm³) | Composite Application |
|---------|--------------|-----------|-----------|----------------|----------------------|
| Polyethylene | PE | -120 | 130 | 0.95 | Consumer goods |
| Polypropylene | PP | -20 | 165 | 0.91 | Automotive |
| Polyamide 6,6 | PA66 | 50 | 265 | 1.14 | Gears, structural |
| Polycarbonate | PC | 150 | - | 1.20 | Aircraft canopies |
| Polyetheretherketone | PEEK | 143 | 343 | 1.30 | **Aerospace matrices** |
| Polyphenylene sulfide | PPS | 90 | 285 | 1.35 | Chemical-resistant parts |
| Polyetherimide | PEI | 217 | - | 1.27 | Aircraft interiors |

> [!NOTE]
> **PEEK** is a **high-performance thermoplastic** increasingly replacing epoxy in aerospace primary structures (e.g., Airbus A350, Boeing 787 secondary structures) due to its **toughness, recyclability, and damage tolerance**.

---

## 2.2 Elastomeric Polymers — Structure and Behavior

### 2.2.1 Why Elastomers are Different

Elastomers differ from thermoplastics in that they possess a **three-dimensional network** formed by **sparse chemical cross-links (covalent bonds)** between chains. The critical design parameter is the **cross-link density**, typically defined as the number of network chains per unit volume:

$$\nu = \frac{\rho N_A}{2 M_c}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = density of the polymer (g/cm³)
- $N_A$ = Avogadro's number $= 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹
- $M_c$ = average molecular weight between cross-links (g/mol)

The **average distance between cross-links** governs the maximum extension ratio.

### 2.2.2 Thermoelastic Behavior — The Gaussian Network Model

The elastic force in an elastomer arises from **entropic elasticity**, not from bond stretching. When a rubber band is stretched:
- The chains uncoil → **configurational entropy decreases**
- The system opposes this decrease → **retractive force is generated**

The classical Gaussian theory gives the **shear modulus** of a cross-linked rubber as:

$$G = \nu k_B T = \frac{\rho R T}{2 M_c}$$

where:
- $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant $= 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- $R$ = universal gas constant $= 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this important for KTU?** This equation is a **favourite ESE question**. Note that elastomer modulus is **directly proportional to absolute temperature** — opposite to metals and thermoplastics!

### 2.2.3 Stress-Strain Behavior of Elastomers

| Region | Strain Range | Behavior | Mechanism |
|--------|--------------|----------|-----------|
| I | 0–5% | Linear, low modulus | Bond angle bending |
| II | 5–200% | Non-linear, modulus rises | Chain uncoiling |
| III | 200–700% | Strain-induced crystallization | Crystallites act as physical cross-links |
| IV | >700% | Failure | Network rupture |

### 2.2.4 Common Elastomers Used in Composites

| Elastomer | Full Name | Special Feature | Composite Use |
|-----------|-----------|-----------------|---------------|
| NR | Natural Rubber | High tensile strength | Tire treads |
| SBR | Styrene-Butadiene Rubber | Good abrasion resistance | Tire carcasses |
| NBR | Nitrile Butadiene Rubber | Oil/fuel resistance | Hoses, gaskets |
| EPDM | Ethylene-Propylene-Diene | Weather/ozone resistance | Roof membranes |
| CR (Neoprene) | Chloroprene Rubber | Chemical resistance | Wetsuits, belts |
| Silicone (PDMS) | Polydimethylsiloxane | Extreme temp range | Seals, biomedical |
| PU | Polyurethane | Excellent abrasion | Roller wheels, shoe soles |
| IIR (Butyl) | Isobutylene-Isoprene | Low gas permeability | Inner tubes |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $M = (DP) \times M_0$ | Molecular weight relation | g/mol |
| 2 | $T_m = \Delta H_f / \Delta S_f$ | Melting point (thermodynamic) | K |
| 3 | $T_g \approx \frac{2}{3} T_m$ (K) | Empirical rule for symmetric polymers | K |
| 4 | $\nu = \rho N_A / (2 M_c)$ | Cross-link density | mol/cm³ |
| 5 | $G = \nu k_B T$ | Shear modulus of rubber (Gaussian) | Pa |
| 6 | $G = \rho R T / (2 M_c)$ | Alternate form using R | Pa |
| 7 | $E \approx 3G$ | Young's modulus (incompressible) | Pa |
| 8 | $\lambda_{max} \approx \sqrt{N}$ | Max extension ratio (Gaussian chain) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\eta_0 = K M^{3.4}$ | Zero-shear viscosity of polymer melt | Pa·s |
| 10 | $\sigma = G(\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$ | Neo-Hookean stress-strain | Pa |

> [!TIP]
> For numerical problems, remember **$E = 3G$ for incompressible elastomers** (Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.5$).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Thermoplastic composites (e.g., GF/PP, CF/PEEK):** Used in Airbus A350 wing leading edges (PEEK matrix). Offer **weldability, recyclability, and short cycle times** in injection molding.
- **Elastomeric composites (e.g., carbon black filled SBR, silica-filled NR):** Used in tire treads, conveyor belts, vibration dampers, and flexible electronics.
- **Engineering Significance:** Selecting a thermoplastic vs. an elastomer matrix determines whether the composite is **rigid-structural** or **flexible-damping** in function.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Cross-link Density and Modulus of an Elastomer

### Step 1: Define the Network Model

Consider a cube of cross-linked rubber of side $L$ containing $N$ polymer chains. Each chain spans between two cross-link junctions and has a mean-square end-to-end distance $\langle r^2 \rangle_0$ in the undeformed state.

### Step 2: Apply Affine Deformation

When the cube is stretched uniaxially by an extension ratio $\lambda = L/L_0$, each chain's end-to-end vector transforms affinely:

$$\langle r^2 \rangle = \lambda^2 \langle r^2 \rangle_0$$

### Step 3: Use the Entropic Force on a Gaussian Chain

The entropic force on a single Gaussian chain is:

$$f = \frac{3 k_B T}{\langle r^2 \rangle_0} \cdot r$$

The total entropic free energy change per chain is:

$$\Delta A = \frac{3 k_B T}{2 \langle r^2 \rangle_0} \left( r^2 - r_0^2 \right)$$

### Step 4: Sum Over All Chains in Unit Volume

For a network with cross-link density $\nu$ (number of chains per unit volume), the total free energy density is:

$$\Delta A_{total} = \frac{3 \nu k_B T}{2} \left( \lambda^2 + 2\lambda^{-1} - 3 \right)$$

This is the **Neo-Hookean** strain energy function (the constant term 3 arises from $\lambda^2 \lambda^{-1} = 1$ at $\lambda = 1$).

### Step 5: Differentiate to Get Engineering Stress

The engineering stress is obtained by differentiation:

$$\sigma = \frac{\partial \Delta A_{total}}{\partial \lambda} = \nu k_B T \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-2} \right)$$

$$\boxed{\sigma = G (\lambda - \lambda^{-2}), \quad G = \nu k_B T}$$

This is the famous **Neo-Hookean constitutive law** for elastomers.

### Step 6: Convert Engineering Stress to True Stress

True stress $\sigma_{true}$ relates to engineering stress by $\sigma_{true} = \lambda \sigma$:

$$\sigma_{true} = G (\lambda^2 - \lambda^{-1})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation appears verbatim in KTU question papers. **Memorize the final boxed equation.**

---

## 3.2 Numerical Example: Determining Cross-link Density from Modulus

**Problem:** A vulcanized natural rubber sample has a density of $\rho = 0.92$ g/cm³ and a Young's modulus of $E = 2.0$ MPa at $T = 300$ K. Calculate the average molecular weight between cross-links, $M_c$.

### Step 1: Convert to Shear Modulus

For an incompressible elastomer, $E = 3G$:

$$G = \frac{E}{3} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^6}{3} = 6.667 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$

### Step 2: Apply the Modulus Equation

From the Gaussian network theory:

$$G = \frac{\rho R T}{2 M_c}$$

Rearranging for $M_c$:

$$M_c = \frac{\rho R T}{2 G}$$

### Step 3: Substitute Values (Watch Units!)

Convert $\rho$ to SI: $\rho = 0.92 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 920 \text{ kg/m}^3$

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_c &= \frac{(920 \text{ kg/m}^3)(8.314 \text{ J/mol·K})(300 \text{ K})}{2 \times (6.667 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa})} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{920 \times 8.314 \times 300}{2 \times 6.667 \times 10^5} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2{,}294{,}664}{1.3334 \times 10^6} \\[6pt]
&= 1.721 \text{ kg/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Convert to Standard Units

$$M_c = 1.721 \text{ kg/mol} = 1721 \text{ g/mol}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **typical value for a soft vulcanized rubber**. Values of $M_c$ in engineering rubbers range from **1000 to 10,000 g/mol**.

### Step 3.3 Worked Example: Degree of Polymerization for Nylon 6,6

**Problem:** Calculate the degree of polymerization (DP) of Nylon 6,6 with a number-average molecular weight $M_n = 25{,}000$ g/mol. The repeat unit (C₁₂H₂₂O₂N₂) has molecular weight $M_0$.

### Step 1: Calculate $M_0$

Using atomic masses (C = 12, H = 1, O = 16, N = 14):

$$M_0 = 12(12) + 22(1) + 2(16) + 2(14) = 144 + 22 + 32 + 28 = 226 \text{ g/mol}$$

### Step 2: Compute DP

$$
\begin{aligned}
DP &= \frac{M_n}{M_0} = \frac{25{,}000}{226} \\[6pt]
&= 110.6
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A typical Nylon 6,6 fiber has on average ~110 monomer units in a single chain. The value lies in the expected range $10^2$–$10^5$.

---

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix: Thermoplastic vs. Elastomer vs. Thermoset (for ESE Questions)

| Property | Thermoplastic | Elastomer | Thermoset (for contrast) |
|----------|---------------|-----------|--------------------------|
| Structure | Linear/branched chains | Lightly cross-linked | Heavily cross-linked |
| Bonds between chains | Weak (secondary) | Covalent (sparse) | Covalent (dense) |
| $T_g$ | High (rigid at RT) | Below RT (rubbery at RT) | Very high |
| On heating | Softens & flows | Degrades (does not flow) | Degrades (does not flow) |
| Recyclable? | **Yes** | No (permanent network) | No |
| Reprocessable? | **Yes** (re-melt) | No | No |
| Elongation at break | 5–100% | 200–1000% | <5% |
| Young's modulus (E) | 1–5 GPa | 0.001–0.1 GPa | 2–5 GPa |
| Matrix use | High-temp aerospace, auto | Flexible composites, tires | Aerospace (epoxy/carbon) |
| Processing | Injection, extrusion, thermoforming | Molding, extrusion (pre-vulc) | Lay-up, RTM, autoclave |
| Toughness | High (especially semicrystalline) | Very high (elastic) | Low (brittle) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Polymer Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Polymer Matrices] --> B[Thermoplastic]
    A --> C[Thermosetting]
    A --> D[Elastomeric]
    B --> B1[Amorphous]
    B --> B2[Semi Crystalline]
    B1 --> B1A[PS Polystyrene]
    B1 --> B1B[PC Polycarbonate]
    B1 --> B1C[PMMA Acrylic]
    B2 --> B2A[PE Polyethylene]
    B2 --> B2B[PP Polypropylene]
    B2 --> B2C[PEEK High Performance]
    B2 --> B2D[PPS Polyphenylene Sulfide]
    D --> D1[Natural Rubber NR]
    D --> D2[Synthetic SBR NBR EPDM]
    D --> D3[Specialty Silicone PU]
    C --> C1[Epoxy]
    C --> C2[Polyester]
    C --> C3[Vinylester]
    C --> C4[Phenolic]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Molecular Behavior with Temperature

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Glass T less than Tg] --> B[Tg Glass Transition]
    B --> C[Tg less than T less than Tm Rubbery]
    C --> D[Tm Melting]
    D --> E[T greater than Tm Viscous Flow]
    A2[Elastomer at Room T] --> R1[T greater than Tg Rubbery State]
    R1 --> R2[Td Degradation]
    R1 -.high E.-> M1[Network Restricts Flow]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** Thermoplastics go through **four distinct states** as temperature rises. Elastomers, however, are already in their rubbery state at room temperature, and increasing temperature further only leads to **degradation**, not flow.

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of an Elastomer Network

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Linear Polymer Chain] --> X1[Crosslink 1]
    P2[Linear Polymer Chain] --> X1
    X1 --> X2[Crosslink 2]
    P3[Linear Polymer Chain] --> X2
    P4[Linear Polymer Chain] --> X2
    X2 --> X3[Crosslink 3]
    P5[Linear Polymer Chain] --> X3
    P6[Linear Polymer Chain] --> X3
    X1 -.low density.-> X2
    X2 -.low density.-> X3
    S[Applied Stress] --> U[Uncoiling of Chains]
    U --> RF[Retractive Entropic Force]
    RF --> REC[Elastic Recovery to Original Shape]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **low density of cross-links** is the key to elastomeric behavior. If cross-link density is too high, the network becomes a **thermoset** (rigid, no recovery).

---

## 4.4 Process Flow: Thermoplastic Composite Manufacturing (LFT)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Thermoplastic Resin Pellets] --> B[Extruder Melt]
    C[Reinforcement Fibers Rovings] --> D[Fiber Tensioning]
    B --> E[Impregnation Die]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Pultruded Thermoplastic Tape]
    F --> G[Cutting to Length]
    G --> H[Stacking in Mold]
    H --> I[Hot Pressing]
    I --> J[Final Composite Part]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **LFT-D (Long Fiber Thermoplastic - Direct)** is a key KTU industrial topic. The process enables **cycle times <60 seconds**, a major advantage over thermoset autoclave cycles (4–8 hours).

---

## 4.5 Stress-Strain Behavior Comparison (Schematic)

```mermaid
graph TD
    SA[Thermoplastic Stress Strain] --> SA1[Linear Elastic up to Yield]
    SA1 --> SA2[Yield Point]
    SA2 --> SA3[Strain Hardening or Necking]
    SA3 --> SA4[Failure at 5 to 100 percent Strain]
    SB[Elastomer Stress Strain] --> SB1[Low Initial Modulus]
    SB1 --> SB2[Progressive Stiffening with Strain]
    SB2 --> SB3[Strain Induced Crystallization]
    SB3 --> SB4[Failure at 200 to 1000 percent Strain]
    SC[Thermoset Stress Strain] --> SC1[Linear Elastic up to Failure]
    SC1 --> SC2[Brittle Failure less than 5 percent]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define thermoplastic polymers. Give two examples. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Thermoplastic polymers are high molecular weight polymers in which the molecular chains are held together by weak secondary forces (van der Waals, dipole-dipole, hydrogen bonding). They soften upon heating and harden upon cooling, with the process being **fully reversible** without chemical change.

> **Examples (1 mark):** (1) Polyethylene (PE), (2) Polypropylene (PP) — *[Any two valid examples: PS, PVC, PMMA, Nylon, PEEK, PC, etc.]*

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between thermoplastic and elastomeric polymers. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Thermoplastic | Elastomeric |
|--------|---------------|-------------|
| Chain structure | Linear/branched | Lightly cross-linked network |
| Inter-chain bonds | Weak (secondary) | Strong covalent (sparse) |
| Behavior on heating | Softens & flows | Does not flow, degrades |
| Elastic recovery | 5–100% elongation | 200–1000% reversible |
| Typical $T_g$ | Above room T | Below room T |
| Examples | PE, PP, PEEK | Natural rubber, SBR, silicone |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A: Thermoplastic Matrices in Advanced Composites

> `[KTU University Exam - 2024 ESE Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the molecular structure of thermoplastic polymers. Discuss the differences between **amorphous** and **semi-crystalline** thermoplastics with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With neat schematics, describe the **Long Fiber Thermoplastic (LFT)** process for manufacturing thermoplastic composites. List **four advantages** of thermoplastic matrix composites over thermoset matrix composites. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**1. Molecular Structure (3 marks):**
- Thermoplastics are **linear or branched** long-chain molecules.
- Chains are held together by **secondary bonds** (van der Waals, dipole-dipole, H-bonding).
- No covalent cross-links between chains → **re-meltable** and **recyclable**.
- Molecular weight related to DP: $M = DP \times M_0$.
- Higher molecular weight → higher entanglement density → better mechanical strength and higher melt viscosity.

**2. Amorphous Thermoplastics (2 marks):**
- Chains are **randomly coiled** with no long-range order.
- Properties: **transparent**, isotropic mechanical behavior, **gradual softening** (no sharp melting point).
- Service temperature limited by **$T_g$**.
- Examples: Polystyrene (PS), Polycarbonate (PC), PMMA.

**3. Semi-Crystalline Thermoplastics (2 marks):**
- Contain **both** ordered (crystallites) and disordered (amorphous) regions.
- **% Crystallinity** determines stiffness, strength, and chemical resistance.
- Show a **sharp melting point $T_m$** and a distinct $T_g$.
- Examples: PE, PP, Nylon, PEEK, PPS.
- Crystallites act as physical cross-links, increasing strength and creep resistance.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**1. LFT-D Process (4 marks):**
- **Stage 1:** Thermoplastic pellets (e.g., PP, PA) are fed into an **extruder** where they melt.
- **Stage 2:** Continuous fiber rovings (glass or carbon) are **tensioned** and pulled through a **die**.
- **Stage 3:** In the impregnation die, the polymer melt is **forced into the fiber bundles** under pressure, ensuring complete wetting.
- **Stage 4:** The consolidated tow is **pultruded**, **cooled**, and **cut** into pellets or directly into charge for compression molding.
- **Stage 5:** Charge is **hot-pressed** in a mold to form the final composite part.

**2. Four Advantages of Thermoplastic vs. Thermoset Composites (3 marks):**
1. **Shorter cycle times** — LFT-D cycle is <60 s, vs. 4–8 h for autoclave-cured thermosets.
2. **Recyclability** — Thermoplastic matrices can be **re-melted and reprocessed**, supporting circular economy.
3. **Higher toughness and damage tolerance** — Thermoplastics are less brittle than cured epoxies.
4. **No chemical cure required** — Eliminates volatile organic compounds (VOCs) and long post-cure schedules.
5. **Weldable & thermoformable** — Parts can be joined by **resistance welding, ultrasonic welding, or induction heating**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often confuse **amorphous** with **semi-crystalline** behavior. Remember: amorphous thermoplastics do **not** have a sharp $T_m$, only a $T_g$. Saying "PS melts at 100°C" will fetch **zero marks**.

---

### Question B: Elastomeric Polymers and Cross-link Theory

> `[KTU University Exam - 2024 ESE Model Paper]`

**(a)** Define an **elastomer**. Explain **thermoelastic behavior** with reference to the **Gaussian network theory**. Derive the expression for the **shear modulus** of a vulcanized rubber in terms of cross-link density and temperature. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sample of vulcanized natural rubber has density $\rho = 950$ kg/m³ and Young's modulus $E = 3.0$ MPa at $T = 298$ K. Calculate **(i)** the shear modulus, **(ii)** the average molecular weight between cross-links ($M_c$), and **(iii)** the cross-link density. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**1. Definition of Elastomer (1 mark):**
An elastomer is a polymer exhibiting **large, reversible elastic deformation** (up to several hundred percent elongation) at room temperature, made possible by a **lightly cross-linked** network of long, flexible polymer chains operating above their $T_g$.

**2. Thermoelastic Behavior — Gaussian Network Theory (4 marks):**
- The retractive force in an elastomer originates from **entropy**, not bond stretching.
- A single Gaussian chain in the network has mean-square end-to-end distance $\langle r^2 \rangle_0 = N b^2$ (where $N$ = number of segments, $b$ = segment length).
- Under uniaxial extension by $\lambda$, the entropic free energy per chain is:
  $$\Delta A = \frac{3 k_B T}{2} \left( \lambda^2 + 2\lambda^{-1} - 3 \right)$$
- For a network with $\nu$ chains per unit volume, the strain energy density is the **Neo-Hookean** form:
  $$\Delta A_{total} = \frac{\nu k_B T}{2} \left( \lambda^2 + 2\lambda^{-1} - 3 \right)$$

**3. Derivation of Shear Modulus (2 marks):**
Differentiating with respect to $\lambda$ gives engineering stress:
$$\sigma = \frac{\partial \Delta A_{total}}{\partial \lambda} = \nu k_B T \left( \lambda - \lambda^{-2} \right)$$

Comparing with linear elasticity for small strain ($\lambda \approx 1 + \epsilon$):
$$\sigma \approx 3 \nu k_B T \cdot \epsilon = G \cdot \gamma \cdot \tfrac{1}{3} \text{ form}$$

In simple shear, the shear modulus is:
$$\boxed{G = \nu k_B T = \frac{\rho R T}{2 M_c}}$$

This shows **modulus increases with temperature** — a unique signature of entropic elasticity.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $\rho = 950 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $E = 3.0 \text{ MPa} = 3.0 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$, $T = 298 \text{ K}$, $R = 8.314 \text{ J/mol·K}$.

**(i) Shear modulus (2 marks):**

For an incompressible elastomer, $E = 3G$:

$$G = \frac{E}{3} = \frac{3.0 \times 10^6}{3} = 1.0 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 1.0 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Molecular weight between cross-links $M_c$ (3 marks):**

From $G = \rho R T / (2 M_c)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_c &= \frac{\rho R T}{2 G} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{(950)(8.314)(298)}{2 \times (1.0 \times 10^6)} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{950 \times 8.314 \times 298}{2{,}000{,}000} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2{,}353{,}653.4}{2{,}000{,}000} \\[6pt]
&= 1.177 \text{ kg/mol} = 1177 \text{ g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Cross-link density $\nu$ (2 marks):**

From $\nu = \rho N_A / (2 M_c)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nu &= \frac{950 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}}{2 \times 1.177} \text{ (using } M_c \text{ in kg/mol)} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{5.721 \times 10^{26}}{2.354} \\[6pt]
&= 2.43 \times 10^{26} \text{ chains/m}^3 \\[6pt]
&= 2.43 \times 10^{20} \text{ chains/cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** A common mistake is forgetting the factor of **2** in $\nu = \rho N_A / (2 M_c)$. The factor 2 arises because each cross-link is shared between **two** chains in the network. **Lose 1 mark if omitted.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #3:** Students often confuse $E$ and $G$. For elastomers, use $G = E/3$, not $G = E/2(1+\nu)$. While the latter is more general, the **incompressibility assumption** ($G = E/3$) is standard for KTU numerical problems on rubbers.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

### Thermoplastic Polymers
- [x] **Definition:** Linear/branched chains with **weak secondary bonds**; reversible softening on heating.
- [x] **Amorphous** (PS, PC, PMMA) vs. **Semi-crystalline** (PE, PP, PEEK, PPS) classification.
- [x] Thermal transitions: $T_g$ (glass) and $T_m$ (melt). Semi-crystalline has both; amorphous has only $T_g$.
- [x] Relation: $M = (DP) \times M_0$ and $T_m = \Delta H_f / \Delta S_f$.
- [x] **PEEK, PPS, PEI** are high-performance aerospace matrix choices.
- [x] **Recyclable, weldable, fast cycle times** — key industrial drivers.

### Elastomeric Polymers
- [x] **Definition:** Lightly cross-linked polymer showing >200% reversible elastic strain.
- [x] Entropic (not enthalpic) origin of elasticity.
- [x] **Gaussian network theory:** $G = \nu k_B T = \rho R T / (2 M_c)$.
- [x] Modulus **increases with temperature** (unique to rubbers).
- [x] **Neo-Hookean** stress: $\sigma = G(\lambda - \lambda^{-2})$.
- [x] Incompressibility: $E = 3G$.
- [x] Examples: NR, SBR, NBR, EPDM, CR, silicone, PU, IIR.

### Critical Differences for KTU
- [x] Thermoplastic = "re-melt, re-process, recycle." Elastomer = "stretch, recover, but do not re-melt."
- [x] Thermoplastic composite manufacturing: **LFT, GMT, CF/PEEK tape placement**.
- [x] Elastomer composite manufacturing: **milling, calendering, extrusion, vulcanization**.
- [x] **Cross-link density** is the master variable controlling elastomer modulus.

### Formulae to Memorize
$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= (DP) \cdot M_0 \\[4pt]
T_m &= \frac{\Delta H_f}{\Delta S_f} \\[4pt]
G &= \nu k_B T = \frac{\rho R T}{2 M_c} \\[4pt]
\nu &= \frac{\rho N_A}{2 M_c} \\[4pt]
\sigma &= G\left(\lambda - \lambda^{-2}\right) \\[4pt]
E &= 3G \quad (\text{incompressible rubber}) \\[4pt]
\lambda_{max} &\approx \sqrt{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Good luck with your KTU 2024 examinations! Master the formulae, draw the structures, and remember: a polymer is only as good as its matrix's ability to transfer load to the reinforcement.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: PROPERTIES OF COMPOSITE MATERIALS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Properties of Composite Materials** refer to the synergistic combination of physical, mechanical, thermal, electrical, and chemical characteristics that arise from the **rule of mixtures** interaction between the **matrix phase** and the **reinforcement phase** within a composite system. These properties are generally **anisotropic**, meaning they vary with the direction of measurement, and are classified as either **intensive** (independent of size/mass, e.g., density, modulus) or **extensive** (dependent on size/mass, e.g., strength, stiffness).

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Code: **PEMET413**), the study of composite properties is fundamentally important because the **prime advantage** of composites lies in their ability to deliver **tailored, directional, and superior performance** compared to monolithic engineering materials.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]  
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Concrete & Steel Rods" Model**  
> Imagine a **sticky mud brick reinforced with straw** (an ancient form of composite). The mud (matrix) holds everything together but is weak under tension, while the straw (reinforcement) carries the tensile loads. Together, they form a wall that resists both compressive and tensile forces—properties neither could achieve alone. Modern composites (e.g., **CFRP — Carbon Fiber Reinforced Polymer**) work on the same principle: a **soft polymer matrix** (epoxy) is **stiffened and strengthened** by embedding **high-strength carbon fibers**.

> [!TIP]  
> **Key Takeaway:** The properties of a composite are **not the average** of its constituents—they emerge from the **load transfer mechanism** at the **fiber-matrix interface** (the *interphase*). This is what makes composites *engineered* materials rather than *natural* mixtures.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants in Composite Analysis

| Property Category | Key Metric | Typical Value Range |
|---|---|---|
| Density ($\rho$) | **kg/m³** or **g/cc** | 1.5 – 2.0 (CFRP) |
| Specific Strength | $\sigma / \rho$ | Up to **5× steel** |
| Specific Modulus | $E / \rho$ | Up to **8× aluminum** |
| Volume Fraction ($V_f$) | Dimensionless | 0.3 – 0.7 (typical) |
| Fiber-Matrix Interface | Adhesion Energy | **J/m²** |

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Composite Properties

The properties of composites are systematically classified as follows:

- **Physical Properties**: Density, porosity, void content, moisture absorption, specific gravity.
- **Mechanical Properties**: Tensile strength, compressive strength, flexural strength, shear strength, impact resistance, hardness, fatigue life, creep resistance, fracture toughness.
- **Thermal Properties**: Coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE), thermal conductivity, specific heat capacity, glass transition temperature ($T_g$), heat distortion temperature (HDT).
- **Electrical Properties**: Electrical conductivity, dielectric strength, volume/surface resistivity, dielectric constant.
- **Chemical Properties**: Chemical resistance, corrosion resistance, UV/weathering resistance, fire retardancy, solvent resistance.
- **Specific (Performance) Properties**: Specific strength ($\sigma / \rho$), specific modulus ($E / \rho$), specific damping capacity.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Composite Properties

- **Why do we study composite properties?** Because composite design is a **multi-objective optimization** problem—an aerospace skin must be **light, stiff, strong, fatigue-resistant, and thermally stable** simultaneously.
- **How are they determined?** Through the **Rule of Mixtures (ROM)** for first-order estimation and **micromechanics models** (Halpin-Tsai, Cox shear lag) for refined analysis.
- **What governs the final property?** The **fiber volume fraction ($V_f$)**, **fiber orientation ($\theta$)**, **fiber-matrix adhesion quality**, and **fiber aspect ratio ($l/d$)**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The following table contains the **high-yield formulas** for solving KTU Board Examination problems on Composite Properties. Memorize these!

| Property | Formula (Rule of Mixtures) | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | $\rho$: density, $V$: volume fraction | kg/m³ |
| Longitudinal Modulus (Voigt) | $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | $E$: Young's Modulus | GPa |
| Transverse Modulus (Reuss) | $1/E_T = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$ | Inverse ROM | GPa |
| Longitudinal Strength | $\sigma_L = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ | $\sigma$: strength | MPa |
| Poisson's Ratio | $\nu_{LT} = \nu_f V_f + \nu_m V_m$ | $\nu$: Poisson's ratio | Dimensionless |
| CTE (Longitudinal) | $\alpha_L = \frac{\alpha_f E_f V_f + \alpha_m E_m V_m}{E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | $\alpha$: CTE | /°C |
| Electrical Conductivity | $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ | Conductivity parallel to fibers | S/m |
| Specific Strength | $SS = \sigma / \rho$ | Performance index | kNm/kg |
| Specific Modulus | $SM = E / \rho$ | Stiffness-to-weight | kNm/kg |
| Critical Fiber Length | $l_c = \frac{\sigma_f d}{2 \tau_i}$ | $d$: fiber dia, $\tau_i$: interfacial shear | m |
| Fiber Efficiency (Halpin-Tsai) | $E_L = E_m \frac{1 + 2(l/d)\eta_L V_f}{1 - \eta_L V_f}$ | $\eta_L$: shape parameter | GPa |
| Shear Modulus (Composite) | $G_{LT} = \frac{G_m}{(1-V_f) + V_f \frac{G_m}{G_f}}$ | Halpin-Tsai form | GPa |
| Voids Effect on Density | $\rho_c^{actual} = \rho_c^{theor} (1 - V_v)$ | $V_v$: void fraction | kg/m³ |

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Pitfall:** In the transverse modulus formula, the **Reuss (inverse)** model is used (not Voigt). Many students incorrectly use Voigt for both directions and lose marks!

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace Industry**: CFRP used in Boeing 787 (50% composite by weight) for high **specific strength** and **fatigue resistance**.
- **Automotive Sector**: Glass fiber reinforced polymer (GFRP) body panels for **lightweighting** and **corrosion resistance**.
- **Civil Engineering**: FRP rebars in concrete for **non-corrosive, high-strength** reinforcement.
- **Sports Equipment**: Carbon fiber tennis rackets, golf shafts for **high specific stiffness**.
- **Wind Energy**: GFRP/CFRP turbine blades exploit **fatigue resistance** and **low density**.
- **Electronics**: Carbon fiber composites for **EMI shielding** and **thermal management** in satellites.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Density of Composite (Rule of Mixtures)

**Statement:** Derive an expression for the density of a fiber-reinforced composite using the Rule of Mixtures.

**Assumptions:**
- Fibers and matrix are perfectly bonded (no voids).
- $V_f + V_m = 1$ (volume conservation).

**Step-by-step derivation:**

Let $V_c$ be the total volume of the composite, $V_f$ the fiber volume, and $V_m$ the matrix volume.

$$V_c = V_f + V_m$$

Let the masses be denoted as $m_c, m_f, m_m$. Since the fibers and matrix together constitute the composite:

$$m_c = m_f + m_m$$

By definition of density ($\rho = m/V$):

$$m_c = \rho_c V_c, \quad m_f = \rho_f V_f, \quad m_m = \rho_m V_m$$

Substituting:

$$\rho_c V_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$$

Dividing both sides by $V_c$:

$$\rho_c = \rho_f \frac{V_f}{V_c} + \rho_m \frac{V_m}{V_c}$$

Defining volume fractions $V_f^c = V_f/V_c$ and $V_m^c = V_m/V_c$ (commonly written as $V_f$ and $V_m$ in composite notation):

$$\boxed{\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Longitudinal Young's Modulus (Voigt Model)

**Statement:** Show that for a unidirectional composite loaded parallel to fibers, the modulus follows the **iso-strain condition**.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

Under longitudinal loading, both fiber and matrix experience the **same strain** (iso-strain assumption):

$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon$$

The composite carries the total load $P_c$ as the sum of loads carried by each phase:

$$P_c = P_f + P_m$$

Since stress $\sigma = P/A$:

$$\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$$

Using Hooke's law ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$) and the iso-strain condition:

$$E_c \varepsilon A_c = E_f \varepsilon A_f + E_m \varepsilon A_m$$

Cancelling $\varepsilon$ and defining area fractions as volume fractions for aligned continuous fibers:

$$\boxed{E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Transverse Young's Modulus (Reuss Model)

**Statement:** Derive the transverse modulus under the **iso-stress condition**.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

When load is applied **perpendicular** to fibers, both phases experience the **same stress** (iso-stress):

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f = \sigma_m = \sigma$$

The total strain is the sum of strains in each phase:

$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f V_f + \varepsilon_m V_m$$

Using Hooke's law ($\varepsilon = \sigma/E$):

$$\frac{\sigma}{E_T} = \frac{\sigma}{E_f} V_f + \frac{\sigma}{E_m} V_m$$

Cancelling $\sigma$:

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Critical Fiber Length

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the critical fiber length required for full load transfer.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

Consider a single fiber of diameter $d$ embedded in matrix, with interfacial shear stress $\tau_i$ acting along the fiber surface.

The tensile force in the fiber builds up from zero (at fiber end) to a maximum at a distance $l_c/2$ (the critical transfer length):

$$F_{fiber} = \sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

The shear force transferred at the interface over the critical length:

$$F_{shear} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot \frac{l_c}{2}$$

For **full load transfer**, these must be equal:

$$\sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot \frac{l_c}{2}$$

Solving for $l_c$:

$$\boxed{l_c = \frac{\sigma_f d}{2 \tau_i}}$$

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Effect of Voids on Composite Density

**Statement:** Show how void content reduces the theoretical density of a composite.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

The theoretical density (assuming no voids) is:

$$\rho_{th} = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$$

With voids of volume fraction $V_v$, the actual fiber and matrix volume fractions become $V_f(1-V_v)$ and $V_m(1-V_v)$ respectively, since voids occupy a fraction $V_v$ of total volume:

$$\rho_{act} = \rho_f V_f (1 - V_v) + \rho_m V_m (1 - V_v)$$

Factoring out $(1 - V_v)$:

$$\boxed{\rho_{act} = \rho_{th} (1 - V_v)}$$

This shows that each 1% increase in void content reduces the density by 1% of the theoretical value—a key **quality control metric** in KTU lab assessments.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Define the term "specific strength" of a composite material and explain its significance in aerospace applications.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Specific Strength** is defined as the **ratio of a material's strength (ultimate tensile strength, $\sigma_u$) to its density ($\rho$)**, expressed in units of **kNm/kg** or **MPa/(g/cc)**.

$$SS = \frac{\sigma_u}{\rho}$$

**Significance in Aerospace (1 Mark each):**
- In aerospace, **weight reduction** is the primary design driver since lift force must overcome weight.
- CFRP has $SS \approx 2500$ kNm/kg, which is **5–6 times that of steel** ($\approx 400$ kNm/kg).
- A higher specific strength means the structure can carry **more load per unit weight**, leading to **fuel efficiency, payload increase, and reduced emissions**.
- This is why the Boeing 787 Dreamliner uses **50% composites by weight**.

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (1), Formula (1), Application (1).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**State the Rule of Mixtures. How is it applied to predict the longitudinal Young's modulus of a unidirectional fiber-reinforced composite?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Rule of Mixtures (ROM):** It is a **weighted average model** that predicts composite properties as a linear combination of constituent properties, weighted by their volume fractions (1 Mark).

For longitudinal Young's modulus ($E_L$), under the **iso-strain assumption**, the ROM gives (1 Mark):

$$E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$$

Where:
- $E_f$ = Fiber Young's Modulus
- $E_m$ = Matrix Young's Modulus
- $V_f, V_m$ = Volume fractions of fiber and matrix

This equation is valid when fibers are **continuous, aligned, and perfectly bonded** to the matrix. For example, for carbon fiber ($E_f = 230$ GPa) at $V_f = 0.6$ in epoxy ($E_m = 3$ GPa): $E_L = 230(0.6) + 3(0.4) = 139.2$ GPa (1 Mark).

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Module Internal Choice - Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

### Question A:

**(a)** Derive the expressions for the **longitudinal** and **transverse Young's modulus** of a unidirectional fiber-reinforced composite using the Rule of Mixtures. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A unidirectional glass fiber-reinforced epoxy composite contains **60% volume fraction of fibers**. Given: $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\sigma_{f,u} = 2400$ MPa, $\sigma_{m,u} = 80$ MPa. Calculate:
- (i) Longitudinal Young's Modulus $E_L$
- (ii) Transverse Young's Modulus $E_T$
- (iii) Longitudinal Tensile Strength $\sigma_L$ **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

*Longitudinal Modulus — Voigt Model (Iso-Strain) (3.5 Marks):*

**Assumptions:**
- Fibers are **continuous, parallel, and uniformly distributed**.
- Perfect bonding at the fiber-matrix interface (no slip).
- Both phases experience the **same strain** $\varepsilon$ under longitudinal load.
- Linear elastic behavior of both constituents.

**Derivation:** Under iso-strain $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon$, the load-sharing gives $P_c = P_f + P_m$:

$$E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$$

*Transverse Modulus — Reuss Model (Iso-Stress) (3.5 Marks):*

**Assumptions:**
- Fibers are continuous and aligned.
- Both phases experience the **same stress** $\sigma$ under transverse load.
- The load applied is uniform across the cross-section.

**Derivation:** Under iso-stress $\sigma_c = \sigma_f = \sigma_m = \sigma$, the strain compatibility gives:

$$\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$$

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

Given: $V_f = 0.6$, $V_m = 0.4$, $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\sigma_{f,u} = 2400$ MPa, $\sigma_{m,u} = 80$ MPa.

**(i) Longitudinal Modulus (2 Marks):**

$$E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$$
$$E_L = (72)(0.6) + (3.5)(0.4)$$
$$E_L = 43.2 + 1.4 = 44.6 \text{ GPa}$$

**(ii) Transverse Modulus (2.5 Marks):**

$$\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$$
$$\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{0.6}{72} + \frac{0.4}{3.5}$$
$$\frac{1}{E_T} = 0.00833 + 0.1143 = 0.1226 \text{ GPa}^{-1}$$
$$E_T = 8.16 \text{ GPa}$$

**(iii) Longitudinal Tensile Strength (2.5 Marks):**

$$\sigma_L = \sigma_{f,u} V_f + \sigma_{m,u} V_m$$
$$\sigma_L = (2400)(0.6) + (80)(0.4)$$
$$\sigma_L = 1440 + 32 = 1472 \text{ MPa}$$

**Note:** This is a **first-order upper bound**; actual strength may be lower due to fiber misalignment, interface defects, and stress concentrations.

---

### Module Internal Choice - Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

### Question B:

**(a)** Explain the concept of **critical fiber length**. Derive an expression for it. Why is it important in composite design? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A carbon fiber ($d = 7 \mu m$, $\sigma_f = 4.0$ GPa) is embedded in an epoxy matrix. The interfacial shear strength is $\tau_i = 50$ MPa. Determine:
- (i) The **critical fiber length** $l_c$.
- (ii) The **aspect ratio** ($l/d$) at critical length.
- (iii) If the actual fiber length is **3 mm**, comment on whether the fiber is **efficient in load transfer**. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Critical Fiber Length Theory (7 Marks):**

**Concept (2 Marks):** The **critical fiber length ($l_c$)** is the **minimum fiber length** required to achieve the **maximum possible stress** in the fiber through shear stress transfer at the matrix-fiber interface. Below $l_c$, the fiber will **pull out** from the matrix before reaching its ultimate strength, leading to inefficient reinforcement.

**Derivation (3 Marks):** As shown in Section 3.4, equating the tensile load in the fiber to the shear load transferred at the interface:

$$l_c = \frac{\sigma_f d}{2 \tau_i}$$

**Importance in Design (2 Marks):**
- For efficient reinforcement, **actual fiber length $l \gg l_c$** (typically $l \geq 10 l_c$ or $15 l_c$).
- $l_c$ determines whether short or long fibers are appropriate for a given matrix system.
- It governs **fiber selection** (e.g., carbon fibers in epoxy typically require only short lengths due to high $\sigma_f$ and reasonable $\tau_i$).

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

Given: $d = 7 \mu m = 7 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $\sigma_f = 4.0 \times 10^9$ Pa, $\tau_i = 50 \times 10^6$ Pa.

**(i) Critical Fiber Length (2.5 Marks):**

$$l_c = \frac{\sigma_f d}{2 \tau_i}$$
$$l_c = \frac{(4.0 \times 10^9)(7 \times 10^{-6})}{2 \times 50 \times 10^6}$$
$$l_c = \frac{28 \times 10^3}{10^8} = 2.8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$
$$l_c = 0.28 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Aspect Ratio at Critical Length (2 Marks):**

$$\frac{l_c}{d} = \frac{0.28 \times 10^{-3}}{7 \times 10^{-6}} = 40$$

**(iii) Efficiency Comment (2.5 Marks):**

Given actual fiber length $l = 3$ mm. Comparing with $l_c = 0.28$ mm:

$$\frac{l}{l_c} = \frac{3}{0.28} \approx 10.7$$

Since $l/l_c \geq 10$, the fiber is **efficient in load transfer** and will be loaded close to its ultimate tensile strength. The fiber behaves essentially as a **continuous fiber** for design purposes. (If the ratio were $< 5$, the fiber would be considered inefficient and behave more like a *short fiber reinforcement* with end effects dominating.)

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**  
> 1. **Unit consistency** is a common place where students lose 1–2 marks. Always convert $\mu m$ to m, GPa to Pa before substituting in formulas.  
> 2. **Volume fraction sum**: Many students forget that $V_f + V_m = 1$ and use incorrect values. If the problem gives only $V_f$, calculate $V_m = 1 - V_f$.  
> 3. **Transverse modulus**: Use the **inverse ROM (Reuss)**, not Voigt. This is the **#1 mistake** in KTU answer sheets.  
> 4. **Voigt vs Reuss**: Voigt gives the **upper bound** (iso-strain); Reuss gives the **lower bound** (iso-stress). Actual values lie between these.  
> 5. **Strength prediction via ROM** assumes **simultaneous failure** of fiber and matrix, which is rarely true. Mention this limitation in answers to fetch full marks.  
> 6. **Don't confuse specific strength** (strength/density) with **specific modulus** (modulus/density). These are different performance indices.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Rule of Mixtures (ROM)** is the **foundation** for all composite property predictions—memorize both forms (Voigt & Reuss).
- [x] **Longitudinal properties** follow **iso-strain** (Voigt) → use direct addition.
- [x] **Transverse properties** follow **iso-stress** (Reuss) → use harmonic mean (inverse).
- [x] **Specific strength** ($\sigma/\rho$) and **specific modulus** ($E/\rho$) are the **key performance indices** for aerospace applications.
- [x] **Critical fiber length** $l_c = \sigma_f d / (2\tau_i)$ determines whether a fiber is "effective" in load transfer.
- [x] For efficient reinforcement, **$l \geq 10 l_c$** (or more conservatively, $15 l_c$).
- [x] **Void content** reduces density as $\rho_{act} = \rho_{th}(1 - V_v)$—a critical quality parameter in KTU labs.
- [x] **Halpin-Tsai equations** are used for more accurate predictions (especially for transverse modulus) when ROM is insufficient.
- [x] **Composite properties are anisotropic**—they depend on **fiber orientation** ($\theta$) and **volume fraction** ($V_f$).
- [x] The **fiber-matrix interface (interphase)** governs load transfer—weak interface = poor composite performance.
- [x] **CFRP** offers the highest specific strength and modulus; **GFRP** is the most cost-effective; **Kevlar/epoxy** offers excellent impact resistance.
- [x] For **thermal expansion** in the longitudinal direction, the formula accounts for stiffness-weighted expansion: $\alpha_L = (\alpha_f E_f V_f + \alpha_m E_m V_m)/(E_f V_f + E_m V_m)$.
- [x] **Ductile matrices** (e.g., polymers) provide **toughness**; **brittle fibers** (e.g., carbon) provide **strength**—composites combine these synergistically.

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: Why Properties are "Engineered"

> [!NOTE]  
> **Core Concept:** Unlike metals or ceramics, the properties of composites are **not intrinsic**—they are **designed** by manipulating fiber type, volume fraction, orientation, and stacking sequence. This is what makes composites "engineered materials."

### 2.1.1 The Four Pillars of Composite Property Design

1. **Fiber Selection** (E-Glass, S-Glass, Carbon, Aramid, Boron)
2. **Matrix Selection** (Thermoset: Epoxy, Polyester; Thermoplastic: PEEK, PPS; Metal: Al, Ti; Ceramic: SiC, Al₂O₃)
3. **Fiber Volume Fraction** ($V_f$, typically 0.3 – 0.7)
4. **Fiber Orientation & Architecture** (Unidirectional, Woven, Braided, Random, 3D)

### 2.1.2 The Property Hierarchy

Properties of composites can be ranked by the **degree of micromechanical complexity**:

| Level | Model | Accuracy | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rule of Mixtures (ROM) | ±20–30% | First-order design |
| 2 | Halpin-Tsai | ±10–15% | Refined design |
| 3 | Shear Lag (Cox) | ±5–10% | Short fiber analysis |
| 4 | Finite Element (FEM) | <2% | Final validation |

## 2.2 Detailed Property Analysis

### 2.2.1 Physical Properties

- **Density ($\rho_c$):** Calculated via ROM. Critical for weight-sensitive applications.
- **Void Content ($V_v$):** Measured via ASTM D2734. Should be <1% for high-performance composites.
- **Moisture Absorption:** Polymer composites absorb 0.1–3% moisture, reducing $T_g$ and mechanical properties.

### 2.2.2 Mechanical Properties — Detailed Breakdown

- **Tensile Strength:** Maximum tensile stress before failure. For unidirectional CFRP, $\sigma_L$ can reach **3000–5000 MPa**.
- **Compressive Strength:** Usually **50–70%** of tensile strength for polymer composites.
- **Flexural Strength:** Determined by 3-point bending test (ASTM D790). Critical for beam-like structures.
- **Shear Strength:** Interlaminar shear strength (ILSS) tested via short beam shear (ASTM D2344). Typical CFRP: **70–100 MPa**.
- **Impact Resistance:** Measured by Charpy/Izod tests. Aramid composites have the highest impact resistance.
- **Fatigue Life:** CFRP shows **superior fatigue resistance** (S-N curve nearly flat) compared to metals.
- **Fracture Toughness ($K_{IC}$):** Typically **10–50 MPa√m** for CFRP—lower than metals but acceptable for design.

### 2.2.3 Thermal Properties

- **CTE ($\alpha$):** Can be **near zero** (or even negative) in some CFRP systems—useful for precision instruments.
- **Thermal Conductivity:** Carbon fiber composites have high conductivity (good for heat dissipation); GFRP is insulating.
- **Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$):** Maximum service temperature for polymer composites. Epoxy: $T_g \approx 120–180°C$.

### 2.2.4 Electrical Properties

- **Resistivity:** GFRP is insulating ($\rho > 10^{14}$ Ω·cm); CFRP can be conductive along fibers.
- **Dielectric Strength:** Important for radomes and electrical insulators.

### 2.2.5 Chemical Properties

- **Corrosion Resistance:** Polymer composites do not rust—major advantage over steel.
- **Chemical Resistance:** Depends on matrix. Epoxy resists most solvents; polyester is vulnerable to alkalis.
- **UV Degradation:** Unprotected polymer composites degrade under UV—surface coatings (gel coat) required.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **This is the EXAM-CRITICAL formula list. Practice solving at least 3 numerical problems from each formula.**

| # | Property | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Composite Density | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | Direct ROM |
| 2 | Longitudinal Modulus | $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Voigt (iso-strain) |
| 3 | Transverse Modulus | $\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}$ | Reuss (iso-stress) |
| 4 | Shear Modulus | $\frac{1}{G_{LT}} = \frac{V_f}{G_f} + \frac{V_m}{G_m}$ | Inverse ROM |
| 5 | Longitudinal Strength | $\sigma_L = \sigma_{f,u} V_f + \sigma_{m,u} V_m$ | Direct ROM |
| 6 | Poisson's Ratio | $\nu_{LT} = \nu_f V_f + \nu_m V_m$ | Direct ROM |
| 7 | Specific Strength | $SS = \sigma_u / \rho_c$ | Performance index |
| 8 | Specific Modulus | $SM = E / \rho_c$ | Performance index |
| 9 | Critical Fiber Length | $l_c = \sigma_f d / (2 \tau_i)$ | Minimum length |
| 10 | Fiber Efficiency Factor | $\eta = 1 - (l_c / 2l)$ for $l < l_c$ | 1.0 for $l \gg l_c$ |
| 11 | Halpin-Tsai (Transverse) | $E_T = E_m \frac{1 + 2\eta V_f}{1 - \eta V_f}$ where $\eta = \frac{E_f/E_m - 1}{E_f/E_m + 2}$ | Refined model |
| 12 | Halpin-Tsai (Shear) | $G_{LT} = G_m \frac{1 + \eta V_f}{1 - \eta V_f}$ where $\eta = \frac{G_f/G_m - 1}{G_f/G_m + 1}$ | Refined model |
| 13 | CTE (Longitudinal) | $\alpha_L = \frac{\alpha_f E_f V_f + \alpha_m E_m V_m}{E_f V_f + E_m V_m}$ | Stiffness-weighted |
| 14 | CTE (Transverse) | $\alpha_T = \alpha_f V_f + \alpha_m V_m$ | Direct ROM |
| 15 | Thermal Conductivity (L) | $K_L = K_f V_f + K_m V_m$ | Parallel model |
| 16 | Void Correction | $\rho_{act} = \rho_{th}(1 - V_v)$ | Quality metric |
| 17 | Electrical Conductivity (L) | $\sigma_{c,L} = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ | Direct ROM |
| 18 | Effective Fiber Stress | $\sigma_f^{eff} = \sigma_f \eta$ | For short fibers |
| 19 | Volume Fraction to Weight | $W_f = \frac{V_f \rho_f}{V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m}$ | Conversion |
| 20 | Stress in Matrix (long.) | $\sigma_m = E_m \varepsilon$ | Under iso-strain |

> [!TIP]  
> **Exam Strategy:** In numerical problems, always show the substitution step with **units in parentheses**. This earns you **1 free mark** for "unit awareness" in KTU valuation.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Comprehensive Derivation: Longitudinal Strength with Partial Fiber Efficiency

**Problem Setup:** In practice, fibers may not all be perfectly oriented or fully efficient. The **modified ROM** for strength includes an **orientation factor** $K$ and a **fiber length factor** $\eta_l$:

$$\sigma_L = K \eta_l \sigma_{f,u} V_f + \sigma_{m,u} V_m$$

Where:
- $K$ = fiber orientation factor (1.0 for $0°$, 0.25 for $90°$, ~0.4 for random in-plane)
- $\eta_l$ = fiber length efficiency factor
  - For $l \geq l_c$: $\eta_l \approx 1.0$
  - For $l < l_c$: $\eta_l = 1 - (l_c / 2l)$

**Derivation of $\eta_l$ for short fibers ($l < l_c$):**

The stress in the fiber builds up from zero at the ends to a maximum at the midpoint:

$$\sigma_f(x) = \frac{2 \tau_i x}{d}$$

For $x = l/2$ (maximum stress at center):

$$\sigma_{f,\max} = \frac{\tau_i l}{d}$$

The **average fiber stress** is half the maximum (linear profile):

$$\bar{\sigma}_f = \frac{\sigma_{f,\max}}{2} = \frac{\tau_i l}{2d}$$

Dividing by the ultimate fiber stress $\sigma_{f,u}$:

$$\eta_l = \frac{\bar{\sigma}_f}{\sigma_{f,u}} = \frac{\tau_i l}{2d \sigma_{f,u}} = \frac{l}{l_c}$$

But for $l \geq l_c$, $\eta_l$ saturates at 1.0. A smooth interpolation gives:

$$\eta_l = \begin{cases} \frac{l}{2 l_c} & \text{if } l < l_c \\ 1.0 & \text{if } l \geq l_c \end{cases}$$

**Combined form:**

$$\eta_l = 1 - \frac{l_c}{2l} \quad \text{for } l < l_c$$

## 3.2 Comprehensive Derivation: Specific Strength Comparison

**Objective:** Compare the specific strength of CFRP vs. structural steel.

**Given Data:**
- CFRP: $\sigma_u = 2500$ MPa, $\rho = 1.6$ g/cc
- Steel (AISI 4340): $\sigma_u = 1100$ MPa, $\rho = 7.85$ g/cc

**Calculation:**

For CFRP:

$$SS_{CFRP} = \frac{2500 \text{ MPa}}{1.6 \text{ g/cc}} = 1562.5 \text{ MPa·cc/g} = 1562.5 \text{ kNm/kg}$$

For Steel:

$$SS_{steel} = \frac{1100 \text{ MPa}}{7.85 \text{ g/cc}} = 140.1 \text{ kNm/kg}$$

**Ratio:**

$$\frac{SS_{CFRP}}{SS_{steel}} = \frac{1562.5}{140.1} \approx 11.15$$

**Conclusion:** CFRP has **~11× the specific strength** of high-strength steel—this is why it is preferred in aerospace primary structures (fuselage, wings).

## 3.3 Worked Example: Multi-Property Composite Analysis

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**

A unidirectional glass fiber-reinforced polyester composite is fabricated with the following properties:
- $V_f = 0.55$, $V_m = 0.45$
- $E_f = 85$ GPa, $E_m = 3.2$ GPa
- $\sigma_{f,u} = 3500$ MPa, $\sigma_{m,u} = 65$ MPa
- $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cc, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cc
- $d_f = 10 \mu m$, $\tau_i = 45$ MPa
- $l_f = 5$ mm (actual fiber length)

**Calculate:** (a) $\rho_c$, (b) $E_L$, (c) $E_T$, (d) $\sigma_L$, (e) $SS$, (f) $SM$, (g) $l_c$, (h) $\eta_l$, (i) Modified $\sigma_L$ accounting for short fibers.

### Solution:

**(a) Composite Density:**

$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m = (2.55)(0.55) + (1.20)(0.45)$$
$$\rho_c = 1.4025 + 0.54 = 1.9425 \text{ g/cc}$$

**(b) Longitudinal Modulus:**

$$E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m = (85)(0.55) + (3.2)(0.45)$$
$$E_L = 46.75 + 1.44 = 48.19 \text{ GPa}$$

**(c) Transverse Modulus:**

$$\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} = \frac{0.55}{85} + \frac{0.45}{3.2}$$
$$\frac{1}{E_T} = 0.006471 + 0.1406 = 0.1471 \text{ GPa}^{-1}$$
$$E_T = 6.80 \text{ GPa}$$

**(d) Longitudinal Strength (Ideal ROM):**

$$\sigma_L = \sigma_{f,u} V_f + \sigma_{m,u} V_m = (3500)(0.55) + (65)(0.45)$$
$$\sigma_L = 1925 + 29.25 = 1954.25 \text{ MPa}$$

**(e) Specific Strength:**

$$SS = \frac{\sigma_L}{\rho_c} = \frac{1954.25 \text{ MPa}}{1.9425 \text{ g/cc}} = 1006.04 \text{ kNm/kg}$$

**(f) Specific Modulus:**

$$SM = \frac{E_L}{\rho_c} = \frac{48.19 \text{ GPa}}{1.9425 \text{ g/cc}} = 24.81 \text{ M Nm/kg} = 24.81 \times 10^3 \text{ kNm/kg}$$

Wait—unit correction. Since $E_L$ is in GPa = $10^3$ MPa:

$$SM = \frac{48.19 \times 10^3 \text{ MPa}}{1.9425 \text{ g/cc}} = 24,810 \text{ kNm/kg}$$

This is a **common unit error** in KTU papers—always convert to consistent units.

**(g) Critical Fiber Length:**

$$l_c = \frac{\sigma_f d}{2 \tau_i} = \frac{(3500 \times 10^6)(10 \times 10^{-6})}{2 \times 45 \times 10^6}$$
$$l_c = \frac{35}{90} = 0.389 \text{ mm} = 389 \mu m$$

**(h) Length Efficiency Factor:**

Since $l_f = 5$ mm $> l_c = 0.389$ mm:

$$\eta_l = 1.0$$

(Or more precisely, $l/l_c = 5/0.389 \approx 12.9$, which is well above the threshold of 10.)

**(i) Modified Longitudinal Strength:**

Since $\eta_l = 1$ and assuming aligned fibers ($K = 1$):

$$\sigma_L^{mod} = (1.0)(1.0)(3500)(0.55) + (65)(0.45) = 1954.25 \text{ MPa}$$

The strength is unchanged because fibers are long enough for full efficiency.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Property Comparison Table

| Composite | $\rho$ (g/cc) | $E_L$ (GPa) | $\sigma_L$ (MPa) | $SS$ (kNm/kg) | $SM$ (kNm/kg) | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| CFRP (HS Carbon) | 1.6 | 140 | 2500 | 1562 | 87,500 | Very High |
| GFRP (E-Glass) | 1.9 | 45 | 1800 | 947 | 23,684 | Low |
| AFRP (Kevlar) | 1.4 | 75 | 2400 | 1714 | 53,571 | High |
| Al 6061-T6 | 2.7 | 69 | 310 | 115 | 25,556 | Low |
| Steel (AISI 4340) | 7.85 | 200 | 1100 | 140 | 25,478 | Medium |
| Ti-6Al-4V | 4.43 | 114 | 950 | 214 | 25,734 | Very High |

> [!TIP]  
> **Exam Insight:** Notice that **GFRP has higher specific modulus than Al and Steel**—this is why wind turbine blades (long, stiffness-critical) use glass fiber composites, not metals!
