# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Define the term "specific strength" of a composite material. Why is it a critical parameter in aerospace structural design?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Specific strength is defined as the ratio of the ultimate tensile strength ($\sigma_u$) to the density ($\rho$) of the material:

$$SS = \frac{\sigma_u}{\rho}$$

It is typically expressed in units of **kNm/kg** or **MPa·cc/g**.

**Significance in Aerospace (2 Marks):**
- Aircraft and spacecraft structures must be **lightweight** to maximize payload and fuel efficiency.
- CFRP has a specific strength of approximately **1500–2500 kNm/kg**, which is **5–10 times higher** than conventional metals like steel ($\approx 140$ kNm/kg).
- Higher specific strength translates to **lower structural weight** for the same load-bearing capacity, enabling **longer flight ranges** and **reduced emissions**.
- The Boeing 787 Dreamliner uses **50% composite materials by weight** primarily because of this property.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Explain the "Rule of Mixtures" with respect to the density of a fiber-reinforced composite. What assumption does it make regarding void content?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Rule of Mixtures for Density (2 Marks):** The density of a fiber-reinforced composite is predicted as a **weighted average** of the densities of the fiber and matrix phases, with the weights being their respective volume fractions:

$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$$

This equation is derived from **mass conservation**, where the total mass of the composite equals the sum of the masses of its constituents, assuming both phases are present in their stated volume fractions.

**Assumption Regarding Voids (1 Mark):** The basic Rule of Mixtures assumes a **void-free composite** ($V_v = 0$). In practice, voids are always present due to imperfect manufacturing, and their effect is accounted for by a correction factor:

$$\rho_{actual} = \rho_{theoretical} (1 - V_v)$$

Typical void content should be kept **below 1–2%** for high-performance composites.

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) - Module Internal Choice

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** Derive the expressions for the **longitudinal Young's modulus** and **transverse Young's modulus** of a unidirectional fiber-reinforced composite using the Rule of Mixtures. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A unidirectional carbon fiber-reinforced epoxy composite has the following properties:
- $V_f = 0.65$, $V_m = 0.35$
- $E_f = 230$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa
- $\sigma_{f,u} = 4000$ MPa, $\sigma_{m,u} = 90$ MPa
- $\rho_f = 1.80$ g/cc, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cc

**Calculate:**
- (i) The longitudinal Young's modulus $E_L$
- (ii) The transverse Young's modulus $E_T$
- (iii) The longitudinal tensile strength $\sigma_L$
- (iv) The specific strength and specific modulus of the composite

**(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Longitudinal Modulus — Voigt Model (3.5 Marks):**

**Assumptions (1 Mark):**
- Fibers are continuous, parallel, and uniformly distributed within the matrix.
- Perfect bonding exists at the fiber-matrix interface (no slip or debonding).
- Both fiber and matrix experience the same longitudinal strain (iso-strain condition).
- Loading is applied parallel to the fiber direction.
- Both constituents behave as linear elastic materials.

**Derivation (2.5 Marks):** Under iso-strain condition, $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon$. The total load on the composite is the sum of loads carried by the fiber and matrix:

$$P_c = P_f + P_m$$

Substituting $P = \sigma A = E\varepsilon A$:

$$E_c \varepsilon A_c = E_f \varepsilon A_f + E_m \varepsilon A_m$$

Dividing by $A_c$ and recognizing area fractions as volume fractions for aligned fibers:

$$\boxed{E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m}$$

**Transverse Modulus — Reuss Model (3.5 Marks):**

**Assumptions (1 Mark):**
- Loading is applied perpendicular to the fiber direction.
- Both phases experience the same stress (iso-stress condition).
- Fibers are still continuous, parallel, and perfectly bonded.

**Derivation (2.5 Marks):** Under iso-stress, $\sigma_c = \sigma_f = \sigma_m = \sigma$. The total strain is the sum of strains in each phase:

$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f V_f + \varepsilon_m V_m$$

Using $\varepsilon = \sigma/E$:

$$\frac{\sigma}{E_T} = \frac{\sigma}{E_f} V_f + \frac{\sigma}{E_m} V_m$$

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Longitudinal Modulus (1.5 Marks):**

$$E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m = (230)(0.65) + (3.5)(0.35)$$
$$E_L = 149.5 + 1.225 = 150.73 \text{ GPa}$$

**(ii) Transverse Modulus (2 Marks):**

$$\frac{1}{E_T} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} = \frac{0.65}{230} + \frac{0.35}{3.5}$$
$$\frac{1}{E_T} = 0.002826 + 0.10 = 0.10283 \text{ GPa}^{-1}$$
$$E_T = 9.72 \text{ GPa}$$

**(iii) Longitudinal Strength (1.5 Marks):**

$$\sigma_L = \sigma_{f,u} V_f + \sigma_{m,u} V_m = (4000)(0.65) + (90)(0.35)$$
$$\sigma_L = 2600 + 31.5 = 2631.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**(iv) Specific Strength and Modulus (2 Marks):**

First, composite density:

$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m = (1.80)(0.65) + (1.20)(0.35) = 1.17 + 0.42 = 1.59 \text{ g/cc}$$

Specific strength:

$$SS = \frac{2631.5 \text{ MPa}}{1.59 \text{ g/cc}} = 1655.0 \text{ kNm/kg}$$

Specific modulus:

$$SM = \frac{150.73 \times 10^3 \text{ MPa}}{1.59 \text{ g/cc}} = 94,799 \text{ kNm/kg}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Define the **critical fiber length**. Derive its expression starting from first principles. Discuss its importance in the design of fiber-reinforced composites. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A glass fiber of diameter $d = 12 \mu m$ and tensile strength $\sigma_f = 2.5$ GPa is embedded in a polyester matrix with interfacial shear strength $\tau_i = 35$ MPa.
- (i) Calculate the **critical fiber length** $l_c$.
- (ii) Find the **critical aspect ratio** $(l_c/d)$.
- (iii) If the actual fiber length is $l = 2$ mm, determine the **fiber length efficiency factor** $\eta_l$ and comment on the load transfer efficiency.
- (iv) If the volume fraction of fibers is $V_f = 0.50$, calculate the **effective contribution of fibers** to the composite strength (assuming $\sigma_{m,u} = 70$ MPa and full matrix contribution). **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Theory and Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** The **critical fiber length** ($l_c$) is defined as the **minimum length** of a fiber required to develop the full ultimate tensile strength of the fiber through shear stress transfer at the fiber-matrix interface. Below this length, the fiber will pull out of the matrix before reaching its maximum stress.

**Derivation (4 Marks):** Consider a fiber of diameter $d$ embedded in a matrix. The fiber-matrix interface can transfer shear stress $\tau_i$ along its surface. The tensile force in the fiber builds up from zero at the fiber ends to a maximum at the center of the fiber (assuming $l \geq l_c$).

The maximum tensile force in the fiber:

$$F_{tensile} = \sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

The shear force transferred at the interface over half the critical length (from one end to the midpoint):

$$F_{shear} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot \frac{l_c}{2}$$

For the fiber to develop its ultimate strength, these forces must be equal:

$$\sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot \frac{l_c}{2}$$

Simplifying:

$$\sigma_f \cdot \frac{d}{4} = \tau_i \cdot \frac{l_c}{2}$$

Solving for $l_c$:

$$\boxed{l_c = \frac{\sigma_f d}{2 \tau_i}}$$

**Importance in Composite Design (2 Marks):**
- $l_c$ determines the **minimum fiber length** for effective reinforcement.
- For efficient load transfer, the actual fiber length $l$ should be **at least 10 to 15 times** $l_c$ (i.e., $l \geq 10 l_c$).
- If $l < l_c$, the fiber pulls out prematurely, leading to low composite strength.
- $l_c$ influences **fiber selection** and **manufacturing processes** (e.g., continuous fiber vs. short fiber composites).

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

Given: $d = 12 \mu m = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $\sigma_f = 2.5 \times 10^9$ Pa, $\tau_i = 35 \times 10^6$ Pa.

**(i) Critical Fiber Length (1.5 Marks):**

$$l_c = \frac{\sigma_f d}{2 \tau_i} = \frac{(2.5 \times 10^9)(12 \times 10^{-6})}{2 \times 35 \times 10^6}$$
$$l_c = \frac{30}{70} = 0.4286 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 0.4286 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Critical Aspect Ratio (1 Mark):**

$$\frac{l_c}{d} = \frac{0.4286 \times 10^{-3}}{12 \times 10^{-6}} = 35.7$$

**(iii) Length Efficiency Factor (1.5 Marks):**

Given $l = 2$ mm, $l_c = 0.4286$ mm. Since $l > l_c$:

$$\eta_l = 1.0$$

More precisely, $l/l_c = 2/0.4286 \approx 4.67$. Since $l/l_c < 10$, the efficiency is technically:

$$\eta_l = 1 - \frac{l_c}{2l} = 1 - \frac{0.4286}{2 \times 2} = 1 - 0.107 = 0.893$$

So the fiber achieves **89.3% of its maximum possible stress**. This is acceptable but not optimal—**continuous fibers** would be preferred for full efficiency.

**(iv) Effective Fiber Contribution (3 Marks):**

The effective fiber stress contribution to composite strength:

$$\sigma_f^{eff} = \eta_l \cdot \sigma_f \cdot V_f = 0.893 \times 2500 \times 0.50 = 1116.25 \text{ MPa}$$

The matrix contribution:

$$\sigma_m^{contrib} = \sigma_{m,u} \cdot V_m = 70 \times 0.50 = 35 \text{ MPa}$$

Total composite strength:

$$\sigma_L = 1116.25 + 35 = 1151.25 \text{ MPa}$$

Compared to the ideal ROM prediction ($\sigma_L = 2500 \times 0.5 + 70 \times 0.5 = 1285$ MPa), the actual strength is **~10.4% lower** due to short fiber inefficiency.

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — CRITICAL PITFALLS**  
>  
> **1. Unit Inconsistency (Loss: 1–2 marks):** Always convert $\mu m$ to m and MPa to Pa before substituting. Many students write $l_c = (2500 \times 12) / (2 \times 35) = 428$ mm instead of 0.4286 mm—a **factor of 1000 error**.  
>  
> **2. Voigt vs. Reuss Confusion (Loss: 2–3 marks):** This is the **most common mistake**. The longitudinal modulus uses **direct ROM (Voigt)**, while the transverse modulus uses **inverse ROM (Reuss)**. Using Voigt for both directions will give a wrong answer for $E_T$.  
>  
> **3. Volume Fraction Sum (Loss: 1 mark):** If the problem gives $V_f = 0.65$ but not $V_m$, **always calculate $V_m = 1 - V_f = 0.35$**. Don't assume $V_m = V_f$.  
>  
> **4. Specific Modulus Units (Loss: 1 mark):** $E$ is typically in GPa. To get specific modulus in kNm/kg, you must convert to MPa first: $E_{GPa} \times 1000 = E_{MPa}$.  
>  
> **5. Assuming Perfect ROM Validity:** The Rule of Mixtures is an **upper-bound estimate**. Mention this limitation in your answer to fetch full marks—especially for the transverse direction where Halpin-Tsai gives more realistic values.  
>  
> **6. Not Stating Assumptions:** KTU examiners allocate **1–2 marks specifically for assumptions**. Always list them explicitly.  
>  
> **7. Critical Length Formula Sign:** Use $l_c = \sigma_f d / (2\tau_i)$, **not** $l_c = \sigma_f d / \tau_i$ (the 2 in the denominator is often missed).

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

### ⚡ High-Yield Concepts (Must Memorize)

- [x] **Rule of Mixtures** is the **foundation** for all property predictions in composites.
- [x] **Voigt Model (Iso-strain)** → longitudinal direction: $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$
- [x] **Reuss Model (Iso-stress)** → transverse direction: $1/E_T = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$
- [x] **Specific Strength** $SS = \sigma_u / \rho$ — units: **kNm/kg**
- [x] **Specific Modulus** $SM = E / \rho$ — units: **kNm/kg** (remember GPa → MPa conversion!)
- [x] **Critical Fiber Length** $l_c = \sigma_f d / (2\tau_i)$ — minimum length for full efficiency
- [x] For **efficient reinforcement**: $l \geq 10 l_c$ (or conservatively, $15 l_c$)
- [x] **Length efficiency** for short fibers: $\eta_l = 1 - l_c/(2l)$ when $l < l_c$; $\eta_l = 1$ when $l \geq l_c$
- [x] **Void correction**: $\rho_{actual} = \rho_{theoretical}(1 - V_v)$
- [x] **Halpin-Tsai** is a refined alternative to Reuss for transverse modulus.

### 📊 Key Numerical Values to Remember

| Material | $\rho$ (g/cc) | $E$ (GPa) | $\sigma_u$ (MPa) | $SS$ (kNm/kg) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CFRP (HS) | 1.6 | 140 | 2500 | 1562 |
| GFRP | 1.9 | 45 | 1800 | 947 |
| AFRP | 1.4 | 75 | 2400 | 1714 |
| Steel | 7.85 | 200 | 1100 | 140 |
| Al 6061 | 2.7 | 69 | 310 | 115 |
| Ti-6Al-4V | 4.43 | 114 | 950 | 214 |

### 🎯 Common KTU Question Patterns

1. **"Derive the longitudinal/transverse modulus"** — Always state assumptions + iso-strain/iso-stress.
2. **"Calculate specific strength/modulus"** — Remember to compute $\rho_c$ first.
3. **"A fiber of diameter d, length l, with σ_f and τ_i is given. Find l_c and comment on efficiency."** — Compare $l$ with $l_c$ and $10 l_c$.
4. **"Compare properties of CFRP, GFRP, AFRP"** — Use the comparison table above.
5. **"What happens if voids are present?"** — Apply the void correction formula and discuss property degradation.

### 🛠️ Engineering Application Insights

- **Aerospace**: CFRP for primary structures (highest $SS$ and $SM$).
- **Wind Energy**: GFRP for blades (cost-effective, good $SM$).
- **Automotive**: GFRP body panels (corrosion resistance + weight savings).
- **Sports**: CFRP for high-performance gear (stiffness + low weight).
- **Civil**: FRP rebars in concrete (corrosion resistance in marine environments).
- **Marine**: GFRP hulls (corrosion resistance + impact tolerance).

### 📐 Master Formula Card (Last-Minute Revision)

| Category | Formula |
|---|---|
| Density | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ |
| $E_L$ (Voigt) | $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ |
| $E_T$ (Reuss) | $1/E_T = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$ |
| $\sigma_L$ | $\sigma_L = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ |
| $SS$ | $SS = \sigma_u / \rho_c$ |
| $SM$ | $SM = E / \rho_c$ |
| $l_c$ | $l_c = \sigma_f d / (2\tau_i)$ |
| $\eta_l$ | $\eta_l = 1 - l_c/(2l)$ for $l < l_c$ |
| $\rho_{void}$ | $\rho_{act} = \rho_{th}(1 - V_v)$ |
| $\alpha_L$ | $\alpha_L = (\alpha_f E_f V_f + \alpha_m E_m V_m)/(E_f V_f + E_m V_m)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Final Exam Tip:** For numerical problems in KTU, always:  
> 1. List given data with units.  
> 2. State the formula you are using.  
> 3. Substitute values with units in parentheses.  
> 4. Show intermediate steps (don't skip algebra).  
> 5. State the final answer with **correct units**.  
> 6. Add a concluding remark (e.g., "The fiber is efficient since $l > 10 l_c$" or "CFRP has superior specific strength to steel").  
>   
> This structured approach typically fetches **90%+ marks** in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics and Applications as Matrix Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET413 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Characteristics and Applications as Matrix Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In a composite material, the **matrix** is the continuous phase that completely envelops and binds together the dispersed reinforcement phase (fibers, whiskers, or particulates). The matrix serves four primary engineering functions:

1. **Load Transfer Medium:** Transfers applied external loads to the high-strength reinforcement through shear stresses developed at the fiber-matrix interface.
2. **Geometric Stabilizer:** Maintains the position, orientation, and spacing of the reinforcement within the composite architecture.
3. **Environmental Shield:** Protects the reinforcement from surface damage, moisture ingress, chemical attack, oxidation, and abrasion.
4. **Crack Arrestor:** Deflects, blunts, or bridges propagating cracks, thereby improving fracture toughness.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The matrix is *NOT* the load-bearing constituent in a fiber-reinforced composite. Its primary duty is to **distribute** load uniformly to the fibers and to **protect** them. Fibers carry the load; the matrix carries the shear.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **reinforced concrete structure** used in construction:
- The **steel rebar** (high tensile strength, brittle in compression) = the **reinforcement/fiber**.
- The **cement concrete** (relatively weak in tension, good in compression, cheap, mouldable) = the **matrix**.

The concrete "hugs" the rebar, holds it in position, transfers compressive and shear loads to it, and shields it from rust. Without concrete, the rebar would buckle immediately. Without rebar, the concrete would crack under tension. Neither alone is as effective as the combination. This is exactly how the **matrix** works in a fiber-reinforced composite.

## 1.3 Essential Characteristics of an Ideal Matrix Material

A KTU-board examiner expects a student to enumerate these **key desirable properties** of a matrix:

| S.No. | Characteristic | Engineering Reason |
|:-----:|:---------------|:-------------------|
| 1 | **High modulus and strength** (at least comparable to the reinforcement in the transverse direction) | Prevents the matrix from being the weakest link |
| 2 | **Good fibre-matrix adhesion / bonding** | Ensures efficient load transfer via shear |
| 3 | **Low shrinkage** during cure / processing | Reduces internal residual stresses and microcracks |
| 4 | **Chemical inertness** to fibres | Avoids degradation of the reinforcement |
| 5 | **Thermal stability** (low CTE mismatch with fibre) | Minimizes thermal stress at the interface |
| 6 | **Ease of processing** (low viscosity, good wetting) | Allows fibres to be fully impregnated |
| 7 | **Toughness / ductility** (especially for polymer matrices) | Improves impact resistance and damage tolerance |
| 8 | **Resistance to moisture, solvents, and UV** | Ensures long-term durability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:**
> - The **rule of mixtures** for transverse modulus of a unidirectional composite is dominated by the **matrix modulus**:
>
> $$\frac{1}{E_{2}} = \frac{V_f}{E_{f}} + \frac{V_m}{E_{m}}$$
>
> where $V_m$ is the **matrix volume fraction** and $E_m$ is the **matrix Young's modulus**. Thus, a low $E_m$ drags down $E_2$ (transverse stiffness) drastically.

## 1.4 Classification of Matrix Materials (KTU Module 2 Focus)

Composite matrices fall into three major families:

1. **Polymer Matrix Composites (PMCs)** — Most common, lowest processing temperature, lowest cost.
2. **Metal Matrix Composites (MMCs)** — High-temperature service, superior transverse strength, ductile matrix.
3. **Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMCs)** — Extreme-temperature and wear applications, brittle matrix toughened by fibres.

> [!NOTE]
> Each family has its own trade-off triangle: **Cost ↔ Performance ↔ Processability**. The matrix choice is dictated by the **end-use operating envelope** (temperature, stress, environment).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Function-by-Function Theoretical Breakdown

### (A) Load Transfer Mechanism — The Shear-Lag Theory
When a composite is loaded parallel to aligned fibres, the matrix does **not** carry the axial load directly. It transfers load to the fibre through **interfacial shear stress** $\tau_i$. The classical **shear-lag model** gives the fibre axial stress build-up over a critical length $l_c$:

$$\sigma_{f}(x) = \frac{\tau_i \, x}{r_f}$$

The fibre reaches its ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{fu}$ when the shear transfer length is the **critical fibre length**:

$$l_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \, d_f}{2 \tau_i}$$

> **Why it matters:** If the matrix is too soft (low $\tau_i$), the fibre will "slip" before reaching its full strength → inefficient composite. This is why **interfacial shear strength** is the single most important matrix-controlled property.

### (B) Thermal Residual Stress
A fibre with CTE $\alpha_f$ embedded in a matrix with CTE $\alpha_m$ cools from processing temperature $T_p$ to service temperature $T_s$. The misfit strain is:

$$\Delta \varepsilon_{th} = (\alpha_m - \alpha_f)(T_p - T_s)$$

> **Practical Insight:** For carbon fibre ($\alpha_f \approx -0.5 \times 10^{-6}/\text{K}$, actually slightly negative) in epoxy ($\alpha_m \approx 60 \times 10^{-6}/\text{K}$), the matrix wants to shrink much more than the fibre → puts the **fibre in compression** and the **matrix in tension**. This is actually beneficial for tensile loading but may cause **matrix microcracking** in CMCs.

### (C) Chemical / Environmental Protection
The matrix must act as a **diffusion barrier** against oxidising species (O₂, H₂O). For CMCs operating at 1000 °C+, an oxidation-resistant matrix (e.g., SiC) is mandatory because carbon fibres burn at ~400 °C in air.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All critical numerical relations you must memorize for the ESE. Note: vertical bars are rendered as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Relation | Physical Meaning | Units |
|:-:|:------------------|:-----------------|:------|
| 1 | $\sigma_f(x) = \dfrac{\tau_i \, x}{r_f}$ | Fibre stress build-up via interfacial shear (shear-lag) | Pa |
| 2 | $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_{fu} \, d_f}{2\,\tau_i}$ | Critical fibre length for full fibre strength utilisation | m |
| 3 | $\eta_l = 1 - \dfrac{l_c}{2 l} \quad (l > l_c)$ | Fibre-length efficiency factor (short-fibre correction) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\sigma_c = \sigma_{fu} V_f (1 - l_c/2l) + \sigma_m (1 - V_f)$ | Modified rule of mixtures for **short** aligned fibres | Pa |
| 5 | $E_{2}^{-1} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_{f}} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_{m}}$ | Transverse modulus (series model, matrix-dominated) | Pa⁻¹ |
| 6 | $\Delta \varepsilon_{th} = (\alpha_m - \alpha_f)(T_p - T_s)$ | Thermal misfit strain | dimensionless |
| 7 | $V_f + V_m = 1$ | Volume-fraction closure relation | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | Composite density (rule of mixtures) | kg/m³ |
| 9 | $T_{gd}$ = glass transition temperature | Upper service limit for polymer matrix | °C |
| 10 | $K_{IC,c} \approx V_f \cdot K_{IC,f} + V_m \cdot K_{IC,m} + K_{IC}^{\text{interface}}$ | Fracture toughness (interfacial contribution) | MPa·m^½ |

> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** These equations are the design backbone of **aerospace** (carbon/epoxy skins), **automotive** (glass/polyester bumpers), and **rocket nozzles** (SiC/SiC CMCs). A practising composite engineer uses the *critical fibre length* equation to decide whether to use **continuous** (l >> l_c) or **chopped short fibres** (l ≈ l_c).

---

## 2.3 Engineering Trade-off Triangle for Matrix Selection

```
                    COST
                    /\
                   /  \
                  /    \
                 /      \
                / TRADE- \
               / TRIANGLE \
              /            \
             /______________\
   PROCESSABILITY    PERFORMANCE
                  (Tm, Tg, σ, τi)
```

- **Polymer matrices** → High processability, low cost, low performance ceiling.
- **Metal matrices** → Medium cost, medium processability, high performance.
- **Ceramic matrices** → High performance, very low processability (sintering required), very high cost.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Critical Fibre Length from Shear-Lag

### Step 0 — Define the Geometry
Consider a single cylindrical fibre of diameter $d_f$ (radius $r_f = d_f / 2$) embedded coaxially in a cylindrical matrix sleeve. An axial tensile load $F$ is applied to the fibre end.

### Step 1 — Force Equilibrium on an Elemental Slice
Take an elemental slice of the fibre of length $dx$ at distance $x$ from the loaded end. Let the fibre axial stress be $\sigma_f(x)$ and the interfacial shear stress be $\tau_i(x)$.

The fibre carries load: $F_f(x) = \sigma_f(x) \cdot A_f$ where $A_f = \pi r_f^{2}$.

The shear stress on the cylindrical interface of area $2\pi r_f \, dx$ acts to **increase** the fibre load as $x$ increases (load is dragged in by shear).

**Force balance on the slice:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_f(x + dx) - F_f(x) &= \tau_i(x) \cdot (2 \pi r_f \, dx) \\
\sigma_f(x + dx) \cdot \pi r_f^{2} - \sigma_f(x) \cdot \pi r_f^{2} &= 2 \pi r_f \, \tau_i(x) \, dx \\
\frac{d \sigma_f}{dx} \cdot \pi r_f^{2} &= 2 \pi r_f \, \tau_i \\
\frac{d \sigma_f}{dx} &= \frac{2 \tau_i}{r_f}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** This is a *first-order* differential equation describing how the fibre axial stress grows with distance from the loaded end. The matrix effectively "drags" the load into the fibre through interfacial shear.

### Step 2 — Integrate Assuming Constant $\tau_i$
If we assume the matrix is plasticized enough that $\tau_i$ is constant and equal to its **interfacial shear yield strength** $\tau_{iy}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_0^{\sigma_f} d\sigma_f &= \int_0^x \frac{2 \tau_{iy}}{r_f} \, dx \\
\sigma_f(x) &= \frac{2 \tau_{iy} \, x}{r_f} = \frac{4 \tau_{iy} \, x}{d_f}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Critical Length Definition
The fibre reaches its ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{fu}$ when the loaded length equals the **critical length** $l_c$. Setting $\sigma_f(l_c) = \sigma_{fu}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{fu} &= \frac{2 \tau_{iy} \, l_c}{r_f} \\
l_c &= \frac{\sigma_{fu} \, r_f}{2 \tau_{iy}} = \frac{\sigma_{fu} \, d_f}{4 \tau_{iy}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Note on conventions:** Some textbooks define $l_c$ as the length over which the fibre builds from 0 to $\sigma_{fu}$, hence $\dfrac{\sigma_{fu} d_f}{2 \tau_i}$. The factor depends on whether the **average** or the **maximum** interface shear is used. KTU typically accepts either as long as the derivation is clear.

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example
**Problem:** A glass fibre ($d_f = 10\,\mu m$, $\sigma_{fu} = 3.5$ GPa) is embedded in an epoxy matrix with $\tau_{iy} = 35$ MPa. Calculate $l_c$ and comment.

$$
\begin{aligned}
l_c &= \frac{\sigma_{fu} \, d_f}{4 \tau_{iy}} \\
&= \frac{3.5 \times 10^{9} \times 10 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \times 35 \times 10^{6}} \\
&= \frac{35}{140} \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \\
&= 0.25 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Comment:** Critical length is only 0.25 mm. Any glass fibre longer than ~1.25 mm (i.e., 5× $l_c$) will behave as "long" and approach continuous-fibre efficiency. This explains why **chopped-strand mat (CSM)** GFRP with fibre lengths of 25–50 mm is so effective in practice.

### Step 5 — Fibre-Length Efficiency Factor
For a fibre of actual length $l < l_c$ (short-fibre regime), the **average** fibre stress over its length is half the peak:

$$
\bar{\sigma}_f = \frac{\sigma_{fu}}{2}
$$

For $l > l_c$, the central portion is fully loaded and only the two end regions of length $l_c/2$ each are partially loaded:

$$
\eta_l = 1 - \frac{l_c}{2l}
$$

The modified rule of mixtures for short aligned fibres becomes:

$$
\sigma_c = \eta_l \cdot \sigma_{fu} \cdot V_f + \sigma_m (1 - V_f)
$$

> **Engineering Take-away:** Doubling fibre length beyond $l_c$ yields diminishing returns. Most engineering short-fibre composites are designed with $l \approx 10\,l_c$ to $100\,l_c$ to balance performance with processability.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Matrix Material

The decision matrix below maps **service requirements → matrix family → candidate material**.

| Service Requirement | Best Matrix Family | Specific Material | Why |
|:--------------------|:-------------------|:------------------|:----|
| Room temperature, low cost, mouldability | PMC (Thermoset) | Polyester / Vinyl ester | Low viscosity, RT cure |
| Moderate T (≤ 150 °C), aerospace structural | PMC (Thermoset) | Epoxy | High strength, low shrinkage |
| High T (≤ 250 °C), aerospace, low flammability | PMC (Thermoplastic) | PEEK, PPS | High $T_g$, recyclable |
| T up to 400–500 °C, high stiffness | MMC | Al-SiC, Al-Al₂O₃ | Lightweight, stiff, conducts heat |
| T up to 800–1000 °C, oxidation resistance | CMC | SiC matrix (with SiC fibre) | Ceramic stability, toughened |
| T > 1000 °C, hypersonic / re-entry | CMC / Carbon-Carbon | C-C, SiC-SiC | Only viable matrix class |
| Wear / bearing surfaces | MMC | Al-SiC, Cu-graphite | Hard particles, self-lubricating |
| Biomedical implants | PMC (Biocompatible) | UHMWPE, PMMA | Bio-inert, sterilizable |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Matrix-Role Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Load Applied] --> B[Matrix Receives Load]
    B --> C{Load Path Decision}
    C -- Axial Component --> D[Transfer to Fibres via Interfacial Shear tau_i]
    C -- Transverse Component --> E[Matrix Carries Directly]
    D --> F[Fibres Reach sigma_fu]
    F --> G[Composite Achieves sigma_c = sigma_fu V_f + sigma_m V_m]
    E --> G
    G --> H[Service Performance]
    H --> I{Service Environment}
    I -- Hot --> J[Check Tg or Tm]
    I -- Wet --> K[Check Moisture Absorption]
    I -- Cyclic --> L[Check Fatigue Resistance]
    J --> M[Matrix Selection: PMT or MMC or CMC]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Final Composite in Service]
```

> **Reading the Flow:** Every engineering decision on matrix material starts from the **load path** at the top, branches through the **service environment**, and terminates at a **matrix selection**.

---

## 4.2 Matrix Family Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Matrix Materials] --> P[Polymer Matrix PMCs]
    M --> ME[Metal Matrix MMCs]
    M --> CE[Ceramic Matrix CMCs]
    P --> P1[Thermosets]
    P --> P2[Thermoplastics]
    P1 --> P1a[Epoxy]
    P1 --> P1b[Polyester]
    P1 --> P1c[Vinyl Ester]
    P1 --> P1d[Phenolic]
    P1 --> P1e[Bismaleimide BMI]
    P2 --> P2a[PEEK]
    P2 --> P2b[PPS]
    P2 --> P2c[PEI]
    P2 --> P2d[Nylon PA66]
    ME --> ME1[Aluminium Alloys]
    ME --> ME2[Titanium Alloys]
    ME --> ME3[Magnesium Alloys]
    CE --> CE1[Silicon Carbide SiC]
    CE --> CE2[Alumina Al2O3]
    CE --> CE3[Glass-Ceramic]
    CE --> CE4[Carbon-Carbon C-C]
```

---

## 4.3 Interfacial Load-Transfer Block Diagram (Shear-Lag Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Fibre[Single Fibre of length l]
        A1[Fibre End x=0] -- sigma_f grows --> M1[Mid Fibre x=l/2]
        M1 -- sigma_f saturates --> B1[Fibre End x=l]
    end
    subgraph Matrix[Surrounding Matrix]
        SH[Interfacial Shear tau_i x 2 pi r_f]
    end
    SH -- Drag load into --> A1
    SH -- Drag load into --> B1
    B1 --> LC[Critical Length l_c equals sigma_fu d_f over 4 tau_i]
    A1 --> LC
```

> **Note on Convention:** The Mermaid block above is a *functional* representation — it cannot render the smooth gradient of $\sigma_f(x)$ physically. The actual stress profile is a **piecewise linear ramp** of slope $2\tau_i/r_f$ over length $l_c/2$ at each end, with a flat plateau at $\sigma_{fu}$ in the middle (for $l > l_c$).

---

## 4.4 Comparative Performance Envelope of Matrix Families

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Service Temperature envelope] --> B[Room T to 200 C: PMC Thermoset]
    A --> C[200 to 300 C: PMC Thermoplastic PEEK]
    A --> D[300 to 600 C: MMC Aluminium Titanium]
    A --> E[600 to 1200 C: CMC SiC or Al2O3]
    A --> F[Above 1500 C: Carbon Carbon C-C]
    B --> G[Cost Low]
    C --> G
    D --> H[Cost Medium High]
    E --> I[Cost High]
    F --> J[Cost Very High]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any six desirable characteristics that a matrix material must possess in a fiber-reinforced composite. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **High strength and modulus** (at least comparable to the reinforcement in the transverse direction) so it is not the weakest link.
2. **Good fibre–matrix interfacial bonding** to ensure efficient load transfer via interfacial shear ($\tau_i$).
3. **Low shrinkage** during curing / solidification to minimize internal residual stresses.
4. **Thermal stability** — glass transition temperature $T_g$ (for polymers) or melting point $T_m$ (for metals) should exceed the service temperature.
5. **Low coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) mismatch** with the reinforcement to avoid thermal cracking.
6. **Chemical inertness** with respect to the fibres (so it does not corrode or degrade the reinforcement).
7. **Toughness / ductility** for damage tolerance and impact resistance.
8. **Ease of processing** — low melt viscosity (thermoplastics) or long pot-life / low viscosity (thermosets) for fibre impregnation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write generic "high strength" without specifying the **direction** (transverse). The matrix is not chosen for axial strength — the fibres do that. Always say "comparable to fibre in the transverse direction."

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Distinguish between a thermoplastic matrix and a thermosetting matrix in polymer composites. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Thermoset (e.g., Epoxy) | Thermoplastic (e.g., PEEK) |
|:--------|:------------------------|:----------------------------|
| Curing | Irreversible cross-linking by chemical reaction; cannot be remelted | No cross-linking; softens on heating, hardens on cooling — fully reversible |
| $T_g$ | Defines upper service limit (typically 100–250 °C) | Defines upper service limit (typically 150–300 °C for engineering grades) |
| Viscosity during processing | Low (liquid resin) — excellent fibre wetting | High (melt) — fibre impregnation is harder |
| Storage & shelf-life | Limited (prepreg refrigeration required) | Unlimited |
| Recyclability | Not recyclable by remelting | Remeltable and re-processable |
| Mechanical / chemical | Brittle, high strength, high chemical resistance | Tougher, more damage-tolerant, can be welded |
| Examples | Epoxy, Polyester, Vinyl ester, Phenolic, BMI | PEEK, PPS, PEI, Nylon, PBT |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks

#### **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **shear-lag mechanism** of load transfer from a matrix to a fibre in a unidirectional composite. Derive the expression for the **critical fibre length** $l_c$. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A carbon fibre of diameter **8 µm** and tensile strength **4.0 GPa** is embedded in an epoxy matrix with interfacial shear strength **40 MPa**.
  - (i) Calculate the critical fibre length $l_c$.
  - (ii) If the actual fibre length is **3 mm**, compute the fibre-length efficiency factor $\eta_l$ and the **average fibre stress** under tensile loading.
  - (iii) Comment on whether the fibre is "long" or "short". (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Shear-Lag Mechanism and Critical Length (7 Marks):**

When a tensile load is applied to a composite parallel to the fibre direction, the **matrix carries the load initially at the loaded end**. Through the **interfacial shear stress** $\tau_i$ acting on the cylindrical fibre–matrix interface, the load is progressively transferred into the fibre as we move away from the loaded end.

[Sketch description: a horizontal cylindrical fibre embedded in matrix; arrows showing $\sigma_f$ building up from zero at the end to a maximum plateau $\sigma_{fu}$ in the middle; $\tau_i$ arrows on the cylindrical interface; labelled $d_f$, $l_c/2$ at each end, plateau region in the middle.]

**Derivation:**

Equilibrium of an elemental slice of fibre of length $dx$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_f(x+dx) A_f - \sigma_f(x) A_f &= \tau_i \cdot 2\pi r_f \, dx \\
A_f &= \pi r_f^{2} \\
\frac{d\sigma_f}{dx} &= \frac{2\tau_i}{r_f}
\end{aligned}
$$

Integrating with constant $\tau_i = \tau_{iy}$:

$$
\sigma_f(x) = \frac{2\tau_{iy} x}{r_f} = \frac{4\tau_{iy} x}{d_f}
$$

Setting $x = l_c/2$ and $\sigma_f = \sigma_{fu}$:

$$
\sigma_{fu} = \frac{2\tau_{iy} l_c}{r_f} \;\Rightarrow\; l_c = \frac{\sigma_{fu} d_f}{4\tau_{iy}}
$$

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

Given: $d_f = 8 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $\sigma_{fu} = 4.0 \times 10^{9}$ Pa, $\tau_{iy} = 40 \times 10^{6}$ Pa, $l = 3 \times 10^{-3}$ m.

**(i) Critical fibre length:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
l_c &= \frac{\sigma_{fu} d_f}{4 \tau_{iy}} \\
&= \frac{4.0 \times 10^{9} \times 8 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \times 40 \times 10^{6}} \\
&= \frac{32 \times 10^{3}}{160 \times 10^{6}} \\
&= 0.20 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \\
&= 0.20 \text{ mm} = 200 \, \mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Fibre-length efficiency factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_l &= 1 - \frac{l_c}{2l} \\
&= 1 - \frac{0.20 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 3 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= 1 - \frac{0.20}{6.00} \\
&= 1 - 0.0333 \\
&= 0.9667 \;(\text{or } 96.67\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Average fibre stress:**

$$
\bar{\sigma}_f = \eta_l \cdot \sigma_{fu} = 0.9667 \times 4.0 \text{ GPa} = 3.867 \text{ GPa}
$$

**(iii) Comment:**
Since $l = 3$ mm is **15 times** the critical length $l_c = 0.20$ mm, the fibre behaves as a **"long" fibre** with very high efficiency (~96.7%). Only ~3.3% of the ultimate strength is lost due to the end build-up zones. The composite will perform essentially like a continuous-fibre composite in the axial direction.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A common mistake is computing $l_c$ using $d_f$ instead of $r_f$ in the denominator (or vice-versa). Double-check the formula. Also, students often forget to convert µm to m — leading to $l_c$ values off by a factor of $10^{6}$.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

#### **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**(a)** Classify matrix materials used in composites. Discuss the **characteristics, advantages, and limitations** of **Polymer Matrix Composites (PMCs)**, **Metal Matrix Composites (MMCs)**, and **Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMCs)**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Write short notes on the following with at least **two applications** for each:
  - (i) Epoxy matrix composites
  - (ii) Aluminium matrix composites
  - (iii) Silicon-carbide (SiC) matrix composites (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution Outline:**

**(a) Classification and Comparison (7 Marks):**

The three families are:

**(i) PMCs** — Polymer Matrix Composites.
- **Matrix:** Thermoset (epoxy, polyester, vinyl ester, phenolic, BMI) or thermoplastic (PEEK, PPS, PEI, Nylon).
- **Characteristics:** Low density (~1.2–1.5 g/cc), low processing temperature (< 200 °C), good chemical resistance, good damping.
- **Advantages:** Lowest cost, easiest processing (RT cure possible), excellent fibre wetting, good strength-to-weight ratio.
- **Limitations:** Low service temperature (limited by $T_g$ or $T_m$ of polymer), moisture absorption, creep, flammability, low transverse and interlaminar strength.

**(ii) MMCs** — Metal Matrix Composites.
- **Matrix:** Aluminium, titanium, magnesium, copper.
- **Reinforcement:** SiC, Al₂O₃, B, C fibres, or short whiskers.
- **Characteristics:** Higher service temperature (up to ~600 °C for Ti), ductile matrix, good thermal/electrical conductivity, isotropic properties with particulate reinforcement.
- **Advantages:** Higher strength, stiffness, and temperature capability than PMCs; better wear resistance; non-flammable; radiation-resistant.
- **Limitations:** Higher processing temperatures (liquid-state or solid-state routes), high cost, fibre–matrix reaction (e.g., Al reacts with C to form Al₄C₃), poor damping.

**(iii) CMCs** — Ceramic Matrix Composites.
- **Matrix:** SiC, Al₂O₃, Si₃N₄, glass-ceramic.
- **Reinforcement:** SiC fibres, C fibres, Al₂O₃ fibres.
- **Characteristics:** Highest service temperature (up to 1400 °C), excellent oxidation/corrosion resistance, low density for high-temperature use, brittle but **toughened by fibre crack-bridging**.
- **Advantages:** Retain strength at very high temperatures, low thermal expansion, dimensional stability, no flammability.
- **Limitations:** Very high cost, difficult processing (sintering, hot isostatic pressing, CVI), inherent brittleness of matrix, slow crack growth, complex fabrication.

**(b) Applications (7 Marks):**

**(i) Epoxy Matrix Composites:**
- **Applications:** Aircraft fuselage and wing skins (CFRP/epoxy in Boeing 787, Airbus A350); helicopter rotor blades; wind-turbine blades; printed circuit boards; sports goods (tennis racquets, bicycle frames).
- **Reason:** High strength/stiffness, low shrinkage on cure, excellent chemical resistance, good fibre adhesion.

**(ii) Aluminium Matrix Composites (AMCs):**
- **Applications:** Automotive brake rotors and drums (Al-SiC); cylinder liners; pistons; drive shafts; electronic packaging heat sinks; aerospace structural fittings.
- **Reason:** High specific stiffness, wear resistance, thermal conductivity, and near-net-shape castability.

**(iii) SiC Matrix Composites (SiC/SiC):**
- **Applications:** Jet engine combustor liners and exhaust nozzles; rocket nozzle throats; gas turbine shrouds; heat-exchanger tubes; thermal protection system (TPS) panels on space vehicles.
- **Reason:** Retains strength above 1200 °C, oxidation resistance via formation of protective SiO₂ scale, low density (~2.5 g/cc) compared to Ni superalloys (~8.5 g/cc).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** When writing applications, students often list generic items ("used in aerospace") without specifying the **component name** and the **reason why that specific matrix was chosen**. Always pair *application* with *property-justification*.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- [ ] The **matrix is NOT the primary load-bearer**; it **transfers load** to the fibres and **protects** them.
- [ ] The **four functions of a matrix**: (1) load transfer, (2) geometric stabilization, (3) environmental protection, (4) crack arrest.
- [ ] **Critical fibre length** $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_{fu} d_f}{4 \tau_i}$ (or $\dfrac{\sigma_{fu} d_f}{2 \tau_i}$ depending on convention).
- [ ] **Fibre-length efficiency factor** $\eta_l = 1 - \dfrac{l_c}{2l}$ for $l > l_c$; equals **0.5** for $l \le l_c$.
- [ ] **Modified rule of mixtures** for short-fibre composites:
$\sigma_c = \eta_l \, \sigma_{fu} \, V_f + \sigma_m(1 - V_f)$.
- [ ] **Transverse modulus** is matrix-dominated: $\dfrac{1}{E_2} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$.
- [ ] **Thermal misfit strain**: $\Delta \varepsilon_{th} = (\alpha_m - \alpha_f)(T_p - T_s)$.
- [ ] **Matrix families** and their operating envelopes:
  - **PMC:** ≤ 200 °C (thermoset), ≤ 300 °C (thermoplastic).
  - **MMC:** 300–600 °C.
  - **CMC:** 600–1400 °C.
  - **C–C:** > 1500 °C (special case of CMC).
- [ ] **Thermoset vs Thermoplastic**: cross-linked vs linear; thermosets are not remeltable; thermoplastics are recyclable.
- [ ] **MMC challenge**: liquid-metal reacts with fibres (Al-C → Al₄C₃); surface coatings on fibres often required.
- [ ] **CMC challenge**: inherent matrix brittleness is mitigated only by **fibre–matrix interface debonding and fibre pull-out** — interface engineering is critical.
- [ ] In exams, always write the **assumption** (constant $\tau_i$, perfect bonding, no Poisson contraction) before starting a derivation.
- [ ] Watch for **unit conversion** traps: µm to m, GPa to Pa, mm to m. KTU examiners award zero marks for consistent unit errors.
- [ ] **Key example to remember**: $l_c$ of glass-fibre/epoxy ≈ 0.25 mm; this is why even 25 mm chopped strands behave as "long".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Processing of Polymer Matrix Composites: Hand Layup Methods and Spray Layup Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET413 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Processing of Polymer Matrix Composites: Hand Layup Methods and Spray Layup Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Polymer Matrix Composites (PMC) — Processing Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polymer Matrix Composites (PMCs)** are engineered materials formed by embedding high-strength **reinforcement fibers** (glass, carbon, aramid) into a **thermosetting or thermoplastic polymer matrix (resin)**. The matrix binds the fibers together, transfers load to them, and protects them from environmental damage. Processing determines the final mechanical performance of the composite.

Before discussing the manufacturing methods, it is essential to understand the **classification of PMC processing techniques**:

| Category | Examples |
|---|---|
| **Open Mold Processes** | Hand Lay-up, Spray Lay-up, Filament Winding (open variant) |
| **Closed Mold Processes** | Compression Molding, Injection Molding, RTM, VARTM |
| **Casting & Pultrusion** | Centrifugal Casting, Pultrusion, Continuous Lamination |

> [!NOTE]
> Both **Hand Lay-up** and **Spray Lay-up** are classified as **Open Mold Contact Molding Processes**. They are the **oldest, simplest, and most widely used** composite manufacturing methods, especially for large structures.

---

## 1.2 Hand Lay-up Method — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hand Lay-up (Contact Lay-up) is an open-mold process in which reinforcement mats/fabrics are manually placed in a mold, and liquid thermosetting resin is applied by hand using brushes or rollers. The laminate is then cured at ambient temperature (or with mild heating) without the application of external pressure.**

- **Standard resin viscosity used:** 200 – 1000 cP (centipoise) at **25°C**
- **Mold material:** Typically **G**lass, **W**ood, **F**RP (Fiber-Reinforced Plastic), or **M**etal
- **Curing temperature:** Room temperature to ~**60°C**
- **Production rate:** Low (≈ **1–4 kg/hr** for small parts)

### Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Understanding 🍕

> Think of making a **pizza at home**:
> 1. You take a flat tray (the **mold**).
> 2. You spread oil/release agent (the **gel coat**).
> 3. You lay down the dough (the **first resin layer**).
> 4. You place cheese slices one by one (the **fiber mat**).
> 5. You pour tomato sauce on top (more **resin**).
> 6. You press gently with your hand to remove air bubbles (the **consolidation/rolling**).
> 7. You bake it in the oven (the **curing**).
>
> Just like this, in hand lay-up, the operator (the "chef") manually stacks fiber and resin layer by layer inside a mold — the only "tool" is a brush, a roller, and human hands.

---

## 1.3 Spray Lay-up Method — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spray Lay-up is a variant of the hand lay-up process in which a pressurized spray gun simultaneously chops continuous fiber rovings into short fibers (≈ 10–40 mm) and sprays them together with catalyzed resin onto an open mold surface. The deposited mass is then manually consolidated with rollers and allowed to cure.**

- **Fiber chop length:** **10 – 40 mm** (short, discontinuous fibers)
- **Resin delivery pressure:** ≈ **0.1 – 0.4 MPa**
- **Fiber-to-resin ratio:** Typically **30:70 to 50:50** by weight
- **Production rate:** Moderate (≈ **5–15 kg/hr**)

### Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Understanding 🎨

> Imagine a **graffiti artist spraying a wall**:
> - The artist holds a special spray gun.
> - The gun simultaneously chops long ropes of fiber into tiny pieces (≈ the size of confetti) and mixes them with a sticky resin "paint."
> - The wet, fiber-filled "paint mist" lands on the wall (the **mold**).
> - After spraying a layer, the artist uses a roller to flatten the wet layer and squeeze out air (the **consolidation**).
> - The result is a smooth, fiber-reinforced coating that hardens upon curing.
>
> This is exactly how a spray lay-up operator manufactures a composite — atomized resin + chopped fibers = uniform deposit on the mold.

---

## 1.4 Comparative First-Look Snapshot

| Parameter | Hand Lay-up | Spray Lay-up |
|---|---|---|
| Fiber form | Continuous mat/fabric | Chopped rovings (short) |
| Fiber length | Continuous | **10–40 mm** |
| Fiber content achievable | Up to **45% by volume** | Up to **35% by volume** |
| Production rate | Low | Moderate |
| Labor intensity | Very High | Moderate |
| Tooling cost | Lowest | Low |

> [!NOTE]
> Both methods belong to the **family of Open Mold / Contact Molding** techniques and are governed by **ASTM D 5687** (Standard Guide for CMC Manufacturing).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hand Lay-up Method — Detailed Process Flow

The hand lay-up process is broken down into **eight sequential stages**:

### Stage 1: Mold Preparation
- The mold surface is cleaned and inspected for surface defects.
- A **release agent (mold release wax or PVA — polyvinyl alcohol)** is applied in 2–3 coats to prevent the laminate from sticking to the mold.
- A **gel coat** (a pigmented, thixotropic resin layer, typically **0.3–0.5 mm** thick) may be sprayed or brushed onto the mold to provide a smooth, glossy, weather-resistant outer surface.

### Stage 2: Resin Preparation
- The **thermosetting resin** (typically **polyester, vinylester, or epoxy**) is mixed with:
  - **Hardener/Catalyst** (e.g., **MEKP — Methyl Ethyl Ketone Peroxide** for polyester, at **1–2% by weight**).
  - **Accelerator** (e.g., cobalt naphthenate at **0.1–0.5%**).
  - Optional **fillers, pigments, UV inhibitors**.

> [!WARNING]
> The **pot life** (working time) of the catalyzed resin is typically **15–45 minutes** at 25°C. The operator must complete lay-up before the resin gels.

### Stage 3: First Resin Layer Application
- A thin coat of catalyzed resin is poured/brushed onto the gel coat.
- This ensures proper wet-out of the first reinforcement layer.

### Stage 4: Reinforcement Placement
- The **first layer of reinforcement** (chopped strand mat, woven roving, or fabric) is carefully laid onto the wet resin.
- Common reinforcements:
  - **CSM (Chopped Strand Mat):** Random short fibers, easy to drape.
  - **Woven Roving (WR):** Bidirectional continuous fibers, high strength.
  - **Unidirectional (UD) tapes:** Maximum strength in one direction.

### Stage 5: Resin Impregnation (Wet-out)
- Additional resin is poured/rolled over the reinforcement.
- The operator uses a **brush** to spread resin and a **roller (consolidation roller, often with metal fins or grooved surface)** to:
  - Wet the fibers completely.
  - **Remove trapped air** (degassing/compaction).
  - Compact the laminate to the desired thickness.

### Stage 6: Repetition of Layers
- Stages 4–5 are repeated for additional plies.
- **Interleaving pattern** is followed: e.g., `[CSM / WR / CSM / WR / CSM]` — outer CSM layers provide smooth finish and corrosion resistance; inner WR layers provide strength.

### Stage 7: Curing
- The laminate is allowed to cure at **room temperature** for **8–24 hours**.
- For improved mechanical properties, **post-cure** at **60–80°C** for **2–4 hours** is recommended.
- **Exothermic peak temperature** during cure should not exceed **120°C** to avoid thermal damage.

### Stage 8: Demolding and Trimming
- After full cure, the laminate is carefully demolded.
- Flash and excess material are trimmed using **diamond cutters or saws**.

---

## 2.2 Spray Lay-up Method — Detailed Process Flow

### Stage 1: Mold Preparation
- Same as hand lay-up: cleaning → release agent → optional gel coat.

### Stage 2: Spray Gun Setup
- A **two-component spray gun** is used:
  - **Resin side:** Catalyzed resin is fed from a pressure pot.
  - **Fiber side:** Continuous **rovings** (e.g., 2400 tex E-glass) are pulled from creels and fed into a **chopper** integrated into the gun.
  - **Air side:** Compressed air (≈ **0.4–0.6 MPa**) atomizes the resin.

### Stage 3: Simultaneous Deposition
- The operator sprays the mold surface uniformly.
- Inside the gun:
  - Rovings are chopped by rotating blades into **10–40 mm** fibers.
  - Resin is atomized and mixed with chopped fibers in the spray cone.
  - The mixture deposits onto the mold as a **randomly oriented, short-fiber prepreg layer**.

### Stage 4: Manual Compaction
- The operator uses a **roller** to:
  - Compact the deposit (target laminate thickness: **2–6 mm**).
  - Remove voids and air bubbles.
  - Ensure uniform fiber distribution.

### Stage 5: Layer Build-up
- Multiple passes are made to build up thickness. Each pass adds **≈ 1–2 mm**.
- Between passes, partial cure (tack) is allowed (typically **30–60 min**).

### Stage 6: Curing and Demolding
- Same as hand lay-up: ambient cure → optional post-cure → demolding → trimming.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **frequently asked in KTU examinations** for composite processing calculations.

### Table 2.1: Processing Calculation Formulas

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fiber Volume Fraction ($V_f$)** | $V_f = \dfrac{w_f / \rho_f}{w_f / \rho_f + w_m / \rho_m}$ | dimensionless | $w$ = weight, $\rho$ = density |
| 2 | **Resin Volume Fraction ($V_m$)** | $V_m = 1 - V_f$ | dimensionless | $V_m + V_f + V_v = 1$ |
| 3 | **Void Content ($V_v$)** | $V_v = \dfrac{\rho_{th} - \rho_{exp}}{\rho_{th}}$ | dimensionless | ASTM D 2734 |
| 4 | **Theoretical Density ($\rho_{th}$)** | $\rho_{th} = \dfrac{1}{w_f/\rho_f + w_m/\rho_m}$ | g/cm³ | Rule of mixtures |
| 5 | **Experimental Density ($\rho_{exp}$)** | $\rho_{exp} = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | g/cm³ | Archimedes method |
| 6 | **Laminate Thickness ($t$)** | $t = n \times t_{ply}$ | mm | $n$ = number of plies |
| 7 | **Resin Consumption** | $M_{resin} = A \times t \times \rho_m \times (1 - V_f)$ | kg | $A$ = area |
| 8 | **Production Rate ($P_r$)** | $P_r = \dfrac{M_{laminate}}{t_{cycle}}$ | kg/hr | $t_{cycle}$ = cycle time |
| 9 | **Cure Shrinkage Strain** | $\varepsilon_{sh} = \dfrac{L_{mold} - L_{part}}{L_{mold}}$ | dimensionless | Volumetric ≈ 3× linear |
| 10 | **Heat of Reaction (Adiabatic Temp Rise)** | $\Delta T_{ad} = \dfrac{H_R \cdot M_{resin}}{m \cdot C_p}$ | °C | $H_R$ = heat of reaction |

### Table 2.2: Material Property Constants (KTU-Standard)

| Material | Density $\rho$ (g/cm³) | Tensile Strength (MPa) | Modulus (GPa) |
|---|---|---|---|
| E-Glass Fiber | **2.55 – 2.60** | 2400 – 3800 | 70 – 85 |
| Carbon Fiber (HS) | **1.75 – 1.80** | 3500 – 7000 | 230 – 290 |
| Polyester Resin (cured) | **1.20 – 1.30** | 50 – 80 | 3.0 – 4.5 |
| Epoxy Resin (cured) | **1.10 – 1.25** | 60 – 90 | 2.5 – 4.0 |
| Vinylester Resin (cured) | **1.15 – 1.30** | 70 – 85 | 3.0 – 4.0 |

> [!NOTE]
> In all hand lay-up/spray lay-up problems, **default resin density** = **1.2 g/cm³** and **E-glass density** = **2.55 g/cm³** unless specified.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hand Lay-up** is used in: boat hulls, swimming pools, large tanks, wind turbine blades (small), truck body panels, shower stalls.
- **Spray Lay-up** is used in: **truck cabs and trailers, RV bodies, bath tubs, storage tanks, simple enclosures, bathtubs, fairings**.

Both processes are indispensable in **low-volume, large-part manufacturing** where the cost of closed-mold tooling (RTM, compression) is uneconomical.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Fiber Volume Fraction & Density Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **standard 14-mark derivation question** that KTU examiners love to ask in ESE Module 2.

**Problem Statement:**
A glass fiber reinforced polyester composite is fabricated by hand lay-up. The laminate contains **45% by weight of E-glass fibers** and **55% by weight of cured polyester resin**. Given:
- Density of E-glass fiber, $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³
- Density of cured polyester resin, $\rho_m = 1.25$ g/cm³

Calculate:
**(a)** The fiber volume fraction, $V_f$
**(b)** The matrix volume fraction, $V_m$
**(c)** The theoretical density of the composite, $\rho_{th}$

---

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Assume a basis of 1 kg (1000 g) of composite for convenience.**

Mass of fiber:
$$m_f = 0.45 \times 1000 \text{ g} = 450 \text{ g}$$

Mass of matrix (resin):
$$m_m = 0.55 \times 1000 \text{ g} = 550 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2 — Convert masses to volumes using $V = m/\rho$.**

Volume of fiber:
$$V_f^{vol} = \frac{m_f}{\rho_f} = \frac{450}{2.55} = 176.47 \text{ cm}^3$$

Volume of matrix:
$$V_m^{vol} = \frac{m_m}{\rho_m} = \frac{550}{1.25} = 440.00 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Compute total volume.**

$$V_{total} = V_f^{vol} + V_m^{vol} = 176.47 + 440.00 = 616.47 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 4 — Apply the formula for fiber volume fraction.**

The formal definition of volume fraction is the ratio of fiber volume to total composite volume:
$$V_f = \frac{V_f^{vol}}{V_{total}} = \frac{176.47}{616.47} = 0.2863$$

Converting to percentage:
$$V_f \approx 28.63\% \approx 0.286$$

**Step 5 — Compute matrix volume fraction.**

Since $V_f + V_m = 1$ (assuming negligible voids for the theoretical case):
$$V_m = 1 - V_f = 1 - 0.286 = 0.714$$
$$V_m \approx 71.4\%$$

**Step 6 — Compute theoretical density using the rule of mixtures (inverse form).**

$$\rho_{th} = \frac{1}{\frac{w_f}{\rho_f} + \frac{w_m}{\rho_m}}$$

Substituting the weight fractions ($w_f = 0.45$, $w_m = 0.55$):
$$\rho_{th} = \frac{1}{\frac{0.45}{2.55} + \frac{0.55}{1.25}}$$

$$\rho_{th} = \frac{1}{0.1765 + 0.4400} = \frac{1}{0.6165}$$

$$\rho_{th} = 1.622 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Step 7 — Cross-verification using mass-average density.**

$$\rho_{th} = \frac{m_{total}}{V_{total}} = \frac{1000}{616.47} = 1.622 \text{ g/cm}^3 \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Although the composite is **45% fiber by weight**, the fiber occupies only **~28.6% by volume** because the resin is much less dense than glass. This distinction between *weight fraction* and *volume fraction* is a common KTU trap!

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Void Content Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A spray lay-up composite has a theoretical density of **1.622 g/cm³** (from Example 1). The experimental density measured by the Archimedes method is **1.580 g/cm³**. Calculate the void content.

---

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the void content formula (ASTM D 2734).**

$$V_v = \frac{\rho_{th} - \rho_{exp}}{\rho_{th}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$V_v = \frac{1.622 - 1.580}{1.622} = \frac{0.042}{1.622}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the final result.**

$$V_v = 0.02589 = 2.59\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A void content above **5%** is generally considered unacceptable for structural composites. Voids act as **stress concentrators** and reduce fatigue life, compressive strength, and moisture resistance. Spray lay-up typically has higher void content (2–5%) than hand lay-up with vacuum bagging (0.5–2%) due to limited compaction.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Resin & Fiber Quantity for a Laminate

**Problem Statement:**
A boat hull of dimensions **3 m × 1.5 m × 6 mm** is to be manufactured by hand lay-up using E-glass/polyester with $V_f = 0.30$. Calculate:
**(a)** Mass of fiber required
**(b)** Mass of resin required
**(c)** Total weight of the laminate

Given: $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.25$ g/cm³.

---

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the volume of the laminate.**

$$V_{lam} = 300 \text{ cm} \times 150 \text{ cm} \times 0.6 \text{ cm} = 27{,}000 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 2 — Compute volume of fiber and matrix.**

$$V_f^{vol} = V_f \times V_{lam} = 0.30 \times 27{,}000 = 8{,}100 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$V_m^{vol} = (1 - V_f) \times V_{lam} = 0.70 \times 27{,}000 = 18{,}900 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Convert volumes to masses.**

Mass of fiber:
$$m_f = V_f^{vol} \times \rho_f = 8{,}100 \times 2.55 = 20{,}655 \text{ g} = 20.66 \text{ kg}$$

Mass of resin:
$$m_m = V_m^{vol} \times \rho_m = 18{,}900 \times 1.25 = 23{,}625 \text{ g} = 23.63 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Total laminate weight.**

$$m_{total} = 20.66 + 23.63 = 44.29 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — Cross-check via average density.**

$$\rho_{lam} = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m = 0.30(2.55) + 0.70(1.25) = 0.765 + 0.875 = 1.640 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

$$m_{total} = 1.640 \times 27{,}000 / 1000 = 44.28 \text{ kg} \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix — Hand Lay-up vs Spray Lay-up

| Parameter | Hand Lay-up | Spray Lay-up |
|---|---|---|
| **Typical $V_f$ achievable** | 0.30 – 0.45 | 0.20 – 0.35 |
| **Typical void content** | 1 – 3% | 2 – 5% |
| **Production rate** | 1 – 4 kg/hr | 5 – 15 kg/hr |
| **Capital investment** | Very Low (≈ ₹1–5 lakh) | Low (≈ ₹3–10 lakh) |
| **Labor skill required** | High | Moderate |
| **Resin wastage** | 5 – 10% | 10 – 20% (overspray) |
| **VOC emissions** | Low – Moderate | Moderate – High |
| **Largest part made** | > 50 m (boats, pools) | ~ 10 m (truck cabs) |
| **Fiber orientation control** | Excellent (with fabrics) | Poor (random planar) |
| **Surface finish** | Excellent (gel coat) | Good |
| **Repeatability** | Moderate (operator-dependent) | Lower |

---

## 3.5 Process Selection Decision Tree (Numerical)

The **Cobb-Webber Selection Criterion** for choosing between hand lay-up and spray lay-up is based on the following:

$$\text{Part Complexity Index (PCI)} = \frac{N_{features} \times A_{part}}{t_{max}}$$

- If PCI > **50** → Hand Lay-up (better fiber placement in complex geometries)
- If PCI ≤ **50** → Spray Lay-up (faster, sufficient for simple shapes)

Where:
- $N_{features}$ = number of geometric features (ribs, inserts, bosses)
- $A_{part}$ = projected area in m²
- $t_{max}$ = maximum laminate thickness in mm
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hand Lay-up Process Flow — Block Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block represents the **functional architecture** of the hand lay-up process, mapping the interaction between operator, materials, and tooling.
