<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define Hand Lay-up Method. Mention any two advantages.

**Model Answer:**

Hand lay-up is an **open-mold composite manufacturing process** in which reinforcement fabrics or mats are manually placed in a mold, impregnated with liquid thermosetting resin using brushes and rollers, and cured at room temperature without applying external pressure.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Low tooling cost** — suitable for large parts like boat hulls and swimming pools.
2. **Simple equipment** — only brushes, rollers, and a mold are required, making it ideal for small-scale and prototype production.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Differentiate between Hand Lay-up and Spray Lay-up methods in terms of fiber form and production rate.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Hand Lay-up | Spray Lay-up |
|---|---|---|
| Fiber form | Continuous mat/fabric | **Chopped short fibers (10–40 mm)** |
| Production rate | Low (1–4 kg/hr) | Higher (5–15 kg/hr) |

In hand lay-up, the operator places continuous reinforcement layers manually, resulting in slow production. In spray lay-up, a spray gun simultaneously chops rovings and sprays them with resin, increasing the deposition rate.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** With the help of a neat process flow diagram, explain the **Hand Lay-up method** for manufacturing polymer matrix composites. Mention its **advantages, limitations, and applications**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hand lay-up composite is made of **60% by weight polyester resin** and **40% by weight E-glass fibers**. Calculate the **(i) fiber volume fraction, (ii) matrix volume fraction, and (iii) theoretical density** of the composite. Given $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.30$ g/cm³. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a)

The hand lay-up process consists of the following steps:

1. **Mold preparation** — The mold is cleaned, polished, and coated with a **release agent (wax or PVA)** to prevent sticking.
2. **Gel coat application** — A pigmented resin layer of **0.3–0.5 mm** thickness is applied to provide a smooth, weather-resistant surface.
3. **Resin mixing** — The thermosetting resin (polyester/vinylester/epoxy) is mixed with **catalyst (MEKP, 1–2% by weight)** and **accelerator (cobalt naphthenate, 0.1–0.5%)**.
4. **Lay-up** — A layer of catalyzed resin is applied, followed by a layer of reinforcement (CSM, woven roving, or fabric). The sequence is repeated to achieve the required thickness.
5. **Consolidation** — A **roller** is used to wet the fibers, remove entrapped air, and compact the laminate.
6. **Curing** — Curing is done at **room temperature (25°C) for 8–24 hours**, followed by optional post-cure at **60–80°C**.
7. **Demolding and trimming** — After full cure, the part is demolded and excess material is trimmed.

**Advantages:**
- Lowest tooling and capital cost
- Suitable for very large parts (>50 m possible)
- No size limitation; flexible design
- Simple equipment — only brush, roller, mold

**Limitations:**
- Low production rate; labor intensive
- Quality depends on operator skill
- High void content (1–3%) without vacuum
- Health hazards from styrene emissions (polyester resin)

**Applications:** Boat hulls, swimming pools, water tanks, roof panels, small wind turbine blades, chemical storage tanks, shower trays.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b)

**Step 1 — Assume 1 kg basis.**

Mass of fiber: $m_f = 0.40 \times 1000 = 400$ g
Mass of matrix: $m_m = 0.60 \times 1000 = 600$ g

**Step 2 — Volume of each constituent.**

$$V_f^{vol} = \frac{400}{2.55} = 156.86 \text{ cm}^3$$

$$V_m^{vol} = \frac{600}{1.30} = 461.54 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Total volume.**

$$V_{total} = 156.86 + 461.54 = 618.40 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 4 — (i) Fiber Volume Fraction.**

$$V_f = \frac{156.86}{618.40} = 0.2537 = 25.37\%$$

**(ii) Matrix Volume Fraction.**

$$V_m = 1 - 0.2537 = 0.7463 = 74.63\%$$

**(iii) Theoretical Density.**

$$\rho_{th} = \frac{1}{\frac{0.40}{2.55} + \frac{0.60}{1.30}} = \frac{1}{0.1569 + 0.4615} = \frac{1}{0.6184}$$

$$\boxed{\rho_{th} = 1.617 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

---

### 📘 Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **Spray Lay-up method** with a schematic. Discuss its **advantages and disadvantages** compared to hand lay-up. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A spray lay-up laminate has a **theoretical density of 1.617 g/cm³**. The **experimental density** measured using the Archimedes method is **1.575 g/cm³**. Calculate the **void content**. If the laminate is used in a marine application, is this void content acceptable? Justify. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a)

The spray lay-up process involves the simultaneous deposition of **chopped fibers and catalyzed resin** onto an open mold using a **specialized spray gun**. The process is summarized below:

1. **Mold and gel coat** — same as hand lay-up.
2. **Spray gun operation** — A two-component gun:
   - Rovings from creels are pulled into a **chopper**, which cuts them into **10–40 mm** lengths.
   - Catalyzed resin is atomized using compressed air (≈ 0.4 MPa).
   - The chopped fibers and resin mist are mixed in the spray cone and deposited on the mold.
3. **Compaction** — A roller is used to compact the deposit, squeeze out air, and achieve uniform thickness.
4. **Curing and demolding** — similar to hand lay-up.

**Advantages over Hand Lay-up:**
- Higher production rate (5–15 kg/hr)
- Lower labor cost per kg of laminate
- Better suited for large, simple-curvature parts
- Uniform coating on complex surfaces

**Disadvantages compared to Hand Lay-up:**
- Only short, randomly oriented fibers → lower strength
- Higher resin wastage due to overspray (10–20%)
- Higher void content (2–5%)
- Higher VOC emissions and styrene exposure
- Lower fiber volume fraction (max ~35%)
- More expensive spray equipment

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b)

**Step 1 — Recall void content formula.**

$$V_v = \frac{\rho_{th} - \rho_{exp}}{\rho_{th}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$V_v = \frac{1.617 - 1.575}{1.617} = \frac{0.042}{1.617} = 0.02597$$

$$\boxed{V_v \approx 2.60\%}$$

**Step 3 — Justification for marine application.**

A void content of **2.6%** is **borderline acceptable** for marine applications. The general industry limits are:
- General structural: ≤ **5%** (acceptable)
- Marine/load-critical: ≤ **2%** (preferred)
- Aerospace: ≤ **1%** (mandatory)

**Conclusion:** Since the void content (2.6%) is **marginally above the preferred marine limit of 2%**, this laminate is **not ideal** for critical marine structural use (e.g., hull bottom), but it is **acceptable for non-critical marine parts** such as interior panels, deck housings, or small enclosures. To improve, **vacuum-assisted consolidation** or **vacuum bagging** is recommended during lay-up.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up weight fraction and volume fraction** — Always read the question carefully! Convert *masses* to *volumes* using density before computing $V_f$.
> 2. **Forgetting to use $V_m = 1 - V_f$** — Matrix volume fraction is *never* equal to weight fraction. Always derive from volume ratio.
> 3. **Neglecting the release agent and gel coat** in the process flow — KTU examiners deduct **1–2 marks** if these steps are missed.
> 4. **Not stating the catalyst percentage** — Always mention **MEKP 1–2%** for polyester and a corresponding hardener ratio for epoxy.
> 5. **Confusing hand lay-up with spray lay-up fiber length** — Hand lay-up uses **continuous** reinforcement; spray lay-up uses **chopped (10–40 mm)** fibers. This is a guaranteed **2-mark trap**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2**

### A. Core Process Concepts
- [x] Hand lay-up is the **oldest, simplest, and lowest-cost** open-mold PMC process.
- [x] Spray lay-up is a **variant of hand lay-up** with **chopped short fibers** sprayed via a gun.
- [x] Both processes are **open-mold, low-pressure, ambient-cure** techniques.
- [x] Hand lay-up uses **continuous reinforcement (CSM, WR, fabric)**; spray lay-up uses **chopped rovings (10–40 mm)**.
- [x] Both are limited to **thermosetting resins** (polyester, vinylester, epoxy).

### B. Material Constants to Memorize
- [x] $\rho_{E-glass} = 2.55$ g/cm³
- [x] $\rho_{carbon} = 1.80$ g/cm³
- [x] $\rho_{polyester} = 1.25$ g/cm³
- [x] $\rho_{epoxy} = 1.20$ g/cm³
- [x] **MEKP catalyst** for polyester: **1–2% by weight**
- [x] **Cobalt naphthenate accelerator**: **0.1–0.5%**
- [x] **Pot life** of polyester resin: **15–45 min** at 25°C

### C. Process Parameters
- [x] Hand lay-up $V_f$ = **30–45%**; Spray lay-up $V_f$ = **20–35%**
- [x] Hand lay-up void content = **1–3%**; Spray lay-up void content = **2–5%**
- [x] Hand lay-up rate = **1–4 kg/hr**; Spray lay-up rate = **5–15 kg/hr**
- [x] Gel coat thickness = **0.3–0.5 mm**
- [x] Curing: ambient **8–24 hr**; post-cure **60–80°C for 2–4 hr**
- [x] Laminate thickness typical: **2–10 mm**

### D. Key Formulas (Top 4 to Remember)
- [x] $V_f = \dfrac{w_f / \rho_f}{w_f / \rho_f + w_m / \rho_m}$
- [x] $V_v = \dfrac{\rho_{th} - \rho_{exp}}{\rho_{th}}$
- [x] $\rho_{th} = \dfrac{1}{w_f/\rho_f + w_m/\rho_m}$
- [x] $\rho_{exp} = \dfrac{m}{V}$ (Archimedes method)

### E. Process Step Sequence (Mnemonic — **"M-R-R-C-R-D-T"**)
- [x] **M**old preparation
- [x] **R**elease agent & Gel coat
- [x] **R**esin mixing
- [x] **C**onsolidation (rolling)
- [x] **R**epeat layers
- [x] **D**emolding
- [x] **T**rimming

### F. Applications Snapshot
- [x] **Hand lay-up** → boat hulls, pools, large tanks, wind blades
- [x] **Spray lay-up** → truck cabs, bathtubs, enclosures, RV bodies
- [x] **Both unsuitable for**: high-precision, high-volume, or aerospace structural parts (use RTM, autoclave, or pultrusion instead).

### G. Common KTU Marks Distribution
- [x] **Process flow with diagram** — 4 marks
- [x] **Advantages & Limitations** — 3 marks
- [x] **Numerical on $V_f$, $V_m$, $\rho_{th}$, $V_v$** — 7 marks (typical weightage)
- [x] **Materials, catalyst, and cure** — 2–3 marks

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In every KTU answer on this topic, **draw a flow diagram** (even a simple block diagram). Examiners allocate **1–2 marks** specifically for a labeled diagram. Always include the **mold, gel coat, resin, reinforcement, roller, and curing station** in your sketch.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Moulding Methods: Pressure Bagging and Bag Moulding Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET413 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Moulding Methods: Pressure Bagging and Bag Moulding Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Bag Moulding Methods** are a family of closed-mould processes used in polymer matrix composite (PMC) manufacturing in which a flexible membrane (the "bag") is used in conjunction with a rigid mould to consolidate, compact, and cure a fibre-reinforced laminate. Pressure is transmitted through the membrane to remove entrapped air, squeeze out excess resin, and consolidate the plies into a dense, void-free, fibre-rich composite.

The bag-moulding family is broadly classified into three principal variants in the KTU 2024 PEMET413 syllabus:

1. **Vacuum Bag Moulding (VBM):** Atmospheric pressure (≈ **1 atm = 101.325 kPa**) is used as the consolidation force after air is evacuated from beneath a sealed bag.
2. **Pressure Bag Moulding (PBM):** A positive pressure (typically **0.2 – 0.7 MPa** of steam, hot water, or compressed air) is applied inside a flexible bag that presses the laminate against an outer rigid mould.
3. **Autoclave Moulding (VAC + Autoclave):** A hybrid process combining an internal vacuum (to remove volatiles) with an external autoclave pressure of **0.5 – 1.5 MPa** at elevated temperatures (**120 °C – 200 °C**) for aerospace-grade laminates.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Terminology Mapping:** In the official PEMET413 syllabus, the term *"Pressure Bagging and Bag Moulding Methods"* collectively refers to VBM, PBM, and Autoclave variants. The phrase *pressure bagging* is sometimes used interchangeably with *pressure bag moulding*, but technically **pressure bag moulding applies internal fluid pressure** while **autoclave moulding applies external gaseous pressure**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Suction Cup / Pressure Cooker" Intuition

Imagine you are gluing a sheet of paper onto a flat wooden table:

- **Vacuum Bag Analogy:** You lay the paper (laminate) on the table (mould), cover it with a plastic sheet, and suck the air out from one corner using a straw connected to your mouth. The atmosphere pushes the plastic down with a force of about **10 tonnes per square metre** — your paper gets glued firmly.

- **Pressure Bag Analogy:** Now imagine a hollow boat hull. You cannot push the laminate onto the inside curved surface from outside. Instead, you insert a rubber balloon inside the hull and inflate it with compressed air. The balloon pushes the laminate firmly against the outer rigid shell.

- **Autoclave Analogy:** Think of a giant industrial pressure cooker used in kitchens. You seal the food (the vacuum-bagged laminate) inside, then heat it and pressurise it simultaneously. The combined heat and high pressure produce a perfectly cooked (cured) part.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Bag Moulding?** Hand lay-up gives only **contact pressure (~kPa)**. Bag moulding boosts consolidation to **100 kPa – 1.5 MPa**, drastically reducing void content from **~5 %** (hand lay-up) to **<1 %** (autoclave), which is critical for aerospace structural laminates.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics for Bag Moulding

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | KTU High-Yield Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Atmospheric pressure | $P_{atm}$ | — | **101.325 kPa (14.7 psi)** |
| Vacuum bag consolidation | $P_{VB}$ | 0.09 – 0.10 MPa | **~0.1 MPa** |
| Pressure bag pressure | $P_{PB}$ | 0.2 – 0.7 MPa | **0.3 – 0.5 MPa** |
| Autoclave pressure | $P_{AC}$ | 0.5 – 1.5 MPa | **0.7 MPa (typical)** |
| Cure temperature | $T_{cure}$ | 25 – 200 °C | **120 °C – 180 °C** |
| Typical void content | $V_v$ | 0.5 – 5 % | **<1 % (autoclave)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Bag Moulding is Needed — The Void & Consolidation Problem

When a stack of prepreg plies (or wet lay-up fabric) is placed in a mould, three problems must be solved:

1. **Air Entrapment:** Each ply carries ~5 – 8 % air by volume. Without external pressure, this remains as **voids**, which act as crack-initiation sites and reduce strength by **up to 30 % per 1 % voids**.
2. **Resin Excess:** Uncured laminates contain **35 – 50 % resin by weight**; the final cured composite needs only **30 – 40 %**. Excess resin must be bled out.
3. **Ply Compaction:** Fibres must be compacted to a target fibre volume fraction $V_f$ = **55 – 65 %** for structural composites.

Bag moulding provides the **external hydrostatic pressure** needed to address all three simultaneously.

## 2.2 Detailed Process Logic — Vacuum Bag Moulding (VBM)

The VBM process executes the following sequence:

1. **Mould Preparation:** A rigid mould (aluminium, steel, or composite tooling) is cleaned and coated with release agent.
2. **Lay-up:** Prepreg plies (or wet fabric layers) are stacked to the required thickness and stacking sequence [0°/45°/-45°/90°].
3. **Release Film:** A perforated PTFE (Teflon) film is placed over the laminate to allow resin/air bleeding while preventing sticking.
4. **Bleeder & Breather:** An absorbent bleeder cloth (fibreglass) and a breather blanket (non-woven polyester) are stacked on top. The bleeder absorbs excess resin; the breather ensures uniform vacuum distribution.
5. **Vacuum Bag Placement:** A flexible nylon or polyester film (typically **0.05 – 0.1 mm** thick) is draped over the stack and sealed at the edges using **vacuum tacky tape** (butyl rubber sealant).
6. **Vacuum Application:** A vacuum pump evacuates air to **≥ 28 inHg (~95 kPa absolute, ~95 % vacuum)**.
7. **Atmospheric Compression:** With ~95 kPa vacuum inside, the outside atmosphere pushes the bag down with **~95 kPa effective pressure (≈ 9.5 tonnes/m²)**.
8. **Cure:** The part is heated (room-temperature cure for some epoxies; **120 °C – 180 °C** for aerospace epoxies).
9. **De-bag & Demould:** After cure, the bag and consumables are stripped; the part is demoulded.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** The consolidation pressure in VBM is **NOT 1 atm (101.325 kPa)**; it is **(101.325 − P_absolute_inside)** kPa. Since practical vacuum pumps achieve **~95 kPa absolute** (≈ 5 kPa residual), the effective compaction is **≈ 95 kPa**, not 101.325 kPa. Examiners love this distinction.

## 2.3 Detailed Process Logic — Pressure Bag Moulding (PBM)

PBM is essentially the **inverse geometry** of VBM. Used primarily for **concave / hollow parts** (boat hulls, aircraft fuselage sections, tub/shower enclosures):

1. Lay-up is made on a **male mould** or on the inside of a **female (hollow) mould**.
2. A **flexible rubber bag** (silicone or natural rubber, **2 – 5 mm** thick) is placed against the laminate.
3. The mould is closed with a rigid outer cover.
4. A pressurising fluid (**hot water, steam, or compressed air**) is pumped into the bag.
5. Pressure builds up to **0.3 – 0.5 MPa**, pressing the laminate firmly against the mould wall.
6. Heat is supplied via the same fluid (steam at **120 – 180 °C** cures epoxy/Bismaleimide systems).
7. After cure, pressure is released, fluid is drained, and the part is demoulded.

**Advantages over VBM:** Higher pressures achievable, better surface finish on concave parts, suitable for long cure cycles.

## 2.4 Detailed Process Logic — Autoclave Moulding

The **gold standard** of bag moulding for primary aerospace structures (Boeing 787 fuselage, Airbus A350 wing skins):

1. Lay-up is vacuum-bagged exactly as in VBM.
2. The entire assembly is loaded into a **pressure vessel (autoclave)** — a large heated pressure chamber.
3. Vacuum is maintained inside the bag while the autoclave is pressurised to **0.5 – 1.5 MPa** with nitrogen or air.
4. The autoclave is heated to the cure temperature following a **ramp-soak-cool** cycle (e.g., 1.5 °C/min ramp → 180 °C soak 2 h → 2 °C/min cool-down).
5. The external autoclave pressure adds to the bag's vacuum compaction, yielding a total effective consolidation pressure of **~0.6 – 1.6 MPa**.
6. After cure, the autoclave is depressurised and cooled, then the part is removed.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Autoclave is the only process that achieves both **<1 % voids** AND **>60 % fibre volume** simultaneously, which is the FAA/EASA requirement for primary aircraft structure composites.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are KTU high-yield — they appear regularly in module tests and ESE.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$ | Standard atmospheric pressure | kPa |
| 2 | $P_{eff}^{VBM} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ | Effective VBM consolidation pressure | kPa |
| 3 | $P_{eff}^{AC} = P_{atm} + P_{autoclave}$ | Effective autoclave consolidation | kPa |
| 4 | $F = P \cdot A$ | Total compaction force on laminate | N |
| 5 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A} = P$ | Compaction stress equals applied pressure | Pa |
| 6 | $V_f = \dfrac{A_f \cdot N}{t \cdot \rho_f \cdot w}$ | Fibre volume fraction (rule of mixtures form) | fraction |
| 7 | $V_v = 1 - V_f - V_m$ | Void volume fraction | fraction |
| 8 | $P_{Hoop} = 2 \cdot \sigma_{bag} \cdot \dfrac{t}{D}$ | Hoop stress in pressure bag (cylindrical part) | kPa |
| 9 | $Q = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$ | Darcy's law — resin bleed rate through laminate | m³/s |
| 10 | $P_{critical} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \sigma_{film} \cdot t_{film}}{R_{curvature}}$ | Bag-burst pressure for curved surface | kPa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU exams, the most frequently tested formula is **Formula 2 ($P_{eff} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$)** and **Formula 4 ($F = P \cdot A$)**, often combined into a "force on a laminate of given area" problem.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Process Used | Typical Application | Pressure |
|---|---|---|---|
| Marine / Boat-building | VBM + PBM | Hulls, decks, bulkheads | 0.1 – 0.5 MPa |
| Wind Energy | VBM | Wind turbine blade shells | 0.09 – 0.1 MPa |
| Aerospace (Secondary) | Autoclave | Fairings, control surfaces | 0.4 – 0.7 MPa |
| Aerospace (Primary) | Autoclave | Fuselage skins, wing skins | 0.7 – 1.5 MPa |
| Automotive (F1, supercars) | Autoclave / RTM hybrid | Carbon monocoque | 0.6 – 1.0 MPa |
| Sporting goods | VBM | Tennis rackets, hockey sticks | 0.1 MPa |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Effective Consolidation Pressure in VBM

**Problem:** A vacuum pump evacuates a sealed bag to a gauge pressure of **−95 kPa** (i.e., 95 kPa below atmospheric). Find the effective consolidation pressure on the laminate.

**Step 1 — Define pressures**
By definition, gauge pressure $P_g$ and absolute pressure $P_{abs}$ are related by:

$$
P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_g
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values**
With $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa and $P_g = -95$ kPa (because the bag is under vacuum):

$$
P_{abs} = 101.325 + (-95) = 6.325 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply effective-pressure relation**
The bag membrane transmits the *difference* between outside atmospheric pressure and inside absolute pressure to the laminate:

$$
P_{eff} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and solve**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{eff} &= 101.325 \text{ kPa} - 6.325 \text{ kPa} \\
        &= 95.000 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion**
The effective compaction pressure is **95 kPa ≈ 0.095 MPa**, equivalent to a column of water **9.68 m tall** pressing on every square metre of the laminate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write $P_{eff} = 101.325$ kPa. The correct value depends on the achievable vacuum. Perfect vacuum would give 101.325 kPa; practical systems give **80 – 95 kPa**.

## 3.2 Derivation — Total Compaction Force on a Laminate

**Problem:** A 1 m × 2 m carbon-fibre laminate is consolidated in an autoclave at 0.7 MPa gauge. The bag is simultaneously under −95 kPa vacuum. Calculate the total consolidation force on the part.

**Step 1 — Effective pressure inside the autoclave**

The autoclave pressurises the *outside* of the bag, while the bag interior is held at vacuum. The differential pressure across the bag is:

$$
P_{diff} = P_{autoclave} - P_{bag,abs}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine bag absolute pressure**

With −95 kPa gauge inside the bag:

$$
P_{bag,abs} = 101.325 + (-95) = 6.325 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute autoclave pressure**

Autoclave gauge pressure 0.7 MPa = 700 kPa, so absolute = 700 + 101.325 = 801.325 kPa.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{diff} &= 801.325 - 6.325 \\
         &= 795.000 \text{ kPa} \\
         &= 0.795 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute force**

$$
F = P_{diff} \times A
$$

Area of laminate:

$$
A = 1 \text{ m} \times 2 \text{ m} = 2 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Final calculation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 0.795 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \times 2 \text{ m}^2 \\
  &= 1.59 \times 10^6 \text{ N} \\
  &= 1.59 \text{ MN} \;\;(\text{meganewtons}) \\
  &= \approx 162 \text{ tonnes-force}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A modest 0.7 MPa autoclave on a 2 m² part generates a **162-tonne compaction force** — this is why autoclave tooling must be designed for **massive loads**.

## 3.3 Derivation — Hoop Stress in a Cylindrical Pressure Bag

**Problem:** A pressure bag for a 1.5 m diameter cylindrical mould is pressurised to 0.4 MPa. The bag is 3 mm thick natural rubber with ultimate tensile strength 25 MPa. Verify the bag will not burst.

**Step 1 — Thin-wall cylinder hoop-stress equation**

For a thin-walled pressure vessel:

$$
\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert dimensions to consistent units**

$D = 1.5$ m, $t = 3$ mm = $0.003$ m, $P = 0.4 \times 10^6$ Pa.

**Step 3 — Substitute**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{hoop} &= \frac{(0.4 \times 10^6) \times 1.5}{2 \times 0.003} \\
              &= \frac{600\,000}{0.006} \\
              &= 100 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \\
              &= 100 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with ultimate strength**

$\sigma_{hoop} = 100$ MPa $\;>\;$ $\sigma_{ult} = 25$ MPa.

**Step 5 — Conclusion with safety factor**

The bag **will burst**. Required thickness for a safety factor of 4:

$$
t_{req} = \frac{4 \cdot P \cdot D}{2 \cdot \sigma_{ult}} = \frac{4 \times 0.4 \times 1.5}{2 \times 25} = 0.048 \text{ m} = 48 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students use **diameter $D$** instead of **radius $R$** in the hoop-stress formula. The correct form is $\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P \cdot R}{t}$ or equivalently $\frac{P \cdot D}{2t}$. Memorise both forms.

## 3.4 Derivation — Resin Bleed Using Darcy's Law

**Problem:** A 4-ply carbon-fibre laminate bleeds resin through the bleeder cloth. Permeability $k = 5 \times 10^{-13}$ m², area $A = 0.25$ m², viscosity $\mu = 0.5$ Pa·s, laminate thickness $L = 2$ mm, applied $\Delta P = 0.1$ MPa. Find the volumetric bleed rate $Q$.

**Step 1 — Darcy's Law**

$$
Q = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values**

$k = 5 \times 10^{-13}$ m², $A = 0.25$ m², $\Delta P = 0.1 \times 10^6 = 10^5$ Pa, $\mu = 0.5$ Pa·s, $L = 0.002$ m.

**Step 3 — Evaluate numerator and denominator**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= (5 \times 10^{-13}) \times 0.25 \times 10^5 = 1.25 \times 10^{-8} \\
\text{Denominator} &= 0.5 \times 0.002 = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final result**

$$
Q = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{-8}}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.25 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 12.5 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation**

Over a 60-minute cure, total resin bled = $1.25 \times 10^{-5} \times 3600 = 0.045$ m³ = **45 litres**. This shows that **bleeder cloth design is critical** to avoid resin-starved laminates.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | VBM | PBM | Autoclave |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure source | Atmospheric (1 atm) | Internal fluid pressure | External pressurised gas |
| Max pressure | ~0.1 MPa | 0.3 – 0.7 MPa | 0.5 – 1.5 MPa |
| Heat source | External oven | Pressurising fluid | Autoclave heater |
| Max temp | 200 °C | 200 °C | 250 °C |
| Void content | 2 – 5 % | 1 – 3 % | <1 % |
| Fibre $V_f$ achievable | 50 – 55 % | 55 – 60 % | 60 – 65 % |
| Part size limit | ~30 m | ~10 m | ~10 m (autoclave dia) |
| Capital cost | Low | Medium | **Very High (₹5 – 50 Cr)** |
| Part cost | Low | Medium | High |
| Best for | Wind blades, boats | Boat hulls, tubs | **Aircraft primary structure** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between Vacuum Bag Moulding (VBM) and Pressure Bag Moulding (PBM) with respect to pressure source, magnitude, and typical applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ~80 words):**

| Aspect | VBM | PBM |
|---|---|---|
| **Pressure source** | Atmospheric (outside) | Pressurised fluid (inside bag) |
| **Magnitude** | ~0.1 MPa (1 atm differential) | 0.3 – 0.5 MPa |
| **Geometry suited** | Convex / flat parts | Concave / hollow parts |
| **Typical use** | Wind blades, boat decks | Boat hulls, tubs, fuselage sections |

*(1 mark each for source, magnitude, application — examiner key pattern)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: List any six consumables used in the vacuum bag moulding process and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Release film (perforated PTFE)** — Allows air and excess resin to escape; prevents laminate sticking to bleeder. (0.5)
2. **Bleeder cloth (fibreglass)** — Absorbs excess resin to control resin content. (0.5)
3. **Breather blanket (polyester non-woven)** — Distributes vacuum uniformly; prevents bag collapse. (0.5)
4. **Vacuum bag (nylon film)** — Seals the layup; contains the vacuum. (0.5)
5. **Vacuum sealant tape (butyl rubber)** — Seals the bag perimeter to the tool. (0.5)
6. **Vacuum port / connector** — Connects bag to vacuum pump via hose. (0.5)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Every Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, and you must answer **either Option A OR Option B in full**. Attempting both leads to **deduction of 1 mark** for excess writing.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the Vacuum Bag Moulding (VBM) process for polymer matrix composites. Include a labelled sketch of the lay-up sequence, the function of each consumable, and the mechanism of pressure generation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1.5 m × 1.0 m carbon-fibre/epoxy laminate is processed in a vacuum bag at 90 % vacuum. Calculate the total compaction force on the laminate. If the laminate is instead processed in an autoclave at 0.6 MPa gauge pressure with the same internal vacuum, calculate the new compaction force. Comment on the significance of the difference. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — VBM Process (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Process Definition (1 mark):**
VBM is a closed-mould process where a flexible membrane (bag) is sealed over a fibre-reinforced lay-up; air is evacuated to create a pressure differential, and atmospheric pressure consolidates the laminate against a rigid mould during cure.

**Step 2 — Lay-up Sequence (2 marks):**
The stack from bottom to top is:
- (i) Rigid mould (with release agent)
- (ii) Laminate plies (prepreg or wet lay-up)
- (iii) Perforated release film
- (iv) Bleeder cloth
- (v) Breather blanket
- (vi) Vacuum bag
- (vii) Sealed with tacky tape

**Step 3 — Function of Each Consumable (2 marks):**

| Consumable | Function |
|---|---|
| Release film | Permits resin/air bleed, prevents sticking |
| Bleeder | Absorbs excess resin |
| Breather | Distributes vacuum uniformly |
| Vacuum bag | Seals the system |
| Sealant tape | Hermetic edge seal |
| Vacuum port | Connects to pump |

**Step 4 — Pressure Generation Mechanism (1 mark):**
Vacuum pump reduces internal pressure to ~5 kPa absolute. Outside atmosphere at 101.325 kPa pushes the bag down with effective pressure = $P_{atm} - P_{abs} \approx 95$ kPa.

**Step 5 — Curing (1 mark):**
Heat is supplied externally (oven or heated tool). Resin flows, wets fibres, bleeds into the breather, and cross-links to form a void-free consolidated laminate with $V_f \approx 55 - 60$ %.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — Compaction Force Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Area $A = 1.5 \times 1.0 = 1.5$ m²
- Vacuum level = 90 % (i.e., $P_{abs} = 10$ % of 101.325 kPa = 10.1325 kPa)
- Autoclave gauge pressure = 0.6 MPa

**Step 1 — VBM Effective Pressure (2 marks):**

$$
P_{eff}^{VBM} = P_{atm} - P_{abs} = 101.325 - 10.1325 = 91.19 \text{ kPa} \approx 91.2 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 2 — VBM Force (1 mark):**

$$
F_{VBM} = P_{eff}^{VBM} \times A = 91.19 \times 10^3 \times 1.5 = 136.8 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Autoclave Effective Pressure (2 marks):**

Autoclave absolute = 600 + 101.325 = 701.325 kPa. Bag absolute = 10.1325 kPa.

$$
P_{eff}^{AC} = 701.325 - 10.1325 = 691.19 \text{ kPa} \approx 0.691 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Autoclave Force (1 mark):**

$$
F_{AC} = 691.19 \times 10^3 \times 1.5 = 1036.8 \text{ kN} \approx 1.04 \text{ MN}
$$

**Step 5 — Comparison and Significance (1 mark):**

Force ratio = $1.04 / 0.137 = 7.58$. Autoclave generates **~7.6× the compaction force** of VBM, yielding lower void content (<1 % vs 2 – 5 %), higher $V_f$ (60 – 65 % vs 50 – 55 %), and superior mechanical properties — essential for primary aerospace structures.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Describe the Pressure Bag Moulding (PBM) process. Draw a labelled schematic showing the arrangement of mould, laminate, and pressure bag. Discuss its advantages and limitations compared to autoclave moulding. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A natural-rubber pressure bag of diameter 1.2 m and thickness 4 mm is used in a PBM operation. If the bag is pressurised to 0.5 MPa, calculate the hoop stress developed. Will the bag survive if its ultimate tensile strength is 20 MPa? If not, suggest a safer thickness assuming a safety factor of 5. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — PBM Process (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
PBM is a closed-mould process where a flexible rubber bag is pressurised internally with hot fluid (steam, water, or air) to consolidate a laminate against an outer rigid mould. It is the **inverse geometry** of VBM.

**Step 2 — Process Steps (2 marks):**
1. Apply release agent to rigid female mould.
2. Lay-up fibre/resin on mould surface.
3. Insert flexible rubber bag inside the lay-up.
4. Close mould with rigid cover; seal edges.
5. Pump hot fluid (steam/air) into bag; raise to 0.3 – 0.5 MPa and 120 – 180 °C.
6. Hold during cure (1 – 4 h).
7. Release pressure, drain fluid, demould.

**Step 3 — Schematic (2 marks):**
(Labelled diagram expected: outer rigid mould, laminate, rubber bag, pressurising fluid inlet, mould cover, pressure gauge. The Mermaid flowchart in Section 4.2 should be redrawn by hand in the answer script.)

**Step 4 — Advantages and Limitations vs Autoclave (2 marks):**

| Aspect | PBM | Autoclave |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure | 0.3 – 0.5 MPa | 0.7 – 1.5 MPa (higher) |
| Part size | Large hollow parts possible | Limited by autoclave dia |
| Capital cost | Low | **Very high** |
| Void content | 1 – 3 % | **<1 %** |
| Best for | Hulls, tubs | Aerospace primary |
| Limitation | Lower pressure, lower $V_f$ | Expensive, size-restricted |

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — Hoop Stress Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Diameter $D = 1.2$ m, thickness $t = 4$ mm = 0.004 m
- Internal pressure $P = 0.5$ MPa = $0.5 \times 10^6$ Pa
- $\sigma_{ult} = 20$ MPa, safety factor $n = 5$

**Step 1 — Hoop Stress Equation (2 marks):**

$$
\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitution (2 marks):**

$$
\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{(0.5 \times 10^6) \times 1.2}{2 \times 0.004} = \frac{600\,000}{0.008} = 75 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 75 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison with Ultimate Strength (1 mark):**

$\sigma_{hoop} = 75$ MPa $\;>\;$ $\sigma_{ult} = 20$ MPa. **The bag will fail catastrophically** (factor of safety = 20/75 = 0.27, far below 1).

**Step 4 — Required Thickness with SF = 5 (2 marks):**

Allowable stress = $\sigma_{ult} / n = 20 / 5 = 4$ MPa. Setting hoop stress = 4 MPa:

$$
t_{req} = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allow}} = \frac{0.5 \times 1.2}{2 \times 4} = 0.075 \text{ m} = 75 \text{ mm}
$$

**Conclusion:** Use a 75-mm-thick rubber bag (or a reinforced composite/elastomer bag) for safe operation, or use multi-layer construction with steel wire reinforcement.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Bag Moulding Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert gauge to absolute** pressure when the problem mixes "vacuum %" with "autoclave gauge pressure" — examiners specifically test this in 7-mark numericals.
> 2. **Confusing VBM and PBM geometry** — VBM uses atmospheric pressure outside, PBM uses fluid pressure inside. Drawing them swapped costs the entire schematic mark.
> 3. **Hoop-stress formula sign** — The factor is $\frac{P \cdot D}{2t}$ for a thin cylinder; using $D$ in place of $R$ without the factor of 2 is a frequent error.
> 4. **Listing consumables without their functions** in Part A — KTU key awards **0.5 mark per consumable + 0.5 mark per function**; a mere list without function loses 50 % of the credit.
> 5. **Not commenting on engineering significance** after numericals — KTU's outcome-based education (OBE) requires a concluding sentence interpreting the result; absence costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Mixing up autoclave and pressure bag** — Autoclave = external gas pressure on a vacuum-bagged part; PBM = internal fluid pressure on a non-bagged lay-up. Examiners love this trap.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Last-Minute Revision — Memorise These 12 Bullets Before the Exam:**

- **Three bag-moulding variants:** VBM (atmospheric, ~0.1 MPa), PBM (internal fluid, 0.3 – 0.5 MPa), Autoclave (external gas, 0.5 – 1.5 MPa).
- **Atmospheric pressure standard value:** $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa = 14.7 psi = 1.01325 bar.
- **VBM effective pressure formula:** $P_{eff} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$; practical systems achieve ~**95 kPa**, not 101.325 kPa.
- **Autoclave effective pressure formula:** $P_{eff} = P_{atm} + P_{autoclave,gauge} - P_{bag,abs}$ (when vacuum is also applied).
- **Consumables stack (bottom-to-top):** Mould → Release agent → Laminate → Release film → Bleeder → Breather → Vacuum bag → Sealant tape.
- **Function triad:** Release film = bleed/stick prevention; Bleeder = resin absorption; Breather = vacuum distribution.
- **Quality progression:** Hand lay-up (5 % voids) → VBM (2 – 5 %) → PBM (1 – 3 %) → Autoclave (**<1 % voids**).
- **Fibre volume fraction progression:** $V_f$ increases from ~45 % (hand lay-up) to **>60 % (autoclave)**.
- **Hoop stress in cylindrical pressure bag:** $\sigma_{hoop} = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2t}$; must be below $\sigma_{ult}/n$.
- **Cure temperature range:** Epoxy systems **120 – 180 °C**; BMI systems up to **250 °C**.
- **Typical autoclave pressure for aerospace:** **0.7 MPa**; high-performance parts use **1.0 – 1.5 MPa**.
- **Real-world applications:** VBM → wind blades; PBM → boat hulls; Autoclave → Boeing 787 / Airbus A350 primary structure.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 OBE Mapping:** This topic maps to **CO2 (Understand various composite manufacturing processes)** and **CO3 (Apply moulding methods to real engineering problems)** at Bloom's Levels **L2 – L4** (Understand → Apply → Analyse). Expect 1 Part A (3 marks) + 1 Part B sub-part (7 marks) per ESE paper from this module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autoclave-based Processing with Prepregs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET413 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Autoclave-based Processing with Prepregs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Autoclave-based Processing with Prepregs** is the industry-standard, high-pressure, thermo-curing manufacturing route used to produce **advanced fibre-reinforced polymer matrix composites (FRPCs)** of the highest mechanical and aerospace-grade quality. In this process, a pre-impregnated, partially cured fibrous reinforcement (the **prepreg**) is hand-laid or machine-laid onto a tool in a defined stacking sequence, sealed under a multi-layer vacuum bagging system, and then placed inside a pressurised, heated pressure vessel (the **autoclave**). The simultaneous application of **elevated temperature** (typically **120 °C – 180 °C**), **external isostatic pressure** (typically **0.3 MPa – 1.0 MPa**), and **full vacuum** (typically **≤ –0.085 MPa**, i.e. **–85 kPa gauge**) drives resin flow, fibre wet-out, void compaction, and final crosslinking (cure) of the thermoset matrix.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMET413 – Module 2):**
> "Autoclave moulding is the **benchmark process** for primary structural aerospace components (wing skins, fuselage barrels, engine nacelles) and high-end sporting goods, because it produces laminates with **fibre volume fractions ($V_f$) of 55 – 65 %** and **porosity content < 1 %** — qualities unattainable by out-of-autoclave (OOA) or liquid moulding alone."

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Pressure Cooker + Lasagna" Model

Imagine you are baking a multi-layered **lasagna** (the laminate), but instead of putting it in an ordinary oven, you place it inside a **pressure cooker** (the autoclave). The lasagna already has sauce (the resin) smeared between each pasta sheet (the fibre layer) — it is a "prepreg." 

- The **heat** melts the cheese and sauce, making them flow and bond the layers.
- The **vacuum** sucks out any trapped air bubbles (voids) *before* and *during* the cook — like pricking a pie to release steam.
- The **pressure** from above pushes the layers tightly together so no gaps remain, just like stacking books with a heavy weight on top.
- After a set time, the cheese hardens (the resin **cures** into a rigid network) and you have a perfectly bonded, dense, void-free composite — stronger than the sum of its parts.

This is **exactly** what happens in an autoclave, except the "lasagna" is carbon/glass fibres and the "cheese" is epoxy, bismaleimide (BMI), or cyanate ester resin.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Terms (Board-Examiner Favourites)

> [!NOTE]
> **Must-Memorise Board Terminology:**
> - **Prepreg:** A reinforcement (unidirectional tape, woven fabric, NCF) pre-impregnated with a controlled amount of B-staged (partially cured) thermoset resin, ready for layup.
> - **Tack & Drape:** The prepreg's stickiness and conformability — controlled by resin viscosity at room temperature.
> - **Out-time / Shelf-life:** Maximum time prepreg can stay at room temperature before it over-cures and becomes unusable.
> - **Cure Cycle:** The pre-programmed time-temperature-pressure profile inside the autoclave.
> - **Consolidation:** The physical compaction of plies to remove entrapped air and inter-ply voids.
> - **Cure:** The irreversible chemical crosslinking of the thermoset resin.
> - **Tool (Mould):** Typically aluminium, Invar, or carbon-FRP, machined to net laminate shape with thermal expansion matched to the part.
> - **Vacuum Bagging System:** Multi-layer stack of release film, breather, bleeders, and sealant that allows vacuum to be applied uniformly over the laminate.

**Standard Reference Values (industry norms):**

| Parameter | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Autoclave pressure | **0.3 – 1.0** | **MPa** |
| Cure temperature (epoxy 3501-6) | **116 – 177** | **°C** |
| Vacuum level | **–85 to –95** | **kPa (gauge)** |
| Heating rate | **1 – 3** | **°C / min** |
| Cool-down rate | **1 – 5** | **°C / min** |
| Resin content (prepreg) | **30 – 45** | **wt %** |
| Fibre volume fraction (final part) | **55 – 65** | **%** |
| Final void content | **< 1 – 2** | **%** |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Prepreg — Foundation of the Process

A **prepreg** is a "B-staged" composite raw material: fibres are pre-impregnated with a partially reacted (partially crosslinked) thermoset resin, giving the user a handleable, tacky sheet with a known **resin content**, **fibre areal weight (FAW)**, and **tack life**.

**Two principal prepreg formats used in autoclave processing:**

1. **Unidirectional (UD) Tape** — Continuous fibres aligned in one direction, held together by the resin. Used where maximum directional stiffness is needed (wing skins, stiffeners).
2. **Woven Fabric Prepreg** — Fibres woven into a 0/90, ±45, or other weave pattern and then pre-impregnated. Used where **drapeability** over complex contours is needed.

**Typical Prepreg Resin Chemistries (frequently appearing in KTU questions):**

| Resin System | Cure Temp (°C) | Service Temp (°C) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Epoxy (e.g., 3501-6) | **177** | **120** | Aerospace primary structure |
| Toughened Epoxy (e.g., 977-3) | **180** | **130** | Large fuselage, wing |
| BMI (Bismaleimide) | **200 – 250** | **200 – 230** | Military, supersonic |
| Cyanate Ester | **220 – 250** | **180 – 250** | Radome, space |
| Phenolic | **150 – 170** | **200+** | Fire-resistant interiors |

## 2.2 The Autoclave — Hardware Anatomy

An **autoclave** is a large, pressure-rated, gas-heated (or electric-heated) cylindrical pressure vessel, typically **2 m – 8 m diameter** and **4 m – 30 m long**, capable of sustaining simultaneous internal pressure, vacuum (on the part), and temperatures up to **450 °C** and pressures up to **2.5 MPa**.

**Key sub-systems (each is a frequent 3-mark KTU short question):**

- **Pressure vessel** — ASME-coded, gas-tight cylindrical chamber.
- **Heating system** — Plenum or internal fans circulate heated nitrogen or air for uniform temperature.
- **Pressure system** — Compressed air or inert N₂ supply (inert gas preferred to prevent resin oxidation/fire).
- **Vacuum system** — External vacuum pump connected through the bag to the laminate.
- **Control system** — PLC-based closed-loop control of temperature, pressure, vacuum, ramp rates, dwells, cool-down.
- **Loading system** — Rails/rollers and tool-tray for moving the layup in and out.

## 2.3 The Layup & Bagging Sequence (Step-by-Step Process Logic)

The prepreg → finished part workflow follows a **strictly ordered** sequence — examiners love asking the student to "list the steps" in correct order.

**Step 1 — Tool Preparation:** The tool (mould) is cleaned, release agent applied, and surface inspected for defects.
**Step 2 — Prepreg Cutting:** Prepreg is cut (by CNC knife plotter or laser) into ply shapes from a flat layup plan.
**Step 3 — Layup:** Plies are stacked in the designed **stacking sequence** (e.g., [0/45/90/–45]₂ₛ) on the tool, with attention to fibre orientation, ply orientation reference, and ply compaction between each ply using a **de-bulking** step (interim vacuum of 5–10 min).
**Step 4 — Vacuum Bagging:** The laminate is sealed under a multi-layer bagging stack (see §2.4).
**Step 5 — Vacuum Leak Check:** A pre-cure vacuum hold of **15–30 min at –85 kPa** verifies the bag integrity.
**Step 6 — Loading into Autoclave:** The bagged assembly is transferred onto the autoclave rails.
**Step 7 — Cure Cycle Execution:** The PLC runs the time-temperature-pressure profile.
**Step 8 — De-bag & Inspection:** After cool-down to < 60 °C, the part is removed, debagged, and NDT-inspected (ultrasonic C-scan).

## 2.4 The Vacuum Bagging Stack — Bottom-to-Top

| Layer (from tool up) | Function |
|---|---|
| 1. Tool (mould) | Provides shape & surface finish |
| 2. Release film (perforated or solid) | Prevents laminate sticking to tool |
| 3. Laminate (stacked prepreg plies) | The part being made |
| 4. Release film (perforated) | Allows volatiles/resin bleed escape |
| 5. Bleeder/breather fabric (glass or polyester) | Absorbs excess resin & distributes vacuum |
| 6. Vacuum port & sealant (e.g., tacky tape) | Seals bag |
| 7. Vacuum bag (nylon or polyimide film) | Encloses the assembly; transmits autoclave pressure |
| 8. Caul plate (optional) | Controls surface finish & thickness on the bag side |

## 2.5 The Cure Cycle — The Heart of the Process

A **cure cycle** is a precisely engineered time–temperature–pressure (TTP) profile. A typical 3501-6 epoxy cycle (classic KTU exam question) is:

> **Room Temp → ramp 1.5 °C/min → 116 °C (1st dwell, 30 min) → ramp 1.5 °C/min → 177 °C (2nd dwell, 60–120 min) → cool-down 2 °C/min → room temp.**

**Why two temperature dwells?**
- **First dwell (116 °C, "intermediate hold"):** Lowers resin viscosity to its **minimum point** (the "viscosity window"), allowing maximum **resin flow, fibre wet-out, and void migration** under vacuum + pressure. This is when **consolidation** happens.
- **Second dwell (177 °C, "cure dwell"):** Resin has begun to gel; this dwell drives the **irreversible crosslinking** reaction to > 90 % cure, achieving the design glass transition temperature ($T_g$).

**Pressure application logic:** Pressure is generally applied *during* the first dwell, held through the second dwell, and maintained until cool-down is well below $T_g$. This prevents **porosity re-formation** (void re-growth) during cool-down.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Examiner's Master Formula Table — Memorise These:**

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_f = \dfrac{\rho_m \cdot W_f}{\rho_f \cdot t + \rho_m (W_f - \rho_f t)}$ (from prepreg data) | Fibre volume fraction from fibre areal weight $W_f$ and cured ply thickness $t$ |
| 2 | $V_f + V_m + V_v = 1$ | Volume balance of fibre, matrix, voids |
| 3 | $V_v = 1 - (V_f + V_m)$ | Void volume fraction (target < 0.02) |
| 4 | $\text{Resin Content} \; RC = \dfrac{W_r}{W_r + W_f} \times 100\%$ | Prepreg resin content (wt %), typically **30 – 45 %** |
| 5 | $W_f = \dfrac{\rho_f \cdot t \cdot V_f}{1}$ | Fibre areal weight (g/m²) |
| 6 | $P_{consol} = P_{atm} - P_{vac} + P_{auto}$ | Net consolidation pressure on laminate |
| 7 | $\text{Heat of reaction} \; \Delta H_{cure} \approx 300 - 500 \; J/g$ (epoxy) | Used in cure-kinetics & exotherm management |
| 8 | $\text{Darcy's Law: } \vec{v} = -\dfrac{K}{\mu} \nabla P$ | Resin flow through fibrous porous medium (void removal) |
| 9 | $K = \dfrac{d_f^2}{k_1} \cdot \dfrac{(1-V_f)^3}{V_f^2}$ (Kozeny-Carman) | Permeability $K$ of fibre bed — governs bleed rate |
| 10 | $\text{Volumetric Shrinkage} \; \Delta V_{cure} \approx 4 - 7\%$ (epoxy) | Causes residual stresses if cure temp ≠ service temp |
| 11 | $\sigma_{residual} = \dfrac{E_m E_f V_f \Delta\alpha \Delta T}{1 - \nu^2}$ | Cured-in thermal residual stress (CTE mismatch) |
| 12 | $T_{g,\text{wet}} \approx T_{g,\text{dry}} - 25 \,^{\circ}\text{C}$ (rule of thumb) | Moisture-plasticised $T_g$ depression |

> [!NOTE]
> **Never write** absolute values or mod operations using the pipe symbol $\vert$ inside a KTU table — examiners flag it as bad form. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or "the magnitude of $x$" instead.

## 2.7 Why This Process Dominates Aerospace — The Engineering Utility

- **Highest achievable $V_f$ and lowest porosity** → best specific stiffness/strength.
- **Tightly controlled fibre orientation** → directional tailoring of stiffness.
- **Repeatable, validated, traceable** process → flight certification (FAA, EASA).
- **Handles large, complex, thick laminates** (up to 50+ plies, areas of 30+ m²).
- **Disadvantages:** extremely high capital cost (autoclave **₹ 5 – 50 crore**), long cycle times (**6 – 24 h**), batch process, energy-intensive, limited to small-to-medium production rates.
- This is the **engineering trade-off** that motivates the development of **Out-of-Autoclave (OoA)** processes (covered in later modules).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fibre Volume Fraction from Prepreg Areal Weight

**Given:** A carbon-fibre/epoxy prepreg has fibre areal weight $W_f = 196 \; g/m^2$, cured ply thickness $t = 0.20 \; mm$, fibre density $\rho_f = 1.80 \; g/cm^3$, matrix density $\rho_m = 1.20 \; g/cm^3$. Find $V_f$, $V_m$, and void content $V_v$ for a laminate of cured thickness $t_{lam} = 2.0 \; mm$ containing 10 plies (assuming $V_v = 0$ in the cured laminate — a common KTU assumption).

**Step 1 — Mass of fibre per unit area of one ply**

$$W_f = 196 \; g/m^2 \; \text{(given)}$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric mass of fibre per unit area (mass per unit volume of ply contributed by fibre)**

$$m_f / A = W_f = 196 \; g/m^2$$

**Step 3 — Fibre volume per unit area (using $\rho_f$)**

$$V_f / A = \frac{W_f}{\rho_f \cdot t}$$

**Step 4 — Plug in numbers**

$$V_f / A = \frac{196 \; g/m^2}{1.80 \; g/cm^3 \times 0.020 \; cm} = \frac{196}{0.0360} \; cm^3 / m^2$$

$$V_f / A = 5444.44 \; cm^3 / m^2$$

**Step 5 — Convert to thickness equivalent (the "fibre-equivalent thickness")**

$$t_f = \frac{W_f}{\rho_f} = \frac{196 \; g/m^2}{1.80 \; g/cm^3} = 108.89 \; \mu m = 0.10889 \; mm$$

**Step 6 — Compute $V_f$ in the cured ply**

$$V_f = \frac{t_f}{t_{ply}} = \frac{0.10889}{0.20} = 0.5444 \approx 54.4\%$$

**Step 7 — Compute $V_m$ (assuming no voids initially)**

$$V_m = 1 - V_f - V_v = 1 - 0.5444 - 0 = 0.4556 \approx 45.6\%$$

**Step 8 — Verify with the rule of mixtures for the laminate thickness**

For 10 plies: $t_{lam, \text{expected}} = 10 \times 0.20 = 2.0 \; mm$ ✓ matches given.

**Step 9 — Compute $W_m$ (resin areal weight)**

$$W_m = \rho_m \cdot t \cdot V_m = 1.20 \times 0.020 \times 0.4556 \; g/cm^2$$

$$W_m = 0.01093 \; g/cm^2 = 109.3 \; g/m^2$$

**Step 10 — Final resin content check**

$$RC = \frac{W_m}{W_f + W_m} \times 100\% = \frac{109.3}{196 + 109.3} \times 100\% = 35.8\%$$

This matches a typical aerospace prepreg (**RC ≈ 33 – 38 %**). ✔

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Net Consolidation Pressure on the Laminate

**Given:** Atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} = 101.325 \; kPa$, vacuum gauge pressure $P_{vac} = -85 \; kPa$, autoclave gauge pressure $P_{auto} = 0.7 \; MPa = 700 \; kPa$.

**Step 1 — Absolute vacuum pressure inside the bag**

$$P_{bag, abs} = P_{atm} + P_{vac} = 101.325 - 85 = 16.325 \; kPa$$

**Step 2 — Absolute autoclave (external) pressure**

$$P_{auto, abs} = P_{atm} + P_{auto} = 101.325 + 700 = 801.325 \; kPa$$

**Step 3 — Net pressure difference across the bag = consolidation pressure on laminate**

$$\Delta P = P_{auto, abs} - P_{bag, abs} = 801.325 - 16.325 = 785.0 \; kPa \approx 0.785 \; MPa$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**

This is the **net effective pressure** that compacts the laminate, drives out entrapped air through the breather/bleeder, and forces resin to flow into fibre tows. Note that the autoclave gauge reading alone (700 kPa) would have **under-estimated** the actual pressure by ~ 12 %.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Kozeny-Carman Permeability & Bleed Time Estimate

**Given:** Carbon-fibre bed, fibre diameter $d_f = 7 \; \mu m$, $V_f = 0.55$, resin viscosity at cure temp $\mu = 0.5 \; Pa \cdot s$, laminate thickness $t_{lam} = 4 \; mm$, Kozeny constant $k_1 \approx 0.5$.

**Step 1 — Compute permeability $K$**

$$K = \frac{d_f^2}{k_1} \cdot \frac{(1-V_f)^3}{V_f^2}$$

$$K = \frac{(7 \times 10^{-6})^2}{0.5} \cdot \frac{(1-0.55)^3}{(0.55)^2}$$

$$K = \frac{4.9 \times 10^{-11}}{0.5} \cdot \frac{(0.45)^3}{0.3025}$$

$$K = 9.8 \times 10^{-11} \cdot \frac{0.0911}{0.3025}$$

$$K = 9.8 \times 10^{-11} \cdot 0.3012 = 2.95 \times 10^{-11} \; m^2$$

**Step 2 — Apply Darcy's law to estimate volumetric flow rate per unit area through thickness**

$$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{K \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$$

where $L$ = flow path length (use $t_{lam}/2$ for half-thickness symmetry, $L = 2 \; mm$).

$$v = \frac{2.95 \times 10^{-11} \times 785,000}{0.5 \times 0.002}$$

$$v = \frac{2.316 \times 10^{-5}}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.02316 \; m/s$$

**Step 3 — Estimate time to bleed out a typical inter-ply void (radius ~ 5 mm)**

Time to displace the void volume through half-thickness path: $t_{bleed} \approx L / v$

$$t_{bleed} \approx \frac{0.002}{0.02316} = 0.0864 \; s \approx 0.09 \; s$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation**

The calculation shows void bleed-out at the **minimum-viscosity dwell** is **kinetically trivial** (< 1 s) provided the resin is in the low-viscosity window. This is why the **first dwell** of the cure cycle is so critical — it is the brief moment when the resin is fluid enough for Darcy flow to evacuate voids. If the ramp is too fast (insufficient dwell), the resin gels before voids are removed → **high porosity** part. ✔

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cured-in Thermal Residual Stress

**Step 1 — Stress-free temperature:** $T_0 = T_{cure} = 177 \; °C$.
**Step 2 — Service/use temperature:** $T = 25 \; °C$.
**Step 3 — Temperature drop:** $\Delta T = T_0 - T = 152 \; K$.
**Step 4 — CTE values:** $\alpha_f = -0.5 \times 10^{-6} \; /K$ (carbon, longitudinal), $\alpha_m = 60 \times 10^{-6} \; /K$ (epoxy).
**Step 5 — CTE mismatch:** $\Delta \alpha = \alpha_m - \alpha_f = 60.5 \times 10^{-6} \; /K$.

**Step 6 — Compute residual matrix stress (1-D simplified model, $V_f = 0.6$, $V_m = 0.4$, $E_m = 3.5 \; GPa$, $E_f = 230 \; GPa$):**

$$\sigma_{m, res} = \frac{E_m (V_f E_f / V_m E_m) \cdot \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T}{1 + (V_f E_f / V_m E_m)}$$

$$\sigma_{m, res} = \frac{E_m \cdot r \cdot \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T}{1 + r}$$

where $r = \dfrac{V_f E_f}{V_m E_m} = \dfrac{0.6 \times 230}{0.4 \times 3.5} = \dfrac{138}{1.4} = 98.57$.

$$\sigma_{m, res} = \frac{3.5 \times 10^9 \times 98.57 \times 60.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 152}{1 + 98.57}$$

$$\sigma_{m, res} = \frac{3.5 \times 10^9 \times 98.57 \times 9.196 \times 10^{-3}}{99.57}$$

$$\sigma_{m, res} = \frac{3.173 \times 10^9}{99.57} = 3.19 \times 10^7 \; Pa \approx 31.9 \; MPa$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation:** The matrix is in **tension** (≈ 32 MPa) and the fibre in **compression** at room temperature. This is why **microcracking** of the matrix is often observed in cross-ply or ±45° plies after cool-down, and why **toughened epoxies with rubber/thermoplastic particles** are used in modern primary structures.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameters vs. Defect Modes

| Process Parameter | Increase → Effect on $V_f$ | Effect on $V_v$ (voids) | Effect on Resin-Richness | Effect on Residual Stress | Common Defect if Mis-Set |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Autoclave pressure** ↑ | ↑ | ↓↓ (strongly) | ↓ (more resin squeezed out) | ↑ (higher $\Delta T$ locked-in) | Too high → fibre wrinkling; too low → porosity |
| **Vacuum level** ↑ (more negative) | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ | ↔ | Vacuum leak → voids |
| **Cure temperature** ↑ | ↔ (slight ↓) | ↓ (lower resin viscosity) | ↔ | ↑ (more $\Delta T$) | Exotherm in thick parts → thermal degradation |
| **1st-dwell time** ↑ | ↑ | ↓↓ | ↔ | ↔ | Excessive dwell → resin-starved, dry fibres |
| **Heating rate** ↑ | ↓ (less time to flow) | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ | Fast ramp → trapped voids |
| **Cooling rate** ↓ | ↔ | ↓ (no void regrowth) | ↔ | ↓ | Fast cool → microcracking, warpage |
| **$V_f$ in layup** ↑ (more plies) | ↑ | ↑ (if not enough resin) | ↓ | ↑ | Too dry → poor interlaminar shear |

