# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP, PEMET413]`
**Define the term "prepreg". Mention any two prepreg resin systems used in autoclave processing.**

**Model Answer (memorise the structure):**
A **prepreg** is a ready-to-use composite raw material in which reinforcing fibres (unidirectional tape, woven fabric, or non-crimp fabric) are pre-impregnated with a **partially cured (B-staged) thermoset resin** in a controlled fibre-to-resin ratio. It is tacky, drapeable, and has a defined shelf-life at room temperature (or storage life at –18 °C).

**Two resin systems:**
1. **Epoxy** (e.g., 3501-6) — cure at 177 °C, service up to 120 °C; used in primary aircraft structure.
2. **Bismaleimide (BMI)** — cure at 200 – 250 °C, service up to 230 °C; used in military, supersonic, and engine applications.

> *(2 marks: definition; 1 mark: two resin systems)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP, PEMET413]`
**What is a "vacuum bag" in autoclave processing? List any three components of a typical vacuum bagging stack.**

**Model Answer:**
A **vacuum bag** is an airtight polymeric film (nylon or polyimide) sealed over the laid-up laminate on the tool using tacky sealant tape. It enables uniform application of vacuum over the laminate, evacuation of volatiles/voids, and transmission of autoclave pressure onto the part.

**Three components of a vacuum bagging stack:**
1. **Release film** (perforated, between laminate and bleeder).
2. **Bleeder/breather fabric** (absorbs excess resin and distributes vacuum uniformly).
3. **Sealant tape** (provides the air-tight seal between bag and tool).
   *(Acceptable alternatives: vacuum port, caul plate, solid release film on tool side.)*

> *(1 mark: definition; 2 marks: any three components)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module-Internal-Choice Questions

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern (Module 2):** *Answer ONE full question. Each question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), 7 marks each.*

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *"Process Engineering of an Autoclave Cure"*

**`(a) [7 marks]`** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of an **autoclave** used for processing polymer matrix composites. Mention the function of any four important components.

**Model Solution (board-exam style — write in this order):**

**Definition (1 mark):** An autoclave is a **pressure-rated, heated cylindrical vessel** used to apply simultaneous **heat, pressure, and vacuum** to a vacuum-bagged composite layup, to cure the thermoset matrix under controlled conditions.

**Construction (3 marks):** The autoclave consists of:
- A **pressure vessel** — a heavy-walled cylindrical steel shell, hemispherical heads, rated as per ASME pressure-vessel code, with quick-opening doors and safety interlocks.
- A **gas circulation system** — internal fans and ducted plenums to circulate heated nitrogen (preferred — inert, no resin oxidation) or air uniformly.
- **Heating system** — electrical heaters or gas burners heating the gas to the set cure temperature.
- **Pressure supply** — compressed air or nitrogen reservoir and regulators.
- **Vacuum system** — external vacuum pump, vacuum lines, and gauges connected to the bag inside the autoclave.
- **PLC control system** — thermocouples (on part & in plenum), pressure transducers, vacuum gauges; closed-loop PID control of T, P, and vacuum versus time.

**Working (2 marks):** The bagged layup is rolled into the autoclave. The door is closed and sealed. The programmed cure cycle is started:
- Vacuum is applied first to consolidate and remove entrapped air.
- Heat is ramped at 1–3 °C/min, raising the part temperature.
- Pressure is applied (typically 0.3 – 0.7 MPa) once resin viscosity drops.
- Temperature dwells at the cure temperature (e.g., 177 °C for 60 – 120 min for epoxy).
- The part is cooled under pressure to below $T_g$, removed, debagged, and inspected.

**Function of four components (1 mark):**
- **Heating system** → raises resin temperature to enable flow and cure.
- **Pressure supply** → compacts plies, suppresses voids, drives resin flow.
- **Vacuum system** → removes entrapped air and volatiles.
- **PLC control** → ensures repeatability, traceability, and certification compliance.

---

**`(b) [7 marks]`** Explain the typical **time–temperature–pressure cure cycle** for a 177 °C-cure epoxy prepreg (e.g., 3501-6). Discuss the **role of the first (intermediate) temperature dwell**.

**Model Solution:**

**Time–Temperature–Pressure (TTP) Profile (3 marks):**

| Stage | Temperature | Pressure | Vacuum | Duration | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Initial heat-up | 25 → 116 °C @ 1.5 °C/min | Ramping to 0.7 MPa | –85 kPa held | ~ 60 min | Bring part to first dwell |
| 2. First dwell | **116 °C** (hold) | **0.7 MPa** | Released at end of dwell | **30 – 60 min** | Resin viscosity minimum, void removal, fibre wet-out |
| 3. Second heat-up | 116 → 177 °C @ 1.5 °C/min | Held at 0.7 MPa | Off | ~ 40 min | Approach cure temperature |
| 4. Cure dwell | **177 °C** (hold) | Held at 0.7 MPa | Off | **60 – 120 min** | Crosslinking, $T_g$ development |
| 5. Cool-down | 177 → < 60 °C @ 1.5–2 °C/min | Held, then released below $T_g$ | Off | ~ 60 min | Avoid residual stress, no void re-growth |

**Role of the First (Intermediate) Dwell at 116 °C (3 marks):**
1. **Viscosity minimum window:** Epoxy resin at ~ 110 – 120 °C reaches its **lowest viscosity (typically 0.1 – 1 Pa·s)** before gelation begins. This is the only window where the resin can flow easily.
2. **Void removal:** The combination of **full vacuum + autoclave pressure** during this low-viscosity phase evacuates entrapped air through the bleeder, driven by **Darcy flow** of resin through the fibre bed.
3. **Fibre wet-out:** The resin flows around and into the fibre tows, ensuring complete impregnation and good fibre–matrix interface.
4. **Consolidation:** Pressure collapses inter-ply voids and compacts the laminate to the target $V_f$ (55 – 65 %).
5. **Avoidance of dry spots / porosity:** Insufficient dwell → trapped voids, dry patches, and poor mechanical properties (especially interlaminar shear strength, ILSS).
6. **Exotherm control:** In thick laminates, the first dwell also provides time for the **exothermic cure reaction heat** to dissipate, avoiding thermal runaway.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The first dwell is the **physically critical** stage of the cure cycle — it is where the *quality* of the laminate is determined. The second dwell is the **chemically critical** stage — it is where the *strength* of the laminate is set.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *"Defects, Bagging & Prepreg Layup"*

**`(a) [7 marks]`** With a neat labelled sketch, explain the **vacuum bagging sequence** used in autoclave processing. Discuss the function of each layer.

**Model Solution:**

**Sketch (2 marks):** A labelled vertical cross-section of the layup stack on the tool, with seven layers as shown in §2.4 of these notes.

**Layer-by-Layer Function (5 marks):**

| # | Layer | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Tool (mould)** | Provides geometry and surface finish; matched-CTE material (Invar, Al, CFRP) |
| 2 | **Release film (solid, on tool side)** | Prevents laminate adhering to tool; allows clean release after cure |
| 3 | **Laminate (stacked prepreg plies)** | The part being made; orientation, stacking sequence, $V_f$ control |
| 4 | **Release film (perforated)** | Allows volatiles and excess resin to bleed out into the bleeder |
| 5 | **Bleeder / Breather fabric** | Absorbs excess resin; distributes vacuum uniformly over large area |
| 6 | **Vacuum bag + sealant tape** | Airtight enclosure; transmits autoclave pressure to laminate via differential pressure |
| 7 | **Caul plate (optional, bag side)** | Controls bag-side surface finish and laminate thickness uniformity |

**Why this sequence (1 mark):** The order is critical — the perforated release must be on the *laminate* side, not the tool side, to allow bleed-out. The breather must be *above* the perforated release to absorb what bleeds. The bag must be the *outermost* layer to contain vacuum and transmit autoclave pressure. Mis-ordering any of these layers is a common layup defect that leads to **porosity, dry spots, or delamination**.

---

**`(b) [7 marks]`** List and explain **any five common defects** encountered in autoclave-processed composites and the process parameters that cause them.

**Model Solution:**

| # | Defect | Process Cause | Effect on Part |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Porosity (voids > 2 %)** | Inadequate vacuum, vacuum leak, too-fast ramp, insufficient 1st-dwell | ↓ ILSS, ↓ fatigue life, ↑ scatter in strength |
| 2 | **Delamination** | Trapped air/plies, contamination, insufficient pressure, poor inter-ply tack | Catastrophic loss of compressive strength |
| 3 | **Wrinkles / fibre waviness** | Excessive pressure on mis-aligned ply, mismatch in ply lengths on tapered laminate | ↓ compressive strength, ↑ BVID (barely-visible impact damage) sensitivity |
| 4 | **Resin-starved (dry) regions** | Excessive bleed, too long first dwell, vacuum held too long | ↓ $V_f$ in local area, ↓ strength, fibre–fibre contact |
| 5 | **Resin-rich (pools)** | Insufficient pressure, too-rapid gel, breather clogged | ↑ weight, ↓ $V_f$, ↑ CTE mismatch, crack-initiation sites |
| 6 | **Warpage / spring-in** | Cure at high T, cool to room T, CTE mismatch tool-vs-laminate | Dimensional inaccuracy, assembly fit issues |
| 7 | **Incomplete cure ($T_g$ low)** | Heater failure, thermocouple mis-location, under-cure dwell time | ↓ $T_g$, ↓ solvent resistance, ↓ mechanical properties |
| 8 | **Fibre misalignment** | Manual layup error, ply not aligned to reference datum | ↓ directional stiffness, ↓ strength in design direction |

**Prevention (1 mark):** Use **qualified prepreg**, validated cure cycle, calibrated thermocouples & transducers, leak-checked bag, NDT (ultrasonic C-scan, thermography), statistical process control (SPC) on every critical parameter.

> **Conclusion (1 mark):** Each defect traces back to a *specific, controllable process variable* — the role of the manufacturing engineer is to identify and lock down the root cause through **PFMEA** and **process qualification** per **NADCAP / AS9100** standards.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on this topic (Dec 2023 / June 2024 patterns observed):**
> 1. **Confusing "vacuum" and "pressure":** Vacuum is *inside* the bag (pulls resin/air out); autoclave pressure is *outside* the bag (pushes laminate together). Many students describe them as the same thing. **Examiner docks 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Forgetting units:** Always write "**0.7 MPa**" not just "0.7". KTU examiners want SI units with every numerical answer.
> 3. **Skipping the "WHY" of the first dwell:** Many students state "first dwell at 116 °C" without explaining it is the **viscosity minimum** for void removal. This is the single most-missed concept.
> 4. **No sketch in 7-mark answers:** When the question says "with a neat sketch", a missing sketch costs **1 – 2 marks** even if the text is perfect. Always draw a labelled block diagram.
> 5. **Wrong layer order in vacuum bagging:** A common error is putting the bleeder *below* the laminate. The correct order is: **Tool → release (solid) → laminate → release (perforated) → bleeder → bag.**
> 6. **No mention of NADCAP/AS9100/process control** when discussing "defects" — examiners increasingly reward industrial-quality process knowledge.

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Prepreg = B-staged fibre + resin**, ready to lay up; stored at **–18 °C**; limited out-time at room temperature.
- **Autoclave** = pressure vessel giving simultaneous **heat + pressure + vacuum**; the gold-standard process for aerospace PMCs.
- **Layup sequence** must follow design stacking sequence (e.g., [0/45/–45/90]ₛ) with **de-bulking** between plies.
- **Vacuum bagging stack** (bottom-up): Tool → release film (solid) → laminate → release film (perforated) → bleeder/breather → sealant tape → vacuum bag.
- **Cure cycle** has **two temperature dwells**:
  - **1st dwell (lower T):** resin **viscosity minimum** → **void removal** + fibre wet-out.
  - **2nd dwell (higher T):** **crosslinking** → final $T_g$ achieved.
- **Pressure is held through cure and cool-down** to prevent void re-formation and warpage.
- **Key targets:** $V_f$ **55 – 65 %**, void content **< 1 – 2 %**, $T_g$ (wet) **> 130 °C** for 177 °C-cure epoxy.
- **Controlling equations:** Volume balance $V_f + V_m + V_v = 1$; **Darcy's law** governs void bleed-out; **Kozeny-Carman** gives permeability; **CTE mismatch** drives residual stress on cool-down.
- **Quality control:** vacuum leak check, thermocouples on part, C-scan, $T_g$ check by DSC or DMA, acid digestion for $V_f$/$V_v$.
- **Process flow:** Prepreg → cut → layup → de-bulk → bag → leak check → autoclave → cure → cool → de-bag → NDT → trim → part.
- **Industrial standards:** **NADCAP** (supplier audit), **AS9100** (aerospace QMS), **ASTM D3171** (void content), **ASTM D5023** (DMA for $T_g$).
- **Comparison hook for KTU essays:** Autoclave = highest quality, highest cost, slowest; motivates **OoA / VBO / RTM** alternatives covered in later modules.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pultrusion and Filament Winding Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET413 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pultrusion and Filament Winding Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pultrusion Process

### Formal Definition
**Pultrusion** is a continuous, automated, closed-mold manufacturing process used to produce fiber-reinforced polymer (FRP) composite profiles of constant cross-section. The term *pultrusion* is derived from the words **"pull"** and **"extrusion"**, signifying that the reinforcement is *pulled* (rather than pushed, as in extrusion) through a resin bath and a heated die where curing occurs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Pultrusion is the only continuous process in composites manufacturing that produces long, straight, constant cross-section structural profiles. It is a **thermoset-dominant** process where the resin cures inside the heated die.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Soaking Spaghetti Noodle Maker"
Imagine you have a bundle of dry spaghetti strands (fibers). You dip them into a bowl of thick tomato sauce (resin bath), making sure every strand is well-coated. Now imagine the wet, sauce-coated strands are pulled through a hot, shaped tube (heated die). The sauce bakes hard, and out comes a solid, sauce-encased spaghetti rod of a fixed shape. As it emerges, a cutter chops it to length. That is **pultrusion in a kitchen**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** In *extrusion* (metals/plastics), the *material is pushed* through a die. In *pultrusion* (composites), the *fibers are pulled* — hence the name.

---

## 1.2 Filament Winding Process

### Formal Definition
**Filament Winding** is a manufacturing process in which continuous fiber rovings (tows) are wound under controlled tension over a rotating mandrel in predetermined geometric patterns. The resin is applied either by passing the fibers through a resin bath (**wet winding**) or by using pre-impregnated fibers (**dry/prepreg winding**). After winding, the part is cured (typically in an oven or autoclave) and the mandrel is removed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Filament Winding is the dominant process for manufacturing **axisymmetric hollow composites** like pressure vessels, pipes, rocket motor casings, and chemical storage tanks.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Candy Floss Machine Around a Rolling Pin"
Think of a candy-floss (cotton-candy) machine that dispenses thin sugar threads onto a rotating stick. Now replace the stick with a rotating cylindrical drum (mandrel) and the sugar threads with resin-impregnated fiber tows. The drum spins, the dispensing head travels back and forth, and you build up a multi-layered, geometrically patterned fiber shell. After baking (curing), the drum is removed, leaving a hollow composite tube. That is **filament winding in a sweet shop**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in industry:** Filament winding gives the highest fiber volume fraction (often **60–70%**) and best mechanical performance of any composites process because the fibers are kept under tension and laid in mathematically optimized paths.

---

## 1.3 Comparison Snapshot

| Feature | Pultrusion | Filament Winding |
|---|---|---|
| Cross-section | Constant | Axisymmetric / variable |
| Process type | Continuous (pull) | Batch (rotational) |
| Typical products | Rods, channels, ladder rails | Pressure vessels, pipes, tanks |
| Fiber volume fraction | 50–65% | 60–70% |
| Resin system | Thermoset (polyester, vinylester, epoxy) | Thermoset (epoxy dominant) |
| Curing location | Inside heated die | Oven/autoclave after winding |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pultrusion — Process Steps in Detail

The pultrusion line is a sequence of **seven functional stations**, each performing a critical role:

### Step 1 — Creel / Fiber Roving Supply
Multiple spools (called *creels*) supply continuous fibers (glass, carbon, aramid) and/or mats/veils. Fibers are guided into a *preformers* system to control positioning.

### Step 2 — Resin Impregnation (Resin Bath)
Fibers pass through a tank of catalyzed liquid thermoset resin. Wet-out is enhanced by:
- **Roller guides** (mechanical impregnation)
- **Ultrasonic bath** (acoustic cavitation)
- **Injection die** (pressure impregnation — modern variant)

### Step 3 — Preformer
A shaped guide that squeezes out excess resin, aligns fibers, and pre-shapes the bundle to closely match the die cavity. Removes entrapped air.

### Step 4 — Heated Die (Curing Station)
The heart of the process. Typically **3-zone** temperature controlled:
- **Pre-heat zone:** $T_1 \approx 80–100°C$ (resin viscosity drops, gel begins)
- **Gel/exotherm zone:** $T_2 \approx 120–160°C$ (cure exotherm peaks)
- **Final cure zone:** $T_3 \approx 150–180°C$ (full cross-linking)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three zones?** A single temperature causes a runaway exotherm (heat released by cure accelerates cure → more heat → thermal damage). A profiled temperature curve prevents this.

### Step 5 — Pulling Mechanism
Either **reciprocating clamps** (caterpillar tracks) or **hydraulic grippers** continuously pull the part at speeds of **0.3–1.5 m/min**.

### Step 6 — Cut-Off Saw
A diamond-tipped or abrasive saw cuts the cured profile to the required length. Often synchronized with an *in-line* length sensor.

### Step 7 — Staging / Packaging
Cut parts are stacked, inspected, and packed.

### Why Pultrusion is "The Why" of Composites Economics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility:** Pultruded profiles replace steel, aluminum, and wood in corrosive environments — chemical plants, marine walkways, electrical transmission cross-arms, and cooling-tower structural members. The **high fiber alignment** gives near-theoretical strength along the pulling direction.

---

## 2.2 Filament Winding — Process Steps and Winding Patterns

### Fundamental Operation
A fiber tow impregnated with resin is laid down on a rotating mandrel. The relative motion between the **mandrel rotation** ($N$ rev/min) and the **fiber delivery carriage traverse** ($v$ mm/min) determines the **winding angle** $\alpha$.

### Winding Pattern Classification

| Pattern | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Hoop (Circumferential)** | Fibers wound nearly perpendicular to mandrel axis ($\alpha \approx 90°$) | Hoop reinforcement of pipes/tanks |
| **Helical (Geodesic)** | Fibers wound at a constant intermediate angle ($0° < \alpha < 90°$) | Pressure vessels (multi-axis load) |
| **Polar (Equatorial)** | Fibers pass over the polar caps, two bands on opposite sides | Spherical pressure vessels |
| **Planar** | Fibers laid on a flat mandrel | Specialty flat components |

### Winding Parameters

1. **Winding angle** $\alpha$ — angle between fiber path and mandrel axis.
2. **Bandwidth** $b$ — width of the fiber tow laid per circuit.
3. **Winding pitch** $p$ — axial advance of the carriage per mandrel revolution.
4. **Mandrel speed** $\omega$ (rad/s) and **carriage speed** $v$ (mm/s).
5. **Tension** $T$ — applied to keep fibers taut and consolidate laminate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why fiber tension matters:** Insufficient tension → voids, poor compaction, low $V_f$. Excess tension → fiber breakage, resin-rich starved regions, residual stress.

### Why Filament Winding Dominates Pressure-Vessel Manufacturing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility:** A filament-wound composite pressure vessel has a **specific strength** (strength/density) **5–10× higher** than a steel vessel of equivalent pressure rating. The fibers can be placed along the **isotensoid lines** (paths of constant stress) so that every fiber carries equal load — the *theoretical optimum* of material usage.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All equations below are **exam-critical** for the KTU End-Semester Examination.

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tan\alpha = \dfrac{v}{\pi D N}$ | Winding angle relation (basic kinematic) | rad |
| 2 | $\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{p}{\pi D}\right)$ | Winding angle from pitch $p$ and diameter $D$ | rad |
| 3 | $p = \dfrac{v}{N}$ | Pitch = carriage advance per revolution | mm/rev |
| 4 | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p_r D}{2 t}$ | Hoop stress in thin-walled pressure vessel | Pa |
| 5 | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{p_r D}{4 t}$ | Longitudinal (axial) stress | Pa |
| 6 | $\sigma_{f1} = \dfrac{p_r R}{t \cos^{2}\alpha}$ | Fiber stress along fiber direction in helical wrap | Pa |
| 7 | $\sigma_{f2} = \dfrac{p_r R}{t \sin^{2}\alpha}$ | Fiber stress in transverse wrap (hoop) | Pa |
| 8 | $V_f = \dfrac{A_{fiber}}{A_{total}}$ | Fiber volume fraction | dimensionless |
| 9 | $T_{curing} \approx T_{g} + 20°C$ | Curing die temperature guideline (above glass transition) | °C |
| 10 | $V_{pull} \propto \dfrac{1}{L_{die}}$ | Pull speed inversely proportional to die length (residence time) | m/min |

> [!NOTE]
> **In Formula 4 and 5:** $p_r$ is internal pressure, $D$ is vessel diameter, $t$ is wall thickness, and $R = D/2$ is radius. The ratio $\sigma_h / \sigma_l = 2$ confirms that hoop stress is always **twice** the axial stress — driving the need for more fibers in the hoop direction.

### Isotensoid Pressure Vessel — Optimal Winding Angle

For a closed-end pressure vessel, the **optimal winding angle** that makes both fiber directions equally loaded (the *isotensoid condition*) is:

$$\alpha_{opt} = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\sqrt{2}\right) \approx 54.7°$$

This is the magic number — any filament-wound pressure vessel aiming for minimum weight uses fibres at $\pm 54.7°$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Filament Winding Angle Equation

### Statement
Show that the winding angle $\alpha$ between a fiber tow and the mandrel axis satisfies the relation:

$$\tan\alpha = \dfrac{v}{\pi D N}$$

### Given
- Mandrel diameter = $D$ (m)
- Mandrel rotational speed = $N$ (revolutions per minute)
- Carriage delivery (axial) speed = $v$ (m/min)
- One complete mandrel revolution corresponds to one helical circuit of the fiber.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Circumferential distance per revolution.**
In one revolution, a point on the mandrel surface travels along the circumference:

$$L_{circ} = \pi D$$

**Step 2 — Axial advance per revolution.**
The carriage moves a distance $v$ per minute. In the time for $N$ revolutions ($t = 1/N$ minute if expressed in rev/min), the axial advance is:

$$p = \dfrac{v}{N}$$

This $p$ is the **pitch** — the axial distance advanced per revolution.

**Step 3 — Geometric setup.**
Consider the fiber laid on the cylindrical surface as a helix. In a *developed* (unrolled) view of the cylinder, the fiber appears as a straight line making angle $\alpha$ with the mandrel axis. The horizontal direction corresponds to the **circumferential** distance, and the vertical direction corresponds to the **axial** distance.

**Step 4 — Apply the definition of tangent.**
From the unrolled right triangle:

$$\tan\alpha = \dfrac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}} = \dfrac{L_{circ}}{p}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the expressions.**

$$\tan\alpha = \dfrac{\pi D}{p} = \dfrac{\pi D}{v / N} = \dfrac{\pi D N}{v}$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to the canonical KTU form.**

$$\boxed{\;\tan\alpha = \dfrac{v}{\pi D N}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** To *decrease* the winding angle (more axial), either reduce carriage speed $v$ or increase mandrel speed $N$ or diameter $D$. To *increase* the angle (more hoop), do the opposite.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Hoop and Axial Stress in a Thin-Walled Pressure Vessel

### Statement
For a thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel of internal radius $R$, wall thickness $t$, and internal gauge pressure $p_r$, show that:

$$\sigma_h = \dfrac{p_r D}{2t}, \qquad \sigma_l = \dfrac{p_r D}{4t}$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Geometry assumption (thin-wall).**
The wall is *thin* if $t \le D/20$. The stress is uniform across the thickness.

**Step 2 — Hoop direction force balance.**
Consider a longitudinal diametrical cut through the vessel. The internal pressure acts on a projected area $D \times L$ (where $L$ is vessel length). This force is balanced by hoop stress acting on two wall cross-sections of area $2 t L$:

$$p_r \cdot D L = 2 \sigma_h t L$$

Solve for $\sigma_h$:

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_h = \dfrac{p_r D}{2 t}\;}$$

**Step 3 — Axial direction force balance.**
Now cut the vessel with a transverse plane. The pressure acts on the circular end cap of area $\pi D^2 / 4$. This is balanced by longitudinal stress acting on the full circular wall of area $\pi D t$:

$$p_r \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \sigma_l \cdot \pi D t$$

Solve for $\sigma_l$:

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_l = \dfrac{p_r D}{4 t}\;}$$

**Step 4 — The crucial ratio.**

$$\dfrac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_l} = \dfrac{p_r D / (2t)}{p_r D / (4t)} = 2$$

This proves that hoop stress is **twice** the longitudinal stress — the most tested statement in KTU pressure-vessel theory.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

### Problem
A filament-wound pressure vessel has an internal diameter of **400 mm**, a winding angle of **55°**, and an internal pressure of **4 MPa**. The wall is **8 mm** thick. Calculate:
1. The hoop and longitudinal stresses.
2. The required mandrel speed $N$ if the carriage speed is **30 m/min**.

### Solution

**Part (a) — Stresses.**

Using $\sigma_h = p_r D / (2 t)$:

$$\sigma_h = \dfrac{4 \times 10^{6} \times 0.4}{2 \times 0.008} = \dfrac{1.6 \times 10^{6}}{0.016} = 100 \text{ MPa}$$

Using $\sigma_l = p_r D / (4 t)$:

$$\sigma_l = \dfrac{4 \times 10^{6} \times 0.4}{4 \times 0.008} = 50 \text{ MPa}$$

**Part (b) — Mandrel speed.**

From $\tan\alpha = v / (\pi D N)$, solve for $N$:

$$N = \dfrac{v}{\pi D \tan\alpha} = \dfrac{30 \times 10^{3} \text{ mm/min}}{\pi \times 400 \times \tan 55°}$$

Computing the denominator: $\pi \times 400 \times 1.4281 \approx 1794.3$.

$$N = \dfrac{30000}{1794.3} \approx 16.72 \text{ rev/min}$$

**Final Answer:** $\sigma_h = 100$ MPa, $\sigma_l = 50$ MPa, $N \approx 16.72$ rpm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** The optimum angle was 54.7°; the problem uses 55° — extremely close to isotensoid. Hence the vessel will be **near-equally stressed** in both fiber directions — a hallmark of well-designed filament-wound vessels.

---

## 3.4 Pultrusion Pull-Speed Estimation

### Statement
Derive the relationship between the maximum permissible pull speed $V_{pull}$ and the die length $L_{die}$, given a target residence time $t_{res}$ for cure.

### Derivation

The pull speed is the length of cured profile produced per unit time. If a fiber bundle must remain in the die for at least $t_{res}$ seconds to fully cure, then in that time the bundle advances exactly $L_{die}$:

$$L_{die} = V_{pull} \cdot t_{res}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\;V_{pull} = \dfrac{L_{die}}{t_{res}}\;}$$

**Example:** A die of length **1.2 m** and target residence time **90 s**:

$$V_{pull} = \dfrac{1.2}{90} = 0.0133 \text{ m/s} \approx 0.8 \text{ m/min}$$

This is a typical production rate for a pultrusion line — slow but continuous, yielding many meters per hour of structural profile.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pultrusion Process Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Creel\nFiber Roving Spools] --> B[Resin Bath\nWet-out Station]
    B --> C[Preformer\nShaping Guide]
    C --> D[Heated Die\nCuring Zones]
    D --> E[Puller\nCaterpillar Clamps]
    E --> F[Cut-off Saw\nLength Control]
    F --> G[Finished Profile\nStacking and Packing]

    subgraph H["Inside the Die - 3 Temperature Zones"]
        H1[Zone 1: Pre-heat 80-100 C] --> H2[Zone 2: Gel-Exotherm 120-160 C] --> H3[Zone 3: Final Cure 150-180 C]
    end

    D -.controlled by.-> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dashed line from the die to its internal zone breakdown shows the temperature profile concept. In a real die, the cross-section is fixed, but the *axial* temperature rises in three stages.

---

## 4.2 Filament Winding Process Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R1[Creel\nFiber Rovings] --> R2[Resin Bath\nWet Winding]
    R3[Prepreg Spool\nDry Winding] --> R2
    R2 --> R4[Pay-out Eye\nTension Control]
    R4 --> R5[Carriage\nTranslates Axially]
    R5 --> R6[Rotating Mandrel\nAxis of Revolution]
    R6 --> R7[Wet Layup\nHelical Pattern]
    R7 --> R8[Curing Oven\nor Autoclave]
    R8 --> R9[Cooling Zone]
    R9 --> R10[Mandrel Extraction\nFinished Hollow Vessel]
```

---

## 4.3 Winding-Pattern Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph W1["HOOP WINDING - alpha approx 90 deg"]
        W1A[Fibers almost perpendicular to axis]
        W1B[Maximum circumferential strength]
    end
    subgraph W2["HELICAL WINDING - 0 less alpha less 90"]
        W2A[Fibers at intermediate angle]
        W2B[Balanced biaxial load]
    end
    subgraph W3["POLAR WINDING - on sphere"]
        W3A[Fibers pass over polar caps]
        W3B[Spherical pressure vessels]
    end

    A[Choose Winding Pattern] --> W1
    A --> W2
    A --> W3
```

---

    P[Pultruded Composite Profiles] --> P1[Structural Shapes]
    P --> P2[Electrical Components]

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define pultrusion. List any four advantages of the pultrusion process.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
Pultrusion is a continuous manufacturing process for producing fiber-reinforced polymer (FRP) composite profiles of constant cross-section. Reinforcement fibers are pulled through a resin bath and a heated die where the resin cures, producing a cured profile that is then cut to length.

**Four advantages:**
1. Continuous production with consistent quality.
2. High fiber volume fraction (50–65%) and excellent longitudinal strength.
3. Low labor cost and minimal material wastage.
4. Excellent corrosion resistance — replaces steel in chemical/marine environments.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark allocation tip:** 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for advantages. Examiners reward correctly numbered lists.

---

### Q2. What is an isotensoid pressure vessel? Why is the winding angle ≈ 54.7° considered optimum?
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**
An **isotensoid pressure vessel** is one in which the reinforcement fibers follow the lines of principal stress (isotensoid lines), so that every fiber carries the same tensile load at any given internal pressure.

At winding angle $\alpha \approx 54.7°$ ($\tan\alpha = \sqrt{2}$), the stresses in the helical wrap and the hoop wrap are equal, and the entire laminate reaches design stress simultaneously. This gives the **minimum-weight** pressure vessel for a given pressure rating.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE follows **Internal Choice** in Part B. Students must attempt **one of the two** OR questions in a module. Each OR question is 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Pultrusion Detailed Study
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

#### (a) With a neat schematic, explain the pultrusion process for manufacturing fiber-reinforced plastic (FRP) profiles. Discuss the role of the heated die and preformer. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Schematic (drawn on answer sheet):**
```
Creel → Resin Bath → Preformer → Heated Die → Puller → Cut-off Saw → Product
```

**Step-by-step explanation:**

1. **Creel:** Glass/carbon rovings and continuous strand mats are mounted on a creel and guided into the resin bath.
2. **Resin bath:** Fibers are impregnated with catalyzed thermoset resin (polyester, vinylester, or epoxy). Rollers aid wet-out.
3. **Preformer:** A shaped guide that removes excess resin, aligns the fiber bundle, and pre-shapes it to match the die cavity. Reduces voids.
4. **Heated die:** A steel die maintained at 120–180°C with three zones. Resin viscosity first drops, then gelation occurs, and finally full cure is achieved. Die walls are chrome-plated and polished for surface finish and easy release.
5. **Puller:** Reciprocating clamp or caterpillar track pulls the cured profile at 0.3–1.5 m/min.
6. **Cut-off saw:** Synchronized to cut profiles to required length.

**Role of Heated Die:**
- Provides activation energy for resin cross-linking.
- Three-zone temperature profile prevents exothermic runaway.
- Defines the cross-sectional geometry of the profile.

**Role of Preformer:**
- Squeezes excess resin → improves $V_f$ and reduces weight.
- Pre-shapes the bundle → reduces die wear and surface defects.

#### (b) Discuss the merits and demerits of pultrusion. Compare pultrusion with hand lay-up process. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Merits of Pultrusion:**
1. Continuous and automated → high production rate.
2. High and consistent fiber volume fraction (50–65%).
3. Excellent longitudinal mechanical properties.
4. Low labor cost, low scrap.
5. Reproducible quality; minimal voids.
6. Long lengths possible (limited only by transport).

**Demerits of Pultrusion:**
1. Limited to **constant cross-section** profiles.
2. **High tooling cost** for the heated die.
3. Difficulty in producing thick or complex 3D shapes.
4. Slow cure → low throughput compared to compression molding.

**Comparison: Pultrusion vs. Hand Lay-up**

| Feature | Pultrusion | Hand Lay-up |
|---|---|---|
| Process type | Continuous, automated | Batch, manual |
| Cross-section | Constant | Any |
| $V_f$ | 50–65% | 20–40% |
| Production rate | High (m/min) | Very low |
| Labor cost | Low | High |
| Tooling cost | High (steel die) | Low (mold) |
| Void content | Low | High |
| Part size | Long profiles | Large but limited |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Filament Winding Detailed Study
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

#### (a) Explain the filament winding process with a schematic. Differentiate between wet winding and dry winding. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Schematic (drawn on answer sheet):**
```
Roving Spool → Resin Bath (wet) / Prepreg (dry) → Tensioner → Carriage → Rotating Mandrel
                                                                       ↓
                                                              Curing Oven
                                                                       ↓
                                                                Mandrel Removal
```

**Process description:**
Continuous fiber rovings are pulled from a creel, impregnated with resin, and laid down on a rotating mandrel at a controlled angle. The carriage traverses axially as the mandrel rotates, creating a helical pattern. After winding, the entire assembly is cured in an oven or autoclave. The mandrel is extracted, leaving a hollow composite shell.

**Wet vs. Dry Winding:**

| Aspect | Wet Winding | Dry Winding |
|---|---|---|
| Resin application | At point of winding (resin bath) | Pre-applied (prepreg tow) |
| Resin content control | Less precise | Highly precise |
| Equipment | Simpler | More complex (heated pay-out) |
| Shelf life | Resin must be used quickly | Long shelf life of prepreg |
| Common use | Large tanks, pipes | Aerospace, rocket motors |
| Volatile emissions | Higher (open bath) | Lower (enclosed) |

#### (b) A filament-wound pressure vessel has an internal diameter of 500 mm and a winding angle of 55°. The mandrel rotates at 20 rpm. The internal pressure is 5 MPa and the wall thickness is 10 mm. Calculate: (i) Hoop and longitudinal stresses, (ii) Carriage delivery speed, (iii) Comment on whether the design is near-isotensoid. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Hoop and longitudinal stresses:**

Using $D = 0.5$ m, $t = 0.01$ m, $p_r = 5 \times 10^6$ Pa:

$$\sigma_h = \dfrac{p_r D}{2t} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{6} \times 0.5}{2 \times 0.01} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{6}}{0.02} = 125 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_l = \dfrac{p_r D}{4t} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{6} \times 0.5}{4 \times 0.01} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{6}}{0.04} = 62.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Carriage delivery speed:**

From $\tan\alpha = v / (\pi D N)$, solve for $v$:

$$v = \pi D N \tan\alpha$$

Converting units: $D = 0.5$ m, $N = 20$ rev/min, $\tan 55° = 1.4281$.

$$v = \pi \times 0.5 \times 20 \times 1.4281 \approx 44.86 \text{ m/min}$$

Or in mm/min: $v \approx 44,860$ mm/min.

**(iii) Isotensoid comment:**

The optimum isotensoid angle is $\alpha_{opt} = \tan^{-1}(\sqrt{2}) = 54.74°$. The given angle is **55°**, which is within 0.3° of the optimum. Hence the design is **near-isotensoid** — both fiber directions will reach design stress nearly simultaneously, giving near-minimum weight.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in the winding equation** — Mixing $v$ in m/min with $D$ in mm and $N$ in rev/s. Always pick *one consistent unit system* and stick to it.
> 2. **Forgetting the diameter-vs-radius** in the stress formula. $\sigma_h = p_r D / (2t)$, not $p_r R / (2t)$ (the two are equal only when written correctly). Examiners look for $D$ specifically.
> 3. **Calling pultrusion "extrusion of composites"** — wrong. The fibers are *pulled*, not pushed.
> 4. **Confusing the three die zones** — students often say "die is heated uniformly" and lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the schematic in long answers** — KTU expects a labeled diagram even if words are perfect. No diagram = up to 2 marks deducted.
> 6. **Ignoring the isotensoid angle 54.7°** — a free 1–2 mark question in nearly every KTU exam on this topic.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pultrusion** = *pulling* fibers through resin + heated die → constant cross-section profiles, continuous process.
- **Filament winding** = winding resin-impregnated fibers on a rotating mandrel → hollow axisymmetric parts.
- Pultrusion line: **Creel → Resin bath → Preformer → Heated die → Puller → Saw**.
- Pultrusion die has **3 temperature zones** (pre-heat, gel-exotherm, final cure) to prevent thermal runaway.
- Filament winding patterns: **hoop (≈90°), helical (0–90°), polar (spherical), planar**.
- **Critical winding equation:** $\tan\alpha = v / (\pi D N) = p / (\pi D)$.
- **Hoop stress:** $\sigma_h = p_r D / (2t)$.
- **Longitudinal stress:** $\sigma_l = p_r D / (4t)$.
- **Ratio:** $\sigma_h / \sigma_l = 2$ — always.
- **Isotensoid optimum winding angle:** $\alpha_{opt} = \tan^{-1}(\sqrt{2}) \approx 54.7°$.
- **Wet vs. dry winding:** wet = resin bath at point of layup; dry = pre-impregnated tows.
- **Pultrusion pull speed:** $V_{pull} = L_{die} / t_{res}$ (die length / residence time).
- **Pultrusion $V_f$:** 50–65%; **Filament winding $V_f$:** 60–70%.
- **Industries served:** Pultrusion → construction, electrical, cooling towers. Filament winding → aerospace (rocket casings), CNG tanks, chemical storage, pipes.
- **KTU favorite numbers:** $\alpha_{opt} = 54.7°$, ratio = 2, $V_f$ in 50–70% range.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metal Matrix Composites (MMC): Classification of Metals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET413 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Metal Matrix Composites (MMC): Classification of Metals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metal Matrix Composites (MMC)** are engineered composite materials in which a **metallic matrix phase** is reinforced with a second phase (typically a ceramic reinforcement such as fibers, whiskers, or particles) to obtain a combination of properties — high specific strength, high specific modulus, good elevated-temperature performance, and tailored thermal/electrical behavior — that is **not attainable in the monolithic base metal alone**.

The **metal matrix** is the continuous phase that:

- Binds the reinforcement together.
- Transfers and distributes applied load to the reinforcement.
- Protects the reinforcement from environmental degradation.
- Provides ductility and toughness to the otherwise brittle ceramic reinforcement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification of Metals for MMC (Module 3 Focus):** The metal matrix in an MMC is **not restricted to pure metals**. In practice, it spans **light alloys, refractory metals, and high-temperature superalloys**, each selected based on the operating temperature, density, and strength requirements of the target application.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Metal Scaffolding and the Ceramic Bricks"**
>
> Imagine constructing a **strong, lightweight house wall**:
> - The **bricks** represent the ceramic reinforcement (e.g., SiC, Al₂O₃, B, C fibers) — they are extremely **strong and stiff but brittle**.
> - The **mortar (cement paste)** represents the metal matrix (e.g., Al, Ti, Mg) — it is **ductile, tough, and can flow around the bricks** to hold them together.
>
> Just as the mortar binds the bricks and shares the load, the **metal matrix binds the reinforcement and shares the load**. Remove the mortar, the bricks collapse; remove the metal matrix, the reinforcement has no structural integrity. The combination yields properties **superior to either phase alone**.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics for MMC Selection

The selection of a metal matrix is governed by the following quantitative metrics:

- **Density (ρ):** Expressed in **g/cm³** or **kg/m³**. Lighter matrices (Al ≈ 2.7 g/cm³, Mg ≈ 1.74 g/cm³) yield higher specific properties.
- **Melting Point (Tₘ):** In **°C** or **K**. Determines the upper use temperature and processing window.
- **Young's Modulus (E):** In **GPa**. The matrix stiffness baseline.
- **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE / α):** In **× 10⁻⁶ /°C**. Critical for thermal-matching with reinforcement.
- **Wettability (θ):** Contact angle in **degrees (°)**. Lower θ = better reinforcement-matrix bonding.
- **Rule of Mixtures Bounds:** Voigt (upper) and Reuss (lower) bounds for property estimation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The classification of metals for MMC is **not just a list** — it is a **decision matrix** linking matrix chemistry to (a) reinforcement type, (b) processing route, and (c) target service application (aerospace, automotive, electronics, defense).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Classify Metals for MMCs?

The classification serves **three engineering purposes**:

1. **Property Tailoring** — Different metal families offer distinct property envelopes (lightweight Al-MMCs vs. high-temperature Ni-superalloy MMCs).
2. **Process Compatibility** — Matrix melting point dictates whether stir casting, squeeze casting, powder metallurgy, or in-situ processing is feasible.
3. **Application Mapping** — Aerospace demands low density + high stiffness; electronic packaging demands low CTE + high thermal conductivity; turbine blades demand creep resistance.

### 2.2 Primary Classification Schemes (KTU Module 3)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The classification of metals for MMC matrices is presented under three orthogonal schemes:**

#### A. Classification by Matrix Chemistry (Most Common — KTU Standard)

| Sl. | Matrix Family | Typical Examples | Key Characteristics | Representative Application |
|-----|---------------|------------------|---------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | **Light Metal — Aluminum (Al)** | Pure Al, Al-Si, Al-Cu, Al-Mg-Si (6xxx), Al-Zn-Mg-Cu (7xxx) | Low density (~2.7 g/cm³), good thermal conductivity, easy castability, cost-effective | Automotive pistons, brake rotors, drive shafts, aerospace skins |
| 2 | **Light Metal — Magnesium (Mg)** | Pure Mg, AZ91, AM60, WE43 | Lowest density among structural metals (~1.74 g/cm³), poor corrosion resistance | Aerospace, portable electronics, automotive steering columns |
| 3 | **Refractory Light Metal — Titanium (Ti)** | Commercially Pure Ti (CP-Ti), Ti-6Al-4V, Ti-6Al-2Sn-4Zr | High specific strength, excellent corrosion resistance, moderate density (~4.5 g/cm³) | Jet engine compressor blades, biomedical implants, rocket casings |
| 4 | **High-Temp Superalloys** | Ni-based (Inconel 718, Waspaloy), Co-based (Stellite), Fe-based (A286) | Retain strength > 650 °C, excellent creep and oxidation resistance | Gas turbine blades, rocket nozzles, nuclear reactor parts |
| 5 | **Copper (Cu) and Copper Alloys** | Pure Cu, Cu-W, Cu-Mo | Outstanding thermal/electrical conductivity, high density (~8.96 g/cm³) | Heat sinks, electrical contacts, electronic packaging |
| 6 | **Refractory Metals** | W, Mo, Nb, Ta, Cr | Extreme melting points (> 2000 °C), high stiffness, high density | Hypersonic vehicle leading edges, rocket thrust chambers |
| 7 | **Intermetallic Compounds** | Ni-Al, Ti-Al (γ-TiAl), Fe-Al, MoSi₂ | Ordered crystal structure, low density, semi-brittle, high-temp capability | Future aero-engine components, turbocharger rotors |

#### B. Classification by Reinforcement Form (Cross-Referenced with Matrix)

| Reinforcement Form | Geometry | Aspect Ratio (L/D) | Compatible Matrices | Property Direction |
|-------------------|----------|--------------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| **Continuous Fibers** | Long, aligned | ≥ 1000 | Al, Ti, Ni-superalloys, Cu | Highly anisotropic (max along fiber) |
| **Short Fibers / Whiskers** | Discontinuous, random or aligned | 10 – 1000 | Al, Mg, Ti | Moderately anisotropic |
| **Particulates** | Equiaxed, < 50 µm | ≈ 1 | Al, Mg, Cu, Zn | Nearly isotropic |
| **Laminated Foils** | Sheet/foil layers | — | Ti, Al, Ni | 2D planar anisotropy |

#### C. Classification by Processing Temperature Regime

| Regime | Process | Typical Matrix | T_process Range |
|--------|---------|----------------|------------------|
| **Liquid-State** | Stir casting, squeeze casting, compocasting, spray deposition | Al, Mg, Cu | Above Tₘ of matrix |
| **Solid-State** | Powder metallurgy, diffusion bonding, roll bonding, extrusion, forging | Al, Ti, Ni, W | 0.4 – 0.8 Tₘ |
| **In-Situ** | Reactive synthesis (XD™), Lanxide, controlled solidification | Al, Ti, Ni | Variable |
| **Deposition** | Physical vapor deposition, spray forming, plasma spray | Ni-superalloys, Ti | Variable |

### 2.3 Key Governing Equations — KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations form the analytical backbone for predicting MMC properties. Memorize the form, the physical meaning of each term, and the units.**

| Property | Equation (Rule of Mixtures — Voigt / Upper Bound) | Equation (Inverse Rule of Mixtures — Reuss / Lower Bound) | Units |
|----------|---------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------|-------|
| **Density** | $$\rho_c = \rho_m V_m + \rho_f V_f$$ | — | g/cm³ |
| **Young's Modulus (Load parallel to fiber)** | $$E_c = E_m V_m + E_f V_f$$ | $$\frac{1}{E_c} = \frac{V_m}{E_m} + \frac{V_f}{E_f}$$ | GPa |
| **Tensile Strength (parallel, fiber-dominated)** | $$\sigma_c = \sigma_m V_m + \sigma_f V_f$$ | $$\frac{1}{\sigma_c} = \frac{V_m}{\sigma_m} + \frac{V_f}{\sigma_f}$$ | MPa |
| **Thermal Conductivity (parallel)** | $$K_c = K_m V_m + K_f V_f$$ | $$\frac{1}{K_c} = \frac{V_m}{K_m} + \frac{V_f}{K_f}$$ | W/(m·K) |
| **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE)** | $$\alpha_c = \frac{\alpha_m E_m V_m + \alpha_f E_f V_f}{E_m V_m + E_f V_f}$$ (Schapery bound) | — | × 10⁻⁶ /°C |
| **Volume Fraction Constraint** | $$V_m + V_f = 1 \quad \text{or} \quad V_m + V_f + V_v = 1$$ (including voids) | — | Dimensionless |

Where:
- Subscript **c** = composite, **m** = matrix, **f** = fiber/reinforcement, **v** = void
- **V** = volume fraction (dimensionless, 0 ≤ V ≤ 1)
- **E** = Young's modulus, **σ** = tensile strength, **K** = thermal conductivity, **α** = CTE

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Al/SiCp MMCs replace cast iron in **helicopter rotor blade sleeves** (weight saving ≈ 25 %).
- **Automotive:** Al/Al₂O₃ short-fiber MMC pistons (Toyota, Honda) improve wear resistance and reduce reciprocating mass.
- **Electronics:** Cu/W and Cu/Mo MMCs used as **heat spreaders** in high-power semiconductor modules.
- **Defense:** B/Al MMCs (boron fibers in Al matrix) used in structural stiffeners of advanced fighter aircraft.
- **Biomedical:** Ti/HA (hydroxyapatite) MMCs for **load-bearing implants** with bone-matching modulus.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Density of an MMC (Linear Rule of Mixtures)

**Physical Assumption:** The composite is *void-free* and the two phases (matrix and reinforcement) retain their individual intrinsic densities.

**Step 1 — Mass Conservation:**
Let $M_c$, $M_m$, and $M_f$ denote the mass of the composite, matrix, and reinforcement, respectively. Conservation of mass gives:

$$M_c = M_m + M_f$$

**Step 2 — Substitute mass as (density × volume):**

$$M_c = \rho_m \cdot \mathcal{V}_m + \rho_f \cdot \mathcal{V}_f$$

**Step 3 — Volume Conservation:**
For a void-free composite, the total composite volume is the sum of phase volumes:

$$\mathcal{V}_c = \mathcal{V}_m + \mathcal{V}_f$$

**Step 4 — Divide both sides by the composite volume $\mathcal{V}_c$:**

$$\frac{M_c}{\mathcal{V}_c} = \rho_m \cdot \frac{\mathcal{V}_m}{\mathcal{V}_c} + \rho_f \cdot \frac{\mathcal{V}_f}{\mathcal{V}_c}$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the terms:**
- $M_c / \mathcal{V}_c = \rho_c$ (composite density)
- $\mathcal{V}_m / \mathcal{V}_c = V_m$ (matrix volume fraction)
- $\mathcal{V}_f / \mathcal{V}_c = V_f$ (reinforcement volume fraction)

**Step 6 — Final Form:**

$$\boxed{\rho_c = \rho_m V_m + \rho_f V_f}$$

with the constraint $V_m + V_f = 1$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because the rule of mixtures for density is *exact* (mass and volume are extensive, additive properties), it is a **hard lower/upper bound simultaneously** — there is no Reuss equivalent for density.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Young's Modulus (Voigt / Iso-Strain / Upper Bound)

**Physical Assumption (Voigt Model):** The matrix and reinforcement experience **equal strain** ($\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon_f$). This corresponds to loading **parallel to continuous aligned fibers**.

**Step 1 — Force Equilibrium:**
The total load on the composite is shared additively by the two phases:

$$F_c = F_m + F_f$$

**Step 2 — Express each force as (stress × area):**

$$\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_m A_m + \sigma_f A_f$$

**Step 3 — Divide by the composite cross-sectional area $A_c$:**

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_m \cdot \frac{A_m}{A_c} + \sigma_f \cdot \frac{A_f}{A_c}$$

For a unit composite cross-section with constant cross-section, area fractions equal volume fractions:
$A_m / A_c = V_m$ and $A_f / A_c = V_f$.

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_m V_m + \sigma_f V_f$$

**Step 4 — Apply Hooke's Law ($\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$) under the iso-strain condition $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon$:**

$$E_c \varepsilon = E_m \varepsilon \cdot V_m + E_f \varepsilon \cdot V_f$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $\varepsilon$ from both sides:**

$$\boxed{E_c = E_m V_m + E_f V_f}$$

This is the **Voigt / Rule of Mixtures / Iso-Strain upper bound** for modulus.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Young's Modulus (Reuss / Iso-Stress / Lower Bound)

**Physical Assumption (Reuss Model):** The matrix and reinforcement experience **equal stress** ($\sigma_c = \sigma_m = \sigma_f$). This corresponds to loading **transverse to fibers** or a particulate-reinforced composite.

**Step 1 — Total elongation of the composite:**

$$\delta_c = \delta_m + \delta_f$$

**Step 2 — Express each elongation as (strain × length):** Under a common gauge length $L$:

$$\varepsilon_c L = \varepsilon_m L + \varepsilon_f L \;\Rightarrow\; \varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_m + \varepsilon_f$$

**Step 3 — Apply iso-stress condition $\sigma_c = \sigma_m = \sigma_f = \sigma$ and Hooke's Law ($\varepsilon = \sigma / E$):**

$$\frac{\sigma}{E_c} = \frac{\sigma}{E_m} V_m + \frac{\sigma}{E_f} V_f$$

(Note: the strain contributions are weighted by their respective volume fractions under iso-stress.)

**Step 4 — Cancel $\sigma$ from both sides:**

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{E_c} = \frac{V_m}{E_m} + \frac{V_f}{E_f}}$$

This is the **Reuss / Inverse Rule of Mixtures / Iso-Stress lower bound**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always identify the **loading direction relative to fiber orientation** before selecting Voigt or Reuss. Mixing them is a common error.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Worked Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** An aluminum matrix composite is reinforced with **40 vol% SiC particulates**. Given $E_{Al} = 70$ GPa, $E_{SiC} = 410$ GPa, $\rho_{Al} = 2.70$ g/cm³, $\rho_{SiC} = 3.20$ g/cm³, $\alpha_{Al} = 23 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C, $\alpha_{SiC} = 4.5 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C. Calculate:
(a) Composite density
(b) Voigt and Reuss bounds for Young's modulus
(c) Schapery CTE estimate

**Solution (a) — Density:**

$$\rho_c = \rho_{Al} V_{Al} + \rho_{SiC} V_{SiC}$$

$$= 2.70 \times 0.60 + 3.20 \times 0.40$$

$$= 1.62 + 1.28 = 2.90 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Solution (b) — Voigt and Reuss Modulus:**

**Voigt (upper bound):**

$$E_c^{Voigt} = 70 \times 0.60 + 410 \times 0.40 = 42 + 164 = 206 \text{ GPa}$$

**Reuss (lower bound):**

$$\frac{1}{E_c^{Reuss}} = \frac{0.60}{70} + \frac{0.40}{410} = 0.008571 + 0.000976 = 0.009547 \text{ GPa}^{-1}$$

$$E_c^{Reuss} = 104.7 \text{ GPa}$$

**Solution (c) — Schapery CTE:**

$$\alpha_c = \frac{\alpha_{Al} E_{Al} V_{Al} + \alpha_{SiC} E_{SiC} V_{SiC}}{E_{Al} V_{Al} + E_{SiC} V_{SiC}}$$

**Numerator:**

$$= (23 \times 10^{-6})(70)(0.60) + (4.5 \times 10^{-6})(410)(0.40)$$

$$= 966 \times 10^{-6} + 738 \times 10^{-6} = 1704 \times 10^{-6} \text{ GPa/°C}$$

**Denominator (this is the Voigt modulus):**

$$= 70 \times 0.60 + 410 \times 0.40 = 206 \text{ GPa}$$

**Result:**

$$\alpha_c = \frac{1704 \times 10^{-6}}{206} = 8.27 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /°C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The composite CTE (~8.3 × 10⁻⁶ /°C) is dramatically lower than pure Al (23 × 10⁻⁶ /°C) and approaches that of Si (≈ 4 × 10⁻⁶ /°C) — a key reason SiC-reinforced Al MMCs are used in **electronic packaging and optical structural components**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
