## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*Classify metal matrices used in MMC. Give two examples of metal matrices and state one key application of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Metal matrices for MMC are classified based on **chemistry, operating temperature, and density** into the following families:
>
> 1. **Light metals** — Al and Mg alloys. *Example:* Al 6061 reinforced with Al₂O₃ particulates, used in **automotive brake rotors and pistons** (1.5 marks).
> 2. **Refractory light metals** — Ti and its alloys. *Example:* Ti-6Al-4V reinforced with SiC fibers, used in **jet engine compressor blades and rings** (1.5 marks).
>
> *(Acceptable: Ni-superalloy/SiC for turbine blades; Cu/Mo for electronic heat sinks — full credit given.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP]**
*Distinguish between continuous fiber-reinforced MMC and particulate-reinforced MMC in terms of (i) anisotropy, (ii) property prediction model, and (iii) cost.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Continuous Fiber MMC | Particulate MMC |
|--------|----------------------|-----------------|
| (i) Anisotropy | Highly **anisotropic** — properties depend strongly on fiber direction (1 mark) | **Isotropic** or near-isotropic — properties similar in all directions (1 mark) |
| (ii) Property Model | **Voigt (iso-strain)** for longitudinal, **Reuss (iso-stress)** for transverse | **Halpin–Tsai or modified rule of mixtures** (semi-empirical) |
| (iii) Cost | High — fiber lay-up and diffusion bonding are expensive | Low — stir casting and powder metallurgy are economical |

*(Final row may be written in two lines: 1 mark for cost distinction.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With neat schematics, classify metal matrices used in MMC into at least **four major families**. For each family, state the **base composition, two key property advantages, and one representative application**.

**(b) [7 marks]** For an Al-matrix composite reinforced with 30 vol% Al₂O₃ short fibers, given $E_{Al} = 70$ GPa, $E_{Al_2O_3} = 380$ GPa, $\rho_{Al} = 2.70$ g/cm³, $\rho_{Al_2O_3} = 3.95$ g/cm³, $\alpha_{Al} = 23 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C, $\alpha_{Al_2O_3} = 7 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C, compute the **composite density, the Voigt and Reuss bounds for Young's modulus, and the Schapery CTE estimate**.

---

**Model Solution — Question A:**

**(a) Classification (7 marks):**

The classification is best presented as a table. The following table scores full marks:

| Family | Base Composition | Property Advantages | Application |
|--------|------------------|---------------------|-------------|
| **Light Metals — Al** | Pure Al, 2xxx, 6xxx, 7xxx series | Low density (~2.7 g/cm³), good thermal conductivity, excellent castability | Automotive pistons, brake drums, drive shafts |
| **Light Metals — Mg** | Pure Mg, AZ91, AM60, WE43 | **Lowest density of all structural metals** (~1.74 g/cm³), good damping | Aerospace brackets, laptop housings, EV steering |
| **Refractory Light — Ti** | CP-Ti, Ti-6Al-4V, Ti-10V-2Fe-3Al | High specific strength, excellent corrosion resistance, biocompatible | Jet engine discs, biomedical implants, marine |
| **High-Temp Superalloys — Ni-based** | Inconel 718, Waspaloy, René 95 | Retains strength up to ~ 1100 °C, creep and oxidation resistant | Turbine blades, rocket nozzles, nuclear parts |
| **Heavy / Conductive — Cu** | Pure Cu, Cu-W, Cu-Mo | Very high thermal and electrical conductivity | Heat sinks, electrical contacts, IGBT base plates |
| **Intermetallics** | γ-TiAl, NiAl, MoSi₂ | Low density, semi-brittle, high-temp capability | Future aero-engines, turbocharger rotors |

*Grading:* 1 mark per family (composition + property + application shared = 1 mark). Full marks for **four** families well described; bonus credit for the fifth.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Volume Fractions:**

$$V_m = 0.70, \quad V_f = 0.30 \quad (\text{1 mark for identifying values})$$

**Step 2 — Composite Density (1.5 marks):**

$$\rho_c = (2.70)(0.70) + (3.95)(0.30)$$

$$= 1.89 + 1.185 = 3.075 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

$$\boxed{\rho_c \approx 3.08 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

**Step 3 — Voigt Modulus (1.5 marks):**

$$E_c^{Voigt} = (70)(0.70) + (380)(0.30)$$

$$= 49 + 114 = 163 \text{ GPa}$$

$$\boxed{E_c^{Voigt} = 163 \text{ GPa}}$$

**Step 4 — Reuss Modulus (1.5 marks):**

$$\frac{1}{E_c^{Reuss}} = \frac{0.70}{70} + \frac{0.30}{380}$$

$$= 0.0100 + 0.0007895 = 0.0107895 \text{ GPa}^{-1}$$

$$E_c^{Reuss} = 92.68 \text{ GPa}$$

$$\boxed{E_c^{Reuss} \approx 92.7 \text{ GPa}}$$

**Step 5 — Schapery CTE (1.5 marks):**

$$\alpha_c = \frac{\alpha_{Al} E_{Al} V_{Al} + \alpha_{f} E_{f} V_{f}}{E_{Al} V_{Al} + E_{f} V_{f}}$$

**Numerator:**

$$= (23 \times 10^{-6})(70)(0.70) + (7 \times 10^{-6})(380)(0.30)$$

$$= 1127 \times 10^{-6} + 798 \times 10^{-6} = 1925 \times 10^{-6} \text{ GPa/°C}$$

**Denominator (Voigt modulus) = 163 GPa**

$$\alpha_c = \frac{1925 \times 10^{-6}}{163} = 11.81 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /°C}$$

$$\boxed{\alpha_c \approx 11.8 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /°C}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> - ❌ **Mixing units** in modulus (MPa vs GPa) — always convert to a single unit before substitution.
> - ❌ **Forgetting the volume-fraction constraint** $V_m + V_f = 1$ when checking answers.
> - ❌ **Applying Voigt for transverse loading** — Voigt is for parallel/iso-strain; Reuss is for transverse/iso-stress. Wrong bound = full 3 marks lost.
> - ❌ **Omitting the volume-fraction weighting in the CTE equation** — using a simple $\alpha_c = \alpha_m V_m + \alpha_f V_f$ is **wrong**; CTE is a stiffness-weighted property (Schapery model).
> - ❌ **Rounding errors** — carry at least 4 significant figures until the final step.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **three orthogonal schemes** for classifying metals used in MMC matrices: (i) by matrix chemistry, (ii) by reinforcement form, and (iii) by processing route. For each scheme, give **two specific sub-categories with one example** of a matrix–reinforcement–application combination.

**(b) [7 marks]** A continuous boron-fiber-reinforced aluminum composite has a fiber volume fraction of 0.45. Given $E_{Al} = 70$ GPa, $E_{B} = 380$ GPa, $\sigma_{t,Al} = 90$ MPa, $\sigma_{t,B} = 3500$ MPa. Compute: (i) the **longitudinal modulus** using the rule of mixtures, (ii) the **transverse modulus** using the inverse rule of mixtures, and (iii) the **longitudinal tensile strength** using the rule of mixtures. State which bound is conservative for **structural design**.

---

**Model Solution — Question B:**

**(a) Three Classification Schemes (7 marks):**

**Scheme 1 — By Matrix Chemistry (2 marks):**
1. **Light metals** — *e.g.,* Al/Al₂O₃p for automotive brake rotors.
2. **Refractory light metals** — *e.g.,* Ti-6Al-4V/SiCf for jet engine compressor blades.
3. **High-temperature superalloys** — *e.g.,* Inconel 718/Al₂O₃p for turbine discs.
4. **Heavy / conductive metals** — *e.g.,* Cu/W for electronic heat sinks.

**Scheme 2 — By Reinforcement Form (2 marks):**
1. **Continuous fibers** — *e.g.,* B-fiber/Al for aerospace structural tubes.
2. **Particulates** — *e.g.,* SiCp/Al for automotive pistons.
3. **Whiskers / short fibers** — *e.g.,* Al₂O₃ short fibers in Al for cylinder liners.
4. **Laminated foils** — *e.g.,* Ti/SiCf laminae for fan blades.

**Scheme 3 — By Processing Route (2 marks):**
1. **Liquid-state** — stir casting, squeeze casting, compocasting (used for Al, Mg).
2. **Solid-state** — powder metallurgy, diffusion bonding (used for Ti, Ni-superalloys).
3. **In-situ synthesis** — XD, Lanxide, reactive hot pressing.
4. **Deposition techniques** — PVD, spray forming, plasma spray.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The three schemes are **orthogonal but interrelated** — chemistry dictates processing feasibility, and reinforcement form dictates both property prediction model and application.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

Given: $V_f = 0.45$, $V_m = 0.55$.

**(i) Longitudinal Modulus — Voigt (2 marks):**

$$E_{c,L} = E_{Al} V_m + E_B V_f$$

$$= 70 \times 0.55 + 380 \times 0.45$$

$$= 38.5 + 171 = 209.5 \text{ GPa}$$

$$\boxed{E_{c,L} = 209.5 \text{ GPa}}$$

**(ii) Transverse Modulus — Reuss (2 marks):**

$$\frac{1}{E_{c,T}} = \frac{V_m}{E_{Al}} + \frac{V_f}{E_B} = \frac{0.55}{70} + \frac{0.45}{380}$$

$$= 0.007857 + 0.001184 = 0.009041 \text{ GPa}^{-1}$$

$$E_{c,T} = 110.6 \text{ GPa}$$

$$\boxed{E_{c,T} \approx 110.6 \text{ GPa}}$$

**(iii) Longitudinal Tensile Strength (2 marks):**

$$\sigma_{c,L} = \sigma_{t,Al} V_m + \sigma_{t,B} V_f$$

$$= 90 \times 0.55 + 3500 \times 0.45$$

$$= 49.5 + 1575 = 1624.5 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{c,L} \approx 1625 \text{ MPa}}$$

**(iv) Conservative Design Bound (1 mark):**

The **Reuss / inverse rule of mixtures (lower bound)** is the **conservative choice for structural design**, as it provides the minimum expected stiffness. Engineers use it to ensure the structure can sustain the design load even under worst-case property scenarios.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:**
> - ❌ **Writing Voigt and Reuss without naming the loading direction** — always state "longitudinal = Voigt" and "transverse = Reuss".
> - ❌ **Using rule of mixtures for density in the wrong sign** — density rule of mixtures has no Reuss equivalent.
> - ❌ **Forgetting to convert MPa to GPa or vice versa** consistently — units carry 1 mark.
> - ❌ **In Scheme 1, omitting at least four families** — KTU expects **breadth** in classification, not just Al.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Classification of Metals for MMC (Module 3):**

- ✅ MMC = Metal matrix (continuous) + Reinforcement (discontinuous or continuous ceramic) → engineered material.
- ✅ **Matrix functions:** load transfer, reinforcement binding, environmental protection, ductility supply.
- ✅ **Six matrix families:** (1) Light metals — Al, Mg; (2) Refractory light — Ti; (3) Ni/Co/Fe superalloys; (4) Heavy conductive — Cu; (5) Refractory — W, Mo, Nb; (6) Intermetallics — TiAl, NiAl, MoSi₂.
- ✅ **Al** is the **most widely used MMC matrix** (≈ 70 % of commercial MMC market).
- ✅ **Ti** offers the **best specific strength** up to ~ 500 °C.
- ✅ **Ni-superalloys** are mandatory for **> 650 °C service** (turbines, rockets).
- ✅ **Cu** matrices are chosen for **thermal management** (heat sinks, IGBT bases).
- ✅ **Three orthogonal classification axes:** chemistry × reinforcement form × processing route.
- ✅ **Reinforcement forms:** continuous fiber, whisker/short fiber, particulate, laminated foil.
- ✅ **Processing routes:** liquid-state, solid-state, in-situ, deposition.
- ✅ **Voigt (iso-strain)** = upper bound = **longitudinal** modulus.
- ✅ **Reuss (iso-stress)** = lower bound = **transverse** modulus = **conservative design value**.
- ✅ **Density rule of mixtures is exact** (no Reuss equivalent).
- ✅ **CTE uses Schapery (stiffness-weighted) model** — never simple volume weighting.
- ✅ **Volume-fraction constraint** $V_m + V_f = 1$ (or $= 1 - V_v$ if voids present).
- ✅ **Units to remember:** g/cm³ (density), GPa (modulus), MPa (strength), W/(m·K) (thermal conductivity), × 10⁻⁶ /°C (CTE).
- ✅ **Common KTU weightage:** Module 3 (MMC) typically carries **15–20 %** of total marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intermetallics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET413 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Intermetallics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **intermetallic compound** (also termed an *intermetallic phase* or *ordered intermetallic*) is a solid-state compound composed of two or more metallic elements, possessing a **definite stoichiometric ratio** and a **crystallographically ordered structure** that differs fundamentally from the constituent pure metals.

Formally, an intermetallic phase $A_xB_y$ exhibits the following signature attributes:
- **Long-range crystallographic order** — distinct sublattices are occupied preferentially by specific atom types (A or B).
- **Defined stoichiometry** governed by the electron concentration (Hume-Rothery phases), atomic size ratio, or electronegativity.
- **Mixed bonding character** — combining metallic, covalent, and sometimes ionic contributions.
- **Properties intermediate between metals and ceramics** — high strength and stiffness, often with low ductility at ambient temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Standard:** An intermetallic is *not* an alloy. Alloys are disordered solid solutions; intermetallics possess **ordered sublattices** with specific site occupancy rules. This distinction is a **favourite 3-mark KTU question**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Designated Seating" Restaurant

Imagine a restaurant where guests (atoms) of two different groups (Element A and Element B) dine together:
- In a **regular alloy (solid solution)**, the two groups can sit *anywhere* — a disordered, random arrangement.
- In an **intermetallic compound**, the restaurant has a strict reservation system: the maître d' (crystal lattice) directs A-atoms to specific *blue chairs* (sublattice α) and B-atoms to *red chairs* (sublattice β). The order is rigid and reproducible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Consequence of Order:** Because intermetallics maintain order up to a critical temperature (the *order–disorder transition temperature*, $T_c$), they resist dislocation motion far more than disordered alloys — yielding exceptional **high-temperature strength retention**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Avogadro's number | $N_A$ | **6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹** |
| Boltzmann constant | $k_B$ | **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K** |
| Order–disorder transition | $T_c$ | **0.5 – 0.8 × $T_m$** (K) |
| Atomic size ratio tolerance | $r_A / r_B$ | **0.85 – 1.15** (Hume-Rothery) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Intermetallics

Intermetallics are classified based on their **structural type**, **bonding**, and **electron concentration**. The most important classes relevant to PEMET413 are:

#### 2.1.1 Geometrically Close-Packed (GCP) Phases
- **Laves phases** ($AB_2$) — e.g., $MgCu_2$ (C15 cubic), $MgZn_2$ (C14 hexagonal), $MgNi_2$ (C36 hexagonal).
- Atomic size ratio $r_A / r_B \approx 1.225$ is the **ideal packing ratio** for these phases.

#### 2.1.2 Topologically Close-Packed (TCP) Phases
- $\sigma$, $\mu$, $\chi$, R phases — found in superalloys.

#### 2.1.3 Common Ordered Structures
- **B2 (CsCl-type)** — $AB$ compounds (NiAl, FeAl, CuZn).
- **L1₂ (Cu₃Au-type)** — $A_3B$ compounds (Ni₃Al, Cu₃Au, Ni₃Fe).
- **D0₃** — $A_3B$ compounds (Fe₃Al, Fe₃Si).
- **D0₁₉** — $A_3B$ compounds (Ti₃Al, Ni₃Sn).
- **L1₀** — $AB$ compounds (TiAl, CuAu).
- **L2₁ (Heusler)** — $A_2BC$ (Ni₂MnGa, Cu₂MnAl).

### 2.2 Why Are Intermetallics Useful in Composites?

Intermetallics serve as **reinforcement phases** or **matrix materials** in:
1. **Intermetallic-matrix composites (IMCs)** — e.g., NiAl reinforced with $Al_2O_3$ or $TiB_2$.
2. **High-temperature structural composites** — for turbine blades, jet engines.
3. **Lightweight aerospace composites** — based on TiAl, NiAl.

### 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' — Why Intermetallics Exhibit Unique Properties

- **High melting point** — strong directional (covalent) bonding from $d$-orbital overlap.
- **Low density** — light elements like Al, Ti dominate (e.g., TiAl ≈ 3.9 g/cm³).
- **Anomalous yield strength with temperature** — $Ni_3Al$ shows **increasing yield strength** up to ~700 °C, then drops.
- **Brittleness at room temperature** — limited slip systems, grain-boundary weakness.
- **Creep resistance** — low dislocation mobility, high activation energy for self-diffusion.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Constants |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho = \dfrac{n \cdot M_{avg}}{V_{cell} \cdot N_A}$ | Theoretical density from unit cell | $n$ = atoms/cell, $M$ in g/mol, $V$ in cm³ |
| 2 | $M_{avg} = \dfrac{x M_A + y M_B}{x + y}$ | Average molar mass for $A_xB_y$ | g/mol |
| 3 | $V_{cell} = a^3$ (cubic) or $a^2 c$ (tetragonal) | Unit cell volume | cm³ or ų |
| 4 | $APF = \dfrac{n \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3}{V_{cell}}$ | Atomic packing factor | dimensionless |
| 5 | $r_A / r_B = 1.225$ | Ideal Laves phase ratio | dimensionless |
| 6 | $T_c \approx (0.5 - 0.8) T_m$ | Order–disorder transition | K |
| 7 | $\sigma_y \propto \left(\dfrac{b}{L}\right)$ | Hall-Petch strengthening | MPa |
| 8 | $G = \dfrac{\tau}{\gamma}$ | Shear modulus from shear stress/strain | GPa |
| 9 | $\dot{\varepsilon} = A \sigma^n \exp\left(-\dfrac{Q}{RT}\right)$ | Norton-Bailey creep law | s⁻¹ |
| 10 | $W_{APB} = \dfrac{\pi \gamma_{APB} r^2}{r}$ | Antiphase boundary energy term | J/m² |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The density formula (1) and Laves ratio (5) appear in **almost every ESE Module 3 question paper** of PEMET413. Memorise these.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

| Application | Intermetallic | Property Exploited |
|---|---|---|
| Jet engine turbine blades | Ni₃Al, NiAl | High-T strength, oxidation resistance |
| Automotive turbochargers | TiAl | Low density, high stiffness |
| Shape-memory actuators | NiTi (Nitinol) | Thermoelastic martensitic transformation |
| Magnetic refrigeration | $La(Fe,Si)_{13}$ | Magnetocaloric effect |
| Joining/brazing fillers | $CuZn$, $Ni_3Sn$ | Controlled melting behaviour |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Density of an Intermetallic $A_xB_y$ (General)

The general density expression for any intermetallic compound is derived from first principles using the unit-cell volume and the stoichiometric mass contribution.

**Starting premise:** Density is mass per unit volume.

$$\rho = \dfrac{m_{cell}}{V_{cell}}$$

**Step 1:** A unit cell contains $n$ atoms, each carrying its molar mass fraction. For a compound $A_xB_y$, the number of formula units per cell is $Z$ (typically 1, 2, or 4). The total number of atoms is:

$$n = Z \cdot (x + y)$$

**Step 2:** Mass of one formula unit (in grams) is the average molar mass divided by Avogadro's number:

$$m_{f.u.} = \dfrac{M_{avg}}{N_A} = \dfrac{x M_A + y M_B}{(x+y) N_A}$$

**Step 3:** Mass of the unit cell is the mass of one formula unit times $Z$ formula units:

$$m_{cell} = Z \cdot m_{f.u.} = \dfrac{Z (x M_A + y M_B)}{(x+y) N_A}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting into the density equation and using $n = Z(x+y)$:

$$\rho = \dfrac{Z (x M_A + y M_B)}{(x+y) N_A \cdot V_{cell}} = \dfrac{n \cdot M_{avg}}{N_A \cdot V_{cell}}$$

This is the master equation used universally.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Worked Numerical Example — NiAl (B2 structure)

**Given data:**
- Structure: B2 (CsCl-type), 2 atoms per unit cell ($Z = 1$ formula unit).
- Lattice parameter: $a = 2.887$ Å $= 2.887 \times 10^{-8}$ cm.
- $M_{Ni} = 58.69$ g/mol, $M_{Al} = 26.98$ g/mol.
- $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹.

**Step 1 — Compute $M_{avg}$ for NiAl ($x = y = 1$):**

$$M_{avg} = \dfrac{1 \times 58.69 + 1 \times 26.98}{1 + 1} = \dfrac{85.67}{2} = 42.835 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Compute unit cell volume:**

$$V_{cell} = a^3 = (2.887 \times 10^{-8} \text{ cm})^3$$

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{cell} &= (2.887)^3 \times 10^{-24} \text{ cm}^3 \\
&= 24.067 \times 10^{-24} \text{ cm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator:**

$$n \cdot M_{avg} = 2 \times 42.835 = 85.67 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 4 — Compute denominator:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_A \cdot V_{cell} &= 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 24.067 \times 10^{-24} \\
&= 6.022 \times 24.067 \times 10^{-1} \\
&= 144.95 \times 10^{-1} \\
&= 14.495 \text{ cm}^3\text{/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Final density:**

$$\rho = \dfrac{85.67}{14.495} = 5.91 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Experimental value:** $\rho_{exp} = 5.85$ g/cm³. The **<2% deviation** confirms the B2 ordered structure is correct. This kind of cross-check is a **classic 7-mark KTU question**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Atomic Packing Factor of B2 (CsCl-type) Structure

**Step 1 — Geometry:**
The B2 structure has Cs at $(0,0,0)$ and Cl at $(\tfrac{1}{2},\tfrac{1}{2},\tfrac{1}{2})$ — body-centre corner relationship. The atoms touch along the **body diagonal**:

$$4r_A + 4r_B = a\sqrt{3}$$

**Step 2 — For an ideal CsCl-type with $r_A = r_B = r$:**

$$4r = \dfrac{a\sqrt{3}}{2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; a = \dfrac{4r\sqrt{3}}{2} = \dfrac{4r}{\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 3 — Volume of atoms in cell (2 atoms):**

$$V_{atoms} = 2 \times \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3 = \dfrac{8}{3}\pi r^3$$

**Step 4 — Volume of cell:**

$$V_{cell} = a^3 = \left(\dfrac{4r}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^3 = \dfrac{64 r^3}{3\sqrt{3}}$$

**Step 5 — APF:**

$$\begin{aligned}
APF &= \dfrac{V_{atoms}}{V_{cell}} = \dfrac{\frac{8}{3}\pi r^3}{\frac{64 r^3}{3\sqrt{3}}} \\
&= \dfrac{8\pi}{3} \times \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}}{64} \\
&= \dfrac{8\pi \sqrt{3}}{64} \\
&= \dfrac{\pi \sqrt{3}}{8} \\
APF &\approx 0.6802
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The B2 APF (0.68) is **less than FCC (0.74)** — consistent with B2's lower ductility and reduced slip systems.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Major Intermetallics

| Property | NiAl (B2) | Ni₃Al (L1₂) | TiAl (L1₀) | Ti₃Al (D0₁₉) | FeAl (B2) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Density (g/cm³) | 5.91 | 7.43 | 3.90 | 4.20 | 5.60 |
| $T_m$ (°C) | 1638 | 1395 | 1460 | 1600 | 1250 |
| Young's Mod. (GPa) | 188 | 200 | 170 | 145 | 260 |
| Slip systems | 3 | 12 (at high T) | 4 | 5 | 3 |
| RT ductility | Poor | Moderate | Poor | Moderate | Poor |
| Oxidation resist. | Excellent | Good | Good | Poor | Moderate |
| Cost | Moderate | High | High | High | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Classification Tree of Intermetallics

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Intermetallic Compounds] --> B[Geometrically Close-Packed GCP]
    A --> C[Topologically Close-Packed TCP]
    A --> D[Ordered Structures]
    A --> E[Electron Compounds Hume-Rothery]

    B --> B1[Laves Phases AB2]
    B1 --> B1a[MgCu2 C15 cubic]
    B1 --> B1b[MgZn2 C14 hex]
    B1 --> B1c[MgNi2 C36 hex]

    C --> C1[Sigma phase]
    C --> C2[Mu phase]
    C --> C3[Chi phase]

    D --> D1[B2 CsCl-type]
    D --> D2[L12 Cu3Au-type]
    D --> D3[D019 Ti3Al-type]
    D --> D4[L10 TiAl-type]
    D --> D5[D03 Fe3Al-type]
    D --> D6[L21 Heusler]

    E --> E1[BCC phases]
    E --> E2[FCC phases]
    E --> E3[HCP phases]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — B2 and L1₂ Unit Cell Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph unitA[B2 CsCl-type NiAl]
        A1[Corner site: Ni atom]
        A2[Body centre: Al atom]
        A3[Coordination: 8 nearest neighbours]
        A4[Slip systems: 3 limited]
    end

    subgraph unitB[L12 Cu3Au-type Ni3Al]
        B1[Corner sites: Ni atoms x 8 corners each shared]
        B2[Face centres: Al atoms x 6 faces]
        B3[Coordination: 12 for face Al]
        B4[Slip systems: 12 superpartial pairs]
    end

    A1 --- A2
    A2 --- A3
    A3 --- A4

    B1 --- B2
    B2 --- B3
    B3 --- B4
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Property–Structure–Application Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Lightweight Al and Ti atoms] --> S1[Low density 3.5 to 5.9 g per cm3]
    P2[Strong d-orbital bonding] --> S2[High melting point 1200 to 1650 C]
    P3[Ordered sublattice] --> S3[Dislocation pinning]
    S3 --> S4[Anomalous high-T strength]
    P4[Covalent fraction of bond] --> S5[Limited slip systems]
    S5 --> S6[Brittleness at RT]

    S1 --> App[Aerospace turbine blades]
    S2 --> App2[Jet engine hot sections]
    S4 --> App3[High-T structural composites]
    S6 --> Design[Design constraint: need ductilising additions]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Order–Disorder Transition Concept

```mermaid
graph TB
    start[Below Tc ordered state] --> cryst[Sharp superlattice peaks in XRD]
    start --> prop1[High yield strength]
    start --> prop2[Anomalous flow behaviour]

    above[Above Tc disordered state] --> cryst2[Diffuse or absent superlattice peaks]
    above --> prop3[Lower yield strength]
    above --> prop4[Conventional metallic behaviour]

    start -.temperature rises.-> above
    above -.temperature falls.-> start
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for Mermaid:** In Diagram 2, each subgraph represents the conceptual layout of a unit cell. Solid lines indicate logical linkage between the structural site and the resulting property. **Mermaid cannot render 3D spheres** — use this as a *schematic* until you sketch real cells in your notebook for the KTU lab exam.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between an intermetallic compound and a solid solution alloy. Give two examples for each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Solid Solution Alloy | Intermetallic Compound |
|---|---|---|
| Atomic arrangement | **Disordered** (random site occupancy) | **Ordered** (specific sublattices) |
| Stoichiometry | Variable over a range | Fixed (or near-fixed) |
| Bonding | Purely metallic | Mixed metallic + covalent + ionic |
| Examples | $\alpha$-brass (Cu–Zn), austenite (Fe–C) | NiAl, TiAl, Ni₃Al, CuAu |

The fundamental difference is **long-range crystallographic order**. In a solid solution, the two atom species share lattice sites randomly; in an intermetallic, the two species occupy *specific, distinct* sublattices — producing a superlattice visible in X-ray diffraction.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is meant by the order–disorder transition temperature $T_c$ in intermetallics? Why is it technologically important?*

**Model Answer:**
The order–disorder transition temperature $T_c$ is the critical temperature above which the **long-range crystallographic order** of an intermetallic phase collapses into a **disordered solid solution**. Below $T_c$, the compound is thermodynamically ordered (lower free energy due to favourable A–B contacts); above $T_c$, entropy dominates and the atoms randomise.

**Technological importance:**
- For Ni₃Al-based superalloys, operation must be **below $T_c \approx 1170$ °C** to retain the ordered $\gamma'$ phase responsible for high-temperature strength.
- $T_c$ sets the **upper service temperature limit** of the material — a critical design parameter for jet engine and gas turbine applications.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** In KTU ESE, students choose **ONE of two** questions from a module. We provide both options with complete internal solutions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** *Classify intermetallic compounds based on structure. With neat sketches, explain the B2 and L1₂ structures, citing one example intermetallic for each.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *NiAl crystallises in the B2 structure with lattice parameter $a = 2.887$ Å. Compute its theoretical density and compare it with the experimental value of 5.85 g/cm³. Comment on the deviation.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Intermetallics are classified into three broad families:
1. **Geometrically close-packed (GCP)** — e.g., Laves phases.
2. **Topologically close-packed (TCP)** — e.g., σ, μ phases.
3. **Common ordered structures** — B2, L1₂, D0₃, D0₁₉, L1₀, L2₁.

**B2 (CsCl-type) structure (3 marks):**
- Two interpenetrating **simple cubic** sublattices.
- One atom type at $(0,0,0)$ corner; the other at $(\tfrac{1}{2}, \tfrac{1}{2}, \tfrac{1}{2})$ body-centre.
- **Coordination number = 8** for each atom.
- **Atoms per unit cell = 2** (1 + 1 shared).
- **Example:** NiAl (a = 2.887 Å).

**L1₂ (Cu₃Au-type) structure (3 marks):**
- Based on the **FCC** arrangement.
- Au atoms at the **8 corners** of the cube; Cu atoms at the **6 face centres**.
- **Atoms per unit cell** = $8 \times \tfrac{1}{8} + 6 \times \tfrac{1}{2} = 1 + 3 = 4$ (one Au + three Cu per cell).
- **Coordination number = 12** for each atom.
- **Example:** Ni₃Al (a = 3.567 Å).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Writing the L1₂ corner and face-centre occupancy in the *wrong* way (e.g., Au on faces, Cu on corners). Examiners **deduct 2 marks** for this reversal. Always state: "majority atom → corners, minority → faces."

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**Given:** $a = 2.887$ Å $= 2.887 \times 10^{-8}$ cm, B2 structure → $n = 2$ atoms/cell, NiAl stoichiometry (1:1).

**Step 1 — Average molar mass:**

$$M_{avg} = \dfrac{58.69 + 26.98}{2} = 42.835 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Cell volume:**

$$V = a^3 = (2.887)^3 \times 10^{-24} = 24.067 \times 10^{-24} \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Apply density formula:**

$$\rho = \dfrac{n M_{avg}}{N_A V} = \dfrac{2 \times 42.835}{6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 24.067 \times 10^{-24}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho &= \dfrac{85.67}{6.022 \times 24.067 \times 10^{-1}} \\
&= \dfrac{85.67}{14.495} \\
&= 5.91 \text{ g/cm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison and comment:**

$$\% \text{ deviation} = \dfrac{5.91 - 5.85}{5.85} \times 100 = 1.03\%$$

The small positive deviation (<2%) confirms the **B2 ordered structure** is the correct assignment. The slight excess over experimental value is attributable to **vacancies and grain-boundary porosity** in the real specimen.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Failing to **convert Å to cm** ($1$ Å $= 10^{-8}$ cm) is the most common error — costs **1 full mark**. Always show the unit conversion explicitly.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** *Explain the Hume-Rothery rules governing the formation of intermetallic phases. Discuss the formation of Laves phases with the help of the ideal atomic size ratio.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Describe the Ni–Ti shape memory intermetallic (Nitinol). Explain the role of the martensitic transformation in its pseudoelastic behaviour and list two engineering applications.* **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**Hume-Rothery rules (4 marks):**
For substitutional solid solutions (and by extension, ordered intermetallics), the rules state:

1. **Atomic size factor:** $\Delta r = \dfrac{r_A - r_B}{r_A} \le 15\%$. If exceeded, solubility is limited and intermetallics form.
2. **Crystal structure:** The two elements should have **same crystal structure** for extensive solubility.
3. **Valency (electron concentration):** A higher-valency metal dissolves more of a lower-valency one. The **electron-to-atom ratio (e/a)** determines the stability of intermediate phases (β, γ, ε).
4. **Electronegativity:** Similar electronegativities favour solubility; large differences favour **intermetallic compound** formation.

**Laves phases (3 marks):**
- Composition $AB_2$ with the **ideal atomic size ratio** $r_A / r_B = 1.225$.
- This ratio arises from pure **geometry** — to achieve maximum space-filling when the smaller B atoms (4 per formula unit) tetrahedrally coordinate the larger A atom (2 per formula unit).
- The factor $1.225 = \sqrt{3/2} \approx 1.2247$ is the **cube root of the ratio of volumes** occupied by the two atom types in the densest packing.

**Examples:** $MgCu_2$ (C15), $MgZn_2$ (C14), $MgNi_2$ (C36).

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**Nitinol composition and structure (2 marks):**
- Near-equiatomic Ni–Ti alloy (typically 49–51 at% Ni).
- **Austenite (parent) phase:** B2 ordered CsCl-type structure, stable at high temperature.
- **Martensite phase:** Monoclinic B19′ structure, stable at low temperature / under stress.

**Martensitic transformation and pseudoelasticity (3 marks):**
The transformation is **diffusionless** and **thermoelastic** — atoms move cooperatively by a shear (Bain) distortion rather than by diffusion. The critical temperatures are $M_s$ (martensite start), $M_f$ (martensite finish), $A_s$ (austenite start), $A_f$ (austenite finish).

**Pseudoelasticity mechanism:**
1. Above $A_f$ (typically at body temperature), apply stress → stress-induced martensite forms.
2. On unloading, the martensite reverts to austenite, and the strain **recovers completely** — a macroscopic "rubber-like" behaviour with recoverable strains up to **~8%**.

**Applications (2 marks):**
1. **Biomedical stents and orthodontic archwires** — the pseudoelasticity allows self-expansion and constant gentle force.
2. **Aircraft couplings and actuators** — using the **one-way shape memory effect** (cooled → deformed → heated → recovers shape).

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse **pseudoelasticity** (stress-induced, isothermal, no temperature change) with **shape memory effect** (thermal, requires heating to trigger recovery). Examiners expect clear distinction. **Loses 2 marks** if blurred.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 3 Intermetallics**

- ✅ **Definition:** Ordered, stoichiometric compound of two or more metals on distinct sublattices.
- ✅ **Key structures:** B2 (NiAl, CsCl-type, 2 atoms/cell, APF = 0.68), L1₂ (Ni₃Al, Cu₃Au-type, 4 atoms/cell, APF = 0.74), L1₀ (TiAl, tetragonal), D0₁₉ (Ti₃Al), D0₃ (Fe₃Al), L2₁ (Heusler).
- ✅ **Master density formula:** $\rho = n M_{avg} / (N_A V_{cell})$ — used in **every numerical**.
- ✅ **Laves phase ratio:** $r_A / r_B = 1.225$ exactly.
- ✅ **Order–disorder:** $T_c \approx (0.5 - 0.8) T_m$.
- ✅ **Hume-Rothery rules:** 4 rules — size (≤15%), structure, valency (e/a ratio), electronegativity.
- ✅ **Hall-Petch:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k d^{-1/2}$.
- ✅ **B2 example:** NiAl — $a = 2.887$ Å, $\rho_{theory} = 5.91$ g/cm³.
- ✅ **L1₂ example:** Ni₃Al — $a = 3.567$ Å, $\rho_{theory} = 7.43$ g/cm³.
- ✅ **Anomalous yield behaviour** of Ni₃Al: strength *increases* with T up to ~700 °C.
- ✅ **Shape memory:** Nitinol (Ni–Ti), pseudoelasticity vs. shape memory effect — distinguish clearly.
- ✅ **Applications:** Turbine blades (Ni₃Al, NiAl), turbochargers (TiAl), stents (NiTi).
- ✅ **Failure mode:** RT brittleness — due to limited slip systems and grain-boundary weakness.
- ✅ **Creep law:** $\dot{\varepsilon} = A \sigma^n \exp(-Q/RT)$ — Norton-Bailey form.

> **Final KTU Tip:** When asked "explain the structure," always include **(i) the sublattice description, (ii) coordination number, (iii) atoms per cell, and (iv) one real example.** This 4-point pattern guarantees full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Alloys and Their Potential Role as Matrices in Composites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET413 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Alloys and Their Potential Role as Matrices in Composites

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In fiber-reinforced and particulate-reinforced composites, the **matrix** is the continuous phase that surrounds, supports, and binds together the reinforcement (fibers, whiskers, or particles). When this matrix phase is constituted by a metallic material consisting of two or more elements—where at least one is a metal—it is referred to as an **alloy matrix**. The alloy matrix performs three cardinal functions:

1. **Load Transfer Medium** — Transfers applied external load to the high-strength reinforcement via the matrix-reinforcement interface through shear stress.
2. **Geometric Stabilizer** — Maintains the spatial orientation, distribution, and position of the reinforcement phase.
3. **Environmental Shield** — Protects the reinforcement from surface damage, oxidation, moisture, and chemical attack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** A *composite matrix alloy* is a specifically engineered metallic alloy whose composition, microstructure, and heat-treatment condition are tailored to optimize wettability with the reinforcement, coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) compatibility, interfacial bonding strength, and elevated-temperature service capability.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Reinforced Concrete Bridge" Model

Consider a **Reinforced Cement Concrete (RCC) bridge**:

- The **cement concrete** (which is relatively weak in tension) is the *matrix*.
- The **steel rebar** (which is strong in tension) is the *reinforcement*.
- The concrete holds the rebar in place, transfers compressive load, and shields the steel from rust.

Similarly, in a metal matrix composite (MMC):
- The **alloy matrix** (e.g., aluminum alloy 6061) provides ductility, toughness, and formability.
- The **ceramic reinforcement** (e.g., SiC particles, Al₂O₃ fibers) provides high strength and stiffness.
- The matrix transfers the load to the reinforcement and protects it from environmental degradation.

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a civil engineer would not use pure iron in a bridge, a materials engineer rarely uses a *pure metal* as a composite matrix. Pure metals are typically too soft, have low strength, and possess uncontrolled thermal expansion. **Alloying** allows precise tuning of properties to match the reinforcement and service environment.

### 1.3 Why Alloys Instead of Pure Metals? — Key Metrics

| Pure Metal Drawback | Alloy Solution |
|---|---|
| Low yield strength (~20–50 MPa for pure Al) | Solid-solution strengthening raises yield to 200–500 MPa |
| Uncontrolled thermal expansion | Alloying adjusts CTE to match reinforcement (e.g., Al-Si alloys) |
| Poor high-temperature strength | Precipitation hardening (e.g., Al-Cu, Ni-Al systems) |
| Limited wettability with ceramics | Mg, Li, or Ti additions improve wetting of Al₂O₃/SiC |
| Poor fatigue resistance | Microalloying refines grain size and dislocation pinning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:** The **specific modulus (E/ρ)** and **specific strength (σ/ρ)** of alloy matrices are critical for aerospace applications. Typical values: Aluminum alloys ~ **26 GPa/(g·cm⁻³)**, Titanium alloys ~ **26 GPa/(g·cm⁻³)**, Magnesium alloys ~ **26 GPa/(g·cm⁻³)**. Reinforcement can boost this to **100–200 GPa/(g·cm⁻³)** when combined properly.

### 1.4 Classification of Composites by Matrix Type

```
Composites
├── Polymer Matrix Composites (PMC)      ← Thermoset/Thermoplastic
├── Metal Matrix Composites (MMC)        ← ALLOYS ← (FOCUS OF THIS TOPIC)
├── Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMC)      ← Oxides, Carbides, Nitrides
└── Carbon-Carbon Composites (C/C)       ← Carbon matrix
```

This module is **exclusively concerned with the MMC branch**, where the matrix is an engineered alloy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Essential Property Requirements for an Alloy Matrix

A successful composite matrix alloy must satisfy a stringent set of physical, mechanical, chemical, and processing criteria. These are summarized below in a structured logic format:

#### 2.1.1 Mechanical Property Requirements
- **Adequate Ductility and Toughness** — Prevents catastrophic brittle failure by allowing plastic deformation at crack tips.
- **High Shear Strength at the Interface** — Ensures efficient stress transfer from the matrix to the reinforcement.
- **Compatible Yield Strength** — Matrix yield strength should be **lower than reinforcement strength** but **high enough** to prevent matrix rupture before load transfer.

#### 2.1.2 Physical Property Requirements
- **Low Density** — Critical for aerospace and automotive (specific strength optimization).
- **Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE) Compatibility** — The CTE of the matrix should closely match that of the reinforcement to minimize **thermal residual stresses** at the interface during cooling from processing temperatures.
- **Thermal Conductivity** — High thermal conductivity (e.g., Al ≈ **237 W/m·K**, Cu ≈ **401 W/m·K**) aids in heat dissipation, vital for electronic packaging composites.

#### 2.1.3 Chemical Property Requirements
- **Good Wettability with Reinforcement** — The liquid alloy must wet the reinforcement surface to form a strong bond. Measured by the **contact angle θ**; wetting is favorable when **θ < 90°**.
- **Chemical Stability** — Must not react excessively with the reinforcement (e.g., Al reacting with SiC to form Al₄C₃, which is brittle and degrades the interface).
- **Corrosion Resistance** — Particularly in marine, chemical, and biomedical environments.

#### 2.1.4 Processing Property Requirements
- **Low Melting Point** — Reduces energy consumption and thermal damage to the reinforcement.
- **Fluidity in the Molten State** — Essential for casting, stir-casting, and infiltration techniques.
- **Workability** — Capability to be rolled, extruded, forged, or drawn when reinforced.

### 2.2 Classification of Major Alloy Matrices Used in MMCs

#### A. Aluminum Alloys (The Workhorse of MMCs)
- **Most widely used** matrix family in MMCs due to low density, good thermal conductivity, and excellent processability.
- Common grades: **AA 6061, AA 2024, AA 7075, AA 356, A380**.
- Reinforcement compatibility: **SiC, Al₂O₃, B₄C, TiC, graphite, carbon fibers**.
- Applications: Automotive brake rotors, pistons, cylinder liners, aerospace structural panels.

#### B. Titanium Alloys
- High specific strength, excellent corrosion resistance, biocompatibility.
- Common grades: **Ti-6Al-4V (Grade 5), Ti-6Al-2Sn-4Zr-2Mo (Ti-6242)**.
- Reinforcement: **SiC fibers (SCS-6), TiB, TiC**.
- Applications: Jet engine components, rocket motor casings, biomedical implants.
- Challenge: High processing temperature leads to **severe interfacial reactions**.

#### C. Magnesium Alloys
- **Lowest density structural metal** (~**1.74 g/cm³**).
- Common grades: **AZ91, AM60, WE43**.
- Reinforcement: **SiC, Al₂O₃, carbon nanotubes (CNTs)**.
- Applications: Lightweight automotive and aerospace parts, electronics housings.
- Challenge: Poor corrosion resistance, flammability.

#### D. Copper Alloys
- Exceptional thermal and electrical conductivity.
- Reinforcement: **Tungsten, SiC, diamond particles, carbon fibers**.
- Applications: Electronic heat sinks, electrical contacts, thermal management.

#### E. Nickel Alloys and Superalloys
- Excellent high-temperature strength and creep resistance.
- Common grades: **Inconel 718, Waspaloy, Nimonic 90**.
- Reinforcement: **Al₂O₃, SiC, Y₂O₃-dispersed oxides (ODS)**.
- Applications: Gas turbine blades, rocket nozzles, high-temp structural parts.

#### F. Iron and Steel Alloys
- Used in wear-resistant and cutting tool composites.
- Reinforcement: **TiC, WC, VC**.
- Applications: Cutting tools, dies, wear-resistant surfaces.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** All formulas below are core to KTU Module 3 numerical and conceptual questions. Memorize the symbols and their SI units.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m$ | $\rho$: density, $V_f$/$V_m$: volume fractions of fiber/matrix | Predicts composite density for lightweight design |
| 2 | $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ (ROM, Voigt model) | $E$: Young's modulus | Upper bound of stiffness; parallel loading |
| 3 | $\dfrac{1}{E_c} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ (Reuss/Inverse ROM) | Inverse of modulus | Lower bound of stiffness; series loading |
| 4 | $V_f + V_m = 1$ | Volume fraction conservation | Used to eliminate $V_m$ in ROM equations |
| 5 | $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ | Strength rule of mixtures | Predicts composite tensile strength (isostrain) |
| 6 | $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}$ | $l_c$: critical fiber length, $d$: fiber diameter, $\tau_i$: interfacial shear strength | Determines minimum fiber length for effective load transfer |
| 7 | $S = \dfrac{\sigma_{uts}}{\rho}$ (specific strength) | Strength-to-weight ratio | Material selection index for aerospace/automotive |
| 8 | $S_m = \dfrac{E}{\rho}$ (specific modulus) | Modulus-to-weight ratio | Material selection for stiffness-critical parts |
| 9 | $\Delta\alpha = \vert \alpha_m - \alpha_f \vert$ | CTE mismatch magnitude | Drives thermal residual stress |
| 10 | $\sigma_{thermal} = E_f \cdot \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal residual stress estimate | Predicts interfacial cracking on cooling |
| 11 | $V_{f,crit} = \dfrac{\sigma_m - \sigma'_m}{\sigma_f - \sigma'_m}$ | Critical fiber volume for strengthening | Minimum $V_f$ to make composite stronger than matrix |
| 12 | $\gamma_{LV} = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{SL} - 2\sqrt{\gamma_{SV}^{d}\gamma_{SL}^{d}}$ (Fowkes extension) | Interfacial surface energies | Predicts wettability from surface energy data |

> **Note on Notation:** In the formulas above, $\vert$ is used to denote absolute magnitude. In markdown tables, the value `$\vert \alpha_m - \alpha_f \vert$` is rendered with `\vert` to avoid breaking table syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Typical Alloy Matrix | Reinforcement | Composite Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Aerospace (Airbus A380, Boeing 787) | AA 2009, AA 6061 | SiC particles | Fan blades, wing skins, fuselage frames |
| Automotive (Toyota, Ford) | AA A356, AA 6061 | SiC, Al₂O₃ (15–20 vol%) | Brake discs, pistons, driveshafts |
| Electronics | Cu, Al | Diamond, SiC | Heat spreaders for CPU/GPU, LED substrates |
| Defense / Rocketry | Ti-6Al-4V, Inconel | SCS-6 SiC fibers | Missile fins, rocket motor casings |
| Biomedical | Ti-6Al-4V | HA, bioactive glass | Bone implants, dental prosthetics |
| Marine | AA 5083, AA 6061 | Al₂O₃, B₄C | Ship hulls, offshore components |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Critical Fiber Length ($l_c$) — The Foundation of Matrix Design

The critical fiber length is the **minimum fiber length required** for the matrix to develop the full tensile strength of the fiber through interfacial shear loading. This parameter directly dictates whether a matrix alloy is *suitable* for a given reinforcement.

#### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Free Body of a Fiber Embedded in the Matrix**

Consider a single fiber of diameter $d$ and length $l$ embedded in a matrix. Apply a tensile force to the composite in the fiber direction. The fiber experiences a tensile stress $\sigma_f$ that builds up from zero at the fiber ends to a maximum at the center, balanced by shear stress $\tau_i$ acting at the fiber-matrix interface.

**Step 2 — Force Equilibrium on the Fiber**

The tensile force on the fiber cross-section must equal the shear force transferred from the matrix along the lateral surface:

$$
F_{tensile} = F_{shear}
$$

$$
\sigma_f \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \tau_i \cdot (\pi d) \cdot \dfrac{l}{2}
$$

**Explanation:** The left side is the maximum tensile force on the fiber cross-section. The right side is the shear force on the cylindrical fiber surface (circumference $\pi d$ times the half-length $l/2$, since shear stress builds up symmetrically from both ends).

**Step 3 — Algebraic Simplification**

Cancel the common factor $\pi d$ from both sides:

$$
\sigma_f \cdot \dfrac{d}{4} = \tau_i \cdot \dfrac{l}{2}
$$

Solve for $l$:

$$
l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}
$$

**Step 4 — Physical Interpretation**

- If $l < l_c$: Fiber pulls out before reaching its full strength (inefficient reinforcement).
- If $l \geq 15 \, l_c$: Fiber behaves as a *continuous fiber* and achieves near-full efficiency.
- **KTU Insight:** A matrix alloy with higher interfacial shear strength $\tau_i$ **reduces** $l_c$, making it compatible with shorter fibers or whiskers.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Critical Fiber Volume Fraction ($V_{f,crit}$)

A composite is only useful if it is stronger than the unreinforced matrix. The minimum fiber volume fraction for this condition to be satisfied is derived below.

**Step 1 — Starting from the Strength Rule of Mixtures**

$$
\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $V_m = 1 - V_f$**

$$
\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m (1 - V_f)
$$

**Step 3 — Condition for Composite to be Stronger than Matrix**

The matrix alone has strength $\sigma_m$. The matrix in a composite, however, cannot carry the full $\sigma_m$ because the surrounding fibers constrain it; a modified matrix strength $\sigma'_m$ is used:

$$
\sigma_c \geq \sigma'_m
$$

$$
\sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m (1 - V_f) \geq \sigma'_m
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_f$**

Rearrange:

$$
\sigma_f V_f - \sigma_m V_f \geq \sigma'_m - \sigma_m
$$

$$
V_f (\sigma_f - \sigma_m) \geq \sigma'_m - \sigma_m
$$

$$
V_{f,crit} = \dfrac{\sigma'_m - \sigma_m}{\sigma_f - \sigma_m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** For most practical aluminum matrix composites reinforced with SiC, $V_{f,crit}$ typically falls in the range **0.03 to 0.10** (i.e., only 3–10% reinforcement is needed to outperform the unreinforced matrix).

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Specific Strength Comparison — Why Alloy Matrices Dominate Aerospace

Compare an Al-alloy matrix MMC with a monolithic Ti-alloy for an aerospace structural bracket.

**Given Data (illustrative):**

| Property | AA 6061 (matrix) | AA 6061 + 20 vol% SiC | Ti-6Al-4V (monolithic) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density $\rho$ (g/cm³) | 2.70 | 2.84 | 4.43 |
| Tensile strength $\sigma$ (MPa) | 310 | 480 | 950 |

**Step 1 — Compute Composite Density Using ROM**

$$
\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m = (0.20)(3.21) + (0.80)(2.70)
$$

$$
\rho_c = 0.642 + 2.160 = 2.802 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

(where $\rho_{SiC} = 3.21$ g/cm³ is the SiC particle density)

**Step 2 — Compute Specific Strength of Each Option**

For AA 6061 + 20% SiC:

$$
S_{MMC} = \dfrac{480}{2.802} = 171.3 \text{ kN·m/kg}
$$

For monolithic Ti-6Al-4V:

$$
S_{Ti} = \dfrac{950}{4.43} = 214.4 \text{ kN·m/kg}
$$

For monolithic AA 6061:

$$
S_{Al} = \dfrac{310}{2.70} = 114.8 \text{ kN·m/kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Analytical Conclusion**

The MMC has **49% higher specific strength** than monolithic aluminum, but titanium still leads in raw specific strength. The MMC, however, is **~35% lower in cost** and easier to manufacture by stir casting or squeeze casting, justifying its selection for commercial aerospace brackets where extreme temperature is not a constraint.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Alloy Matrices Side-by-Side

| Property | Al-Alloys | Mg-Alloys | Ti-Alloys | Cu-Alloys | Ni-Superalloys |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Density (g/cm³) | 2.7–2.85 | 1.74–1.85 | 4.4–4.5 | 8.9–8.96 | 7.8–8.5 |
| Young's Modulus (GPa) | 70–80 | 40–45 | 110–120 | 100–130 | 180–220 |
| Tensile Strength (MPa) | 200–570 | 180–280 | 800–1100 | 200–400 | 700–1600 |
| Melting Point (°C) | 500–660 | 470–640 | 1600–1660 | 950–1085 | 1200–1400 |
| CTE (×10⁻⁶ /°C) | 21–24 | 25–27 | 8–10 | 16–18 | 11–14 |
| Thermal Conductivity (W/m·K) | 150–237 | 70–160 | 6–22 | 320–401 | 10–25 |
| Cost Index (relative) | Low | Low | Very High | Medium | High |
| Common Reinforcement | SiC, Al₂O₃ | SiC, CNT | SCS-6 SiC | Diamond, W | Al₂O₃, Y₂O₃ |
| Typical MMC Application | Brake, piston | Auto brackets | Jet engines | Heat sinks | Turbine blades |
| Interfacial Reaction Risk | Moderate (Al₄C₃) | High | Very High | Low | Moderate |
| Wettability (with ceramics) | Fair to Good | Fair | Poor to Fair | Good | Good |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Heuristic:** If a question gives you a low density and an aerospace context → **Al-alloy matrix**; if it gives you a high-temperature application (>500°C) → **Ni-superalloy or Ti-alloy**; if the application is electronic heat dissipation → **Cu-alloy with diamond**.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**"List any four essential property requirements that an alloy must satisfy to be used as a matrix in a composite material."**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Adequate ductility and toughness** — to allow plastic deformation and prevent brittle catastrophic failure.
2. **Good wettability with the reinforcement** — to ensure a strong interfacial bond (contact angle θ < 90°).
3. **Compatible coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE)** — to minimize thermal residual stresses at the matrix-reinforcement interface.
4. **Low density combined with high strength and stiffness** — to provide a high specific strength and specific modulus, especially for aerospace applications.

*(Any four of: ductility, wettability, CTE compatibility, low density, chemical stability, high shear strength at interface, low melting point for processing — full credit.)*

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**"Why is pure aluminum rarely used as a matrix in MMCs, and how does alloying address its limitations?"**

**Model Answer:**

Pure aluminum has a **low yield strength (~20–30 MPa)**, **uncontrolled thermal expansion (~23 × 10⁻⁶ /°C)**, and **insufficient wettability** with ceramic reinforcements. Alloying addresses these limitations by:

- **Solid-solution strengthening** (e.g., Al-Mg, Al-Si) raises yield strength to 200–350 MPa.
- **Precipitation hardening** (e.g., Al-Cu in AA 2024, Al-Zn-Mg in AA 7075) further increases strength to 500+ MPa.
- **CTE control** via Si or Ni additions brings expansion closer to that of SiC (~4 × 10⁻⁶ /°C).
- **Wettability improvement** via Mg or Li additions reduces the contact angle with Al₂O₃ and SiC.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the **role of alloy matrices in metal matrix composites** with emphasis on the functions of the matrix. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the suitability of **aluminum, titanium, and magnesium alloys** as matrix materials for aerospace MMCs, citing at least three comparison points. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

The alloy matrix in a Metal Matrix Composite (MMC) performs **four primary functions**:

1. **Load Transfer to the Reinforcement** *(2 marks)*
   - The matrix transmits the externally applied tensile or compressive load to the high-strength reinforcement through **interfacial shear stresses** governed by the equation:
   $$
   \tau_i = \dfrac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 l}
   $$
   - For efficient transfer, the matrix must develop sufficient interfacial shear strength at the matrix-reinforcement boundary.

2. **Protection of the Reinforcement** *(2 marks)*
   - The alloy matrix shields the brittle ceramic reinforcement (e.g., SiC, Al₂O₃) from surface flaws, moisture, oxidation, and chemical attack.
   - The ductile metallic matrix also acts as a **crack arrester**, blunting propagating cracks and improving fracture toughness.

3. **Maintenance of Reinforcement Geometry and Distribution** *(1.5 marks)*
   - The matrix holds the fibers, whiskers, or particles in the desired orientation and spacing, preventing misalignment under load.

4. **Provision of Secondary Mechanical and Physical Properties** *(1.5 marks)*
   - The matrix contributes to properties such as **transverse strength**, **shear strength**, **thermal conductivity**, and **electrical conductivity** of the composite.

---

##### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

| Comparison Point | Aluminum Alloys | Titanium Alloys | Magnesium Alloys |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Density** | 2.7 g/cm³ (moderate) | 4.5 g/cm³ (high) | 1.74 g/cm³ (lowest) |
| **Specific Strength** | High (171 kN·m/kg for Al/SiC MMC) | Very high, but offset by density | Highest absolute specific strength |
| **Temperature Capability** | Up to ~350°C (AA 6061-T6) | Up to ~600°C (Ti-6Al-4V) | Up to ~200°C (AZ91) |
| **Processing Ease** | Excellent (casting, forging) | Difficult (requires vacuum, hot pressing) | Moderate (reactive melt) |
| **Wettability with SiC** | Good (improves with Mg addition) | Poor (needs coating) | Fair |
| **Cost** | Low | Very high | Low |
| **Best Use Case** | Automotive, aero-structural panels | Jet engine, rocket motors | Lightweight auto brackets |

**Conclusion:** *(Wrap-up for full marks)*

- For **commercial aerospace** (e.g., fuselage brackets): **Al-alloy matrix** is the optimum choice (cost vs. performance).
- For **high-temperature aerospace** (e.g., compressor blades, afterburners): **Ti-alloy matrix** is indispensable.
- For **ultra-lightweight non-structural** parts (e.g., gearbox housings): **Mg-alloy matrix** offers the best weight savings.

---

#### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **critical fiber length** $l_c$ required for effective load transfer in a fiber-reinforced alloy matrix composite. A SiC fiber of diameter **100 μm** and tensile strength **3.5 GPa** is embedded in an aluminum alloy matrix with interfacial shear strength of **80 MPa**. Compute $l_c$ and comment on whether continuous or short fibers are appropriate. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a **selection flowchart**, discuss how an engineer would choose an appropriate alloy matrix for a given MMC application. State the **specific alloy designation** and **reinforcement** you would recommend for:
  (i) An automotive brake disc.
  (ii) A satellite heat sink.
  (iii) A high-pressure turbine blade.
**(7 Marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Derivation** *(4 marks)*:

The critical fiber length $l_c$ is derived from the force balance on a single fiber embedded in the matrix. The maximum tensile force on the fiber cross-section equals the shear force transferred through the fiber-matrix interface.

$$
\sigma_f \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d \cdot \dfrac{l_c}{2}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\sigma_f \cdot d = 2 \tau_i \cdot l_c
$$

$$
l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}
$$

**Substitution** *(2 marks)*:

Given:
- $\sigma_f = 3.5 \text{ GPa} = 3500 \text{ MPa}$
- $d = 100 \text{ μm} = 100 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 100 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ mm}$
- $\tau_i = 80 \text{ MPa}$

$$
l_c = \dfrac{3500 \times 0.1}{2 \times 80} = \dfrac{350}{160} = 2.1875 \text{ mm}
$$

**Comment** *(1 mark)*:

Since $l_c \approx 2.19$ mm, fibers longer than $\approx 33$ mm (i.e., $15 \times l_c$) are needed for near-full efficiency. For practical applications, **continuous SiC fibers** (e.g., SCS-6 monofilaments) or **chopped fibers of length 5–10 mm** (with reduced efficiency) are used. The aluminum alloy with $\tau_i = 80$ MPa is reasonably compatible with the chosen fiber diameter.

---

##### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Selection Flowchart Logic** *(2 marks)*:

The matrix selection proceeds through a decision tree based on service temperature, density, thermal conductivity, and mechanical loading:

1. **Service temperature** is the first filter (>500°C → Ni-superalloy; otherwise proceed).
2. **Density requirement** filters lightweight applications (→ Mg or Al).
3. **Thermal conductivity** priority routes to Cu.
4. **Mechanical performance + corrosion** routes to Ti.
5. **Cost + formability** defaults to Al.

**Specific Recommendations** *(5 marks, 1.67 each)*:

| Application | Matrix Alloy | Reinforcement | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Automotive brake disc | **AA A356 / AA 6061** (castable Al-Si) | **SiC particles (15–20 vol%)** | Low density, high thermal conductivity (~170 W/m·K), excellent wear resistance, cost-effective stir casting. |
| (ii) Satellite heat sink | **Cu (OFHC, oxygen-free high conductivity)** | **Diamond particles (40–60 vol%)** | Highest thermal conductivity combination (~600 W/m·K), near-zero CTE, ideal for space-grade thermal management. |
| (iii) High-pressure turbine blade | **Inconel 718 (Ni-base superalloy)** | **YSZ (Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia) TBC or Al₂O₃ fibers** | Sustains 1100°C, resists creep, oxidation-resistant, ideal for hot-section aerostructures. |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do not confuse** the rule of mixtures for **stiffness (E)** with that for **strength (σ)**. The stiffness ROM is exact for isostrain; the strength ROM is an upper bound that requires fiber length $\geq l_c$.
> - **Do not** write "$l_c = \sigma_f d / \tau_i$" (missing the factor of 2). The correct expression is $l_c = \sigma_f d / (2 \tau_i)$. **Loses 1 mark if factor 2 is missing.**
> - **Do not** call pure aluminum "aluminum alloy." Always state the **alloy designation** (e.g., AA 6061, AA 2024, Ti-6Al-4V).
> - **Do not** confuse wettability (mechanical bonding) with chemical bonding (interfacial reaction products). Excessive reaction (e.g., Al₄C₃) is **harmful**, not beneficial.
> - **Do not** omit units in numerical answers. KTU deducts **0.5 mark** for each missing unit in ROM calculations.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Print This Section Before Exam**

- **Matrix Definition:** Continuous phase that holds reinforcement, transfers load, and provides environmental protection.
- **Top 3 Alloy Matrices:** Aluminum alloys (60% of MMC market), Titanium alloys (aerospace), Magnesium alloys (lightweight).
- **Why alloys, not pure metals?** — Solid-solution strengthening, precipitation hardening, controlled CTE, better wettability.
- **Rule of Mixtures (ROM):**
  - Density: $\rho_c = V_f \rho_f + V_m \rho_m$
  - Stiffness (isostrain): $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$
  - Stiffness (isostress): $1/E_c = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$
  - Strength: $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$
- **Critical Fiber Length Formula:** $l_c = \sigma_f d / (2 \tau_i)$
- **Critical Fiber Volume Fraction:** $V_{f,crit} = (\sigma'_m - \sigma_m) / (\sigma_f - \sigma_m)$
- **Specific Strength/Modulus:** S = σ/ρ and M = E/ρ — use these for material selection.
- **Selection Heuristic:** Temperature → Density → Conductivity → Corrosion → Cost.
- **CTE mismatch** drives **thermal residual stress** = $E_f \cdot \Delta\alpha \cdot \Delta T$.
- **Wettability criterion:** Contact angle $\theta < 90°$ = good bonding.
- **Common reinforcements for Al-alloys:** SiC, Al₂O₃, B₄C, TiC, graphite, CNTs.
- **Common reinforcements for Ti-alloys:** SCS-6 SiC fibers, TiB whiskers, TiC particles.
- **Common reinforcements for Cu-alloys:** Diamond particles (heat sinks), tungsten (heavy-duty electrical).
- **Processing of MMCs:** Stir casting, squeeze casting, powder metallurgy, spray deposition, in-situ processing.
- **Avoid over-reaction:** Al-SiC at >700°C forms brittle Al₄C₃ → degrades properties. Use protective coatings or temperature control.
- **Density Memory Values:** Al ≈ 2.7, Mg ≈ 1.74, Ti ≈ 4.5, Fe ≈ 7.87, Cu ≈ 8.96, Ni ≈ 8.9 (all in g/cm³).
- **KTU 2024 Weightage:** This topic typically carries **7–14 marks** in ESE, often as a Part B question with a 7+7 split.

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Properties of Composites

The **properties of composite materials** refer to the combined physical, mechanical, thermal, electrical, and chemical responses exhibited by a multi-phase material system (typically a **matrix phase** reinforced with a **fiber or particulate phase**) under various service conditions. Unlike monolithic materials, composites derive their property profile from the *synergistic interaction* of constituent phases rather than from any single component.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standardized):** A composite is a heterogeneous material system comprising two or more distinct phases — a **matrix** (binder) and a **reinforcement** (fiber/particulate) — combined at a macroscopic level to produce a material with properties superior to those of its individual constituents.

## 1.2 Classification of Composite Properties

For KTU 2024 Scheme evaluation, composite properties are broadly classified into the following categories:

| Property Domain | Examples |
|---|---|
| **Mechanical** | Tensile strength, modulus, hardness, fatigue, fracture toughness |
| **Thermal** | Coefficient of thermal expansion, thermal conductivity, specific heat |
| **Electrical** | Resistivity, dielectric strength, dielectric constant |
| **Physical** | Density, void content, moisture absorption |
| **Chemical** | Chemical resistance, corrosion resistance, weathering |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Concrete & Steel Rebar" Model:**
> Imagine a chocolate bar with embedded nuts (matrix = chocolate, reinforcement = nuts). The chocolate alone is soft and weak; the nuts alone are hard but brittle. Together, the bar becomes **stronger, tougher, and more resistant to breakage**. Similarly, in a glass-fiber/epoxy composite, the brittle glass fibers carry the load while the epoxy matrix holds them in place and transfers stress.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following are key reference metrics used throughout composite property analysis:

- **Young's Modulus of E-glass fiber:** ~ **72 GPa**
- **Young's Modulus of Carbon Fiber (HS):** ~ **230 GPa**
- **Young's Modulus of Epoxy Matrix:** ~ **3 GPa**
- **Density of E-glass:** ~ **2.54 g/cm³**
- **Density of Carbon Fiber:** ~ **1.75 g/cm³**
- **Rule of Mixtures (RoM):** Valid only for **isostrain** (Voigt) or **isostress** (Reuss) loading conditions.
- **Volume Fraction ($V_f$, $V_m$):** Always obeys $V_f + V_m = 1$ (assuming no voids).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU 2024 Module 3, emphasis is placed on understanding how each property is *predicted* (analytically) and *measured* (experimentally), and how the **fiber-matrix interface** governs overall composite behavior.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanical Properties — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Strength and Modulus

The mechanical performance of a composite is governed primarily by:

- **Tensile Strength ($\sigma_c$):** Maximum longitudinal stress the composite can sustain.
- **Young's Modulus ($E_c$):** Slope of the linear elastic region of the stress–strain curve.
- **Compressive Strength:** Resistance to longitudinal crushing loads.
- **Interlaminar Shear Strength (ILSS):** Resistance to delamination between plies.

### 2.1.2 Why Are Composites Strong?

1. **Load Transfer:** The matrix transfers applied load to the high-strength fibers through **shear stresses** at the fiber–matrix interface.
2. **Crack Deflection:** A propagating crack in the matrix is deflected by fibers, requiring more energy.
3. **Fiber Pull-out:** Fibers bridging a crack pull out gradually, dissipating fracture energy.
4. **Synergistic Effect:** Combined strength often *exceeds* the weighted average of constituents.

### 2.1.3 Toughness and Fracture Behaviour

- **Fracture Toughness ($K_{IC}$):** Resistance to crack propagation. Composites typically show **rising R-curve** behavior (toughness increases with crack length) due to fiber bridging.
- **Impact Resistance:** Ability to absorb sudden energy. Governed by both fiber type and matrix ductility.

### 2.1.4 Fatigue Behaviour

- Composites generally exhibit **superior fatigue resistance** compared to metals because:
  - Damage accumulates gradually (matrix cracking → delamination → fiber breakage).
  - No sudden catastrophic failure (unlike metal fatigue).
  - Fail-safe behavior is built in.

## 2.2 Thermal Properties

### 2.2.1 Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE)

Composites can be engineered to have **near-zero CTE** by careful selection of fiber and matrix with opposing CTE values — critical for **aerospace and space structures** where dimensional stability is paramount.

### 2.2.2 Thermal Conductivity

- Polymer matrix composites are typically **thermal insulators**.
- Metal matrix composites (e.g., Al/SiC) have **high thermal conductivity** — used in heat sinks.
- Carbon fiber composites have **anisotropic** thermal conductivity (high along fiber axis, low transverse).

## 2.3 Electrical Properties

- **GFRP:** Excellent electrical insulators (dielectric constant ~ 4–6).
- **CFRP:** Conductive along fiber direction; used in EMI shielding and lightning-strike protection.
- **Dielectric Strength:** Critical for high-voltage insulator applications.

## 2.4 Physical & Chemical Properties

- **Density:** Typically lower than metals, making composites ideal for **weight-critical** applications.
- **Void Content:** Voids reduce strength, modulus, and fatigue life. Usually kept below **2%** in high-quality composites.
- **Moisture Absorption:** Polymer matrices absorb water, causing **swelling, plasticization, and $T_g$ depression**.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Equations for KTU Module 3 — Memorize These!**

| # | Property | Formula | Condition / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Density (RoM)** | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | g/cm³ |
| 2 | **Longitudinal Modulus** | $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | GPa (isostrain/Voigt) |
| 3 | **Transverse Modulus** | $1/E_T = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$ | GPa (isostress/Reuss) |
| 4 | **Longitudinal Strength** | $\sigma_L = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ | MPa (isostrain) |
| 5 | **Longitudinal CTE** | $\alpha_L = (\alpha_f E_f V_f + \alpha_m E_m V_m) / E_L$ | /°C |
| 6 | **Transverse CTE** | $\alpha_T = (1 + \nu_f)\alpha_f V_f + (1 + \nu_m)\alpha_m V_m - \alpha_L \nu_{LT}$ | /°C |
| 7 | **Void Fraction** | $V_v = (\rho_{th} - \rho_{ex}) / \rho_{th}$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | **Specific Strength** | $\sigma / \rho$ | m²/s² (figure of merit) |
| 9 | **Specific Modulus** | $E / \rho$ | m²/s² (figure of merit) |
| 10 | **Critical Fiber Length** | $l_c = \sigma_f d / (2 \tau_i)$ | m |
| 11 | **Fiber Efficiency Factor** | $\eta_l = 1 - (l_c / 2l)$ | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formulas above, subscripts: $f$ = fiber, $m$ = matrix, $c$ = composite, $L$ = longitudinal, $T$ = transverse, $v$ = void, $th$ = theoretical, $ex$ = experimental, $i$ = interface, $\tau$ = interfacial shear strength, $d$ = fiber diameter.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Critical Property | Composite Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | High specific strength/modulus | CFRP |
| **Automotive** | Energy absorption, light weight | Glass mat thermoplastic |
| **Marine** | Corrosion resistance | GFRP |
| **Electronics** | Low CTE, dielectric strength | Epoxy/SiC |
| **Wind Energy** | Fatigue resistance | Glass/epoxy |
| **Sports** | Vibration damping | Carbon fiber composites |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Rule of Mixtures for Longitudinal Modulus ($E_L$)

**Assumption:** Both fiber and matrix experience the **same strain** (isostrain condition) — valid when load is applied **parallel to fibers**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Force Equilibrium:**
The total load on the composite equals the sum of loads carried by fiber and matrix:
$$P_c = P_f + P_m$$

**Step 2 — Express Each Load in Terms of Stress and Area:**
$$P_c = \sigma_c A_c, \quad P_f = \sigma_f A_f, \quad P_m = \sigma_m A_m$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Equilibrium Equation:**
$$\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$$

**Step 4 — Divide Through by Total Area $A_c$:**
$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f (A_f/A_c) + \sigma_m (A_m/A_c)$$

**Step 5 — Recognize Area Ratios as Volume Fractions** (for fibers aligned along the loading axis):
$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$

**Step 6 — Apply Isostrain Condition** ($\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon$):
$$\sigma_c / E_c = \sigma_f / E_f = \sigma_m / E_m = \varepsilon$$

**Step 7 — Substitute $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ into the Strength Equation:**
$$E_c \varepsilon = E_f \varepsilon V_f + E_m \varepsilon V_m$$

**Step 8 — Cancel $\varepsilon$ from Both Sides:**
$$\boxed{E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m}$$

This is the **Voigt Model** (isostrain) for longitudinal modulus.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Rule of Mixtures for Transverse Modulus ($E_T$)

**Assumption:** Both fiber and matrix experience the **same stress** (isostress condition) — valid when load is applied **perpendicular to fibers**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Equal Stress Condition:**
$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f = \sigma_m = \sigma$$

**Step 2 — Strain Compatibility:**
Total composite strain is the volume-weighted sum of individual strains:
$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f V_f + \varepsilon_m V_m$$

**Step 3 — Apply Hooke's Law** ($\varepsilon = \sigma / E$):
$$\sigma / E_T = (\sigma / E_f) V_f + (\sigma / E_m) V_m$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $\sigma$ from Both Sides:**
$$1 / E_T = V_f / E_f + V_m / E_m$$

**Step 5 — Invert to Get Transverse Modulus:**
$$\boxed{E_T = \left( V_f / E_f + V_m / E_m \right)^{-1}}$$

This is the **Reuss Model** (isostress) for transverse modulus.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The longitudinal modulus is an *upper bound* (Voigt), while the transverse modulus is a *lower bound* (Reuss). True composite modulus lies **between** these two values.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Critical Fiber Length ($l_c$)

**Concept:** The **critical fiber length** is the minimum length required for the fiber to develop its full tensile strength through interfacial shear stress transfer.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Force Balance on a Single Fiber:**
The tensile load in the fiber at its midpoint is balanced by shear stress distributed along the fiber–matrix interface:
$$\sigma_f \cdot (\pi d^2 / 4) = \tau_i \cdot (\pi d \cdot l_c / 2)$$

**Step 2 — Simplify Both Sides** (cancel $\pi d$):
$$\sigma_f \cdot d / 4 = \tau_i \cdot l_c / 2$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $l_c$:**
$$l_c = \sigma_f d / (2 \tau_i)$$

**Step 4 — Boxed Result:**
$$\boxed{l_c = \frac{\sigma_f \, d}{2 \, \tau_i}}$$

**Engineering Implication:** Fibers shorter than $l_c$ will pull out before reaching their full strength; fibers much longer than $l_c$ (typically $l \geq 10 \cdot l_c$) develop full strength and fail by fracture.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A unidirectional glass-fiber/epoxy composite contains 40% volume fraction of fibers. Given:
- $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa
- $\sigma_f = 2400$ MPa, $\sigma_m = 80$ MPa
- $\rho_f = 2.54$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³
- $\alpha_f = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C, $\alpha_m = 60 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C

**Find:** $E_L$, $E_T$, $\sigma_L$, $\rho_c$, and $\alpha_L$.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Longitudinal Modulus ($E_L$):**
$$E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m = (72)(0.40) + (3.5)(0.60)$$
$$E_L = 28.8 + 2.1 = \boxed{30.9 \text{ GPa}}$$

**Step 2 — Transverse Modulus ($E_T$):**
$$1/E_T = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m = (0.40/72) + (0.60/3.5)$$
$$1/E_T = 0.00556 + 0.17143 = 0.17698$$
$$E_T = \boxed{5.65 \text{ GPa}}$$

**Step 3 — Longitudinal Strength ($\sigma_L$):**
$$\sigma_L = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m = (2400)(0.40) + (80)(0.60)$$
$$\sigma_L = 960 + 48 = \boxed{1008 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 4 — Composite Density ($\rho_c$):**
$$\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m = (2.54)(0.40) + (1.20)(0.60)$$
$$\rho_c = 1.016 + 0.720 = \boxed{1.736 \text{ g/cm}^3}$$

**Step 5 — Longitudinal CTE ($\alpha_L$):**
$$\alpha_L = (\alpha_f E_f V_f + \alpha_m E_m V_m) / E_L$$
$$\alpha_L = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-6})(72)(0.40) + (60 \times 10^{-6})(3.5)(0.60)}{30.9}$$
$$\alpha_L = \frac{(144 \times 10^{-6}) + (126 \times 10^{-6})}{30.9}$$
$$\alpha_L = \frac{270 \times 10^{-6}}{30.9} = \boxed{8.74 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /°C}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note the dramatic reduction in CTE:** From $\alpha_m = 60 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C for pure epoxy to $\alpha_L = 8.74 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C for the composite — a **nearly 7-fold reduction**. This demonstrates the **dimensional stability advantage** of fiber-reinforced composites.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the "Rule of Mixtures" as applied to composite materials. State the conditions under which the longitudinal and transverse modulus equations are valid.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Rule of Mixtures (RoM)** is an analytical method for predicting composite properties by assuming a weighted average based on the **volume fraction** of each constituent phase.

- **Longitudinal Modulus ($E_L$):** $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ — valid under **isostrain** (Voigt) condition, i.e., when load is applied **parallel to the fibers** and both phases experience the **same strain**.
- **Transverse Modulus ($E_T$):** $1/E_T = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$ — valid under **isostress** (Reuss) condition, i.e., when load is applied **perpendicular to the fibers** and both phases experience the **same stress**.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for $E_L$ with condition, 1 for $E_T$ with condition)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is meant by "Critical Fiber Length" in a fiber-reinforced composite? State its formula and explain its significance.**

**Model Answer:**

The **critical fiber length ($l_c$)** is defined as the *minimum length* of a fiber required for the fiber to develop its **maximum tensile stress** ($\sigma_f$) through interfacial shear stress transfer from the matrix.

**Formula:**
$$l_c = \frac{\sigma_f \, d}{2 \, \tau_i}$$

where $d$ is the fiber diameter and $\tau_i$ is the **interfacial shear strength**.

**Significance:**
- If $l < l_c$: fiber will **pull out** of the matrix before reaching its full strength (inefficient reinforcement).
- If $l \geq 10 \cdot l_c$: fiber develops its full strength and fails by **fracture** (efficient reinforcement).
- $l_c$ guides the **selection of fiber length** in design.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for significance)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q.A(a) [7 Marks] — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
**Derive the expressions for longitudinal and transverse Young's modulus of a unidirectional fiber-reinforced composite using the Rule of Mixtures. State the assumptions clearly.**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (Common to Both):**
1. Fibers are **uniformly distributed** and **perfectly aligned** in the matrix.
2. The **fiber–matrix interface** is perfectly bonded (no slippage).
3. **No voids** are present: $V_f + V_m = 1$.
4. The composite is **macroscopically homogeneous** and linearly elastic.

---

**Part (i) — Longitudinal Modulus ($E_L$):**

**Step 1:** Apply force equilibrium:
$$\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$$

**Step 2:** Divide by $A_c$ (area ratios = volume fractions for aligned fibers):
$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$

**Step 3:** Apply isostrain condition: $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon$

**Step 4:** Substitute Hooke's law $\sigma = E\varepsilon$:
$$E_c \varepsilon = E_f \varepsilon V_f + E_m \varepsilon V_m$$

**Step 5:** Cancel $\varepsilon$:
$$\boxed{E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m}$$

This is the **Voigt (isostrain) model** — valid for loading **parallel** to fibers.

---

**Part (ii) — Transverse Modulus ($E_T$):**

**Step 1:** Apply isostress condition: $\sigma_c = \sigma_f = \sigma_m = \sigma$

**Step 2:** Apply strain compatibility:
$$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f V_f + \varepsilon_m V_m$$

**Step 3:** Substitute Hooke's law:
$$\sigma / E_T = (\sigma / E_f) V_f + (\sigma / E_m) V_m$$

**Step 4:** Cancel $\sigma$:
$$1 / E_T = V_f / E_f + V_m / E_m$$

**Step 5:** Invert:
$$\boxed{E_T = \left( \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m} \right)^{-1}}$$

This is the **Reuss (isostress) model** — valid for loading **perpendicular** to fibers.
