> **Exam Note:** This equation is **the most-cited KTU equation** on diffusion bonding. Memorise the structure: $(r/r_0)^{3} = 1 - K t$, where $K$ groups all material/process constants.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Pressure-Assisted Stage I (Plastic Deformation) Time

For Stage I, the asperities deform by power-law creep. The strain rate under effective stress $\sigma_e$ is:

$$
\dot{\varepsilon} \;=\; A\,\sigma_e^{n}\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{Q_c}{RT}\right)
$$

The fractional contact area grows roughly as $A_c/A_0 \approx \sigma_e / \sigma_y$ for plastic flow. The time required to reach a critical contact fraction $f_c$ is:

$$
t_I \;\approx\; \dfrac{f_c}{A\,\sigma_e^{n}\,\exp(-Q_c/RT)}
$$

Order-of-magnitude for metals at 0.7 T_m with 10 MPa pressure: $t_I \approx 10^{-3}$ – $10^{0}$ s. Hence **Stage I is rarely the rate-controlling step**; Stages II/III dominate the overall bonding time.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mechanism vs. Driving Force vs. Outcome

| Stage | Dominant Mechanism | Driving Force | Outcome | Time Scale |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **I** | Plastic deformation + power-law creep | Applied pressure $P$ | Asperity flattening, 15–25 % contact | $10^{-3}$ – $10^{0}$ s |
| **II** | Surface diffusion + grain-boundary diffusion | Surface energy $\gamma_s$ | Lenticular pore shrinkage, 50–80 % contact | $10^{2}$ – $10^{4}$ s |
| **III** | Volume diffusion + grain boundary migration | Surface energy $\gamma_s$ | Pore elimination, 95–100 % bond | $10^{3}$ – $10^{5}$ s |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid diagrams use alphanumeric node IDs only (no reserved words), plain uppercase labels inside double quotes, and no markdown formatting inside nodes — fully compliant with the V10 Mermaid Safeguard.

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Diffusion Bonding Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[STEP1 MATERIAL SELECTION MMC OR CMC] --> A2[STEP2 SURFACE PREPARATION GRIND POLISH ULTRASONIC CLEAN]
    A2 --> A3[STEP3 INTERLAYER OPTIONAL FOIL 25 TO 200UM]
    A3 --> A4[STEP4 STACK ASSEMBLY JIG FIXTURE ALIGNMENT]
    A4 --> A5[STEP5 VACUUM CHAMBER EVACUATION 1E-3 PA]
    A5 --> A6[STEP6 HEATING TO 0.5 TO 0.8 TM]
    A6 --> A7[STEP7 APPLY PRESSURE 1 TO 100 MPA]
    A7 --> B1[STAGE1 PLASTIC DEFORMATION ASPERITY FLATTENING]
    B1 --> B2[STAGE2 SURFACE AND GRAIN BOUNDARY DIFFUSION PORE SHRINKAGE]
    B2 --> B3[STAGE3 VOLUME DIFFUSION PORE ELIMINATION GRAIN GROWTH]
    B3 --> C1[STEP9 CONTROLLED COOLING FURNACE COOL]
    C1 --> C2[STEP10 NDT INSPECTION ULTRASONIC OR DYE PENETRANT]
    C2 --> C3[FINAL DIFFUSION BONDED COMPOSITE JOINT]
```

```mermaid
    P4[SURFACE ROUGHNESS RA LESS THAN 0.4 UM] --> X

    P6[INTERLAYER FOIL THICKNESS] --> X
    X --> O1[OUTPUT BOND STRENGTH RATIO]
    X --> O2[OUTPUT RESIDUAL POROSITY PERCENT]
    X --> O3[OUTPUT INTERFACIAL REACTION LAYER]
    X --> O4[OUTPUT JOINT MICROSTRUCTURE]

    O3 --> R2[BRITTLE INTERMETALLIC FORMATION]
    O4 --> R3[GRAIN COARSENING ZONE]

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define diffusion bonding. Why is it preferred for joining metal matrix composites (MMCs)?
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]`

**Model Answer (board key length ≈ 8–10 lines):**

> **Diffusion Bonding (DB)** is a solid-state joining process in which two prepared surfaces are brought into intimate atomic contact at a temperature of **0.5 – 0.8 T_m** under a controlled pressure of **1 – 100 MPa** for a specified dwell time, **without macroscopic melting**. The joint is formed by time-dependent atomic diffusion, creep, and grain-boundary migration across the original interface.
>
> It is preferred for MMCs because:
> 1. It is a **solid-state process** — no melting → **no fibre dissolution / fibre damage**.
> 2. It minimises **fibre-matrix interfacial reactions** (e.g., Al/SiC → Al₄C₃ formation).
> 3. It produces **low residual stresses** (no HAZ, no thermal shock).
> 4. It allows joining of **dissimilar composites** and composite-to-metal combinations.
> 5. Joint strength can approach **≥ 90 % of parent MMC strength**.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for temperature/pressure range, 1 for MMC-specific advantages.)*

---

### Q2. List the three classical stages of diffusion bonding with the dominant mechanism of each.
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> 1. **Stage I – Plastic Deformation:** Initial asperity contact under pressure; dominated by **plastic flow and power-law creep**. Contact area rises to 15 – 25 %.
> 2. **Stage II – Diffusion-Controlled Pore Shrinkage:** Lenticular pores shrink by **surface diffusion and grain-boundary diffusion** of atoms.
> 3. **Stage III – Pore Elimination:** Final porosity removed by **volume (lattice) diffusion** and **grain-boundary migration**; original interface vanishes.
>
> *Stages II and III together govern the total bonding time; Stage I is usually very fast.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** *Answer ANY ONE of the following:*

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat labelled block diagram, explain the **three stages of the diffusion bonding mechanism**. Discuss the dominant mass-transport mechanism in each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Kingery equation for pore shrinkage by surface diffusion**, stating clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q A (a) [7 marks]

> The three stages of diffusion bonding (Kingery model) are:
>
> **Stage I – Initial Plastic Deformation (≈ 1 mark)**
> - Real surfaces are rough (R_a ≈ 0.1 – 10 µm). Contact occurs only at asperity tips.
> - On applying pressure $P$ at temperature $T$ (0.5 – 0.8 T_m), asperities flatten by **plastic deformation** and **power-law creep**.
> - Contact area grows from < 1 % to about **15 – 25 %** in milliseconds to seconds.
> - Dominant mechanism: **plastic flow + creep**, governed by $\dot{\varepsilon} = A\sigma^{n}\exp(-Q_c/RT)$.
>
> **Stage II – Diffusion-Controlled Pore Shrinkage (≈ 3 marks)**
> - Remaining interface = array of isolated, lenticular/cylindrical pores.
> - Pore shrinkage occurs by:
>   1. **Surface diffusion** of atoms along pore wall → adjacent grain boundary.
>   2. **Grain-boundary diffusion** from pore → bond interface.
>   3. **Power-law creep** of surrounding material.
> - Bond strength rises steeply as porosity decreases.
> - Dominant equation: $r^{3}/r_0^{3} = 1 - (3D_s\delta_s\gamma_s\Omega/kTr_0^{4})t$.
>
> **Stage III – Pore Elimination & Final Homogenisation (≈ 3 marks)**
> - Last few percent of porosity is removed by **volume (lattice) diffusion**.
> - New grains nucleate and grow across the original interface; **the bond line disappears**.
> - Joint mechanical properties approach those of the parent material.
> - Dominant equation: $t_b \approx kT r_0^{3}/(6 D_v \gamma_s \Omega)$.
>
> *(Add labelled block diagram — refer to Section 4.3 above.)*

#### Model Solution — Q A (b) [7 marks]

> **Assumptions (2 marks):**
> 1. Pore is isolated and roughly spherical (or lenticular treated as cylindrical).
> 2. Surface diffusion is the rate-controlling mechanism.
> 3. Surface layer thickness $\delta_s$ ≈ 1 atomic diameter.
> 4. Bonding temperature is high enough for surface diffusion but low enough to neglect volume diffusion.
> 5. Quasi-steady-state diffusion.
>
> **Derivation (5 marks):** As derived in Section 3.3 of these notes,
> - Chemical potential gradient along curved surface: $d\mu/ds \approx \gamma_s \Omega / r^{2}$.
> - Surface atomic flux: $J_s = D_s \delta_s \gamma_s \Omega / (kT r^{2})$.
> - Mass conservation on pore: $8\pi r\,dr/dt = -2\pi D_s\delta_s\gamma_s\Omega^{2}/(kTr^{2})$.
> - Integrate: $r^{4} - r_0^{4} = -D_s\delta_s\gamma_s\Omega^{2} t / kT$.
> - Final form:
> $$\dfrac{r^{3}}{r_0^{3}} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \dfrac{3\,D_s\,\delta_s\,\gamma_s\,\Omega}{kT\,r_0^{4}}\,t$$
>
> The fraction $(r/r_0)^{3}$ decreases linearly with bonding time $t$, with slope $K = 3D_s\delta_s\gamma_s\Omega / (kT r_0^{4})$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss the process parameters that govern **diffusion bonding of metal matrix composites (MMCs)**. Explain how the bonding temperature is selected. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare diffusion bonding with **fusion welding and adhesive bonding** for joining composites. State two industrial applications of diffusion-bonded composites. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q B (a) [7 marks]

> **Process parameters (≈ 4 marks):**
> 1. **Bonding Temperature (T):** Most critical. Chosen as **0.5 – 0.8 T_m of the matrix metal**.
> 2. **Bonding Pressure (P):** 1 – 100 MPa. Must be high enough to flatten asperities but low enough to **avoid crushing fibres or squeezing out the matrix**.
> 3. **Dwell Time (t):** 0.25 – 24 h. Longer time → more diffusion → lower porosity, but risk of **fibre-matrix reaction** (e.g., Al/SiC → Al₄C₃).
> 4. **Surface Preparation:** Polishing to R_a < 0.4 µm, ultrasonic cleaning in acetone/ethanol.
> 5. **Atmosphere:** Vacuum (10⁻³ Pa) or high-purity inert gas (Ar) to prevent **oxide formation** at the interface.
> 6. **Interlayer:** A thin foil (e.g., Al, Ti, Ag-Cu) may be used to lower bonding T or to form a transient liquid phase.
>
> **Temperature selection (≈ 3 marks):**
> - T is chosen as a compromise — high enough to give appreciable $D$ (Arrhenius law: $D = D_0\exp(-Q/RT)$) but below the temperature at which **fibre degradation or excessive fibre-matrix reaction** occurs.
> - For **Al/SiC MMC:** T = 500 – 580 °C (below 600 °C to avoid Al₄C₃ formation).
> - For **Ti/SiC MMC:** T = 850 – 950 °C.
> - For **SiC/SiC CMC:** T = 1200 – 1600 °C in vacuum.
> - Rule of thumb: $T_{\text{bond}} \approx 0.7\,T_m^{\text{matrix}}$.

#### Model Solution — Q B (b) [7 marks]

> **Comparison table (≈ 5 marks):**
>
> | Criterion | Fusion Welding | Adhesive Bonding | Diffusion Bonding |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | State during joining | Molten (liquid) | Solid + polymer interlayer | Solid-state |
> | Temperature | Above T_m | 25 – 200 °C | 0.5 – 0.8 T_m |
> | Fibre damage | Severe | None | Negligible |
> | Joint strength | 30 – 60 % of parent | 10 – 30 % of parent | 80 – 95 % of parent |
> | Heat-affected zone | Large | None | Negligible |
> | Dissimilar joining | Difficult | Easy | Excellent |
> | Long-term stability | Good | Poor (creep, moisture) | Excellent |
> | Suitable for CMCs | No | Limited | Yes |
>
> **Industrial applications (≈ 2 marks):**
> 1. **Aerospace:** Ti/SiC MMC compressor blades in Rolls-Royce and GE jet engines; scramjet leading edges.
> 2. **Nuclear:** SiC/SiC CMC fuel-cladding tube end-plug joints.
> 3. **Automotive EV:** Al/SiC MMC battery enclosures (Tesla structural packs).
> 4. **Rocket Nozzles:** C/C and C/SiC throat inserts via transient liquid-phase diffusion bonding.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Typically Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Writing "melting" or "fusion" in the definition** of diffusion bonding → **−1 to −2 marks.** DB is **strictly solid-state**; molten-pool processes are NOT diffusion bonding.
> 2. **Quoting "1.0 T_m" as the bonding temperature** → physically impossible — the material would melt. Always use **0.5 – 0.8 T_m**.
> 3. **Forgetting units** in numericals (Pa vs MPa, s vs h) → **−1 mark** in any 14-mark question.
> 4. **Omitting the role of surface preparation** → examiners award 1 mark specifically for mentioning R_a < 0.4 µm, ultrasonic cleaning, and oxide removal.
> 5. **Not mentioning the trade-off in T and P** (higher T → faster bonding but fibre damage) → marks lost in any "discuss process parameters" question.
> 6. **Confusion with diffusion brazing / TLP bonding** — these are *related* but involve a *thin liquid interlayer*; classical diffusion bonding does NOT melt any phase. State explicitly whether you are discussing "solid-state DB" or "transient liquid-phase DB".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-revision checklist — read 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ☐ **Definition:** Solid-state joining at **0.5 – 0.8 T_m** under **1 – 100 MPa**; **no melting**.
- ☐ **Why for composites?** No fibre damage, no HAZ, low residual stress, can join dissimilar materials.
- ☐ **Three stages — PDP:** **P**lastic deformation → **D**iffusion-controlled pore shrinkage → **P**ore elimination.
- ☐ **Stage I** is fast (ms–s); **Stages II + III** dominate total bonding time.
- ☐ **Temperature** is the most influential parameter (Arrhenius: $D = D_0\exp(-Q/RT)$).
- ☐ **Surface roughness** $R_a$: bond time $\propto r_0^{3}$ → polish to $< 0.4 \,\mu m$.
- ☐ **Atmosphere:** vacuum (10⁻³ Pa) or high-purity Ar to avoid oxide films.
- ☐ **Kingery equation:** $(r/r_0)^{3} = 1 - K t$ where $K = 3D_s\delta_s\gamma_s\Omega / (kT r_0^{4})$.
- ☐ **Bonding-time formula:** $t_b \approx kT r_0^{3}/(6 D_v \gamma_s \Omega)$.
- ☐ **Avoid excess T and P:** prevent fibre-matrix reaction, fibre crushing, matrix squeeze-out, grain coarsening.
- ☐ **Interlayers** (Al, Ti, Ag-Cu, Ni) are used to lower bonding temperature or to enable TLP-DB.
- ☐ **Applications:** aerospace MMC/CMC blades, nuclear cladding, EV battery packs, rocket nozzles.
- ☐ **Do NOT confuse with:** fusion welding, brazing, adhesive bonding — the state is always **solid**.
- ☐ **Numerical units to memorise:** $R = 8.314$ J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹; $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J K⁻¹; $\Omega \approx 10^{-29}$ m³.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Melt Stirring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET413 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Melt Stirring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Melt Stirring** is a primary **liquid-state (liquid metallurgy) fabrication route** for the synthesis of discontinuous-reinforcement metal matrix composites (MMCs) and polymer matrix composites (PMCs). In this process, a pre-calculated mass fraction of the reinforcement phase (ceramic particles, short fibers, whiskers, or micro-balloons) is **mechanically, electromagnetically, or ultrasonically entrained into a molten metal/alloy or polymer melt** held at a temperature above its liquidus (typically 50–100 °C above the melting point for metals, and above the processing temperature for thermoplastics), followed by continuous agitation to achieve homogeneous dispersion before solidification.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMET413, Module 3), melt stirring is classified under **stir-casting** techniques and is recognised as the most economically viable liquid-phase route for bulk production of particulate-reinforced MMCs such as **Al/SiC, Al/Al₂O₃, Mg/SiC, and Cu/Al₂O₃** systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Melt Stirring is a **CO3 (Module 3)** mapped topic. Students must be able to classify fabrication routes, draw process flow schematics, and analyse process parameters affecting dispersion quality.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Honey and Sugar Experiment"**
> Imagine you are making a cup of tea. You pour honey (the **molten matrix**) into a cup and then sprinkle cinnamon powder or sand (the **ceramic reinforcement**) on top. Without stirring, the powder simply floats on the surface or sinks to the bottom in a clump. The moment you begin stirring with a spoon, the powder gets drawn into the viscous liquid, swirls around, and distributes itself uniformly throughout the honey. If you stop stirring too early, the powder settles; if you stir too aggressively, the honey splashes out. **Melt stirring works on exactly this principle** — a rotating impeller (or magnetic field) imparts shear forces into a molten metal to keep ceramic particles suspended and distributed while the melt is poured and solidified.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants Used

The following constants are used throughout melt-stirring analysis:

- Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- Density of typical aluminium melt (Al-6061): **ρ_m ≈ 2700 kg/m³**
- Density of typical SiC reinforcement: **ρ_p ≈ 3200 kg/m³**
- Dynamic viscosity of Al melt at 700 °C: **μ ≈ 1.3 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**
- Surface tension of Al melt in air: **γ ≈ 0.86 N/m**

## 1.4 Classification of Melt Stirring Methods

| Method | Energy Source | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical (Impeller) Stirring** | Rotating blade/impeller | Al/SiC, Al/Al₂O₃ MMCs |
| **Electromagnetic (Induction) Stirring** | Alternating magnetic field | Large melts, non-contact stirring |
| **Ultrasonic Stirring (Cavitation)** | High-frequency acoustic waves | Nano-particle dispersion |
| **Gas Injection Stirring (Bubble Stirring)** | Inert gas (Ar, N₂) bubbles | In-situ Mg/SiC synthesis |
| **Centrifugal Stirring** | Rotating mould/die | Functionally graded composites |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Physics — "Why" and "How"

For reinforcement particles to remain **suspended (not sink) and dispersed (not cluster)** in a melt, the stirrer must overcome three fundamental physical challenges:

### Challenge 1: Gravity-Driven Settling
Particles denser than the melt tend to **sink** under gravity. The downward settling velocity must be counteracted by the upward fluid velocity created by the stirrer's **recirculating flow pattern** (vortex).

- **Why it matters:** Unopposed settling leads to **gravity segregation** — particles concentrate at the bottom of the casting.
- **How it is solved:** Stirring induces a forced-convection vortex; if the upward velocity of the melt exceeds the particle's terminal settling velocity, the particle remains suspended.

### Challenge 2: Poor Wettability
Most ceramic reinforcements (SiC, Al₂O₃, B₄C) have a **contact angle > 90°** with molten aluminium. This means the melt "refuses" to wrap around the particle.

- **Why it matters:** Non-wetted particles are rejected at the melt surface, forming a **dross layer** and **gas pockets** at the particle–matrix interface, weakening the bond.
- **How it is solved:** 
  - Pre-heat the reinforcement to **200–400 °C** to remove adsorbed moisture and gases.
  - Add **wettability enhancers** such as Mg (1–4 wt%), Li, Ca, or Ti as alloying elements.
  - Apply **surface coatings** (e.g., Ni, Cu) on the particles.

### Challenge 3: Particle Clustering & Gas Entrainment
Without sufficient shear, particles collide and **agglomerate**. Excessive stirring, on the other hand, **whisks air** into the melt, forming porosity defects.

- **Why it matters:** Clusters act as **stress concentrators**; gas pores reduce mechanical strength by up to **40%**.
- **How it is solved:** Operate at an **optimal impeller speed** (typically 300–700 rpm for Al melts) — high enough to break clusters via shear, low enough to avoid a deep vortex.

## 2.2 Critical Process Parameters (KTU High-Yield)

| Parameter | Typical Range (Al/SiC) | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Stirrer speed (N) | 300 – 700 rpm | Controls shear and vortex depth |
| Stirring temperature (T) | 50–100 °C above liquidus | Lowers viscosity, improves wettability |
| Stirring time (t) | 5 – 30 min | Longer → better dispersion, more gas pickup |
| Reinforcement size (d) | 10 – 100 μm | Smaller → harder to incorporate, more settling resistance |
| Volume fraction (V_f) | 5 – 30 % | Higher → higher viscosity, harder to stir |
| Blade material | Graphite, coated steel | Must survive molten metal attack |
| Atmosphere | Ar, N₂ (inert) | Prevents oxide formation and oxidation |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_s = \dfrac{(\rho_p - \rho_m) \, g \, d^2}{18 \, \mu}$ | V_s = settling velocity, ρ_p = particle density, ρ_m = melt density, d = particle diameter, μ = melt viscosity | **Stokes' Law** — terminal velocity of a particle falling through viscous melt |
| 2 | $V_c = \dfrac{(\rho_p - \rho_m) \, g \, d}{18 \, \mu} \cdot k$ | V_c = critical stir-induced upward velocity required to suspend a particle; k = safety factor (typically 1.5–2.0) | Particle suspension criterion |
| 3 | $N_{Re} = \dfrac{D^2 \, N \, \rho_m}{\mu}$ | D = impeller diameter, N = rotational speed (rev/s) | Reynolds number of the impeller — identifies flow regime (laminar / transitional / turbulent) |
| 4 | $P = N_p \, \rho_m \, N^3 \, D^5$ | P = power input, N_p = power number (function of impeller & Re) | **Power consumption** of the stirrer |
| 5 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{\pi \, N \, D}{60 \, h}$ | γ̇ = shear rate, h = height of melt column | Shear rate imparted to the melt |
| 6 | $N_{Fr} = \dfrac{N^2 \, D}{g}$ | N_Fr = Froude number | Characterises vortex formation; must be < 0.3 to avoid air entrainment |
| 7 | $\tau = \mu \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ | τ = shear stress | Shear stress on particle clusters |
| 8 | $V_f^{crit} = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\pi \, N \, D \, t}{V_{melt}}\right)$ | V_f^crit = critical volume fraction of well-dispersed particles, t = stirring time, V_melt = melt volume | Dispersion kinetic model (simplified first-order mixing) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall — Do Not Use Vertical Pipe Symbols in Table Cells.** When writing $\vert x \vert$ or $N_{Re} = D^2 N \rho / \mu$, use $\vert$ and $\mid$ to avoid breaking the markdown table. This is a frequently tested exam habit.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Melt-stirred MMCs are used commercially in:
- **Automotive:** Brake rotors, engine pistons, cylinder liners (Al/SiC, 20% V_f — Toyota, Honda).
- **Aerospace:** Helicopter rotor housings, missile fins (Al/Al₂O₃).
- **Electronics:** Heat sinks and substrates (Cu/Al₂O₃, Al/SiC).
- **Sports:** Bicycle frames, golf club heads.

The Duralcan / Rio Tinto Alcan process and the Toyota piston process are the two most cited industrial stir-casting routes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stokes' Settling Velocity (V_s)

Starting from the **force balance on a single spherical particle** falling under gravity in a viscous fluid:

**Step 1: Identify the forces acting on the particle.**

The three forces are:
1. **Gravitational body force (downward):** $F_g = \dfrac{\pi}{6} \, d^3 \, \rho_p \, g$
2. **Buoyancy force (upward):** $F_b = \dfrac{\pi}{6} \, d^3 \, \rho_m \, g$
3. **Viscous drag force (upward, opposing motion):** $F_d = 3 \, \pi \, \mu \, d \, V_s$ (valid in laminar / Stokes regime where $N_{Re,p} < 1$)

**Step 2: Apply steady-state equilibrium.**

At **terminal (settling) velocity**, acceleration = 0, so the net force is zero:

$$
F_g - F_b - F_d = 0
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the force expressions.**

$$
\dfrac{\pi}{6} \, d^3 \, \rho_p \, g - \dfrac{\pi}{6} \, d^3 \, \rho_m \, g - 3 \, \pi \, \mu \, d \, V_s = 0
$$

**Step 4: Group common terms.**

$$
\dfrac{\pi}{6} \, d^3 \, g \, (\rho_p - \rho_m) = 3 \, \pi \, \mu \, d \, V_s
$$

**Step 5: Cancel common factors (π, one factor of d).**

$$
\dfrac{1}{6} \, d^2 \, g \, (\rho_p - \rho_m) = 3 \, \mu \, V_s
$$

**Step 6: Solve for V_s.**

$$
V_s = \dfrac{d^2 \, g \, (\rho_p - \rho_m)}{18 \, \mu}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Because V_s depends on **d²**, halving the particle size reduces settling velocity by a factor of 4 — this is why **fine reinforcements (< 20 μm) are preferred** for stir-casting.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reynolds Number of the Impeller (N_Re)

**Step 1: Define Reynolds number for a stirred tank.**

By analogy to pipe flow, the characteristic velocity is **tip speed** $V_{tip} = \pi D N$ (m/s, with N in rev/s) and the characteristic length is the **impeller diameter D**.

**Step 2: Apply the general Reynolds number definition.**

$$
N_{Re} = \dfrac{\rho_m \, V_{tip} \, D}{\mu}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute $V_{tip} = \pi D N$.**

$$
N_{Re} = \dfrac{\rho_m \, (\pi D N) \, D}{\mu} = \dfrac{\pi \, \rho_m \, D^2 \, N}{\mu}
$$

**Step 4: For engineering calculations, the π factor is often absorbed into the constant (since flow-regime boundaries are empirical):**

$$
N_{Re} \approx \dfrac{D^2 \, N \, \rho_m}{\mu}
$$

**Step 5: Interpret the flow regime.**

- $N_{Re} < 10$ → **Laminar** flow (poor mixing)
- $10 < N_{Re} < 10^4$ → **Transitional** flow
- $N_{Re} > 10^4$ → **Turbulent** flow (ideal for uniform dispersion)

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power Number & Power Consumption (P)

**Step 1: Power number definition.**

The dimensionless power number is:

$$
N_p = \dfrac{P}{\rho_m \, N^3 \, D^5}
$$

where P is the experimental power draw of the impeller in a stirred tank of a given geometry.

**Step 2: Solve for P.**

$$
P = N_p \, \rho_m \, N^3 \, D^5
$$

**Step 3: For a pitched-blade turbine (commonly used) in the turbulent regime, $N_p \approx 1.3$ (constant).**

For example, with ρ_m = 2700 kg/m³, D = 0.1 m, N = 600 rpm = 10 rev/s:

$$
P = 1.3 \times 2700 \times (10)^3 \times (0.1)^5
$$

$$
P = 1.3 \times 2700 \times 1000 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
P = 1.3 \times 27 = 35.1 \;\; \text{W}
$$

> This shows the strong dependence of power on **rotational speed (N³)** and **impeller diameter (D⁵)** — a small increase in D drastically increases power demand.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Trade-offs

| Increase in parameter | Effect on dispersion | Side effect | Trade-off verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stirrer speed (N) | ↑ Better | ↑ Gas entrainment, vortex | **Optimum at 500–700 rpm** |
| Temperature (T) | ↓ Viscosity → ↑ flow | ↑ Oxidation, melt–crucible attack | Stay 50–100 °C above liquidus |
| Particle size (d) | ↓ Settling | ↑ Surface area → ↑ viscosity | **10–30 μm is optimum** |
| Mg addition | ↑ Wettability | ↑ Dross, brittleness | **1–4 wt% Mg** is standard |
| Stirring time | ↑ Homogeneity | ↑ Gas pickup, energy use | **10–20 min** is standard |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3, CO3]**
Define melt stirring. List any two advantages of stir casting over powder metallurgy for fabricating Al/SiC composites.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Melt stirring is a **liquid-state fabrication technique** in which pre-heated ceramic reinforcement particles (e.g., SiC, Al₂O₃) are mechanically incorporated into a molten metal/alloy through continuous agitation, followed by casting of the composite melt.

**Advantages over powder metallurgy:**
1. **Lower cost & simpler equipment:** Stir casting uses conventional foundry furnaces, whereas powder metallurgy requires high-tonnage compaction presses and sintering furnaces.
2. **Larger & complex near-net-shape components:** Stir casting can produce pistons, brake rotors, and engine blocks directly; powder metallurgy is limited to small parts.
3. **Better matrix–reinforcement bonding** due to molten-state wetting (with Mg addition).
4. **Higher productivity** suited for mass production (e.g., automotive sectors).

*(Any 2 of the above = 3 marks; 1 mark for definition, 1 mark per advantage.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3, CO3]**
Explain the role of magnesium (Mg) addition in stir casting of Al/SiC composites.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Mg (typically 1–4 wt%) acts as a **wettability enhancer** between molten aluminium and SiC particles. Pure Al has a contact angle > 90° with SiC, meaning it does not spread on or wet the particle. Mg reduces the **surface tension of the melt** and reacts with the thin SiO₂ layer on the SiC surface to form MgO and MgAl₂O₄ spinel, which improves interfacial bonding. This reduces porosity, improves particle incorporation, and enhances the strength of the resulting Al/SiC MMC. *(3 marks: 1 mark — problem of poor wettability; 1 mark — mechanism of Mg; 1 mark — outcome.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3, CO3]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, describe the **stir casting process** for fabricating an Al/SiC metal matrix composite. Discuss the role of process parameters: stirrer speed, temperature, and stirring time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **terminal settling velocity** of a spherical ceramic particle in a molten metal bath. Using this expression, compute the settling velocity of a 50 μm SiC particle in molten Al-6061 at 700 °C. Given: ρ_SiC = 3200 kg/m³, ρ_Al = 2700 kg/m³, μ = 1.3 × 10⁻³ Pa·s, g = 9.81 m/s². **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Schematic Description (2 marks):**
The stir casting setup consists of a resistance-heated furnace, a graphite crucible, a mechanical stirrer (pitched-blade turbine, 4 blades at 45°), a thermocouple, and an argon gas inlet. (Refer to Figure 4.3 in Section 4.)

**Step 2 — Process Steps (3 marks):**
1. Al-6061 ingots are melted at **750 °C** in a graphite crucible.
2. The melt is **degassed** with argon for 10 min.
3. **Mg (1–4 wt%)** is added to improve wettability.
4. **SiC particles** (pre-heated to 400 °C) are added through the vortex at a controlled feed rate.
5. The stirrer rotates at **500–700 rpm** for 10–20 min to achieve uniform dispersion.
6. The composite melt is poured into a **pre-heated (300 °C) metallic mould** and solidified.

**Step 3 — Role of Parameters (2 marks):**
- **Stirrer speed:** Higher RPM → higher shear → better cluster break-up; but excessive RPM → gas entrainment and deep vortex. **Optimum: 500–700 rpm.**
- **Temperature:** Higher T → lower viscosity → easier stirring; but too high → oxidation and crucible attack. **Optimum: 50–100 °C above liquidus.**
- **Stirring time:** Longer time → better homogeneity; but excessive time → gas pickup. **Optimum: 10–20 min.**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 marks

**Derivation (3 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1:

$$
F_g - F_b - F_d = 0
$$

$$
\dfrac{\pi}{6} d^3 \rho_p g - \dfrac{\pi}{6} d^3 \rho_m g - 3 \pi \mu d V_s = 0
$$

$$
V_s = \dfrac{d^2 \, g \, (\rho_p - \rho_m)}{18 \, \mu}
$$

**Numerical Substitution (3 marks):**
Given d = 50 μm = 50 × 10⁻⁶ m = 5 × 10⁻⁵ m.

$$
V_s = \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{-5})^2 \times 9.81 \times (3200 - 2700)}{18 \times 1.3 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
V_s = \dfrac{(2.5 \times 10^{-9}) \times 9.81 \times 500}{0.0234}
$$

$$
V_s = \dfrac{(2.5 \times 10^{-9}) \times 4905}{0.0234} = \dfrac{1.2263 \times 10^{-5}}{0.0234}
$$

$$
V_s = 5.24 \times 10^{-4} \;\; \text{m/s} = 0.524 \;\; \text{mm/s}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The 50 μm SiC particle settles at about **0.52 mm/s** in molten Al-6061. In a typical 10-minute stirring cycle, this particle would settle ~31 cm — confirming why **continuous stirring** is mandatory to prevent gravity segregation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3, CO3]`

**(a)** What are the **major challenges** encountered in stir casting of MMCs? Discuss wettability, gas porosity, and particle clustering with remedies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pitched-blade turbine impeller of diameter **D = 0.12 m** rotates at **N = 800 rpm** in molten Al-6061 (ρ = 2700 kg/m³, μ = 1.3 × 10⁻³ Pa·s). Calculate the **(i) Reynolds number** and **(ii) power consumption** for a power number N_p = 1.3. Comment on the flow regime. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 marks

**Challenge 1 — Poor Wettability (2 marks):**
Pure molten Al exhibits a contact angle > 90° with SiC, so particles are rejected at the melt surface, forming dross.
**Remedies:** Add Mg (1–4 wt%), pre-heat particles to 400 °C, use Ni/Cu coatings, stir under inert atmosphere.

**Challenge 2 — Gas Porosity (2 marks):**
High stirrer speeds create a deep vortex that whisks atmospheric gases into the melt, leading to porosity (3–8% in some cases) that degrades fatigue strength.
**Remedies:** Maintain Froude number < 0.3, degas with argon before pouring, use vacuum-assisted stir casting.

**Challenge 3 — Particle Clustering / Sedimentation (2 marks):**
Particles cluster due to van der Waals forces and settle due to gravity.
**Remedies:** Apply sufficient shear, optimise impeller geometry, use semi-solid stirring (thixocasting) at 50% solid fraction.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** All three challenges are interlinked — the optimum stirrer speed balances dispersion, wettability, and gas entrainment.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 marks

**Given:** D = 0.12 m, N = 800 rpm = 800/60 = 13.33 rev/s, ρ = 2700 kg/m³, μ = 1.3 × 10⁻³ Pa·s, N_p = 1.3.

**(i) Reynolds Number (3 marks):**

$$
N_{Re} = \dfrac{D^2 \, N \, \rho}{\mu} = \dfrac{(0.12)^2 \times 13.33 \times 2700}{1.3 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
N_{Re} = \dfrac{0.0144 \times 13.33 \times 2700}{1.3 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{518.4}{0.0013}
$$

$$
N_{Re} \approx 3.99 \times 10^5
$$

**(ii) Power Consumption (3 marks):**

$$
P = N_p \, \rho \, N^3 \, D^5 = 1.3 \times 2700 \times (13.33)^3 \times (0.12)^5
$$

$$
(13.33)^3 = 2369
$$

$$
(0.12)^5 = 2.488 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
P = 1.3 \times 2700 \times 2369 \times 2.488 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
P = 1.3 \times 2700 \times 0.0589 = 1.3 \times 159.0 = 206.7 \;\; \text{W}
$$

**Comment on Flow Regime (1 mark):**
Since $N_{Re} \approx 4 \times 10^5 > 10^4$, the flow is in the **turbulent regime**, which is ideal for uniform particle dispersion.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rev/s** when using N in power/Reynolds formulas → loses 1 mark per occurrence.
> 2. **Mixing up μ (dynamic) and ν (kinematic) viscosity** in Stokes' law — always use dynamic viscosity in Pa·s.
> 3. **Using $N_p$ as 1.0** (a common default) instead of the impeller-specific value (1.3 for pitched-blade, 5.5 for Rushton turbine) → loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Ignoring the Stokes regime check:** Stokes' law is only valid for particle Reynolds number < 1. Always state this assumption.
> 5. **Failing to mention Mg addition** while answering Al/SiC questions — this is a **favourite KTU question** and missing it costs full 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Not drawing a labelled schematic** in 7-mark questions — KTU examiners typically allocate 2 marks for the diagram alone.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Melt stirring** is a **liquid-state, stir-casting route** for particulate MMCs — the most economical industrial method.
- **Three core challenges:** Settling (gravity), Wettability (interfacial), and Clustering (inter-particle) — all must be balanced.
- **Mg addition (1–4 wt%)** is the **standard industrial fix** for poor Al–SiC wettability.
- **Stirrer speed window: 500–700 rpm** for typical Al melts — too low → poor mixing; too high → gas pickup.
- **Stokes' Law:** $V_s = \dfrac{d^2 g (\rho_p - \rho_m)}{18 \mu}$ — settling velocity scales with **d²**, hence fine particles (< 20 μm) are preferred.
- **Power consumption** scales as **N³ D⁵** — doubling impeller diameter increases power by **32×**.
- **Reynolds number** for impeller: $N_{Re} = D^2 N \rho / \mu$. Turbulent regime ($N_{Re} > 10^4$) is ideal for dispersion.
- **Froude number** must be **< 0.3** to avoid air entrainment from vortex.
- **Pre-heating of reinforcement** to 400 °C is **mandatory** to drive off moisture (otherwise → porosity from steam).
- **Inert atmosphere (Ar)** is essential to prevent oxide formation in Mg-bearing Al melts.
- **Industrial applications:** Toyota pistons, Honda engine blocks, aerospace rotor housings, electronic heat sinks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Squeeze Casting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET413 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Squeeze Casting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Squeeze Casting** is a hybrid metal forming process that integrates the characteristics of **gravity die casting** and **closed-die forging**. In this process, a measured quantity of **molten metal** (or a metal-ceramic slurry, in the case of Metal Matrix Composites) is poured into a **preheated, lubricated die cavity** and subsequently solidified under the application of sustained **high mechanical pressure (typically 50–150 MPa)** applied by a direct hydraulic ram until complete solidification occurs.

According to the KTU PEMET413 syllabus, squeeze casting is specifically highlighted as a **primary liquid-state fabrication route for Metal Matrix Composites (MMCs)**, wherein reinforcement particulates or short fibers are incorporated into the molten melt before the pressure is applied. The process is often referred to in literature as **"Liquid Metal Forging"** or **"Squeeze Forming."**

### Key Engineering Parameters (Standard Metrics)

- **Applied Pressure (P):** 50 to 150 MPa (typically 100 MPa for MMCs)
- **Die Temperature (Td):** 200 °C to 350 °C
- **Melt/Pour Temperature (Tm):** 50 °C to 150 °C above the liquidus
- **Dwell/Pressing Time (td):** 30 to 90 seconds
- **Clearance/Gap:** < 0.5 mm at die parting line
- **Density Achieved:** ~ 95 to 99 % of theoretical density

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine making a sandwich using a heavy-duty panini press. You place the *molten cheese and bread* (liquid melt) into a *preheated lower mold* (die). Instead of just closing the lid gently, you press down with a *massive hydraulic ram* (high pressure) and *hold it there* until the sandwich cools and solidifies. The pressure squeezes out any air gaps (porosity) and forces the material to conform perfectly to the shape of the press. The result is a dense, defect-free "casting-forging hybrid" part with properties superior to either method used alone.

For composite materials, the analogy extends: think of the molten metal as a "carrier liquid" and the ceramic particles (e.g., SiC, Al₂O₃) as the "solid spices." Squeeze casting forces these spices to be evenly distributed and tightly packed before the metal freezes around them, producing a uniform MMC.

### Why Use Squeeze Casting for MMCs?

| Motivation | Engineering Benefit |
|---|---|
| Eliminates gas porosity | Pressure collapses gas pockets during solidification |
| Reduces shrinkage voids | Continuous feeding under pressure compensates for solidification shrinkage |
| Improves fiber/matrix bonding | Pressure enhances interfacial contact and wettability |
| Produces near-net shape | Minimal secondary machining required |
| Faster production | Cycle times typically 60–180 seconds |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — The "Why" and "How"

The squeeze casting process exploits **three coupled physical phenomena** simultaneously:

1. **Mechanical compaction** — pressure drives plastic deformation of the solidifying shell.
2. **Thermal solidification** — heat is extracted through the die walls.
3. **Interfacial chemistry** — at the reinforcement/matrix interface, reactions may form brittle phases (e.g., Al₄C₃ in Al-SiC systems).

### Step-by-Step Logic of the Process

1. **Preheating the Die:** The die is preheated (typically 200–350 °C) to:
   - Reduce thermal shock
   - Minimize premature freezing of the melt
   - Improve die life by limiting thermal fatigue

2. **Lubrication:** A thin film of graphite or boron nitride is applied to die surfaces to prevent soldering and ease part ejection.

3. **Metal/Reinforcement Preparation:**
   - For unreinforced alloys: melt is degassed and brought to pour temperature.
   - For MMCs: ceramic reinforcements (SiC, Al₂O₃, TiC) are pre-treated, pre-heated, and mechanically/vortex-stirred into the melt, or pre-placed in the die as a **preform**.

4. **Pouring:** The molten metal (or MMC slurry) is poured into the lower die half. Pouring must be **quiescent** to avoid entrapment of oxides and air.

5. **Application of Pressure:** The upper punch descends and applies pressure (50–150 MPa) within 1–2 seconds. This pressure is **maintained throughout solidification.**

6. **Solidification Under Pressure:** Heat flows from the melt to the die. Pressure forces liquid metal to feed solidification shrinkage, eliminating porosity.

7. **Ejection:** After dwell time, the punch retracts, the die opens, and the part is ejected.

8. **Trimming:** Excess flash at the die parting line is removed (a hallmark of forging-type processes).

## 2.2 Classification of Squeeze Casting

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU examiners frequently test this classification. Memorize the diagram in Section 4.**

### A. Direct Squeeze Casting (Vertical / Horizontal)
- Melt is poured **directly into the die cavity**.
- Pressure is applied by a punch in direct contact with the melt.
- **Advantages:** High pressure accuracy, better for MMCs.
- **Disadvantages:** Slow cycle time, melt handling complexity.

### B. Indirect Squeeze Casting
- Melt is first introduced into a **shot sleeve**, then injected into the die through a gate.
- Pressure is applied by a separate plunger.
- Combines benefits of high-pressure die casting and squeeze casting.
- Suitable for thin-walled and complex parts.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-relevant** and frequently appear in KTU Module 3 derivations and numerical problems.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Variables | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$P_{applied} = \dfrac{4 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{d}$$ | Minimum pressure to overcome capillary resistance for infiltration; $\gamma_{LV}$ = liquid-vapor surface energy, $\theta$ = contact angle, $d$ = fiber diameter | Determines threshold pressure for MMC infiltration |
| 2 | $$\dfrac{dL}{dt} = \dfrac{K \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$$ | **Darcy's Law** for preform infiltration; $L$ = infiltration length, $K$ = permeability, $A$ = cross-section area, $\mu$ = melt viscosity | Predicts infiltration kinetics in squeeze-cast MMCs |
| 3 | $$P_{effective} = P_{applied} - P_{capillary} - P_{friction}$$ | Net pressure available for compaction | Process design |
| 4 | $$q = h \cdot A \cdot (T_{melt} - T_{die})$$ | Heat transfer rate from melt to die; $h$ = interfacial heat transfer coefficient | Die design and cycle time estimation |
| 5 | $$\Delta V_{shrinkage} = V_{liquid} \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T_{f}$$ | Volume contraction during solidification feeding; $\beta$ = volumetric solidification shrinkage coefficient (Al ≈ 6.5 %) | Calculates pressure required to feed shrinkage |
| 6 | $$N_{Re} = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu}$$ | Reynolds number for melt flow; $\rho$ = density, $v$ = velocity, $L$ = characteristic length | Determines flow regime (laminar/turbulent) during infiltration |
| 7 | $$\sigma_{yield} = \sigma_{0} + K \cdot \varepsilon^{n}$$ | Hollomon equation for plastic deformation under pressure during solidification | Predicts forging pressure required |
| 8 | $$t_{solidification} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot H \cdot V}{h \cdot A \cdot (T_m - T_d)}$$ | Chvorinov's rule (modified) for solidification time under pressure; $H$ = latent heat | Dwell time calculation |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Aluminum MMC brake rotors, pistons, cylinder liners (Toyota, Honda, Ford).
- **Aerospace:** Turbine blade preforms, structural brackets.
- **Defense:** Armor inserts, rocket nozzle components.
- **Electronics:** Heat sinks with high thermal conductivity (e.g., Al/SiC).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Threshold Pressure for Preform Infiltration

In squeeze casting of MMCs, the molten metal must infiltrate a porous ceramic **preform** (a bed of reinforcement fibers/particles). The applied pressure must overcome both the **capillary pressure** (which opposes entry) and provide the **driving force for flow**.

### Starting Point — Young-Laplace Equation

For a cylindrical pore of diameter $d$, the pressure difference required to force a non-wetting or partially wetting liquid through a capillary is given by:

$$
P_{cap} = \dfrac{4 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{d}
$$

**Step 1 — Apply Darcy's Law for Infiltration Kinetics**

The volumetric flow rate $Q$ of melt through the preform follows Darcy's law:

$$
Q = \dfrac{K \cdot A}{\mu} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{L}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert Volumetric Flow to Infiltration Velocity**

The infiltration length $L$ grows with time $t$ as $L(t) = \dfrac{Q \cdot t}{A \cdot \varepsilon}$, where $\varepsilon$ is the preform porosity. Differentiating with respect to $t$:

$$
\dfrac{dL}{dt} = \dfrac{K \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L \cdot \varepsilon}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Effective Pressure**

The effective pressure driving infiltration is the applied pressure minus capillary resistance:

$$
\Delta P_{eff} = P_{applied} - \dfrac{4 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{d} - \Delta P_{friction}
$$

Ignoring friction (a common assumption for short, open preforms):

$$
\dfrac{dL}{dt} = \dfrac{K}{\mu \cdot L \cdot \varepsilon} \left( P_{applied} - \dfrac{4 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{d} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate to Obtain Infiltration Length vs. Time**

Separating variables and integrating from $L = 0$ at $t = 0$ to $L$ at time $t$:

$$
\int_{0}^{L} L \cdot dL = \int_{0}^{t} \dfrac{K}{\mu \cdot \varepsilon} \left( P_{applied} - \dfrac{4 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{d} \right) dt
$$

$$
\dfrac{L^{2}}{2} = \dfrac{K \cdot t}{\mu \cdot \varepsilon} \left( P_{applied} - \dfrac{4 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{d} \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Final Result**

$$
\boxed{L^{2} = \dfrac{2 K \cdot t}{\mu \cdot \varepsilon} \left( P_{applied} - \dfrac{4 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{d} \right)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **square-root-of-time** law is the foundation for understanding infiltration in squeeze casting. The KTU board loves to derive this and ask for the threshold pressure.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Pressure Required to Feed Solidification Shrinkage

During solidification, the volume of a metal contracts. Without compensation, shrinkage cavities form. Under pressure, liquid metal flows to feed the contracting region.

**Step 1 — Express Volumetric Shrinkage**

For a casting of liquid volume $V_l$ solidifying with a volumetric shrinkage coefficient $\beta$ (for pure Al, $\beta \approx 0.065$):

$$
\Delta V = \beta \cdot V_l
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Continuity Equation for Liquid Flow**

The rate of liquid flow feeding the solidification front must equal the rate of volume contraction:

$$
Q_{feed} = \dfrac{dV}{dt} = \beta \cdot \dfrac{dV_l}{dt}
$$

**Step 3 — Relate Flow Rate to Pressure Using Hagen-Poiseuille Law**

For flow through interdendritic channels of equivalent diameter $d_c$ and length $L$:

$$
Q_{feed} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_c^{4}}{128 \cdot \mu \cdot L} \cdot \Delta P_{feed}
$$

**Step 4 — Equate the Two Expressions**

$$
\dfrac{\pi \cdot d_c^{4}}{128 \cdot \mu \cdot L} \cdot \Delta P_{feed} = \beta \cdot \dfrac{dV_l}{dt}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Feeding Pressure**

$$
\boxed{\Delta P_{feed} = \dfrac{128 \cdot \mu \cdot L \cdot \beta}{\pi \cdot d_c^{4}} \cdot \dfrac{dV_l}{dt}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This shows that as the dendrite arm spacing $d_c$ **decreases** (faster cooling, finer microstructure), the required feeding pressure **increases sharply** (4th-power dependence). This is why squeeze casting works best with **moderate cooling rates** to keep channels open for feeding.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effects of Process Parameters

| Parameter | Effect on Porosity | Effect on Grain Size | Effect on Strength | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Applied Pressure | ↓↓ Strongly reduces | ↓ Slightly refines | ↑↑ Significantly increases | Higher die cost, machine tonnage |
| ↑ Die Temperature | ↑ Increases | ↑ Coarsens | ↓ Slightly decreases | Longer cycle time, better die life |
| ↑ Pour Temperature | ↑ Increases (gas pickup) | ↑ Coarsens | ↓ Slightly decreases | Better fluidity, more energy |
| ↑ Dwell Time | ↓ Reduces | ↑ Coarsens | ↑ Slightly increases | Lower productivity |
| ↓ Preform Permeability K | ↑ Incomplete infiltration | — | ↓ Sharp decrease in MMC | Need higher P |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Block Diagram (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The diagram below maps the **functional architecture** of a typical vertical direct squeeze casting operation, illustrating component interactions and material flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Preform Preparation: SiC or Al2O3 reinforcement] --> B[Die Preheating: 200-350 C]
    B --> C[Lubricant Application: Graphite or BN]
    C --> D[Preform Placement in Lower Die]
    D --> E[Melting and Degassing: Matrix Alloy 700-800 C]
    E --> F[Pouring of Molten Metal onto Preform]
    F --> G[Upper Punch Descent: 1-2 seconds]
    G --> H[Application of Pressure: 50-150 MPa]
    H --> I[Solidification Under Pressure: Dwell 30-90 s]
    I --> J[Heat Extraction to Die Walls]
    I --> K[Feeding of Shrinkage by Liquid Flow]
    I --> L[Plastic Deformation of Solid Shell]
    I --> M[Interfacial Bonding: Matrix-Reinforcement]
    J --> N[Part Solidified]
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Punch Retraction]
    O --> P[Die Opening and Part Ejection]
    P --> Q[Flash Trimming]
    Q --> R[Final MMC Component: 95-99 percent density]
    R --> S[Quality Inspection: NDT, Density, Microstructure]
```

## 4.2 Comparison Block Diagram: Direct vs. Indirect Squeeze Casting

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DirectProcess[Direct Squeeze Casting]
        A1[Pour Melt into Die] --> A2[Upper Punch Contacts Melt Directly]
        A2 --> A3[High Pressure Applied: 100 MPa]
        A3 --> A4[Solidify Under Load]
    end
    subgraph IndirectProcess[Indirect Squeeze Casting]
        B1[Pour Melt into Shot Sleeve] --> B2[Plunger Injects Melt via Gate]
        B2 --> B3[Die Cavity Fills Under Pressure]
        B3 --> B4[Intensifier Boosts Pressure to 100 MPa]
        B4 --> B5[Solidify Under Load]
    end
    DirectProcess --> Outcome[Near Net Shape MMC Part]
    IndirectProcess --> Outcome
```

## 4.3 Schematic Representation (Text-Based Schematic of Equipment)

> [!NOTE]
> The following ASCII schematic visualizes the die-punch assembly for vertical direct squeeze casting.

```
                ↑ Hydraulic Ram
                |
            [   |   ]   ← Upper Punch
            [   |   ]
        =====================  ← Upper Die Half
        |                   |
        |   * * * * * * *   |   ← Preform (ceramic reinforcement)
        |   * * * * * * *   |
        |   ~~~~~~~~~~~~~   |   ← Molten Metal (matrix)

        =====================  ← Lower Die Half

        |___[Ejector Pin]___|   ← Ejection System
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define squeeze casting. List any three advantages of squeeze casting over conventional gravity die casting.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Squeeze casting is a hybrid metal forming process in which molten metal is poured into a closed die and solidified under sustained high mechanical pressure (50–150 MPa) applied by a hydraulic ram, combining features of casting and forging.

**Three Advantages:**

1. **Reduced Porosity:** Applied pressure eliminates gas porosity and shrinkage voids, yielding parts with density 95–99 % of theoretical, far superior to gravity casting (~85–90 %).

2. **Superior Mechanical Properties:** Due to refined microstructure, absence of defects, and improved interfacial bonding, squeeze-cast parts exhibit higher tensile strength, fatigue life, and ductility.

3. **Near-Net Shape Capability:** Tight dimensional tolerances (±0.05 mm) and excellent surface finish (Ra 1–3 μm) reduce or eliminate secondary machining, saving cost and material.

*(Other valid advantages: shorter solidification time, better fiber-matrix bonding in MMCs, ability to cast MMCs with low wettability reinforcements.)* **[3 Marks]**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] What is a preform in squeeze casting of MMCs? State its function.**

**Model Answer:**

A **preform** is a porous, shaped body made of ceramic reinforcement (typically SiC, Al₂O₃, or carbon fibers) prepared by pressing and sintering or by binder-bonding. It is pre-heated and placed in the die cavity before the molten metal is poured.

**Functions of the Preform:**

1. **Defines reinforcement architecture:** Controls the volume fraction, distribution, and orientation of reinforcement in the final MMC.
2. **Provides mechanical stability:** Holds its shape during melt infiltration.
3. **Enables selective reinforcement:** Areas requiring higher strength (e.g., wear surfaces) can have localized preforms, saving cost.
4. **Facilitates infiltration kinetics:** A permeable, pre-shaped network allows molten metal to flow in under pressure.

**[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B Module questions carry **14 marks**, typically split into two 7-mark sub-parts. **Both Question A and Question B are provided below; students answer either A or B.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the **direct squeeze casting process** for manufacturing a metal matrix composite. Discuss the role of applied pressure, die preheating temperature, and dwell time on the quality of the final MMC component.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **threshold pressure equation** for infiltration of molten metal into a ceramic preform. Discuss how the contact angle and reinforcement size affect the threshold pressure.

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Direct Squeeze Casting Process for MMCs:**

The direct squeeze casting process involves the following sequential steps:

1. **Preform Preparation:** A porous ceramic preform (e.g., 20 vol% SiC particles) is prepared by pressing and sintering. It is pre-heated to 600 °C to avoid thermal shock.

2. **Die Preparation:** The die (typically H13 tool steel) is pre-heated to 250–350 °C using embedded heaters and lubricated with a thin graphite layer.

3. **Melt Preparation:** The matrix alloy (e.g., A356 Al) is melted at 720 °C, degassed with argon, and stirred if needed for particle incorporation. The melt temperature is brought to 50–100 °C above liquidus.

4. **Pouring:** The melt is poured **quiescently** over the preform placed in the lower die cavity.

5. **Application of Pressure:** The upper punch descends in 1–2 s and applies 100 MPa pressure. The pressure is maintained throughout solidification.

6. **Solidification and Dwell:** The part solidifies under load over 30–90 s. Pressure feeds shrinkage, eliminates porosity, and enhances interfacial bonding.

7. **Ejection and Trimming:** Punch retracts, die opens, ejector pins push the part out, and excess flash is trimmed.

**Role of Key Parameters:**

- **Applied Pressure (P):** Higher pressure → lower porosity, finer grains, better MMC bonding. However, excessive pressure causes die wear and machine overload.
- **Die Preheating Temperature (Td):** Higher Td → slower cooling → coarser grains, longer cycle time, but better die life and reduced thermal fatigue.
- **Dwell Time (td):** Adequate dwell ensures complete solidification and shrinkage feeding. Insufficient dwell causes internal voids.

**Neat Sketch:** (Refer to the schematic in Section 4.3 of these notes.)

**[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Derivation of Threshold Pressure:**

The minimum applied pressure required to initiate infiltration of molten metal into a preform pore of diameter $d$ is derived from a **force balance** at the melt-vapor interface.

**Step 1 — Pressure drop across a curved meniscus:** For a non-wetting or partially wetting liquid, the pressure inside the pore is lower than the outside by the Young-Laplace pressure:

$$
P_{outside} - P_{inside} = \dfrac{2 \gamma_{LV}}{r}
$$

where $r$ is the meniscus radius of curvature. For a cylindrical pore of diameter $d$ with contact angle $\theta$, geometry gives $r = d / (2 \cos\theta)$. Substituting:

$$
P_{outside} - P_{inside} = \dfrac{4 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{d}
$$

**Step 2 — Threshold condition:** Infiltration begins when the **applied pressure** equals or exceeds this capillary pressure difference:

$$
\boxed{P_{threshold} = \dfrac{4 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{d}}
$$

**Step 3 — Discussion of Influencing Factors:**

- **Contact Angle ($\theta$):** A **lower contact angle** (better wettability, e.g., $\theta < 90°$) reduces $P_{threshold}$ because $\cos\theta$ becomes more positive. Most metal-ceramic systems are non-wetting ($\theta > 90°$), making infiltration difficult without pressure. Surface coatings (e.g., Ni on SiC) or alloying (Mg to Al) reduce $\theta$.

- **Reinforcement Size ($d$):** A **smaller pore/fiber diameter** **increases** $P_{threshold}$ inversely. Hence, fine reinforcements (e.g., sub-micron fibers) require extremely high pressures, often beyond practical limits. Coarser reinforcements are easier to infiltrate.

**Example:** For Al on SiC: $\gamma_{LV} \approx 0.85$ N/m, $\theta \approx 140°$, $d = 10 \mu m = 10^{-5}$ m:
- $\cos 140° \approx -0.766$
- $P_{threshold} = (4 \times 0.85 \times (-0.766)) / 10^{-5} = -260,000$ Pa $\approx -0.26$ MPa (Negative means the melt is **repelled**; applied pressure must exceed the **magnitude**).

Magnitude: $\vert P_{threshold} \vert \approx 0.26$ MPa, easily overcome by 100 MPa applied pressure.

**[7 Marks]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **direct squeeze casting** and **indirect squeeze casting** with respect to their process steps, pressure application, cycle time, and suitability for MMC production. Use a comparative table.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A ceramic preform of porosity $\varepsilon = 0.4$ and permeability $K = 10^{-10}$ m² is to be infiltrated by molten aluminum ($\mu = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $\gamma_{LV} = 0.85$ N/m, $\theta = 135°$, fiber diameter $d = 15 \mu m$). If the applied pressure is 100 MPa, calculate the time required to infiltrate a depth of $L = 5$ cm. Use the square-root-of-time infiltration law.

---

#### **Model Solution (a):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Direct Squeeze Casting | Indirect Squeeze Casting |
|---|---|---|
| **Melt handling** | Poured directly into die cavity | Poured into a separate shot sleeve |
| **Pressure source** | Direct hydraulic ram on melt | Plunger in shot sleeve + intensifier |
| **Pressure level** | Up to 150 MPa (very high) | 50–100 MPa (limited by intensifier) |
| **Cycle time** | 2–4 minutes (slow) | 30–90 seconds (faster) |
| **Part complexity** | Limited to simple, bulky shapes | Suitable for complex, thin-walled parts |
| **Suitability for MMC** | Excellent — preform stays intact | Moderate — preform may be damaged by gate shear |
| **Porosity in part** | Very low (< 1 %) | Low (1–3 %) |
| **Die life** | Long (gentler thermal cycles) | Shorter (high injection velocities) |
| **Capital cost** | High (tonnage press) | Moderate (modified HPDC machine) |
| **Typical applications** | MMC pistons, brake rotors | Automotive structural parts |

**Conclusion:** For high-quality MMC components with maximum density and superior mechanical properties, **direct squeeze casting** is preferred despite its longer cycle time. For high-volume production of complex geometry parts, **indirect squeeze casting** offers better economics.

**[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution (b):**

**Given Data:**
- $\varepsilon = 0.4$
- $K = 10^{-10}$ m²
- $\mu = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- $\gamma_{LV} = 0.85$ N/m
- $\theta = 135°$ → $\cos 135° = -0.7071$
- $d = 15 \mu m = 15 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $P_{applied} = 100 \times 10^{6}$ Pa
- $L = 0.05$ m

**Step 1 — Calculate Threshold Pressure**

$$
P_{threshold} = \dfrac{4 \times 0.85 \times \cos(135°)}{15 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
P_{threshold} = \dfrac{4 \times 0.85 \times (-0.7071)}{15 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
P_{threshold} = \dfrac{-2.404}{15 \times 10^{-6}} = -160,267 \text{ Pa} \approx -0.1603 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Net Effective Pressure (Take magnitudes for driving force)**

$$
P_{eff} = P_{applied} - \vert P_{threshold} \vert = 100 \text{ MPa} - 0.1603 \text{ MPa} \approx 99.84 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
P_{eff} = 99.84 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Square-Root-of-Time Law**

$$
L^{2} = \dfrac{2 K \cdot t}{\mu \cdot \varepsilon} \cdot P_{eff}
$$

Solve for $t$:

$$
t = \dfrac{L^{2} \cdot \mu \cdot \varepsilon}{2 K \cdot P_{eff}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Numerical Values**

$$
t = \dfrac{(0.05)^{2} \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.4}{2 \times 10^{-10} \times 99.84 \times 10^{6}}
$$

Numerator:

$$
(0.0025) \times (1.2 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.4 = 1.2 \times 10^{-6}
$$

Denominator:

$$
2 \times 10^{-10} \times 99.84 \times 10^{6} = 19.968 \times 10^{-4} = 1.9968 \times 10^{-3}
$$

Therefore:

$$
t = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{-6}}{1.9968 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.01 \times 10^{-4} \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer**

$$
\boxed{t \approx 0.6 \text{ ms}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The result is extremely small because the applied pressure (100 MPa) is orders of magnitude larger than the threshold pressure (~0.16 MPa). In real practice, infiltration times of 0.5–2 seconds are common due to frictional losses, finite cooling, and non-ideal wetting — but this calculation shows the **theoretical kinetic limit** and the dominant role of high applied pressure.

**[7 Marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in threshold pressure:** Many students forget that $\cos\theta$ is **negative** for non-wetting systems ($\theta > 90°$). Always take the **magnitude** to find the pressure that must be overcome.
> 2. **Units mismatch:** Infiltration problems demand SI units throughout (Pa, m, s). Converting $\mu m$ to m and MPa to Pa is the single most common error costing 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Skipping assumptions:** If you ignore friction in the derivation, **state it explicitly**. KTU examiners award marks for justified assumptions.
> 4. **Confusing direct vs. indirect:** Direct = melt in die; Indirect = melt in shot sleeve. Don't mix them up in comparative answers.
> 5. **Forgetting die preheating purpose:** Examiners frequently ask *why* dies are preheated. Mention thermal shock reduction, slower cooling, and die life extension.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- ✅ **Squeeze casting = Casting + Forging hybrid**; pressure (50–150 MPa) is applied during solidification.
- ✅ **Primary MMC fabrication route** for liquid-state processing in PEMET413 Module 3.
- ✅ **Two types:** Direct (high pressure, slow, ideal for MMC) and Indirect (faster, complex shapes, modified HPDC).
- ✅ **Process parameters:** Pressure ↑ → porosity ↓, strength ↑. Die temp ↑ → grain size ↑, cycle time ↑.
- ✅ **Preform:** Porous ceramic body pre-placed in die; defines reinforcement architecture.
- ✅ **Threshold pressure equation:** $P_{th} = \dfrac{4 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{d}$ — capillary resistance to infiltration.
- ✅ **Infiltration law (square-root-of-time):** $L^{2} = \dfrac{2 K t}{\mu \varepsilon} P_{eff}$ — foundation for process design.
- ✅ **Shrinkage feeding:** Pressure feeds solidification contraction; $\Delta V = \beta V_l$.
- ✅ **Hagen-Poiseuille dependence on $d_c^4$:** Finer dendrites → exponentially higher feeding pressure needed.
- ✅ **Density achieved:** 95–99 % theoretical (vs. 85–90 % in gravity casting).
- ✅ **Real-world applications:** MMC pistons (Toyota), brake rotors, aerospace brackets, heat sinks (Al/SiC).
- ✅ **Common MMC systems:** Al/SiC, Al/Al₂O₃, Mg/SiC, Cu/Al₂O₃.
- ✅ **Advantage over stir casting:** No gas entrapment, no vortex-related defects, lower porosity, better fiber integrity.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** High tonnage press, limited part geometry (especially direct), die wear, high capital cost.
- ✅ **Si units always** in numerical problems: convert $\mu m \to m$, MPa $\to$ Pa before substituting.

---

**End of Module 3 Topic Notes — Squeeze Casting**
