missing it costs up to 2 marks. --- ## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print this and revise the night before the exam!** - [x] **Reaction Bonding** = *in-situ* ceramic matrix formation via chemical reaction + infiltration/nitridation. - [x] **RBSiC reaction:** $Si_{(l)} + C_{(s)} \rightarrow SiC_{(s)}$ at 1450 – 1600 °C. - [x] **RBSN reaction:** $3 Si_{(s)} + 2 N_{2(g)} \rightarrow Si_3 N_4(s)$ at 1200 – 1400 °C. - [x] **Driving force for infiltration** = capillary suction → $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\gamma_{lv}\cos\theta}{r_{eff}}$. - [x] **Si contact angle on C** ≈ **30° – 40°** → strong wetting. - [x] **Stoichiometric mass fraction of Si in SiC** = **0.7004** (70.04 %). - [x] **Required carbon preform porosity** ≈ **28 %** for stoichiometric filling. - [x] **Typical RBSiC composition:** 80 – 90 % SiC + 5 – 15 % free Si + 2 – 5 % porosity. - [x] **Typical RBSN composition:** 75 – 85 % Si₃N₄ + 15 – 25 % porosity (no free Si). - [x] **Linear shrinkage** of RBSiC < **1 %**; sintered SiC ≈ 15 – 25 %. - [x] **Free-Si limit:** RBSiC usable only up to **~ 1350 °C** (Si melting) unless Si is removed. - [x] **RBSN exothermic runaway** controlled by slow ramp / pre-oxidation / N₂ dilution. - [x] **Density relation:** $\rho_{RB} = \sum V_i \rho_i$ (volume-weighted rule of mixtures). - [x] **Porosity formula:** $P = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_{RB}}{\rho_{th}}$. - [x] **Key applications:** brake discs, armor, rocket nozzles, heat exchangers, nuclear first walls, pump seals. - [x] **Key advantage:** near-net-shape, large parts, low cost. - [x] **Key disadvantage:** residual free-Si (RBSiC) or residual porosity (RBSN) — limits high-T strength. - [x] **Densities to memorize:** $\rho_{SiC} = 3.21$, $\rho_{Si} = 2.33$, $\rho_{Si_3N_4} = 3.18$, $\rho_C = 2.26$ (all in g/cm³). --- *End of Module 4 — Reaction Bonding Notes* *PEMET413 | KTU 2024 Scheme*

topics.title: Liquid Infiltration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET413 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Liquid Infiltration ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Liquid Infiltration** is a primary liquid-state fabrication route for **Metal Matrix Composites (MMC)** and **Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMC)**, in which a molten matrix (typically a light metal alloy such as **Al**, **Mg**, or **Cu**, or a ceramic melt) is introduced into the interconnected pore network of a porous reinforcement preform (fibres, whiskers, or particles) under the action of capillary forces, external mechanical pressure, vacuum, or a combination of these, followed by **in-situ solidification** to yield a dense, near-net-shape composite. The process is governed by the thermodynamic criterion of **wettability** between the liquid and the solid reinforcement, quantified by the **contact angle ($\theta$)** and the **work of adhesion ($W_a$)**. ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine dipping a dry, porous terracotta flower pot into a bucket of coloured water. - **Capillary action** alone pulls the water up the tiny pores of the pot (this represents *spontaneous* or *pressureless* infiltration). - If the pot's pores are very fine or the water has high surface tension, it won't rise much — this is the *threshold pressure* problem. - To force the water deep inside, you could press the pot down (this is **squeeze casting / pressure infiltration**) or suck the air out from below (this is **vacuum infiltration**). - The water that gets trapped and solidifies inside the pot walls is analogous to the metal/ceramic matrix locked around the reinforcement, giving you a strong, dense composite "pot." In the lab, the "water" is molten aluminium around $700^{\circ}\text{C}$ and the "pot" is a fibrous preform of $\text{SiC}$ or $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$. ### 1.3 Classification of Liquid Infiltration Routes > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 4** explicitly focuses on processing of MMCs. The four canonical variants of liquid infiltration that examiners expect are summarised below. | **Variant** | **Driving Force** | **Typical Pressure** | **Application** | |---|---|---|---| | **Pressureless Infiltration** | Capillary forces only | $\approx 0$ (atmospheric) | Spontaneous wetting systems (e.g., $\text{Al}/\text{SiC}$ with Mg addition) | | **Vacuum Infiltration** | Atmospheric pressure (with internal vacuum) | $\approx 0.1\,\text{MPa}$ | Thin-walled, high-quality components | | **Gas-Pressure Infiltration** | Inert gas (Ar, N₂) pressurisation | $1 - 10\,\text{MPa}$ | Aerospace MMC components | | **Squeeze Casting (Forced)** | Mechanical punch / hydraulic ram | $50 - 150\,\text{MPa}$ | High-volume-fraction automotive parts | ### 1.4 Fundamental Physical Constants and Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > The following standard physical metrics and symbols are used throughout this module. Memorise them — they appear in nearly every numerical. - **Surface tension of liquid metal** ($\gamma_{LV}$): typically $0.5 - 1.0\,\text{N/m}$ for Al-alloys at $700^{\circ}\text{C}$ - **Contact angle** ($\theta$): thermodynamic indicator of wettability; $\theta < 90^{\circ}$ indicates good wetting - **Density of molten Al** ($\rho$): $\approx 2380\,\text{kg/m}^3$ at $750^{\circ}\text{C}$ - **Dynamic viscosity of liquid metal** ($\mu$): $\approx 1.2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa \cdot s}$ - **Preform permeability** ($k$): characteristic of pore structure; typically $10^{-13}$ to $10^{-10}\,\text{m}^2$ - **Porosity of preform** ($\epsilon$): volume fraction of void space; typically $0.3 - 0.6$ ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Wettability — The Thermodynamic Gatekeeper Whether a molten metal will spontaneously enter a ceramic preform depends on the **balance of interfacial energies** at the three-phase contact line. The **Young's equation** is the foundational relationship: $$\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta$$ where: - $\gamma_{SV}$ = solid–vapour interfacial energy ($\text{J/m}^2$ or $\text{N/m}$) - $\gamma_{SL}$ = solid–liquid interfacial energy - $\gamma_{LV}$ = liquid–vapour interfacial energy (= surface tension of the melt) - $\theta$ = equilibrium contact angle measured through the liquid The corresponding **Work of Adhesion** ($W_a$) between the liquid and the solid is: $$W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta) = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering insight:** If $W_a > 2\gamma_{LV}$, then $\cos\theta > 1$, which is physically impossible — meaning the melt **cannot** wet the solid at all. This is why reactive alloying elements (e.g., **Mg in Al alloys**) are added to chemically modify $\gamma_{SL}$ and improve wetting. ### 2.2 Capillary Pressure — The Driving "Pull" Inside Pores For a cylindrical pore of radius $r_p$, the **Young–Laplace pressure drop** across a curved liquid meniscus gives the capillary pressure: $$P_c = -\frac{2 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{r_p}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sign convention critical for KTU:** When $\theta < 90^{\circ}$, $\cos\theta > 0$, and $P_c$ is **negative** — meaning the liquid is *sucked* into the pore (spontaneous infiltration). When $\theta > 90^{\circ}$, $P_c$ is **positive** — the melt *resists* infiltration and external pressure is required. ### 2.3 Threshold Pressure for Forced Infiltration When the wetting condition is unfavourable, an external pressure $P_{th}$ must be applied to overcome the capillary back-pressure: $$P_{th} = -\frac{2 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{r_p} = \frac{2 \gamma_{LV} \vert\cos\theta\vert}{r_p} \quad \text{(for } \theta > 90^{\circ}\text{)}$$ ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the master equation set for Module 4 — Liquid Infiltration. Every derivation and problem below uses one of these.** | **#** | **Equation** | **Name** | **Variables / Units** | **Used For** | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta$ | Young's Equation | $\gamma$ in $\text{N/m}$; $\theta$ in degrees/rad | Wettability check | | 2 | $W_a = \gamma_{LV}(1 + \cos\theta)$ | Work of Adhesion | $W_a$ in $\text{J/m}^2$ | Bonding strength | | 3 | $P_c = -\dfrac{2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{r_p}$ | Young–Laplace Capillary Pressure | $P_c$ in $\text{Pa}$; $r_p$ in $\text{m}$ | Spontaneous infiltration force | | 4 | $P_{th} = \dfrac{2\gamma_{LV}\vert\cos\theta\vert}{r_p}$ | Threshold Pressure | $P_{th}$ in $\text{Pa}$ | Forced infiltration onset | | 5 | $L^2(t) = \dfrac{r_p \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{2\mu} \cdot t$ | Washburn Equation (parabolic) | $L$ in $\text{m}$; $t$ in $\text{s}$; $\mu$ in $\text{Pa \cdot s}$ | Infiltration depth vs. time | | 6 | $u = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot k}{\mu \epsilon}$ | Darcy's Law (seepage velocity) | $u$ in $\text{m/s}$; $k$ in $\text{m}^2$ | Forced infiltration flow | | 7 | $V_f = 1 - \epsilon$ | Fibre Volume Fraction | dimensionless | Final composite $V_f$ | | 8 | $\Delta P_{hyd} = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic Head | $\Delta P$ in $\text{Pa}$; $h$ in $\text{m}$ | Vertical infiltration bonus | | 9 | $t_{fill} = \dfrac{4\mu L^2 \epsilon}{r_p^2 \Delta P}$ | Capillary Fill Time | $t$ in $\text{s}$ | Process time estimation | | 10 | $\text{Ca} = \dfrac{\mu u}{\gamma_{LV}}$ | Capillary Number | dimensionless | Flow regime indicator | ### 2.5 The "Why & How" Behind Each Step — Operational Logic - **Step 1 — Preform Preparation:** Fibres/Particles are arranged into a porous preform, sometimes held by a fugitive binder that burns out. The pore structure controls $r_p$ and $\epsilon$. - **Step 2 — Melt Preparation:** The matrix alloy is melted in a crucible, often above its liquidus by $50 - 100^{\circ}\text{C}$ to ensure full fluidity, then degassed. - **Step 3 — Contact & Infiltration:** The melt contacts the preform. If wettable, capillary suction drives infiltration; if not, external pressure (mechanical or pneumatic) is applied. - **Step 4 — Solidification:** The system is held under pressure during solidification to prevent shrinkage porosity and gas evolution, ensuring a dense matrix. - **Step 5 — Ejection:** The finished composite is ejected, trimmed, and subjected to any required T6 heat-treatment. > [!NOTE] > **Real-world utility:** Liquid-infiltrated MMCs are used in **Toyota pistons** ($\text{Al}/\text{SiC}_p$), **Honda valve guides**, **aerospace structural brackets** ($\text{Al}/\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$), and **electronic heat sinks** ($\text{Cu}/\text{SiC}$). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Washburn Equation (Infiltration Depth vs. Time) **Starting premise:** Consider a single cylindrical pore of radius $r_p$ in the preform, with a liquid of viscosity $\mu$ being drawn in by capillary pressure $P_c$, opposed by viscous drag along the pore wall. **Step 1 — Force balance on a liquid column of length $L(t)$:** The driving capillary force is balanced by the viscous resistance (Poiseuille flow in a tube). $$\Delta P \cdot \pi r_p^2 = 8 \pi \mu L(t) \cdot \frac{dL}{dt}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the capillary pressure $\Delta P = \dfrac{2 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{r_p}$:** $$\left(\frac{2 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{r_p}\right) \pi r_p^2 = 8 \pi \mu L \cdot \frac{dL}{dt}$$ **Step 3 — Cancel $\pi$ and simplify:** $$2 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta \cdot r_p = 8 \mu L \cdot \frac{dL}{dt}$$ **Step 4 — Separate variables and integrate from $L=0$ at $t=0$ to $L$ at $t$:** $$\int_0^L L \, dL = \int_0^t \frac{r_p \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{4 \mu} \, dt$$ **Step 5 — Evaluate both integrals:** $$\frac{L^2}{2} = \frac{r_p \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{4 \mu} \cdot t$$ **Step 6 — Rearrange to obtain the classical Washburn equation:** $$\boxed{\,L^2(t) = \frac{r_p \, \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{2 \mu} \cdot t\,}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical meaning:** Infiltration depth grows as the **square root of time** (parabolic kinetics). Doubling the infiltration time only increases depth by $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$, not 2×. This is the most-tested equation in KTU module-4 problems. ### 3.2 Derivation of the Threshold Pressure from Capillary Equilibrium **Starting premise:** For non-wetting systems ($\theta > 90^{\circ}$), the liquid will not enter the pore unless the externally applied pressure $P_{app}$ exceeds the capillary back-pressure that opposes entry. **Step 1 — Capillary pressure at the meniscus (Young–Laplace):** $$P_c = -\frac{2 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{r_p} \quad \text{(negative for non-wetting)}$$ **Step 2 — For infiltration to proceed, the applied pressure must overcome this back-pressure:** $$P_{app} \geq \vert P_c \vert$$ **Step 3 — Therefore the threshold (minimum) pressure is:** $$\boxed{\,P_{th} = \frac{2 \gamma_{LV} \vert\cos\theta\vert}{r_p}\,}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute typical values for a $\text{SiC}_p / \text{Al}$ system:** - $\gamma_{LV} = 0.85\,\text{N/m}$ - $\theta = 130^{\circ} \Rightarrow \cos\theta = -0.643 \Rightarrow \vert\cos\theta\vert = 0.643$ - $r_p = 10\,\mu\text{m} = 10 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$ $$P_{th} = \frac{2 \times 0.85 \times 0.643}{10 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.093}{10^{-5}} = 1.093 \times 10^{5}\,\text{Pa} \approx 0.11\,\text{MPa}$$ > [!NOTE] > This low threshold explains why simple **vacuum infiltration** (drawing a $0.1\,\text{MPa}$ differential) is often sufficient for this system. For ceramic-fibre preforms with $r_p \sim 1\,\mu\text{m}$, the threshold jumps to $\approx 1.1\,\text{MPa}$, demanding pressure-assisted variants. ### 3.3 Derivation of Darcy's Law for Forced Infiltration **Starting premise:** When external pressure drives a liquid through a porous medium, the **seepage velocity** $u$ is proportional to the pressure gradient $\nabla P$ and inversely proportional to viscosity. **Step 1 — Empirically, for a 1-D channel of length $L$:** $$u = \frac{Q}{A} = \text{(constant)} \times \frac{\Delta P}{\mu L}$$ **Step 2 — Introduce the preform permeability $k$ (depends on pore geometry) and porosity $\epsilon$:** $$u = \frac{k}{\mu \epsilon} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L}$$ **Step 3 — For uniform cross-section, $L$ is fixed, so volumetric flux is:** $$\boxed{\,u = \frac{\Delta P \cdot k}{\mu \epsilon}\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering insight:** Squeeze casting typically uses $50 - 150\,\text{MPa}$ of mechanical pressure, easily giving $u \sim 0.1\,\text{m/s}$ in Al alloys, enabling fill times of a few seconds for components tens of millimetres thick. ### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style) > **Problem:** A cylindrical preform of $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ fibres has an average pore radius $r_p = 15\,\mu\text{m}$ and porosity $\epsilon = 0.45$. Molten aluminium ($\gamma_{LV} = 0.9\,\text{N/m}$, $\mu = 1.1 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa \cdot s}$, $\rho = 2400\,\text{kg/m}^3$) at $750^{\circ}\text{C}$ infiltrates the preform with a contact angle $\theta = 110^{\circ}$ under an applied gas pressure of $P_{app} = 0.5\,\text{MPa}$. > **(a)** Determine the threshold pressure and verify whether spontaneous infiltration is possible. > **(b)** Using Darcy's law, estimate the seepage velocity, and (c) the time to fill a $5\,\text{cm}$ thick preform. Take permeability $k = 1.2 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{m}^2$. #### Part (a) — Threshold Pressure & Wettability Check Given: $\theta = 110^{\circ} \Rightarrow \cos\theta = \cos(110^{\circ}) = -0.342$ $$P_{th} = \frac{2 \times 0.9 \times 0.342}{15 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.6156}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 4.104 \times 10^{4}\,\text{Pa} \approx 41\,\text{kPa}$$ $$\text{Applied pressure } P_{app} = 0.5\,\text{MPa} = 500\,\text{kPa} \gg P_{th} = 41\,\text{kPa}$$ Since $P_{app} > P_{th}$ and $\theta = 110^{\circ} > 90^{\circ}$, infiltration is **non-spontaneous** and requires **forced** (pressure-assisted) infiltration. #### Part (b) — Seepage Velocity via Darcy's Law Net driving pressure: $\Delta P = P_{app} - P_{th} = 500\,\text{kPa} - 41\,\text{kPa} = 459\,\text{kPa} = 4.59 \times 10^{5}\,\text{Pa}$ $$u = \frac{\Delta P \cdot k}{\mu \epsilon} = \frac{4.59 \times 10^{5} \times 1.2 \times 10^{-12}}{1.1 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.45}$$ $$u = \frac{5.508 \times 10^{-7}}{4.95 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.113 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m/s} \approx 1.11\,\text{mm/s}$$ #### Part (c) — Fill Time for 5 cm Preform $$t_{fill} = \frac{L}{u} = \frac{0.05}{1.113 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 44.9\,\text{s} \approx 45\,\text{s}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Exam takeaway:** Always (1) compute $P_{th}$ first to determine infiltration mode, (2) subtract it from $P_{app}$ to get *net* driving pressure, and (3) apply Darcy's law with the correct $\Delta P$, $k$, and $\epsilon$. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Process-Flow Block Diagram of Liquid Infiltration ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Preform Preparation
Fibre or Particle Bed] --> B[Binders Burnout
and Preheating] B --> C[Matrix Melting
in Crucible] C --> D{Wettability
Check theta} D -- theta less than 90 deg --> E[Spontaneous
Capillary Infiltration] D -- theta greater than 90 deg --> F[Pressure Assisted
Forced Infiltration] E --> G[Soaking Stage
Isothermal Hold] F --> G G --> H[Controlled
Solidification] H --> I[Ejection and
Trimming] I --> J[Optional T6
Heat Treatment] J --> K[Final MMC
Composite Part] ``` ### 4.2 Comparative Architecture of Infiltration Variants ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph VAR1 [Variant A: Pressureless] A1[Atmospheric Pressure] --> A2[Capillary Force Only] A2 --> A3[Reactive Wetting Systems] end subgraph VAR2 [Variant B: Vacuum] B1[Atmospheric Outside] --> B2[Vacuum Pump Below Preform] B2 --> B3[Differential Pressure 0.1 MPa] end subgraph VAR3 [Variant C: Gas Pressure] C1[Inert Gas Argon or N2] --> C2[Pressurised Chamber 1 to 10 MPa] C2 --> C3[Aerospace Grade MMC] end subgraph VAR4 [Variant D: Squeeze Casting] D1[Hydraulic Punch] --> D2[Direct Ram Pressure 50 to 150 MPa] D2 --> D3[High Vf Automotive Parts] end ``` ### 4.3 Wettability Decision Tree (Block Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Start: Contact Angle theta] --> P2{theta less than 90 deg} P2 -- Yes --> P3[Pcap is negative
Suction Force] P3 --> P4[Spontaneous Infiltration
Works] P2 -- No --> P5{Papp greater than Pth} P5 -- Yes --> P6[Forced Infiltration
Works with Pth] P5 -- No --> P7[Infiltration Fails
Stops at Surface] P4 --> P8[Final MMC] P6 --> P8 P7 --> P9[Defective Part
Rejected] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why block diagrams and not physical drawings?** A schematic of a squeeze-casting die or a vacuum chamber would require a photograph. The block-architecture diagrams above map the **decision logic, force flow, and parameter relationships** — which is exactly what KTU examiners expect students to be able to *draw and label* in the answer script. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024 Pattern]** **Q1. Define liquid infiltration in the context of composite manufacturing. Differentiate between pressureless and squeeze-casting infiltration.** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** Liquid infiltration is a liquid-state composite fabrication process in which a molten matrix (metal or ceramic) is introduced into a porous reinforcement preform, where it solidifies to form a dense MMC or CMC. - **Pressureless infiltration** relies solely on **capillary forces** (spontaneous, when $\theta < 90^{\circ}$), requires near-atmospheric conditions, and is suitable for reactive wetting systems (e.g., $\text{Al-Mg}/\text{SiO}_2$). - **Squeeze-casting infiltration** uses a **hydraulic ram** to apply $50 - 150\,\text{MPa}$ of mechanical pressure, forcing the melt into non-wetting preforms to give high fibre volume fraction and low porosity. **(2 marks for clear differentiation, 1 mark for the definition.)** --- **Q2. What is the contact angle, and why is it a critical parameter in liquid infiltration?** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** The **contact angle $\theta$** is the angle formed at the three-phase (solid–liquid–vapour) boundary, measured through the liquid. It is a direct measure of the wettability between the molten matrix and the solid reinforcement. It is critical because: 1. It determines the **sign and magnitude of capillary pressure** $P_c = -\dfrac{2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{r_p}$. 2. When $\theta < 90^{\circ}$, infiltration is spontaneous; when $\theta > 90^{\circ}$, external pressure is required. 3. Alloying additions (e.g., Mg in Al) are used specifically to reduce $\theta$ and improve wetting. **(1 mark definition + 2 marks for its critical role.)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – End Semester Evaluation (ESE) Pattern, Module 4]** --- #### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *(Choose A or B)* **Q.A.** *(a)* Derive the Washburn equation for liquid infiltration into a cylindrical pore and explain the physical significance of the parabolic dependence of infiltration depth on time. *(7 marks)* *(b)* A preform of $\text{SiC}$ particles has a mean pore radius $r_p = 12\,\mu\text{m}$. Molten aluminium with surface tension $\gamma_{LV} = 0.87\,\text{N/m}$ and contact angle $\theta = 120^{\circ}$ is used. Determine: - (i) The capillary pressure $P_c$ - (ii) The threshold pressure $P_{th}$ required for forced infiltration - (iii) Whether atmospheric pressure infiltration is feasible. *(7 marks)* #### **Model Solution — Q.A.(a)** **Starting from force balance on a cylindrical liquid column of length $L$ inside a pore of radius $r_p$:** $$\Delta P \cdot \pi r_p^2 = 8 \pi \mu L \frac{dL}{dt}$$ **Substituting the Laplace pressure $\Delta P = \dfrac{2 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{r_p}$:** $$2 \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta \cdot r_p = 8 \mu L \frac{dL}{dt}$$ **Separating variables and integrating from $L=0$ at $t=0$ to $L$ at $t$:** $$\int_0^L L\, dL = \int_0^t \frac{r_p \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{4 \mu}\, dt$$ $$\frac{L^2}{2} = \frac{r_p \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{4 \mu}\, t$$ $$\boxed{\,L^2(t) = \frac{r_p \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{2 \mu}\, t\,}$$ **Physical significance of $L \propto \sqrt{t}$:** - Infiltration depth grows as the **square root of time**, because as the column extends, viscous drag accumulates over a longer length, slowing the meniscus. - **Practical implication:** To double the infiltration depth, you must wait **four times** as long. This is why long preforms require either larger $r_p$ (coarser pores) or external pressure assistance. - It enables **process-time estimation** for industrial scale-up: knowing $r_p$, $\gamma_{LV}$, $\theta$, and $\mu$, the engineer can predict the time needed to infiltrate a part of any thickness $L$. *(Award: 4 marks for derivation steps, 3 marks for significance and engineering insight.)* --- #### **Model Solution — Q.A.(b)** **Given:** $r_p = 12\,\mu\text{m} = 12 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$, $\gamma_{LV} = 0.87\,\text{N/m}$, $\theta = 120^{\circ}$. **Step (i) — Capillary Pressure:** $$\cos(120^{\circ}) = -0.5$$ $$P_c = -\frac{2 \times 0.87 \times (-0.5)}{12 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.87}{12 \times 10^{-6}} = 7.25 \times 10^{4}\,\text{Pa} \approx 72.5\,\text{kPa}$$ **Step (ii) — Threshold Pressure:** $$P_{th} = \vert P_c \vert = 72.5\,\text{kPa}$$ **Step (iii) — Feasibility of Atmospheric Pressure Infiltration:** Standard atmospheric pressure available as differential = $101.3\,\text{kPa}$. Since $P_{atm} = 101.3\,\text{kPa} > P_{th} = 72.5\,\text{kPa}$, the **net driving pressure** is: $$\Delta P_{net} = 101.3 - 72.5 = 28.8\,\text{kPa} > 0$$ **Conclusion:** Atmospheric pressure (e.g., simple gravity or vacuum-assisted) infiltration is **just feasible**, but with a very thin margin. A vacuum-assisted setup is recommended for safety and reliability. *(Award: 3 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 2 marks for (iii) including a clear conclusion.)* --- #### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* **Q.B.** *(a)* Explain the four major variants of liquid infiltration with their typical operating pressures and applications. State the Darcy's law equation for forced infiltration and identify all variables. *(7 marks)* *(b)* In a squeeze-casting operation, molten Al ($\gamma_{LV} = 0.9\,\text{N/m}$, $\mu = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa \cdot s}$) at $720^{\circ}\text{C}$ infiltrates an $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ preform. The preform is $60\,\text{mm}$ thick with porosity $\epsilon = 0.40$ and permeability $k = 5 \times 10^{-13}\,\text{m}^2$. The applied pressure is $100\,\text{MPa}$ and the contact angle is $\theta = 115^{\circ}$, average pore radius $r_p = 8\,\mu\text{m}$. Calculate: - (i) The threshold pressure - (ii) The net driving pressure - (iii) The Darcy seepage velocity - (iv) The fill time. *(7 marks)* #### **Model Solution — Q.B.(a)** **The four variants are:** | **Variant** | **Pressure Range** | **Driving Force** | **Typical Use** | |---|---|---|---| | Pressureless | $0$ (atm) | Capillary | Reactive $\text{Al-Mg}/\text{SiO}_2$ systems | | Vacuum | $\approx 0.1\,\text{MPa}$ differential | Atmospheric + vacuum | Thin-walled tubes, filters | | Gas-pressure | $1 - 10\,\text{MPa}$ | Inert gas (Ar, N₂) | Aerospace MMC brackets | | Squeeze casting | $50 - 150\,\text{MPa}$ | Hydraulic ram | Automotive pistons, high $V_f$ parts | **Darcy's Law for forced infiltration:** $$u = \frac{\Delta P \cdot k}{\mu \epsilon}$$ where $u$ = seepage velocity ($\text{m/s}$), $\Delta P$ = net applied pressure ($\text{Pa}$), $k$ = preform permeability ($\text{m}^2$), $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of liquid ($\text{Pa \cdot s}$), $\epsilon$ = preform porosity (dimensionless). *(Award: 4 marks for variants table, 1 mark for Darcy's law, 2 marks for variable identification and explanation.)* --- #### **Model Solution — Q.B.(b)** **Given:** $\gamma_{LV} = 0.9\,\text{N/m}$, $\mu = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa \cdot s}$, $L = 60\,\text{mm} = 0.06\,\text{m}$, $\epsilon = 0.40$, $k = 5 \times 10^{-13}\,\text{m}^2$, $P_{app} = 100\,\text{MPa} = 10^{8}\,\text{Pa}$, $\theta = 115^{\circ}$, $r_p = 8 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}$. **Step (i) — Threshold Pressure:** $$\cos(115^{\circ}) = -0.4226$$ $$P_{th} = \frac{2 \times 0.9 \times 0.4226}{8 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.7607}{8 \times 10^{-6}} = 9.51 \times 10^{4}\,\text{Pa} \approx 95.1\,\text{kPa}$$ **Step (ii) — Net Driving Pressure:** $$\Delta P = P_{app} - P_{th} = 100 \times 10^{6} - 95.1 \times 10^{3} = 9.9905 \times 10^{7}\,\text{Pa} \approx 99.9\,\text{MPa}$$ (Note: $P_{th}$ is negligible compared to $P_{app}$.) **Step (iii) — Darcy Seepage Velocity:** $$u = \frac{\Delta P \cdot k}{\mu \epsilon} = \frac{9.9905 \times 10^{7} \times 5 \times 10^{-13}}{1.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.40}$$ $$u = \frac{4.995 \times 10^{-5}}{4.8 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.1041\,\text{m/s} \approx 104\,\text{mm/s}$$ **Step (iv) — Fill Time:** $$t_{fill} = \frac{L}{u} = \frac{0.06}{0.1041} \approx 0.576\,\text{s}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** A $60\,\text{mm}$ preform is fully infiltrated in **less than 0.6 seconds** under squeeze casting — this is the key reason squeeze casting is favoured for high-volume automotive production. *(Award: 2 marks each for steps (i), (ii), (iii), 1 mark for (iv) plus a brief engineering interpretation.)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks in Module 4:** > > 1. **Sign error in capillary pressure** — Forgetting the negative sign in $P_c = -\dfrac{2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{r_p}$. When $\theta < 90^{\circ}$, $P_c$ must be negative (suction), not positive. > 2. **Confusing $P_{th}$ with $P_c$** — Threshold pressure is the **magnitude** of capillary pressure needed to be overcome; the applied pressure must be $\geq P_{th}$ for non-wetting systems. > 3. **Using applied pressure directly in Darcy's law** — You must subtract $P_{th}$ from $P_{app}$ to get the **net** driving pressure $\Delta P$. > 4. **Mixing up permeability $k$ and porosity $\epsilon$** — In Darcy's law, $k$ is in the numerator (units $\text{m}^2$) and $\epsilon$ is in the denominator (dimensionless). > 5. **Forgetting to convert $\mu\text{m}$ to m** in the threshold pressure equation — A $10^6$ factor error is the most common numerical slip. > 6. **Writing Washburn as $L \propto t$ instead of $L^2 \propto t$** — Always show the squared relationship and explain its physical meaning. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Liquid Infiltration (Module 4)** - ✅ **Definition:** Liquid infiltration = molten matrix forced into a porous preform → solidifies to form MMC/CMC. - ✅ **Four variants:** Pressureless, Vacuum, Gas-Pressure, Squeeze Casting — know the pressure range of each. - ✅ **Wettability is governed by Young's equation:** $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$. - ✅ **Spontaneous infiltration occurs only when $\theta < 90^{\circ}$.** - ✅ **Capillary pressure:** $P_c = -\dfrac{2\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta}{r_p}$ — negative when wetting, positive when non-wetting. - ✅ **Threshold pressure:** $P_{th} = \dfrac{2\gamma_{LV}\vert\cos\theta\vert}{r_p}$ — minimum external pressure needed to drive non-wetting melt into the preform. - ✅ **Washburn equation:** $L^2 = \dfrac{r_p \gamma_{LV} \cos\theta}{2\mu}\, t$ — depth grows as $\sqrt{t}$. - ✅ **Darcy's law for forced infiltration:** $u = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot k}{\mu \epsilon}$ — always use the **net** $\Delta P$ after subtracting $P_{th}$. - ✅ **Alloying for wetting:** Mg in Al reduces $\theta$ on $\text{SiC}$ / $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ preforms. - ✅ **Squeeze casting ($50 - 150\,\text{MPa}$)** is used for high $V_f$ and non-wetting systems. - ✅ **Vacuum infiltration ($\approx 0.1\,\text{MPa}$)** is sufficient for many Al/SiC systems because $P_{th}$ is comparable. - ✅ **Industrial examples:** Toyota pistons ($\text{Al}/\text{SiC}_p$), Honda valve guides, aerospace MMC brackets. - ✅ **Always check units:** $r_p$ in metres, $\gamma_{LV}$ in $\text{N/m}$, $\mu$ in $\text{Pa \cdot s}$, $\Delta P$ in $\text{Pa}$.

topics.title: Pultrusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET413 - COMPOSITE MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pultrusion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Pultrusion

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Pultrusion** is a *continuous, automated, closed-mold manufacturing process* used to produce constant cross-sectional profile Fiber-Reinforced Polymer (FRP) composites. The word itself is a portmanteau of **"PULL" + "EXTRUSION"**, because the reinforcing fibers are *pulled* (rather than pushed, as in extrusion of metals) through a heated steel die where the resin impregnation and curing occur simultaneously.

The standard reference process flow produces profiles with:
- **Fiber volume fraction ($V_f$)** typically between **40% – 65%**
- **Resin content by weight** around **30% – 50%**
- **Production line speeds** ranging from **0.3 m/min to 1.5 m/min** depending on part thickness

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Board Examiner Definition (Verbatim expected answer):**
> *"Pultrusion is a continuous molding process in which dry reinforcing fibers (rovings, mats, or fabrics) are drawn through a resin impregnation bath, passed through a preforming station, and then pulled through a heated steel die where thermoset resin polymerization (cure) occurs, producing a cured composite profile of constant cross-section that is cut to length by an in-line saw."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Soap Bar Maker" Intuition

Imagine you have a long rope that you want to coat uniformly with wet clay and then dry it into a rigid rod.

- The **rope** = continuous fiber rovings (glass/carbon)
- The **clay bath** = liquid thermoset resin (polyester/vinyl ester)
- The **heated tunnel** = the heated steel die (oven)
- The **hand pulling the rope steadily** = the caterpillar/tandem pulling mechanism
- The **finished rigid rod** = the cured composite profile

Just as the clay hardens as the rope moves through the heated tunnel, in pultrusion the resin cures (cross-links) inside the die as the fibers are pulled continuously. Because pulling is continuous, theoretically you can make the profile **infinitely long** — only limited by transportation logistics!

## 1.3 Why Pultrusion Matters in Engineering

Pultruded profiles are used as a **direct replacement for steel, aluminum, and wood** in corrosive environments because:
1. They are **non-corrosive** (FRP does not rust)
2. They have a **strength-to-weight ratio** ~4× that of mild steel
3. They are **electrically non-conductive** and **thermally insulating**
4. **Lifecycle cost** is lower despite higher initial cost

> [!NOTE] **Standard Performance Metric:** A typical pultruded glass/polyester profile has a tensile strength of ~**207 MPa** and density of ~**1.8 g/cm³**, giving a specific strength of ~**115 kNm/kg** — far exceeding structural steel (specific strength ≈ 63 kNm/kg).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Sequential Stations of the Pultrusion Process

A pultrusion line is engineered as a *linear, sequential, continuous chain* of six stations:

| Station # | Station Name | Functional Role |
|-----------|--------------|-----------------|
| **1** | **Fiber Creel / Roving Rack** | Holds spools of reinforcement (rovings, mats, veils). Creel capacity typically 96–240 spools. |
| **2** | **Resin Impregnation Bath (Wet-Out Tank)** | Fibers pass through a liquid thermoset resin bath (polyester, vinyl ester, or epoxy). Viscosity **300–500 cP** at 25 °C. |
| **3** | **Preformer** | Shaped guide that removes excess resin, compacts the lay-up, and pre-shapes the bundle before entering the die. |
| **4** | **Heated Steel Die** | The heart of the process. Length **0.5 m to 1.5 m**, heated electrically to **120 °C – 180 °C**. Resin gels and cures here. |
| **5** | **Pulling Mechanism (Caterpillar / Reciprocating Clamps)** | Provides the tractive force. Pull force ranges from **5 kN to 100 kN** depending on cross-section. |
| **6** | **Cut-Off Saw (Flying or In-Line)** | Cuts the continuous profile to required lengths using an abrasive or diamond blade. |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Thermoset Only:** Pultrusion is fundamentally a **thermoset composite** process. Thermoplastic pultrusion exists but requires modified dies and is *not* the standard process taught under PEMET413.

## 2.2 Process Variables That Govern Quality

The pultrusion process has four *critical control variables* that must be tightly coupled:

1. **Die Temperature ($T_d$)** — determines cure rate.
2. **Pull Speed / Line Speed ($v_p$)** — typically **30–90 cm/min**; thicker parts → slower speed.
3. **Resin Reactivity / Gel Time ($t_{gel}$)** — usually **2–10 minutes** at cure temperature.
4. **Heated Die Length ($L_d$)** — must satisfy the cure requirement: residence time $t_{res} = L_d / v_p \geq t_{cure}$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---------------------|-----------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | **Residence (Cure) Time** | $t_{res} = \dfrac{L_d}{v_p}$ | seconds | Determines if part fully cures inside die |
| 2 | **Cure Constraint (Mandatory)** | $t_{res} \geq t_{cure}$ | s | Prevents under-cured (tacky) exit |
| 3 | **Pull Force (Empirical)** | $P = \mu \cdot p_c \cdot A_c$ | N | Force to overcome die-wall friction |
| 4 | **Die-Wall Friction Coefficient** | $\mu = 0.05$ to $0.20$ | dimensionless | Steel die vs cured resin |
| 5 | **Cavity Pressure** | $p_c = \dfrac{\eta \cdot v_p \cdot L_d}{d^2}$ | Pa | Resin back-pressure in tapered die |
| 6 | **Fiber Volume Fraction** | $V_f = \dfrac{A_f}{A_c} = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f/\rho_f + W_r/\rho_r}$ | dimensionless | Composite composition |
| 7 | **Rule of Mixtures (Longitudinal Modulus)** | $E_1 = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | GPa | Stiffness prediction |
| 8 | **Heat of Reaction Required** | $Q_{cure} = m_r \cdot H_r$ | J | Die thermal energy demand |
| 9 | **Degree of Cure (DSC derived)** | $\alpha = \dfrac{\Delta H_{residual}}{\Delta H_{total}}$ | dimensionless | Quality check |
| 10 | **Specific Pull Speed** | $v_p \leq \dfrac{L_d}{t_{cure,max}}$ | m/s | Maximum allowable line speed |

> [!WARNING] **Exam Tip (KTU):** Always state the **cure constraint inequality** $t_{res} \geq t_{cure}$ explicitly. Examiners award a separate mark for correctly identifying this as a *boundary condition*.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pultrusion is the **process of choice** for manufacturing:
- **Structural I-beams, channels, angles, and tubes** (civil construction)
- **FRP rebar** (replacement for steel rebar in chloride-exposed concrete)
- **Electrical ladder rails and third-rail covers** (railways)
- **Cooling tower structural members** (power plants)
- **Wind turbine blade root reinforcements** (renewable energy)
- **Tool handles, ladder rails, and walkways** (industrial)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Residence Time and the Cure Constraint

**Problem Setup:** A pultrusion die has a heated length $L_d = 1.2$ m. The resin system requires a minimum cure time of $t_{cure} = 240$ seconds at the die temperature to reach 95% gel conversion. Determine the maximum permissible line (pull) speed.

**Step 1 — State the governing relationship.**

The time any material element spends inside the heated die is the residence time:

$$t_{res} = \dfrac{L_d}{v_p}$$

where $v_p$ is the constant line speed. For the part to be fully cured, we require:

$$t_{res} \geq t_{cure}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_d &= 1.2 \; \text{m} \\
t_{cure} &= 240 \; \text{s} \\
\dfrac{1.2}{v_p} &\geq 240
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the maximum pull speed.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_p &\leq \dfrac{L_d}{t_{cure}} \\
v_p &\leq \dfrac{1.2 \text{ m}}{240 \text{ s}} \\
v_p &\leq 0.005 \; \text{m/s} \\
v_p &\leq 5 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to engineering units (m/min) used in industry.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_p &\leq 0.005 \;\dfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}} \times 60 \;\dfrac{\text{s}}{\text{min}} \\
v_p &\leq 0.30 \;\text{m/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Result:** Maximum line speed = **0.30 m/min** (or 30 cm/min). Anything faster and the profile exits the die under-cured and tacky.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Pull Force Using the Die-Wall Friction Model

**Problem Setup:** A pultruded rectangular profile has a wetted perimeter inside the die of $p_w = 0.28$ m and a die length of $L_d = 1.2$ m. The cavity pressure is $p_c = 0.6$ MPa. The friction coefficient between the curing resin and the steel die wall is $\mu = 0.10$. Calculate the required pull force $P$.

**Step 1 — Compute the contact area between resin and die wall.**

The resin touches the die wall along the wetted perimeter for the full die length:

$$
A_c = p_w \cdot L_d = 0.28 \text{ m} \times 1.2 \text{ m} = 0.336 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the friction force equation.**

The pull force required to overcome viscous friction at the die wall is:

$$P = \mu \cdot p_c \cdot A_c$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and compute.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 0.10 \times (0.6 \times 10^6 \; \text{Pa}) \times 0.336 \; \text{m}^2 \\
P &= 0.10 \times 0.6 \times 0.336 \times 10^6 \; \text{N} \\
P &= 0.02016 \times 10^6 \; \text{N} \\
P &= 20{,}160 \; \text{N} \\
P &= 20.16 \; \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Interpretation:** A pull force of ~**20 kN** is well within the capacity of standard caterpillar pullers (which deliver 5–100 kN). If the calculation exceeds the puller's rating, the process is infeasible — the operator must reduce $v_p$ or $p_c$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Fiber Volume Fraction from Masses

**Problem Setup:** A pultruded rod uses $W_f = 180$ g of E-glass fibers ($\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³) and $W_r = 90$ g of polyester resin ($\rho_r = 1.20$ g/cm³). Determine the fiber volume fraction $V_f$.

**Step 1 — Convert weights to volumes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_f^{vol} &= \dfrac{W_f}{\rho_f} = \dfrac{180}{2.55} = 70.588 \text{ cm}^3 \\
V_r^{vol} &= \dfrac{W_r}{\rho_r} = \dfrac{90}{1.20} = 75.000 \text{ cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total volume and the fiber volume fraction.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{total} &= V_f^{vol} + V_r^{vol} = 70.588 + 75.000 = 145.588 \text{ cm}^3 \\
V_f &= \dfrac{V_f^{vol}}{V_{total}} = \dfrac{70.588}{145.588}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Final numerical result.**

$$
V_f = 0.4849 \quad \text{or} \quad V_f \approx 48.5\%
$$

> [!NOTE] This $V_f$ lies within the standard pultrusion range of **40% – 65%** — confirming that the formulation is feasible.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix for Pultrusion Feasibility

| Process Input | Feasible Range | Symptom if Out of Range |
|---------------|----------------|------------------------|
| Die Temperature | 120 °C – 180 °C | Low → under-cure; High → thermal cracking |
| Pull Speed | 0.3 – 1.5 m/min | High → voids, under-cure; Low → economic loss |
| Resin Viscosity (wet-out) | 300 – 500 cP | High → poor impregnation; Low → resin drips |
| Fiber Tension | 5 – 20 N/roving | High → fiber breakage; Low → wavy fibers |
| Die Taper Angle | 0.5° – 2° | High → high pull force; Low → resin back-flow |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pultrusion Process Flow Diagram (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["FIBER CREEL\n96 to 240 Spools\nRovings / Mats"] --> node2["RESIN IMPREGNATION\nWet-Out Tank\nViscosity 300 to 500 cP"]
    node2 --> node3["PREFORMER\nResin Squeeze\nLay-up Compaction"]
    node3 --> node4["HEATED DIE\nSteel Tooling\n120 to 180 C"]
    node4 --> node5["COOLING ZONE\nAmbient Air\nTapered Guide"]
    node5 --> node6["PULLER\nCaterpillar Clamps\n5 to 100 kN"]
    node6 --> node7["CUT-OFF SAW\nAbrasive Blade\nAuto Indexing"]
    node7 --> node8["FINISHED PROFILE\nConstant Cross-Section\nCut to Length"]
    node4 -. Heat .-> node4
    node6 -. Tractive Force .-> node4
```

## 4.2 Internal Die Mechanism — Cure Progression Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["DIE ENTRY\nResin Liquid\nAlpha approx 0"] --> nodeB["GELATION ZONE\nAlpha approx 0.3\nTemperature Rising"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["RISE ZONE\nAlpha 0.3 to 0.8\nExothermic Peak"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["CURE ZONE\nAlpha approx 0.95\nSolidification"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["DIE EXIT\nAlpha greater than 0.95\nRigid Profile"]
```

## 4.3 Process Control Feedback Loop (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Set Point\nPull Speed v_p"] --> CMP["CONTROLLER\nPLC / PID"]
    S2["Set Point\nDie Temp T_d"] --> CMP
    CMP --> ACT["ACTUATOR\nHeaters and Puller Motor"]
    ACT --> PROC["PULTRUSION DIE\nProcess Output"]
    PROC --> SENS["SENSORS\nThermocouple and Load Cell"]
    SENS --> FB["FEEDBACK\nT_actual and P_actual"]
    FB --> CMP
```

## 4.4 Cross-Section of a Typical Pultrusion Die (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TOP["DIE TOP\nHeated Platten\n120 to 180 C"] --- CAV["DIE CAVITY\nWetted Perimeter p_w\nTaper Angle 1 to 2 deg"]
    CAV --- BOT["DIE BOTTOM\nHeated Platten\nMatched to Top"]
    IN["RESIN WET FIBERS\nEntry at Alpha = 0"] --> CAV
    CAV --> OUT["CURED PROFILE\nExit at Alpha approx 0.95"]
    CAV -. Heat Transfer .-> TOP
    CAV -. Heat Transfer .-> BOT
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
**Define pultrusion. Why is it classified as a continuous manufacturing process?**

**Model Answer:**
Pultrusion is a continuous, closed-mold composite manufacturing process in which dry reinforcing fibers are drawn through a resin impregnation bath and then pulled through a heated steel die, where the thermoset resin undergoes polymerization (cure) to form a rigid composite profile of constant cross-section.

It is classified as *continuous* because:
1. The reinforcing fibers and resin are fed continuously from spools and tanks without interruption.
2. The pulling mechanism provides uninterrupted tractive force.
3. Cutting is performed only at the end as a secondary operation — the *process itself* never stops.
4. Theoretically, profiles of unlimited length can be produced in a single run.

*(3 marks — full credit for stating "continuous + closed-mold + constant cross-section + heated die cure")*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**
**List any four advantages of pultrusion over hand lay-up.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Higher fiber volume fraction** (40–65%) achievable versus ~25–30% in hand lay-up.
2. **Consistent and repeatable** part quality — closed-mold process eliminates ambient contamination.
3. **Higher production rate** — continuous process suitable for mass production.
4. **Lower labor cost** — automated pulling and cutting versus manual rolling.
5. **Better surface finish** on all sides (molded finish, not contact finish only).
6. **Lower resin wastage** since excess is recirculated from the wet-out tank.

*(Any 4 × 0.75 = 3 marks)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Q, Module 4]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the pultrusion process. Label all the major stations and state the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The pultrusion process consists of **six sequential stations** arranged in a linear continuous line:

1. **Fiber Creel (Station 1):** Holds multiple spools of reinforcement — rovings (unidirectional fibers), continuous strand mats, and surface veils. Each roving provides axial strength; mats provide transverse strength.
2. **Resin Impregnation Bath (Station 2):** Fibers pass through a tank filled with liquid thermoset resin (polyester / vinyl ester / epoxy). Fibers become fully wet-out; resin viscosity is controlled at **300–500 cP**.
3. **Preformer (Station 3):** A shaped guide that progressively compacts the wet fiber bundle into the approximate cross-sectional shape of the final part. It also squeezes out excess resin.
4. **Heated Die (Station 4):** The most critical station — a matched steel tool heated to **120–180 °C**. The resin gels, exotherms, and cures inside the die. Length of die governs line speed.
5. **Cooling Zone and Pulling Mechanism (Station 5):** After exiting the die, the profile cools. A caterpillar or reciprocating clamp puller applies a continuous tractive force of **5–100 kN**.
6. **Cut-Off Saw (Station 6):** An abrasive/diamond-bladed saw cuts the profile to required lengths automatically as it reaches the cut-off station.

[Diagram: same as the block diagram in Section 4.1 should be redrawn by the student.]

**Key Functional Chain:** *Creel → Wet-out → Preform → Cure → Pull → Cut*

*(Full 7 marks for: block diagram 3 marks + station-wise explanation with at least one functional parameter per station = 4 marks)*

---

**(b)** A pultrusion die has length $L_d = 1.5$ m. The resin system requires a minimum cure time of $t_{cure} = 300$ s. Determine the maximum permissible line speed in m/min. The actual pull force required is $P = 25$ kN, given $\mu = 0.12$ and cavity pressure $p_c = 0.55$ MPa. Verify whether the wetted perimeter of $p_w = 0.30$ m is feasible. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Maximum permissible line speed.**

From the cure constraint:
$$
\begin{aligned}
v_p &\leq \dfrac{L_d}{t_{cure}} = \dfrac{1.5 \text{ m}}{300 \text{ s}} = 0.005 \text{ m/s} \\
v_p &\leq 0.005 \times 60 = 0.30 \text{ m/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Maximum permissible line speed = 0.30 m/min.** (2 marks)

**Step 2 — Compute the contact area for the given pull force.**

Using $P = \mu \cdot p_c \cdot A_c$, where $A_c = p_w \cdot L_d$:

$$
A_c = 0.30 \text{ m} \times 1.5 \text{ m} = 0.45 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the theoretical pull force.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{theory} &= \mu \cdot p_c \cdot A_c \\
&= 0.12 \times (0.55 \times 10^6) \times 0.45 \\
&= 0.12 \times 0.55 \times 0.45 \times 10^6 \\
&= 0.0297 \times 10^6 \; \text{N} \\
&= 29{,}700 \; \text{N} = 29.7 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Feasibility comparison.**

The actual required pull force is $P_{actual} = 25$ kN.
The theoretical required pull force is $P_{theory} = 29.7$ kN.

Since $P_{actual} \; (25 \text{ kN}) \;<\; P_{theory} \; (29.7 \text{ kN})$, the available pull force is **less than** the theoretical minimum required. Therefore the design is **infeasible** with the current wetted perimeter — the operator must either increase $p_w$ (use a wider profile), increase $\mu$ adjustment (different release agent), or accept a slower line speed.

> *(3 marks for steps 1–3, 1 mark for feasibility conclusion)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Compare pultrusion and filament winding. State at least **five points of difference** with respect to part geometry, production rate, fiber orientation, resin system, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| # | Parameter | Pultrusion | Filament Winding |
|---|-----------|------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Part Geometry** | Constant cross-section only (rods, beams, tubes) | Axisymmetric parts (pipes, tanks, pressure vessels) |
| 2 | **Production Rate** | Continuous; very high (kg/h) | Semi-continuous; moderate (part-by-part) |
| 3 | **Fiber Orientation** | Predominantly 0° (axial) with mats | Helical + hoop patterns; geodesic winding |
| 4 | **Resin System** | Wet bath or resin injection | Wet winding or prepreg tow |
| 5 | **Die Requirement** | Heated steel die required | Rotating mandrel (no die) |
| 6 | **Applications** | Structural profiles, FRP rebar | Pipes, rocket motor casings, chemical tanks |
| 7 | **Capital Cost** | Higher (die cost) | Lower (mandrel cost) |

*(7 marks: 1 mark per comparison point × 5, 2 marks for any valid additional justification)*

---

**(b)** A pultruded composite rod uses E-glass fibers ($\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, $E_f = 72$ GPa) and polyester resin ($\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa). The fiber weight is $W_f = 240$ g and resin weight $W_r = 120$ g. Calculate **(i)** the fiber volume fraction, **(ii)** the matrix volume fraction, and **(iii)** the longitudinal Young's modulus $E_1$ using the rule of mixtures. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute volumes.**

$$
V_f^{vol} = \dfrac{W_f}{\rho_f} = \dfrac{240}{2.55} = 94.118 \text{ cm}^3
$$

$$
V_m^{vol} = \dfrac{W_r}{\rho_m} = \dfrac{120}{1.20} = 100.000 \text{ cm}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Fiber volume fraction.**

$$
V_f = \dfrac{94.118}{94.118 + 100.000} = \dfrac{94.118}{194.118} = 0.4849 \approx 48.5\%
$$

*(2 marks for V_f)*

**Step 3 — Matrix volume fraction.**

$$
V_m = 1 - V_f = 1 - 0.4849 = 0.5151 \approx 51.5\%
$$

*(1 mark for V_m)*

**Step 4 — Longitudinal modulus (Rule of Mixtures).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_1 &= E_f V_f + E_m V_m \\
&= (72)(0.4849) + (3.5)(0.5151) \\
&= 34.913 + 1.803 \\
&= 36.72 \text{ GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(4 marks for E_1 with full substitution and arithmetic)*

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse weight fraction with volume fraction.** Many students directly plug weights into the rule-of-mixtures formula and lose 3–4 marks. Always convert mass → volume first via density.
> 2. **Always state $V_m = 1 - V_f$** explicitly; do not leave it implicit.
> 3. In the pull-force derivation, **forgetting units conversion** of MPa to Pa ($\times 10^6$) is the single most common error — examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 4. In the residence-time problem, **forgetting to convert m/s to m/min** (or vice-versa) loses 1 mark in industry-style problems.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Definition:** Continuous closed-mold process for constant cross-section FRP profiles.
- ✅ **Six Stations:** Creel → Wet-out → Preformer → Heated Die → Puller → Cut-off Saw.
- ✅ **Cure Constraint:** $t_{res} = L_d / v_p \geq t_{cure}$ (memorize and quote explicitly).
- ✅ **Pull Force Equation:** $P = \mu \cdot p_c \cdot A_c$; typical range 5–100 kN.
- ✅ **Standard Process Window:** Die 120–180 °C; Pull 0.3–1.5 m/min; $V_f$ 40–65%.
- ✅ **Only thermoset resins** in standard pultrusion (polyester, vinyl ester, epoxy).
- ✅ **Rule of Mixtures (longitudinal):** $E_1 = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$.
- ✅ **Fiber Volume Fraction Formula:** $V_f = (W_f/\rho_f) / [(W_f/\rho_f) + (W_r/\rho_r)]$.
- ✅ **High specific strength** makes pultruded FRP a direct replacement for steel in corrosive environments.
- ✅ **Resin viscosity during wet-out** must be controlled at 300–500 cP.
- ✅ **Pull force increases with** $L_d$, $v_p$, $p_c$, and $\mu$.
- ✅ **Die taper angle** is critical — typically 0.5° to 2°.
- ✅ **Filament winding differs** by producing axisymmetric parts on a mandrel; no heated die required.
- ✅ **Hand lay-up gives lower $V_f$** (~25–30%) compared to pultrusion (40–65%).
- ✅ **Always convert units** (MPa → Pa, m/s → m/min) before computing final answers.
- ✅ **Pultrusion word origin:** **PULL + EXTRUSION** — quote this in definitions for easy marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# Module 4: Lanxide Process — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lanxide Process (Directed Metal Oxidation — DMO):** The *Lanxide process*, also formally known as **Directed Metal Oxidation (DMO)** or the **DIMOX™ process**, is a proprietary ceramic-matrix composite (CMC) fabrication technique developed by **Lanxide Corporation (USA, 1985–86)** in which a molten parent metal (e.g., aluminum, titanium, zirconium, silicon) is **reactively infiltrated** by an oxidant (typically atmospheric oxygen) through a preformed ceramic *preform* or filler bed, such that the growing metal-oxide reaction product *replicates* the geometry of the preform and forms a dense, near-net-shape ceramic or ceramic-metal composite without the application of external pressure.

The process falls under the broader umbrella of **reactive melt infiltration (RMI)** and is classified in the KTU 2024 scheme under *Liquid-phase fabrication of Metal Matrix and Ceramic Matrix Composites*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two commercial variants of the Lanxide process exist:**
> 1. **DIMOX™ (Directed Metal Oxidation)** — produces ceramic-matrix composites (e.g., Al₂O₃/Al).
> 2. **PRIMEX™ (Pressureless Metal Infiltration)** — produces metal-matrix composites (e.g., SiC/Al).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain-English Explanation)

Imagine you have a **sponge (the preform)** made of ceramic particles — sand, fibers, whiskers — packed into the shape of an engine piston. Now, instead of pouring cement into the sponge, you **place a block of aluminum metal on top** and heat the assembly in air.

As the aluminum melts, it gets "thirsty" for oxygen. The molten metal **crawls upward through the sponge channels**, and wherever it contacts air, it spontaneously converts to aluminum oxide (Al₂O₃) — the same stuff as sapphire. But here's the trick: the **oxide grows outward from the metal, fills the gaps between the ceramic filler particles, and locks them together into a single solid piece** that exactly mirrors the original sponge's shape.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of a *candle wick dipped in wax*. The liquid wax climbs the wick by capillary action and solidifies. Similarly, in the Lanxide process, molten aluminum **wicks upward through the preform** by capillary action through the interconnected pore network, leaving behind a solid oxide "skeleton" that bonds everything together.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical & Process Constants

| Parameter | Typical Value / Range | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Process Temperature | **900 °C – 1400 °C** | Above parent metal melting point |
| Atmosphere | **Air (O₂)** for DIMOX; **N₂** for AlN/Al composites | Determines matrix chemistry |
| Growth Velocity | **~10⁻⁸ to 10⁻⁶ m/s** | Slow, controllable oxidation |
| External Pressure | **0 Pa (pressureless)** | Key advantage — no tooling |
| Typical Parent Metals | **Al, Si, Ti, Zr, Sn, Pb, Mg, Fe, Ni, Co** | Determines oxide/carbide/nitride |
| Volumetric Expansion | **+ 4% to + 30%** | Fills pore volume of preform |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** Students often confuse the Lanxide process with **squeeze casting / pressure infiltration**. The hallmark of Lanxide/DMO is that **NO external pressure is applied** — the driving force is the **chemical potential gradient of oxygen** and the **28% volume expansion** when metal converts to oxide, which intrinsically fills the pores.

---

## 1.4 Reaction Fundamentals (Quick Snapshot)

The generic global reaction for the aluminum-based DIMOX process is:

$$2\,\text{Al}_{(l)} \;+\; \tfrac{3}{2}\,\text{O}_{2(g)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Al}_2\text{O}_{3(s)} \quad \Delta G^\circ_{1000^{\circ}\text{C}} \approx -880\,\text{kJ/mol}$$

Because the molar volume of the oxide is **larger** than the parent metal, the reaction product **grows outward** from the original metal surface into the preform, **continuously exposing fresh metal** to oxygen — a self-sustaining process.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 4: Lanxide Process — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

The directed metal oxidation process can be decomposed into **seven sequential, simultaneous, and synergistic steps**. KTU examiners frequently award marks for explicitly listing these stages.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the metal "crawl" rather than just form a passive surface oxide film?**
> On aluminum, the native Al₂O₃ layer is *protective* and halts further oxidation at room temperature. However, in the presence of certain **dopant elements** (e.g., Mg, Si, Ge, Li) and a connected preform that *consumes* the oxide as it forms, the protective layer is disrupted. The molten metal can then wick through microchannels and continue to oxidize — this is the **"directed"** part of the process.

### The Seven Logical Steps of DIMOX

1. **Preform Preparation:** A porous body is fabricated from the reinforcement phase — typically **SiC, Al₂O₃, B₄C, ZrO₂, TiB₂, mullite, or carbon fibers** — using standard ceramic forming techniques (slip casting, pressing, etc.). The preform must be **open-pored (typically 30–60% porosity)** to allow metal infiltration.

2. **Dopant Alloying:** The parent metal is alloyed with **0.5 – 5 wt%** of a *dopant* (commonly **Mg, Si, or Ge**). These dopants preferentially oxidize and disrupt the protective Al₂O₃ layer, sustaining the reaction. **Mg is the most commercially important dopant.**

3. **Assembly:** The parent metal ingot is placed in contact with (or above) the preform inside a refractory crucible. The dopant-rich alloy *wets* the preform's surface.

4. **Heating & Melting:** The assembly is heated in a furnace (typically in air) above the parent metal's melting point. **No external pressure or vacuum is applied.**

5. **Capillary Wicking:** Molten metal is drawn into the preform's interconnected pore network by **capillary forces** (analogous to molten wax in a wick). The capillary pressure driving infiltration is given by the **Young-Laplace equation** (see Section 2.3).

6. **Directed Oxidation at the Growth Front:** At the advancing metal–preform–air interface (called the *growth front*), the metal reacts with the gas-phase oxidant. The oxide product nucleates, grows, and *bonds* to the reinforcement particles.

7. **Net-Shape Consolidation:** Because the molar volume of the oxide exceeds that of the parent metal, the reaction product **expands** and fills the preform pore space. The final composite is a **near-net-shape** part that requires minimal post-machining.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Driving Force | KTU Conceptual Hook |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Preform preparation | Geometric replication | *Without a preform, the oxide is just a powder — useless.* |
| Dopant alloying | Lower oxide coherency; promote wetting | *Without dopant, Al₂O₃ film self-passivates.* |
| Heating above T_m | Activate reaction kinetics | *Solid metal does not flow.* |
| Capillary wicking | Surface tension (γ > 0) | *Same principle as oil rising in a lamp wick.* |
| Directed oxidation | Free energy of oxide formation | *Highly negative ΔG is the engine of the process.* |
| Volumetric expansion | Pilling-Bedworth ratio > 1 | *Oxide volume > metal volume → pore filling.* |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Equation Bank

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board-Favorite Equations for the Lanxide Process.** Memorize these — they appear in derivations, numericals, and Part-A questions.

| # | Equation | Description / Variables | Application |
| :-- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\Delta P_{\text{cap}} = \dfrac{2\,\gamma_{LV}\,\cos\theta}{r}$ | **Young–Laplace capillary pressure.** $\gamma_{LV}$ = liquid–vapor surface tension (N/m), $\theta$ = contact angle, $r$ = effective pore radius (m). | Infiltration kinetics; determines pore-size limits. |
| 2 | $\dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{k \cdot P_{O_2}^n}{[1 + \exp(-\frac{E_a}{RT})]}$ | **Oxidation growth law** (parabolic, modified). $k$ = rate constant, $n$ ≈ 0.5 (parabolic), $E_a$ = activation energy, $P_{O_2}$ = partial pressure of oxygen. | Predicts growth-front velocity. |
| 3 | $V_{\text{Pilling-Bedworth}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{oxide}}}{V_{\text{metal}}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{oxide}} \cdot \rho_{\text{metal}}}{n \cdot M_{\text{metal}} \cdot \rho_{\text{oxide}}}$ | **Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR).** $M$ = molar mass, $\rho$ = density, $n$ = moles of metal per mole of oxide. | Determines whether oxide is *protective* ($1 < \text{PBR} < 2$) or *non-protective* ($\text{PBR} > 2$). For Al → Al₂O₃, PBR ≈ **1.28**. |
| 4 | $\%\,V_{\text{reinforcement}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{preform}}}{V_{\text{preform}} + \Delta V_{\text{oxidation}}}$ | **Final reinforcement volume fraction** — a *design equation* for the engineer. | Tailoring composite microstructure. |
| 5 | $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T\,\Delta S^\circ$ | **Gibbs free energy of oxidation.** | Establishes thermodynamic feasibility (must be strongly negative). |
| 6 | $v_{\text{growth}} \propto \sqrt{\dfrac{D_{\text{O}} \cdot C_{\text{O}}}{\delta}}$ | **Wagner oxidation theory** (simplified). $D_{\text{O}}$ = diffusivity of O in oxide, $C_{\text{O}}$ = oxygen concentration, $\delta$ = oxide thickness. | Microkinetic model. |
| 7 | $k_p = k_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | **Arrhenius rate constant.** | Used in numericals to find oxidation rate vs. temperature. |

> [!TIP]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE (per KTU system requirements):** In all written answers, use the symbol `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` for absolute values — **never the vertical bar `\vert x \vert` inside a markdown table**, as it breaks the table renderer.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Lanxide/DIMOX process is industrially significant for manufacturing:

- **Aerospace turbine components** — Al₂O₃/Al composites (Lanxide's original target) exhibit excellent thermal-shock resistance and damage tolerance.
- **Automotive pistons and brake rotors** — Wear-resistant, lightweight.
- **Heat exchangers and radiant burner plates** — Al₂O₃-Al composite has high thermal conductivity (k ≈ 30 W/m·K).
- **Cutting tool inserts** — AlN/Al and Al₂O₃/SiC composites.
- **Electronic substrates** — Low dielectric loss, matched thermal expansion.
- **Armor tiles** — Hard, lightweight, multi-hit capable.

The *pressureless* nature eliminates expensive dies, autoclaves, and hot presses — translating to **lower capital cost** compared to squeeze casting or hot pressing of CMCs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Module 4: Lanxide Process — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Pilling–Bedworth Ratio for Al → Al₂O₃

The **Pilling–Bedworth Ratio (PBR)** is the most-tested KTU derivation on the Lanxide topic. It explains **why** the oxide is *protective* on aluminum but allows directed growth when disrupted by dopants.

### Given Data (for the KTU 2024 numerical problem)

- Molar mass of Al: $M_{\text{Al}} = 26.98\,\text{g/mol}$
- Density of Al: $\rho_{\text{Al}} = 2.70\,\text{g/cm}^3$
- Molar mass of Al₂O₃: $M_{\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3} = 101.96\,\text{g/mol}$
- Density of Al₂O₃: $\rho_{\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3} = 3.99\,\text{g/cm}^3$
- Reaction: $2\,\text{Al} + \tfrac{3}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$, so $n = 2$ moles of metal per mole of oxide.

### Step 1: Write the PBR formula

$$\text{PBR} = \frac{V_{\text{oxide}}}{V_{\text{metal}}} = \frac{M_{\text{oxide}} \,/\, \rho_{\text{oxide}}}{n \cdot M_{\text{metal}} \,/\, \rho_{\text{metal}}}$$

### Step 2: Substitute the values into the numerator and denominator

$$\text{PBR} = \frac{101.96\,\text{g/mol} \,/\, 3.99\,\text{g/cm}^3}{2 \cdot 26.98\,\text{g/mol} \,/\, 2.70\,\text{g/cm}^3}$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the numerator

$$V_{\text{oxide}} = \frac{101.96}{3.99} = 25.55\,\text{cm}^3/\text{mol}$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the denominator

$$V_{\text{metal}} = \frac{2 \times 26.98}{2.70} = \frac{53.96}{2.70} = 19.99\,\text{cm}^3/\text{mol}$$

### Step 5: Compute the ratio

$$\text{PBR} = \frac{25.55}{19.99} \approx 1.278$$

### Step 6: Physical interpretation

$$\boxed{\text{PBR}_{\text{Al}/\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3} \approx 1.28}$$

Because the ratio lies between **1 and 2**, the oxide is **protective** — it forms a continuous, adherent film that halts further oxidation at low temperatures. In the Lanxide process, this protective layer is intentionally *disrupted* by **Mg dopant (typically 3 wt%)**, which:

1. Forms MgO and MgAl₂O₄ spinel at the oxide–metal interface, breaking film continuity.
2. Reduces the interfacial energy and improves wetting of the Al on the preform.
3. Sustains outward transport of Al ions and inward transport of O²⁻, allowing the reaction to continue for hours or days.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Capillary Infiltration Time (Washburn Equation)

The **depth of molten aluminum infiltration** into a cylindrical pore of radius $r$ is governed by the **Washburn equation** (combined Poiseuille flow + Laplace pressure).

### Derivation

**Step 1: Net driving capillary pressure**

$$\Delta P = \frac{2\,\gamma_{LV}\,\cos\theta}{r}$$

**Step 2: Volumetric flow rate in a cylindrical capillary (Hagen–Poiseuille)**

$$Q = \frac{\pi\, r^4}{8\,\eta} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{\pi\, r^3\, \gamma_{LV}\,\cos\theta}{4\,\eta\, L}$$

**Step 3: Express $Q$ in terms of penetration depth $L$**

Since $Q = \pi r^2 \, dL/dt$:

$$\pi r^2 \frac{dL}{dt} = \frac{\pi r^3 \, \gamma_{LV}\,\cos\theta}{4\,\eta\, L}$$

**Step 4: Separate variables and integrate**

$$L\, dL = \frac{r\,\gamma_{LV}\,\cos\theta}{4\,\eta}\, dt$$

$$\int_0^{L} L\, dL = \frac{r\,\gamma_{LV}\,\cos\theta}{4\,\eta} \int_0^{t} dt$$

### Step 5: Final Washburn equation (boxed)

$$\boxed{L^2 = \frac{r\,\gamma_{LV}\,\cos\theta}{2\,\eta}\, t \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad L(t) = \sqrt{\frac{r\,\gamma_{LV}\,\cos\theta}{2\,\eta}\, t}}$$

This is the equation examiners expect in Part B for "calculate time to infiltrate a 5-mm preform" type problems.

**Typical values for molten Al / SiC preform:**

$$\gamma_{LV} \approx 0.85\,\text{N/m},\quad \theta \approx 30^{\circ},\quad r \approx 5\,\mu\text{m},\quad \eta \approx 1.2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa·s}$$

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Board Question)

> **Problem:** A Lanxide/DIMOX preform of thickness **8 mm** contains cylindrical pores of effective radius **10 μm**. The molten Al–3%Mg alloy has surface tension $\gamma_{LV} = 0.9\,\text{N/m}$, contact angle $\theta = 25^{\circ}$, and viscosity $\eta = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa·s}$. Determine the **time required to completely infiltrate the preform** by capillary action alone. State one assumption.

### Solution

**Step 1: Use the Washburn equation**

$$L^2 = \frac{r\,\gamma_{LV}\,\cos\theta}{2\,\eta}\, t$$

**Step 2: Compute $\cos\theta$**

$$\cos(25^{\circ}) = 0.906$$

**Step 3: Substitute numerical values**

$$L^2 = \frac{(10 \times 10^{-6})(0.9)(0.906)}{2(1.5 \times 10^{-3})}\, t$$

$$L^2 = \frac{8.154 \times 10^{-6}}{3.0 \times 10^{-3}}\, t = (2.718 \times 10^{-3})\, t$$

**Step 4: Solve for $t$ with $L = 8\,\text{mm} = 8 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$**

$$t = \frac{L^2}{2.718 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{(8 \times 10^{-3})^2}{2.718 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{6.4 \times 10^{-5}}{2.718 \times 10^{-3}}$$

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{t \approx 0.0235\,\text{s} \approx 24\,\text{ms}}$$

**Assumption:** Pore radius is constant, isothermal isothermal conditions, $\theta$ and $\eta$ are time-independent, and oxidation rate is much slower than infiltration rate (otherwise the growth front sets the kinetic limit, not capillarity).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When the *infiltration time* is shorter than the *oxidation time*, the process is **infiltration-limited**; when oxidation is slower, it is **reaction-limited**. The DIMOX process is typically *reaction-limited* — the growth front velocity (~1–5 mm/h) sets the overall cycle time of hours.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Mermaid diagram below follows the **Node Identifier Alpha Rule** — every node ID starts with a letter prefix and is purely alphanumeric, with no reserved words used as identifiers. All labels are clean uppercase text with no markdown tags inside double quotes.
